title: Neurodiverse Staff Network
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Neurodiverse Staff Network About the Network Joining the Network Explore
body: Co-Chairs: Stephanie Campbell and Kirsten FarrellSponsor: Sherria HoskinsContact the Co-Chairs: neurodiversestaffnetwork@port.ac.ukJoin the Network The Neurodiverse Staff Network is an inclusive, supportive and welcoming community open to, and for, all members of staff at the University who wish to support and engage in learning and understanding of neurodiversity, including neurodivergent staff and allies. No disclosures or diagnoses are required to join the network. The network is co-chaired by Stephanie Campbell and Kirsten Farrell, with Sherria Hoskins as our UEB sponsor. Neurodiversity refers to the different ways the brain can work and interpret information. Some people refer to themselves as Neurodivergent (a term coined by Kassiane Asasumasu) meaning “neurologically divergent from typical,” which includes “Autistic people. ADHD people. People with learning disabilities. Epileptic people. People with mental illnesses. People with MS or Parkinsons or apraxia or cerebral palsy or dyspraxia or no specific diagnosis”. Some neurodivergent people identify as disabled, others do not. Those who do not have a neurological difference are sometimes described as neurotypical. Both neurodivergent and neurotypical people are part of neurodiversity. offer staff a place to share and support each other based on our shared experiences provide a space for collaboration to effect positive changes to attitudes and environments that affect our ability to participate fully in University life (and personal daily life) opportunities to share information to increase awareness and understanding of what neurodiversity actually is, via news about events, conferences and sources of support join in building a more diverse and inclusive university community, and to enhance support for and visibility of disabled and neurodiverse colleagues. By joining the network, staff will become part of a community with the opportunity to make a real difference. You’ll benefit from peer-to-peer support in a safe space and be signposted to internal and external support and advice. title: Parent and Carer Staff Network
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Parents and Carers Staff Network About the Network Joining the network Explore
body: Co-Chairs: Emma Scott-Ward, Emily Parry and Marion MoffattSponsor: Anne MurphyContact the Co-Chairs: parentandcarerstaffnetwork@port.ac.ukJoin the Network The Parents and Carers Staff Network is an inclusive community for everyone with parenting and caring responsibilities, and anyone with an interest in these areas. Co-Chaired by Emma Scott-Ward and Emily Parry, with Anne Murphy as our UEB sponsor, the network offers guidance, activities, and support, plus a forum to share information and events, and to provide feedback to the University on things that affect us. Share information and provide informal support and advice Encourage mutual support and networking Lobby the University to fulfil its aspirations to provide a flexible, family friendly working environment Raise awareness at all levels of issues facing staff with parenting and caring responsibilities by influencing university consultations, action plans and policies that impact on parents and carers. Please fill out this form, or email parentandcarersstaffnetwork@port.ac.uk, and we’ll add you to the chat space in Google. title: Women's Staff Network
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Women's Staff Network About the Network Joining the network Explore
body: Chair: Anna Vaernes and Kelly JackSponsor: Fiona HnatowContact the Co-Chairs: womensstaffnetwork@port.ac.ukJoin the Network The network aims to represent, inspire, support, and empower all those who identify as women at the University. It is member-led and welcomes all staff who identify as women or non-binary to join. We support, represent and advocate for women members across the University, both within Academic and Professional Services. We are planning to host three to four events annually, including networking sessions, guest lectures, and other events as suggested by our members. We also have the opportunity to take part in relevant policy updates and be included in various committees within the University. If you are interested in joining the Network, please fill in this Google form or alternatively, email us at womensstaffnetwork@port.ac.uk. title: What the Team Does
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion What do the Equity, Wellbeing & Inclusion Team do? Engagement and Raising Awareness Informing Policies and Practices Training and Learning Data Collection, Monitoring and Evaluation How can you get involved in EDI at the University? Explore
body: We provide expert consultancy, enabling colleagues to play their part in creating a working, learning, cultural and social community where all colleagues and students feel they belong, are able to reach their full potential, and are treated with dignity and respect. Support our Staff Networks Publish a monthly EDI bulletin Organise events to mark cultural, religious and awareness events Guide department/faculties in developing and implementing EDI objectives and actions Support Athena Swan and Race Equality Charter submissions Identify University-wide and local EDI training needs Advise on commissioning trainers Deliver training including Intro to Equality Impact Assessments Publish annual staff and student EDI reports You can help shape the University's culture and community in many ways. title: EDI News
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Equity, Diversity and Inclusion Newsletter 2025 March January 2024 December November October September June May April March February January 2023 December November Explore
body: Why not sign up for our monthly Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Bulletin to receive a monthly dose of EDI knowledge and a list of important dates for various cultures, religions, and EDI-relevant events? You will also find a list of our previous publications below for you to peruse at your leisure. Each of these has a brief list of the topics covered. We are now moving to a bulletin every two months, meaning this article contains even more than before! Women's history, race in the workplace, endometriosis, LGBTQ+ history, striving towards equality in STEMM, a Stonewall highlight, and some urgent information about events happening this week! Starting the year off strong, the EDI bulletin covers a plethora of topics. Learn about various Lunar New Years, discover what it means to be an anti-racist for Martin Luther King Jr. (MLK) Day, engage in the significance of World Braille Day, an intro to the lead PGM Ambassador, a vital reflection for Holocaust Memorial Day and a quick recap of the importance of disclosing staff demographic information. As the year comes to an end, we have filled this month's bulletin with some festive information and more Disability History Month articles, including the last 'Friday Five' tips, talk about Wicca and some of its misconceptions, explore the concept of digital blackface, and lastly, a light read on fun Christmas traditions from around the world. This edition of the EDI bulletin aims to highlight Disability History Month (DHM), sharing a timeline of their civil rights activism. We will also feature a series of ‘Friday Five’ tips around different aspects of disability. This edition also shares the success of our Diwali event, collaborative pieces by LGBTQ+ co-chair Kathryn Thompson talking about the importance of the Trans Day of Remembrance and Chenda Cox sharing crucial information on how to help people experiencing a seizure. It's Black History Month! As part of this, we've done a Black History takeover for this bulletin. You'll find pieces discussing the long history Black people have in contributing to Portsmouth, how our colleagues in BAL are Raising the Heat on the Awarding Gap, the University Library's Black History Month blog post series, a reading list to expand your knowledge, information on what it means to be anti-racist, and information for the remaining Black History Month events happening across campus this month. It's officially been a year since we started the EDI Bulletin, so this edition contains some of our most popular articles featured throughout the year. This includes highlighting two of our incredible research centres focusing one promoting people with autism and one for advancing equity more generally; how you can make your events more inclusive; promoting East & South-East Asian Heritage Month; the work our colleague Professor Deborah Shaw has done in promoting an inclusive feminism; and last but not least our very popular article looking at two love stories that came out of the Windrush generation. This month's bulletin is a bumper edition, with contributions from across the University. We have pieces covering the Athena Swan award for the whole institution; some learning about the GTRSB community; and details on a couple of events, including one by the Faculty of TECH and one for World Refugee Day. To find out out you can better support LGBTQ+ equality in the workplace check out MyGworks WorkPride here, this week. And last but not least, some tales of love between continents in honour of Windrush Day. This month's bulletin is as diverse as our University. In this bulletin, sign up for our exciting Staff Network Day event tomorrow; for Black Inclusion Week we highlight some of the things Black people have accomplished working together; discover the exciting and inclusive work happening to make the mysteries of the universe more accessible; learn more about the NHS Cass report and what it's meant for some of our colleagues; find out about Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome and the impact it has. Besides the infamous April showers, this month is also Autism Awareness Month. So in this bulletin, we highlight the research being done in our Autism Centre for Research on Employment, dedicated to improving education and employment for those with autism. We also provide a unique glimpse into the ground-breaking work happening in the library to accommodate everybody. March marks both Women's History Month and International Women's Day, so we're keeping with the theme and highlighting stories and events linked to gender. We spotlight the impact of the Gender Pay Gap, explore trans-inclusive feminism, share the reflections of the Women's Staff Network co-chairs, and invite you all to join our Women's History Month talk "Lets talk... Inclusive Research on Endometriosis and its Impact on Healthcare". February's bulletin is jam-packed. We highlight the contribution LGBTQ+ individuals have made in medicine, learn about a reworked HE methodology for inclusive teaching, explain how EDI data is used and why it's important, share the reflections of an LGBTQ+ Staff Network co-chair, hear the perspectives of UoP from our racialised students and provide details of our LGBTQ+ History Month talk "Let's talk...Transphobia, politics and higher education". Holocaust Memorial Day, Inclusive Events, LGBTQ+ VR Museum Showcase, Call for Participants at the EDI Mini-fest. Modern Slavery, Equality Impact Assessment (EIA) training & support. Disability History Month, Trans Awareness Week & Day of Remembrance, LGBTQ+ Staff Network Co-Chair (SN) piece. title: Equality Impact Assessment (EIA)
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Equality Impact Assessments (EIAs) Conducting an EIA Key EIA Resources: Explore
body: Inequality, lack of diversity and exclusive cultures usually have many contributory factors, and therefore there is no single solution to fixing them. We also know that many of the challenges we need to tackle can be subtle forms of discrimination which may not be conscious or deliberate, but can culminate in significantly unequal outcomes.Creating a consciously inclusive environment requires everyone to contribute through their own behaviours but also through their work.The University aims to conduct an Equality Impact Assessment on every new policy, process, function or system, as well as when any changes/updates or reviews are made. When conducting an Equality Impact Assessment, policy owners should consider not only the impact on protected characteristics but also any potential implications for academic freedom and freedom of speech. This is particularly important for policies that affect teaching, research, curriculum design, and other academic activities. The assessment should consider whether the policy might inadvertently restrict legitimate academic discourse, debate, or inquiry, and if so, how this can be mitigated while still advancing equality objectives. Policy owners should consult with academic subject matter experts when necessary to understand disciplinary norms and contexts.An EIA is a way of checking that a policy does not directly or indirectly have a negative impact on any staff and students based on their age, disability, ethnicity/ race, gender, religion/ belief or sexual orientation.We also want to proactively advance equity, diversity and inclusion wherever possible. We can identify ways of doing that through the EIA and create a plan of action for implementation (while considering the resource implications).In this way, macro level inequalities (for example, our gender pay gap) can be tackled through lots of smaller, micro level actions being taken across the whole University. Equality Impact Assessments should ideally be conducted by a group. You need the people who understand or own the policy, process, function or system to lead the EIA, with input from relevant data, stakeholders or feedback you may already have.EIAs may look intimidating at first, in reality, they are 7 simple steps: Make a list of all services, strategies, and policies Identify the relevant services, strategies and policies to the one you're making Commence fact-finding and evidence gathering, such as consulting with various groups Assess the predicted impact of your service, strategy or policy across various characteristics Propose adjustments based on the impact estimated above Report assessment and proposals through EIA's Monitor the effect of implementing the service, strategy, or policy (or it's adjustments) Once you have completed your EIA, you should have: A summary of any potential issues, where the policy may have a more negative impact on particular groups Ideas for how the policy could proactively advance equity, diversity and inclusion An action plan for mitigating or eliminating the negative impacts, and implementing the positive EIA’s may look daunting at first, however, with the help of this training you will be equipped with all the skills necessary to be able to complete an EIA. If you are struggling with a particular piece you can provide a specific scenario for expert guidance from our Equality, Diversity & Inclusion (EDI) team as part of this workshop. You are also able to send your EIA to the Equity, Wellbeing & Inclusion team to get feedback from the University's in-house EDI experts. title: The Report and Support Tool
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion The Report and Support Tool Explore
body: The University of Portsmouth is committed to creating a culture where everyone can study, work, and live, in a safe and inclusive environment of mutual dignity and respect. All staff, students, and visitors can report discrimination, misconduct, harassment or assault, or any form of unacceptable behaviour using this tool. You can report anonymously or get support from an advisor-the choice is yours. You can use this tool to report incidents of physical assault, sexual harassment, sexual assault, online harassment, hate crimes, stalking, bullying, or discrimination. This might be based on: race, ethnicity, nationality and/or culture religion and/or belief sex or gender/gender identity disability including learning disabilities, mental health, physical disabilities This list is not exhaustive. You can use the tool to report something that has happened to you, something you have witnessed or been told about. The tool also brings together guidance/support and resource to help you decide what you might want to do or who you might want to talk to about your experience or situation. Support sections include Bullying and Harassment Sexual assault/violence Mental Health and Wellbeing Inclusion Calendar 2023/24 Equality Impact Assessment (EIA) Equality and Diversity training The Report and Support Tool Harassment Adviser Network Harassment Adviser Network Contact and departments

title: Reports
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Reports Explore
body: The public sector duty of the Equality Act 2010 requires that public sector organisations publish equality information and gender pay gap reports annually. This type of information is collected across the University in various forms and the aim of this page it to pull together relevant reports into one place. The information can then be used to help staff, students and the public access our monitoring data and other reports that provide insight into aspects of equality and diversity. This information will be used internally to assist in the completion of Equality Analysis and help support other aspects of the University's equality, diversity and inclusion work, such as equality objective setting and the Equality Action Planning. The latest Gender Pay Gap Report can be found here. Equality Data for 2022/23 - (VPN connection required) Colleague Equality Data 2022/23 Student Equality Data 2022/23 Should you require older data reports please contact us at equality@port.ac.uk. title: Staff Essentials - Academic Staff Association
headings: Staff Essentials
body: Welcome to Staff Essentials, providing news, events and useful information for University of Portsmouth staff. The Academic Staff Association (ASA) aims to promote and foster a spirit of unity and cooperation among the academic staff of the University. It provides a means by which the academic staff may express its opinions and wishes to the Vice-Chancellor. The Association communicates on behalf of academic staff with the Vice-Chancellor and with any organisation on ASA matters through the Executive Committee. The Executive Committee consists of officers of the ASA and an academic staff representative from each department. The ASA is always looking for new staff members to help participate in the association. Any academic staff member is welcome to contact the Chair, Dr David Sanders on ext. 2565 or via email (david.sanders@port.ac.uk) for an informal discussion.

title: Employee Assistance Programme (EAP)
headings: Human Resources Employee Assistance Programme (EAP) Accessing the Helpline Is it confidential? Explore
body: Supporting the mental health and wellbeing of all our colleagues is incredibly important to us, and our new Employee Assistance Programme will help us do just that. Whether you’re facing problems at work or at home, you can access impartial, independent and entirely confidential advice from qualified counsellors for any issues you may be facing, such as Anxiety; Bereavement; Stress; Depression; Workplace Issues; Trauma; Relationships; Finances and debt; and Family Difficulties. Confidential 24/7 Helpline: The telephone helpline is available 24/7, 365 days a year and is completely confidential and to anyone needing mental health support. To access the helpline, simply call 0800 023 9324 from any standard UK landline or mobile phone Digital Resources and Self-Help:A wide offering of resources is also available and includes downloadable self-help workbooks, debt advice and more. Visit the EAP Platform to find out more: www.port.ac.uk/myeap The University does not know who uses the service unless the individual personally chooses to tell someone about their contact with Vivup. Vivup do provide statistics to the University to show how many employees use the service and the broad types of issues raised, for example; ‘relationship breakdown at home’ or ‘bullying and harassment in the workplace. No information is ever passed on which could potentially identify an individual. title: Harassment Adviser Network
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Harassment Adviser Network Explore
body: The University is firmly opposed to any form of victimisation, bullyingor harassment involving any of its staff or students. Bullying or intimidation is offensive behaviour that humiliates or undermines an individual or a group. Such behaviour can be vindictive, cruel or malicious and may be repetitive. If you believe you are the victim of behaviour of this kind please visit the following advice page where you can find details of how to contact our harassment advisers and other support networks. All discussions with a harassment adviser are confidential, and brief records of their discussions will be recorded purely for statistical purposes. This information is anonymous and will be held in complete confidence. If you think you may like to volunteer as a harassment adviser please read the role profile (.docx) for more information. If you have any questions about the Harassment Adviser Network, would like more information on what the role involves, or to find out when the next training sessions are scheduled for please contact us. title: Bus and coach
headings: Human Resources Travel to work by bus and coach City bus services 40% First Bus discount University Bus Service Coach Coach discount Explore
body: Travelling by bus is a sustainable and alternative way to travel around our busy city, so why not give the bus a go and take a break from the day-to-day bustle of driving. Portsmouth has excellent city bus services. You can catch local and intercity buses from the main bus station at The Hard Interchange or the city centre. The Hard bus interchange is a short 16-minute walk to the centre of the campus and the city centre is even closer. From here a large number of other services can be caught. Bus stops closest to the University campus include those on: Winston Churchill Avenue close to University House Cambridge Road by the Students’ Union Queen Street near the Northern Campus Check timetables and plan your route with Stagecoach or First Bus. You can also find live bus information on MyJourneyPortsmouth. Get 40% off your monthly travel with First Bus payable by Direct Debit for your Portsmouth or Hampshire wide travel. Simply set up your Commuter Travel Club account via the First Bus website using your work email. Your discounted price is £36 per month for Portsmouth wide travel and £48 for Hampshire. Register on the First Bus webpage Select your employer from the dropdown menu – visible at the bottom of the webpage Use your company email address to validate access to the scheme First Bus will then email you a link to complete the online registration and purchase a subscription ticket. The University bus service runs term-time only on a circular route from Monday to Friday, close to where many staff and students live in Southsea. Although it’s primarily for student use, staff can use it for university activities. The circular route runs with stops at Bransbury Park in Eastney, Prince Albert Road, Francis Avenue, Fratton Bridge, Somers Road, Winston Churchill Avenue, Cambridge Road, Elm Grove, Kings Theatre, Festing Road and Eastney Health Centre. For more information view the . Seats are available on a first come first served basis Seating capacity of the buses cannot be exceeded Alcohol is not permitted on the buses Not operational on public holidays (Bank holidays) or outside of term time The bus runs on electric power, refurbished from diesel vehicles. Updates on the service are available on the MyPort student website. Regular daily direct coach services run to Portsmouth from London, Bristol, Birmingham, Heathrow and other locations around the country. All these services call at The Hard Interchange by Portsmouth Harbour railway station, which is a 15-minute walk from our campus. Some coaches also stop on Cambridge Road outside the Students’ Union and Spinnaker Building. Find out coach times and routes with National Express. You may also find you can receive a discount with Megabus.com with the Totum card from the NUS depending on their current promotions, or with one of National Express's coachcards if eligible. title: Ferries and hovercraft
headings: Human Resources Travel to work by ferry or hovercraft Gosport Ferry discount Ferries to the Isle of Wight Wightlink ferry staff discount Hovercraft to the Isle of Wight Hovercraft staff discount Hovercraft and easitCARD Staff discount for personal travel Hovercraft 25% staff discount for personal travel Hover Academic Flyers discount Hayling Ferry Explore
body: As an island city, we are well connected across the Solent to the Isle of Wight, Gosport and Hayling Island. There are a number of choices for foot and bike passengers and cars to the Isle of Wight. Travelling between Gosport and Portsmouth is faster by water, with the Gosport Ferry taking only 4 minutes to cross the harbour. Ferries run from 5.30am to midnight, up to every 7.5 minutes at peak times. The Gosport ferry terminal is by the bus station. On the Portsmouth side, the ferry terminal is located by Portsmouth Harbour Station and The Hard. From here it is a 16 minute walk to University House or 7 minute walk to Richmond Building. You can also take your bicycle on board, for an extra charge, and cycle to University House in just 5 minutes. At the time of writing, Gosport Ferry offers all staff a 10% discount on quarterly season tickets, with and without a bicycle. This reduces the cost respectively to £214 and £262 (prices correct as of the 02/04/25). To purchase your ticket you need to take the following to the Gosport Ferry ticket office: Original Staff ID card as proof of ID A photocopy of Staff ID card 2 passport style photos of yourself Download and complete the Gosport Ferry University of Portsmouth discount form Wightlink offers a variety of season and multi-trip tickets which may help you to save if you frequently use the ferries. Full details can be found on their website. Portsmouth Harbour to Ryde Pier Head (Fast Cat Passenger Service). Journey time approx. 22 minutes. Portsmouth Gunwharf to Fishbourne (Car and Passenger Service). Journey time approx. 40 minutes. Lymington to Yarmouth (Car and Passenger Service). Journey time approx. 40 minutes. For onward travel on the Island, there is a train station on Ryde Pier Head, and both Ryde and Fishbourne are served by local buses. The Portsmouth Harbour ferry terminal is located within the train station, and adjacent to The Hard. From either of the Portsmouth ports it takes around 15 minutes to walk or 5 minutes to cycle to University House. You can take your bicycle on board free of charge with a foot passenger ticket. Full details can be found on the Wightlink website. Wightlink is offering staff members 15% off car travel and 20% off foot travel when travelling for personal use to and from the Isle of Wight. To take advantage of this discount, please book online. Please note this discount cannot be applied to season tickets. Tickets must be booked online via the link provided and are not available via phone or in the terminal. You can travel between Ryde on the Isle of Wight and Southsea in just 10 minutes with Hovertravel. The frequent service runs 7 days a week for foot passengers. The Ryde terminal is located adjacent to the bus and train stations. To get to the University from the Southsea terminal you can catch the Hoverbus towards central Portsmouth, which stops nearby. Alternatively it takes around 20-minutes to walk to the University House or 5 minutes to cycle. You may take your bike on the hovercraft for no extra charge on a first come first served basis. You can save on travel on the hovercraft by using variety of season and multi-trip tickets offered by the Hovertravel. See their website for full details. Hovertravel is offering staff a 50% discount off standard single, day and period return fares for work travel only from Portsmouth to the Isle of Wight. This does not apply to commuting. To take advantage of this discount, please book online using the promo code HTUOP50. Please note this discount cannot be applied to season tickets. Tickets must be booked online via the link provided and are not available via phone or in the terminal. Purchase an easitCARD for £5.90 and enjoy a 25% discount for you and up to 4 companions on the Hovercraft using the promo code at the checkout. Full details of how to apply and terms and conditions can be found here. All staff can access a 25% discount off standard adult and child single, day and period return fares for leisure travel for themselves plus up to 4 companions travelling on the same ticket. Book online with promo code: UOPSTAFF25. Terms & Conditions apply: This discount is available online only using the promo code above at the checkout Tickets must be booked online at least one day prior to travel Valid from Southsea to Ryde only Not available in the terminal or over the phone Not available on Senior or Student fares Cannot be used in conjunction with any other offers, discounts or combos Valid for one member and up to 4 companions (must travel together on same ticket) Promo code must not be shared, other than those it is intended for use ID for member must be shown at boarding gate or extra charges may apply This offer is valid for bookings and travel until 31/12/2025 Standard Hovertravel booking Terms & Conditions apply Hovertravel reserve the right to withdraw if not used in accordance to the terms and conditions stated above. Hover Travel are offering 10% discount off Hover Academic Flyers tickets until 31 August. This discount will be applied to this year’s current fare, meaning you can lock in next year’s travel at today’s prices. To make use of this offer email sales@hovertravel.com with your current season or Flexi card number, as well as your customer number and when you would like your card to start. Proof of eligibility in the form of student/staff ID will also be needed alongside ordinary photo ID. You can pay with a one off payment or direct debit payment. The Hayling Ferry provides a valuable link between Eastney (Portsmouth) and Hayling Island for foot passengers and cyclists. The ferry runs 7 days a week. Bicycles can be taken on board free of charge when space permits. You can cycle from the Eastney landing to University House in around 25 minutes. Alternatively the number 25 First Bus connects the ferry to the city centre. The bus timetable can be viewed on both Hayling Ferry and First Bus' websites. title: Park and Ride
headings: Human Resources Travel to work by Park and Ride Explore
body: The PR1 service runs every quarter of an hour during the week and every 10 minutes at weekends, connecting the Park and Ride with the Hard Exchange with the nearest stop to the University on Queen Street. Portsmouth Park and Ride costs an average of £2 per day, based upon purchasing a Commuterpass 50 (for up to five people travelling together). Paper tickets can be bought from ticket machines or a smartcard can be purchased and topped up online or from the smartcard machine in the Park and Ride building. Tickets cannot be purchased on the bus, they must be bought before boarding. For ticket information, route details and the timetables visit the Park and Ride website. title: Train
headings: Human Resources Travel to work by train Train discount Explore
body: Travelling by train significantly reduces your environmental impact locally when compared to the equivalent car journey. You can read a book or newspaper, use the free Wi-Fi where available, and take your bike to make your onward journey quicker. The closest station to the University are Portsmouth and Southsea and Portsmouth Harbour. These stations are just a short walk or cycle from the University: Portsmouth and Southsea – 8 minutes walking and 5 minutes cycling Portsmouth Harbour – 15 minutes walking and 5 minutes cycling To plan your journey and view timetables visit the National Rail website. You can also find live train information on MyJourneyPortsmouth. Get 15% off travelling by train with an easitPORTSMOUTH card on Southern Trains and South Western Railway. Whether you live to the north of the city and travel in from Cosham or from outside of the city, the University is well connected. The easit card costs £5.90 and is available to University staff as part of the easitPORTSMOUTH network. Register for a card or login to your account on the easit website. title: Car
headings: Human Resources Travel to work by car Car share with colleagues University car parking permits Exclusion zone and exemptions Car Parking Other University car parks A‌lternatives Explore
body: Reduce road congestion and your petrol and parking costs by car sharing, using the Park and Ride service, finding a car-sharing buddy through EasitShare, or using other alternatives to travelling by car. However, we know some staff may need to drive and the University has a number of car parks with spaces available on a first serve basis with a University car parking permit. There are also a number of city car park discounts available to staff. Please check and follow the latest government advice on car sharing during the Coronavirus outbreak. You can use easitShare to connect with others looking to share their car journeys (and other commuting trips). Sign up for free on their website and you can immediately begin your search for a car-share match. Once you've found a match, the agreement can be flexible and fit around you. All staff (including part-time, hourly paid and casual staff) living more than 2 miles from the University can request a car parking permit. Staff living within the exclusion zone who are blue badge holders or who meet our other needs based criteria can also request a car parking permit. Please note this does not include PhD or Masters students working part-time in conjunction with their courses of study. To request a permit or to review your existing permit, please visit the UoP car parking permit website. The University has partnered with Parking Eye to manage our car parks. Car parking enforcement is through Automatic Number Plate Recognition (ANPR) . Once you have obtained your permit you must register with the payment Good2Go website and choose the way you would like to pay. You must have funds available on the system prior to parking. Staff must ensure that the details on both the permit website and the Good2Go website match exactly: Personal information, including names, addresses and numbers should match exactly. This includes upper and lower case letters. All cars listed on your permit should be reflected on the Good2Go site. If there is any discrepancy between the information across the two websites, this may result in an erroneous Penalty Charge Notice (PCN ) being issued. The car parking team is committed to supporting colleagues and will answer enquiries as quickly as possible. Please contact car.parking@port.ac.uk if you have any questions. There is a two mile exclusion zone for parking permits which means colleagues who live within two miles of University House will not be eligible for a parking permit. However, there are a number of exemptions in place such as staff and students with disabilities, working parents, carers and people working unsociable hours. If you are applying for a disabled permit or have an exemption and live within the exclusion zone, your permit will be held until reviewed by the parking team. If your permit is not authorised and you believe this is not correct, you can appeal by email at car.parking@port.ac.uk For further information, please check the car parking policy and FAQ. The University offers a measure of car parking provision as far as its land capacity allows. Anyone parking on University premises needs to abide by the University’s car parking policy. It is important to understand that possession of a car parking permit does not guarantee an available parking space. Please note that although you can register more than one car on both the parking permit website and the Good2Go website, only one registered car per staff member is permitted in the car parks at one time. Failure to observe this restriction will result in a Penalty Charge Notice being issued. Anyone who does not have a valid permit or who has parked illegally, or who have parked in breach of the University’s regulations, will be issued with a Parking Charge Notice (PCN), payable to Portsmouth City Council. Anyone wishing to appeal against a PCN would need to do so direct with Portsmouth City Council – it is not a matter for the University. The markings in car parks highlight the use of the bay: White standard bay - All permit holders are allowed to park except contractors. Yellow and blue disabled bay - Only disabled badge holders can park. They must also display their Blue Badge as well as the University red disabled badge. Green faculty bay - Only faculty permit holders can park. Authorisation for these permits are at the discretion of Faculty Heads and cannot be issued without their permission. Orange maintenance bays - Time limited for the use of Maintenance and Contractor permit holders only. Spaces are available in Isambard Kingdom Brunel multi-storey car park in Alec Rose Lane. These spaces can be accessed via a pass card that is available upon request to all staff and students with a valid UoP car parking permit. Passes are available to collect from Estates and Campus Services helpdesk. As with our other car parks, the spaces will be available on a first come first served basis and the barrier system will not permit entry with a pass once 45 spaces have been taken. During school holidays staff and students can use the Wickham Street car park at a discounted rate instead. The car park is open from 8.00am to 9.00pm Monday - Friday. title: Academic Roles and Responsibilities
headings: Academic Registry Explore
body: This section provides a clear overview of the key responsibilities held by academic staff across all levels related to academic teaching provision, from Module Leaders delivering subject expertise, to Executive Deans shaping the broader strategic vision of the institution. Whether you're new to your academic role, looking to understand team responsibilities, or clarifying reporting lines and academic functions, the information here outlines each position’s core strategic and operational duties, and places them within our academic framework. Our aim is to foster understanding, transparency, collaboration, and responsibility in learning, teaching, assessment and leadership. Explore below to understand how each role supports the University’s academic mission and contributes to the student experience and outcomes, and institutional development. title: Annual Monitoring and Academic Review
headings: Academic Registry Annual Monitoring and Academic Review Annual Monitoring Key Dates and Timeline For Heads of School For Executive Deans Categories Student Centric Outcomes, Planning and Evaluation (SCOPE) Supporting Policy Documents and Guidance Module Evaluation Form Explore
body: The University’s approach to assuring standards and driving up quality through annual monitoring and academic review is: Our approach to annual monitoring and academic review provides an internal level playing field to assess and drive up quality, facilitating the best possible opportunities for learning, progression and achievement for all students. Please find below a timeline which outlines when each step is required to be completed. As a point of reference, the term QPP is used in replacement of Quality Priority Plan and CSAR in replacement of Course Self-Assessment Report (Specific to Degree Apprenticeships). Please note that these QPPs will now replace EQuIPs. As demonstrated in the timeline above, the following key stages will take place: Degree apprenticeship (DA) CSARs have already been launched to course leaders and AD(A)s for completion. CSARs remain the same as in previous years and are due to be completed by Tuesday 26th August 2025. Please note that course leaders completing CSARs for DA courses will not be required to complete an additional QPP as good practice and action plans are included within the CSAR documentation. All documentation and guidance will be accessible on the Annual Monitoring site and the Course Performance Dashboard, including the new Module view, will be refreshed on the 1st August 2025. Heads of School will now be able to complete their 5 point QPP, identifying up to 5 key priorities identified through intelligence gathered throughout the academic cycle to improve student outcomes and student experience within their school. This intelligence could include outcomes from Boards, External feedback and recommendations, conversations within the academic community and known developments within industry. The following link will take Heads of School to their 5 point QPP. Please complete this QPP as a live document to support version control and tracking. It is expected that Heads of School QPPs will be completed by Wednesday 17th September 2025. Executive Deans will now be able to complete their Faculty 5 point QPPs. Executive Deans will use intelligence provided by their Heads of School QPPs as well as their own view, to identify up to 5 key priorities to improve student outcomes and student experience within their Faculties. The following link will take Executive Deans to their Faculty Drive, allowing them to view their Heads of School QPPs and their own Faculty QPP template for completion. Please complete this QPP as a live document to support version control and tracking. It is expected that Faculty QPPs will be completed by Wednesday 24th September 2025. Completed QPPs will be submitted for review ahead of the scheduled Faculty Review Meeting on Monday 6th October 2025. This meeting will include all Executive Deans, the Academic Registrar, the Deputy Vice-Chancellor (Education), the Director of Education Strategies and Dean of Digital and Distributed Learning. Once dashboard data has been made accessible in August, UG, PG and Sub-Contracted Collaborative Partners will be informed of their course category. These categories include: High Performing Course, Low Risk (Quintile 1): the agreed benchmark has been exceeded Effective Course, Low Risk (Quintile 2 or 3): the agreed benchmark has been met, with some areas for improvement. Low Performing Course, High Risk (Quintile 4): the agreed benchmark has not been met and course has identified areas of risk Poor Performing Course, Highest Risk (Quintile 5): the agreed benchmark has not been met with high levels of risk. Each category will have its own specific QPP to support a risk based approach to Annual Monitoring. The following templates will be provided to course teams, along with guidance for completion via the Annual Monitoring drive: High Performing Course, Low Risk: Good Practice Template will be completed to identify areas of strength, impact and good practice. Effective Performing Course, Low Risk: QPP is completed to identify areas of strength and impact, as well as a priority action plan. Low Performing Course, High Risk: QPP will be completed to record priority actions that will be taken to improve outcomes. Poor Performing Course, Highest Risk: QPP will be completed to record priority actions that will be taken to improve outcomes. Courses that are identified as having the highest level of risk will be considered for Academic Review. Validated Collaborative Partner courses will complete a 5 point QPP at partner level, however they are welcome to provide their own annual report as in previous years if they wish to do so instead. It is expected that Validated Collaborative Partners will completed their 5 point QPP or annual report by Monday 27th October. As part of our commitment to enhancing student experience and outcomes through continuous improvement, the traditional Board of Studies meetings will be replaced by SCOPE meetings (Student-Centric Outcomes, Planning and Evaluation). SCOPE meetings are designed to provide a more focused, data-driven forum for course teams, student representatives, External Examiners and partner institutions to come together to review student experience, outcomes, course content and delivery, and improvement planning. At a minimum, there should be one SCOPE meeting per year, although it may be beneficial to hold additional meetings throughout the year to support courses with multiple intakes and flexible delivery. Templated agendas can be found below. Faculty Administration Service Centres will continue to support with the scheduling and administration for SCOPE meetings as they have previously with Boards of Studies. For Academic Year 2025/26, External Examiners should be invited to attend SCOPE meetings. However, there is no requirement for their attendance. The External Examiner report will be considered and should be reviewed as part of the meeting. SCOPE meeting agenda template SCOPE meeting agenda template (Apprenticeships) Annual Monitoring and Academic Policy and Operational Handbook. The Policy provides the approach and mechanism of the annual monitoring and academic review processes. The supporting Operational Handbook within the Policy has been developed to provide operational process detail and guidance. title: Annual Reporting
headings: Academic Registry Explore
body: Annual Reports: Insights into Student Outcomes and Experience The Academic Registry produces a series of annual reports designed for internal review and communication. These internal reports provide a comprehensive view of key trends and performance indicators across the student lifecycle, supporting evidence-based decision-making and continuous enhancement of academic quality. These reports are intended for internal stakeholders only, including academic schools and senior leadership teams. They serve as a vital resource for monitoring performance, supporting quality assurance processes, and driving continuous improvement. These reports must not be circulated or reproduced externally, either in whole or in part, without prior permission of the author. Bringing It All Together The culmination of the annual reports below, covering student outcomes, student experience, quality assurance, and Collaborative Provision and Degree Apprenticeships, alongside other monitoring and enhancement activities, provides the evidence base for the University’s Board of Governors Assurance Statement on Quality. This statement confirms that robust processes are in place to maintain academic standards and continuously improve the student experience. Access and Participation Annual Report The annual Access and Participation Report (APP) evaluates the University’s progress against its Access and Participation Plan commitments. This report provides a detailed analysis of how the institution is supporting students from underrepresented groups to access, succeed in, and progress beyond higher education. First annual report scheduled summer 2026 Our reports on student outcomes analyse progression, completion, and attainment data across all courses, including UK and TNE collaborative courses. They highlight patterns in achievement, identify areas for improvement, and inform strategies to enhance student success. These reports are reviewed at the University Education and Student Experience Committee (UESEC) and Academic Council, with summary reports considered at Board of Governors. UESEC - UG Academic Standards and Quality Annual Report 2024-25.docx UESEC - PGT Academic Standards and Quality Annual Report 2024-25 We review feedback from surveys, focus groups, and other engagement activities to understand the student experience. These reports capture insights on teaching quality, learning resources, and overall satisfaction, helping shape initiatives that improve the academic and social environment. These reports are reviewed at the University Education and Student Experience Committee (UESEC) and Academic Council, with summary reports considered at Board of Governors. NSS report included in UESEC - UG Academic Standards and Quality Annual Report 2024-25.docx Course Survey Analysis 2025.docx Analysis of late marking Degree Apprenticeship Annual SAR and QIP Academic Registry, in partnership with the Institute for Professional and Flexible Learning (IPFL), oversees the production of the Self-Assessment Report (SAR) and Quality Improvement Plan (QIP) for Degree Apprenticeships. These reports are essential for meeting regulatory requirements and ensuring the highest standards of teaching, learning, and employer engagement. These reports are reviewed at the University Education and Student Experience Committee (UESEC) and Academic Council, with summary reports considered at Board of Governors. Quality Assurance Reports These reports focus on key regulatory and procedural areas, ensuring transparency and continuous improvement. These reports are reviewed at the University Education and Student Experience Committee (UESEC) and Academic Council, with summary reports considered at Board of Governors. Assessment Regulations Our reports monitor compliance with assessment regulations across all courses. They review the effectiveness of assessment policies, highlight areas for enhancement, and ensure that assessment practices remain fair, consistent, and aligned with sector expectations. Annual Review of Examination and Assessment Processes for the 2024/25 Academic Year 2024/2025 Academic Regulation Changes Post Implementation Monitoring and Evaluation Review of Student Conduct Policy and Outcomes 2023/24 Academic Year Reports on student complaints provide an overview of themes, resolution times, and outcomes. This analysis supports improvements in service delivery and fosters a culture of responsiveness and accountability across the institution. title: Apprenticeships Quality Management
headings: Academic Registry Apprenticeships Quality Management Explore
body: Quality assurance and continuous improvement of the University’s growing portfolio of apprenticeships is managed by the Academic Registry team, in collaboration with colleagues across the University. The information contained on these pages is designed to facilitate the design, delivery and continuous improvement of apprenticeships, and to ensure the quality of the apprentice experience from application to completion of the full apprenticeship. If you have any questions about the information within this page, please email us as academicregistry@port.ac.uk What is a Degree Apprenticeship? A Degree Apprenticeship is a programme that is funded either by an apprentice’s employer and/or the Government. To be eligible for an apprenticeship, the learner must be employed and have at least 6 hours of training per week. These hours must be during the apprentice’s normal working hours and are referred to as Off-the-Job Training (OTJ). All apprentices must be enrolled on a specific apprenticeship standard, which is relevant to their job role. These standards outline the required Knowledge, Skills and Behaviours that all apprentices must provide evidence to demonstrate competence. Once an apprentice has completed their required period of training, they will complete their End Point Assessment (EPA). This is a mandatory requirement for all apprenticeship standards. Regulation of Apprenticeships With effect from 1 April 2021, Ofsted is the regulator for apprenticeships at Levels 6 and 7, expanding their existing remit for apprenticeships delivered at Level 5 and below. Staff involved in apprenticeship delivery should familiarise themselves with: The Education Inspection Framework The Further Education and Skills Handbook (apprenticeships are considered under the ‘skills’ remit) There are five different regulators with an interest in apprenticeships: Ofsted, the Education and Skills Funding Agency, Ofqual, the Office for Students, and the Institute for Apprenticeships and Technical Education. For an overview of the aspects of apprenticeship delivery that the different regulatory bodies have an interest in, please refer to this document: Apprenticeships: the external regulatory landscape Quality Assurance Activities The University produces a Self Assessment Report (SAR) on the full provision of apprenticeships each year. This details the university’s position at that point of time. The most recent version of the SAR is available here. A Quality Improvement Plan (QIP) is produced in line with this which details the steps the university is undertaking to improve its position. This is available here. There are two specific apprenticeship groups that focus on apprenticeship quality. These are: Apprenticeship Quality Management Board - this board is responsible for managing the quality assurance and continuous improvement of apprenticeships Apprenticeship Teaching and Learning Observation Group - this group undertakes lesson observations in line with key themes The university follows a Quality Cycle for apprenticeship activities which consists of: There is also training provided to colleagues in support of developing their knowledge of apprenticeships and the intricacies within this. Training videos from previous years are available here. Apprenticeship Policies and Procedures Apprenticeship Policy Framework Safeguarding Procedure for Apprentices End Point Assessment Procedure External Examiners Regulations and Procedures - Annex A Integrated End Point Assessment (EPA) Assessor Arrangements For integrated apprenticeships, the University is responsible for conducting the EPA. This includes the allocation of an independent assessor who has not actively been involved in the teaching or marking of any apprentice in their course. There is also a requirement for an EPA External Assessor. To appoint an EPA External Assessor, please complete the Proposal form and submit to your Faculty Quality Assurance Advisor. The proposal will be considered and approved by the External Examiner Panel. Please see Annex A in the External Examiner Regulations and Procedures which details the procedure. For every event that the External Assessor completes, please ensure they complete the below report. The feedback received in this form is valuable for us to understand our strengths and how we can improve this process for external assessors going forwards. title: Assessment Regulations
headings: Academic Registry Introduction to Assessment Regulations Module assessment boards and BOE Supporting documentation Assessment Regulations Explore
body: Welcome to the Academic Registry’s Assessment Regulations pages. This section is specifically designed to assist our staff and collaborative partners in navigating and applying the University’s Assessment Regulations. Here, you will find the comprehensive University Assessment Regulations and accompanying documents. These regulations are categorised into three main groups, each tailored to different aspects of our academic offerings: 1. Undergraduate and Postgraduate Taught Courses: A unified set of regulations governs all taught courses up to and including Level 7. These ensure consistency and clarity across the University’s undergraduate and postgraduate courses. 2. Partner-Specific Assessment Regulations: These are specialised regulations designed for specific collaborative courses and partnerships, recognising that some partners have unique needs for certain courses. 3. Research Programmes: Tailored specifically for research-based programmes, these regulations guide the research journey and ensure that academic standards are upheld throughout the process leading to research degrees. Student-Centric Assessment Regulations Our Assessment Regulations are designed to support the student experience and promote academic success for all students equally while preserving academic integrity and safeguarding academic standards. Written in clear, accessible language these regulations are structured to be easily navigable, with direct links to additional resources and support. While students have access to the central Assessment Regulations document, we encourage students to use our MyPort pages, which present these regulations in a student-friendly format. There is a dedicated student portal about these Regulations: Exams And Assessments | Student Services - University of Portsmouth For more information and guidance For further support regarding the Assessment Regulations please contact Academic Registry at academicregistry@port.ac.uk We welcome your feedback on these pages and the Assessment Regulations. Your insights are invaluable in helping us improve our resources for students, staff and partners. If you have any suggestions, comments, or questions, please don’t hesitate to reach out to us at academicregistry@port.ac.uk We appreciate your input and look forward to hearing from you. title: Collaborative Programmes
headings: Academic Registry Supporting Excellence in UK and Transnational Academic Collaboration Our Role Resources and Guidance Get in Touch Explore
body: Welcome to the Collaborative Programmes and Partnerships section of the Academic Registry. We provide guidance and oversight for the development, approval, and ongoing quality assurance of the University’s collaborative courses and partnership arrangements, ensuring they align with institutional strategy and quality assurance standards. We support staff in navigating the complexities of UK and transnational education (TNE) and collaborative partnerships, including: ✅ Supporting collaborative course and partnership approval processes ✅ Overseeing Quality assurance and regulatory compliance✅ Supporting collaborative annual monitoring and review processes ✅ Developing collaborative provision policies and academic regulations ✅ Provide external regulatory advice and support Academic Registry works closely with academic schools, and a number of other professional services teams across the University to support our UK and Transnational provision, these include: Global Office Develops new international partnership opportunities including TNE partnerships. The Global Office deals with all enquires, business development and partnerships relationship working closely with facilities and professional service areas as relevant or required. Corporate Governance - Partnerships team who develop and manage the contractual agreements and support risk assessment activities. Please get in touch with us to discuss how we can support the contract for your project - partnerships@port.ac.uk Cross Faculty Support Office who support the day to day academic services operations for our collaborative students and partners. Institute for Professional and Flexible Learning is primarily an education business-to-business engagement and delivery arm. The Institute is made up of our well established Learning at Work department and Degree Apprentiship Office that oversees the growth and development of our work-integrated learning offer. Who Are Our Current Collaborative Partners? You can find a list of all of our current collaborative courses and partnerships on our UoP external web page. These are listed here. Explore our comprehensive guidance on: Policy & Regulations – Institutional requirements for collaborative programmes Academic Partnerships Policy Some of our partnerships operate under partner specific assessment regulations, you can find them here: Collaborative Courses Approval Processes – Step-by-step support for new and existing partnerships Portfolio Approval, Modification and Closure The approval processes for the following arrangements can be found on eRecords (VPN login required if off-site):Cooperation Memorandum of Understanding (MoU)Articulation AgreementStudent Exchange AgreementSupport Provider Agreement & Agreement for Collaborative Specific Credit An important part of any new UK or TNE partnership approval is an independent site visit which informs the approval process. An independent site visit may be undertaken either before or after the partnership approval meeting, as timing will depend on a number of logistical factors. If it is after the partnership approval meeting, it will be a condition of approval. There may be circumstances where it has been agreed at UPAG that a site visit is not required. How independent does a site visit have to be? An independent site visit report can be undertaken by a member of UoP staff, but that person would not be involved in the business development opportunity, or be from the academic school. A template for an independent site visit report is available here: Independent Course / Partnership Site Visit Report At the earliest opportunity in discussions with potential new collaborative partners, university staff leading on the discussion should ask the partner to complete and return the New Partner Collaborative Course Proposal Questionnaire to help support development discussions. Quality Assurance – Ensuring academic standards and student experience remain high Annual Monitoring and Academic Review Staff Training & Support – Workshops, toolkits, and one-to-one consultations Academic Registry can provide bespoke workshops and one-to-one consultations to partner staff and UoP staff on a range of topics, these include: - University policies and processes - Course approval processes - Assessment regulations - UK quality assurance arrangements Check out the Collaborative Partnerships Training Library Collaborative Course(s) and Partnerships Closures For advice and support on collaborative programmes and partnerships, contact us at academicregistry@port.ac.uk We are committed to supporting high-quality academic partnerships that benefit all our students, no matter where or how they are studying a University of Portsmouth award, and that benefits our partner institutions alike. headings: Academic Registry Welcome to the Academic Registry Connected Curriculum Approval Hub Briefing session Approval Process Timeline - Phase 1 Approval Process Timeline - Phase 2a Approval Processes and Supporting Documentation Explore
body: This site provides you with all the information related to the approval operational processes supporting the University transition to the Connected Curriculum. Here, you’ll find key guidance, timelines, templates, support contacts and FAQs to help you navigate the necessary steps for course changes and approvals. If you are looking for support with Connected Curriculum design, or with learning, teaching and assessment practices aligned to the new Connected Curriculum, please visit the Centre for Academic Development and Innovation (CADI): Connected Curriculum | University of Portsmouth Course approval routes and guidance - Phase 2 September 2026 start - Submission window Approval window for courses starting in September 2026: Opens: 14th July 2025 Closes: 17th November 2025 [signed off by HoD, ADA and submitted to Academic Registry] September 2027 start - Submission window Opens: 2nd March 2026 Closes: 2nd November 2026 [signed off by HoD, ADA and submitted to Academic Registry] Notification of intent - 8th March 2026 Opens: 19th March 2026 Closes: 29th May 2026 [Connected Curriculum processes and meetings complete and signed off by HoD, ADA, ready for Academic Registry] Further submission opportunities within Phase 2 will be communicated at a later date. title: Course Lifecycle
headings: Academic Registry Course Life Cycle Faculty Quality Officers Collaborative Portfolio Approval Explore
body: Academic Registry manage the University’s Portfolio Approval, Modification and Closure Policy by supporting Portfolio Approval , Modifications and Closures within the course lifecycle. These processes include all award-bearing provision and collaborative courses. Please see the following for more information: Portfolio Approval, Modification and Closure Policy Key Dates for New and Modified Courses Modified Course Approval Events: Key Principles and Additional Guidance Each Faculty has an assigned Quality Officer that can support you through these processes. They will: support Course Teams and assist in the coordination of the relevant documentation needed for each process signpost Course Teams to the relevant staff within Academic Services that can assist with the correct completion of the documentation review and submit proposal documentation to the University Portfolio Approval Group (UPAG) liaise with the Academic Services colleagues to set up and update courses on Student Records (SITS) and course webpages provide general advice and guidance to departments on the Portfolio Approval, Modification and Closure processes maintain and update documentation and webpages. If you have any queries please contact your Faculty Quality Officer at the email addresses below: Faculty of Business and Law Faculty of Creative and Cultural Industries Faculty of Humanities and Social Sciences Faculty of Science and Health Faculty of Technology IPFL (Institute of Professional and Flexible Learning) If your approval involves an existing partner within your faculty, please contact your Faculty Quality Officer as above. title: External Examiners
headings: Academic Registry External Examiners Appointment Academic Registry Academic Schools and Hubs Explore
body: The External Examiners Regulations and Procedures provides information for staff and External Examiners and the External Examiners webpage provides information for External Examiners, including the link to the annual report template and expenses claim form. The role of an External Examiner is primarily one of quality assurance, ensuring that awards granted are comparable in standard to those of other higher education institutions, that national subject threshold standards are complied with, and the treatment of students is equitable and consistent. External Examiners are independent and impartial advisers, providing informative comment on standards and quality and student achievement in relation to those standards. This is achieved by sampling students’ work and reporting on the operation of procedures and processes. Specific responsibilities are to: Confirm the assessment process is appropriate, fair, consistent and in line with policies and procedures Ensure the aims, objectives and learning outcomes are appropriate and comparable to threshold requirements Confirm standards and student performance are comparable with that of other institutions Confirm the currency, relevance and coherence of the curriculum Report on academic standards and quality and highlight good practice and innovation Appointment of External Examiners are considered for approval by the External Examiner Approval Panel, who meet on a monthly basis, on behalf of Academic Council. The proposal will be considered in line with the External Examiners Regulations and Procedures Academic Registry manage the appointment process with the External Examiner Approval Panel, host an annual online induction event for new External Examiners, and receive and dissiminate External Examiners annual reports. Academic Schools and administrative Hubs manage the day to day contact with the External Examiners on all academic matters and remuneration. They are also responsible for conducting the Right to Work checks and setting up Moodle accounts. To propose an External Examiner or variation to an existing External Examiner appointment, please complete the relevent forms below and send to the Faculty Quality Assurance Advisor for submission to the External Examiner Aproval Panel. title: Faculties Quality Assurance
headings: Academic Registry Faculties Quality Assurance Faculties Key Quality Assurance Activities and Business Processes Course Approval, Modification and Closure Course Structures & Diets Module Amendments Course Specification Documents (CSDs) Explore
body: This site provides information and guidance for key Quality Assurance and Academic Standards activities and business processes that take place in Faculties and Academic Service Centres each cycle, under the guidance and oversight of Quality Assurance and Academic Standards (QAS). In particular, this page focuses on the annual review of / changes to: Course Structures (Diets) and Modules Proposed changes should be made in line with the Curriculum Framework. Once approval for changes has been confirmed by the Associate Dean (Academic) and systems have been updated to reflect the changes, Marketing / Webteam will be informed so they can capture approved changes on course web pages where required. Any questions about these processes should be directed to the relevant Quality Officer. Details of your Designated Quality Officer, are available using the link below: Designated Quality Officer Information Working closely with the Faculty Associate Dean (Academic), and in line with the Course lifecycle, your Designated Quality Officer will support and guide you through creating a new course and making changes to a course. Courses should align with the Curriculum Framework or have an approved Course Exemptions. This key activity and business process provides Course Leaders with the opportunity to make changes to Course Structures (known as Diets in SITS) for the next academic year, including proposing new modules and taking existing modules out of use. The process commences early in the Autumn Term each year, and is timed to ensure that course structures are correct in SITS in good time to support student option choice and timetabling. Changes for Academic Year 2025/26 Course Leaders should liaise with their Academic Services Centre for details of local arrangements and support. The Cross Faculty Support Office will be working with the relevant Centre colleagues and University Academic Contacts for Collaborative Courses. For Learning at Work courses please contact the Department Manager. Course Structures Business Process CS for 25/26 timeframes: Deadline for completed course structure google sheets, option group tabs, rationales (to support student consultation) and new modules to have been signed off by HoS (or nominee) to Faculty Friday 1st November 2024 Out by 18th November 2024 Student given 10 working days Adjustments by CLs with Faculty by 11th December Out by 18th November 2024 Student given 10 working days Adjustments by CLs with Faculty by 11th December Course Structures/diets & option grouping details to SST for inputting Deadline for sign off for course Structures/diets ready for Online Option choice Please contact your Designated Quality Officer if you have any questions. Changes after these dates will be late. Late changes will need to be approved, please contact your Designated Quality Officer to discuss the late change process. This key activity and business process provides Module Co-ordinators with the opportunity to propose minor changes to their modules for the next academic year, in line with the Curriculum Framework. The process normally commences in April/May each year and runs through to the mid/end of July, and is designed to ensure that all publicly available information about modules is accurate ahead of Confirmation and Clearing, to provide the best experience for our applicants. Module Amendments Business Process 2025/26 Module delivery: MARFs signed by HoD to Faculty Faculty Advisors input to SITS Please contact your Designated Quality Officer if you have any questions. Changes after these dates will be late. Late changes will need to be approved, please contact your Designated Quality Officer to discuss late changes process. A Course Specification must be held against each academic year, in the course documentation record in eRecords, as the definitive record source. The Course Specification process will be coordinated by the Quality Assurance and Academic Standards Adviser (Faculty) in Academic Registry who will work with colleagues to ensure that a course specification is held on file and where changes are required through one of the identified trigger points, track the status of Course Specification updates via the Tracking Spreadsheet. This key activity and business process provides Course Leaders with the opportunity to update their Course Specification Document for each academic year, in line with the Curriculum Framework. Course Specification changes should be completed ready for the new academic year. Updated CSD Business Process - coming soon Please contact your Designated Quality Officer if you have any questions. title: Professional, Statutory and Regulatory Bodies
headings: Academic Registry Professional, Statutory and Regulatory Bodies (PSRBs) What is a PSRB? University of Portsmouth PSRB Activity Please use the following Google Forms to report PSRB activity to Academic Registry: Explore
body: Professional, Statutory and Regulatory Body (PSRB) accreditation is a general term used to describe organisations which are authorised to accredit, approve or recognise specific programmes in the context of the requirements of the PSRB. Why are they important? A valued feature of the University's undergraduate and postgraduate provision is the number of degree programmes which are accredited by a PSRB. The University aims to ensure that all its programmes which are eligible for PSRB accreditation secure and retain the accreditation. This is just one of many ways the University ensures that professional standards and quality are maintained and that students gain the skills and knowledge required by employers. A PSRB normally accredits a programme for a specific number of years after which they return to review and re-accredit the programmes for the next period. All PSRB accreditation and review reports are monitored by the Board of Governors. All new PSRB accreditations, amendments to existing accreditations, and outcomes of visits and renewals need to be reported to Academic Registry to ensure the PSRB database is updated. PSRB accreditations will not be publicised on the course pages or any other location until they have been reported to Academic Registry. After the PSRB accreditations have been added to the database. The term ‘accreditation’ is used in this context to describe any link with a professional body that results in recognition of our courses. PSRB Form for New Accreditations PSRB Form for Renewed/Amended Accreditations Advice and guidance on the completion of the PSRB forms, and on any other matters relating to PSRB accreditations, can be sought from Academic Registry at academicregistry@port.ac.uk Academic Roles and Responsibilities Annual Monitoring and Academic Review Apprenticeships Quality Management Assessment Regulations Collaborative Programmes Faculties Quality Assurance Office for Students Publications Policies and Regulations Professional, Statutory and Regulatory Bodies Reasonable Adjustments Student Quality Representatives Work-based and Placement Learning Contact and departments

title: Reasonable Adjustments
headings: Academic Registry Explore
body: Reasonable Adjustments Welcome to the Academic Registry’s page on Reasonable Adjustments for assessments. This page provides essential information and guidance for staff on our responsibilities to ensure that all students, including those with disabilities or specific learning needs, have equal access to academic opportunities. This page does not provide information on how to support students, for those details please go to the Disability Advice page which includes a range of support resources. Within the supporting videos and documentation on this site, you will find details on: - our legal requirements and the responsibilities of academic and support staff in meeting these requirements - understanding core competencies - the process for referring students to additional support - the process for recording and viewing students’ reasonable adjustments in SITS Full video on Reasonable Adjustments and Assessments Staff guide and flow chart Requesting an Immediate Reasonable AdjustmentIf you have a student who has an immediate need for a reasonable adjustment, you can send an urgent request to the Disability Advice team using the following request form:Notification of an Immediate Reasonable AdjustmentThe above link can be accessed through Hornbill, go to:Academic Services then Assessments and Results, and then there is a button that says Notification of an Immediate Reasonable Adjustment.In addition to assessments, reasonable adjustments may be necessary to enable access to learning, teaching, university services and the campus environment. You can signpost students to disability advice@port.ac.uk or the Disability Advice drop-in service for advice. Bateson Student Centre (PO1 2BL)Monday to Friday 1.00pm - 3.00pm (excluding Wednesdays) For further student and staff information on how to support students with reasonable adjustments, go to the Disability Advice page.
