title: Staff Development
headings: Academic Registry Explore
body: Supporting Professional Growth and Good Practice in Academic Quality Welcome to the Academic Registry Staff Development page. We are committed to fostering continuous learning and professional growth for our team and for staff across the University, ensuring that staff have the knowledge, skills, and support needed to deliver high quality academic provision. We provide a range of ongoing opportunities to support staff in key areas of academic quality, including regular sessions covering topics such as: UK and UoP academic regulations, policies and practices Degree Apprenticeship regulations, policies and practices Assessment and exam board support Course and module data analytics In addition to the scheduled training events, Academic Registry can provide themed training bespoke to academic schools or new developments, along with one-to-one consultation sessions. Our standard programme of events is below, if you would like further information about these events please contact academicregistry@port.ac.uk Quality Matters: Student-Centric Approaches to Quality Assurance Quality Matters: Student-Centric Approaches to Quality Assurance All academic staff QA professionals Course administrators/ managers Course administrators/ By the end of this workshop, participants will have: Identified the principles of quality assurance in higher education Described UoP processes, roles and responsibilities - course-related Developed and applied practical skills to identify and assess academic quality issues Critiqued and reflected on the risks associated with a failure to maintain a high standard of academic quality across our provision Created a rationale for our collective responsibility to maintain high academic standards Aligned quality assurance activities with organisational goals and expectations By the end of this workshop, participants will have: Identified the principles of quality assurance in higher education Described UoP processes, roles and responsibilities - course-related Developed and applied practical skills to identify and assess academic quality issues Critiqued and reflected on the risks associated with a failure to maintain a high standard of academic quality across our provision Created a rationale for our collective responsibility to maintain high academic standards Aligned quality assurance activities with organisational goals and expectations Understanding key data for your course Understanding key data for your course All academic staff QA professionals Course administrators/ managers Course administrators/ 18/05/2026 10:00-12:00 (Online) 07/09/2026 10:00-12:00 (Online) 15/09/2026 14:00-16:00 (In person) Room 5.11 Mercantile The four learning outcomes are: To develop an understanding of key regulations (B3) and external performance indicators like NSS, PTES and Graduate Outcomes. title: Student Casework
headings: Academic Registry Student Casework Complaints The Student Complaints Procedure The Applicant Complaints Procedure The Complaints Procedure for Members of the Public Appeals Appeals Procedure Student Conduct Student Conduct Policy Fitness to Practise Fitness to Practice Policy Fitness to Study Fitness to Study Policy Precautionary Measures Contact Details Explore
body: The Student Casework Team is responsible for the delivery of a high-quality, effective and efficient customer-focused service to all stakeholders in relation to complaints, student conduct and appeals. The Student Casework team are responsible for three complaints procedures: This procedure allows students to complain about the majority of things that the University has/has not done, aside from complaints about academic decisions (which are handled as Appeals), and other matters of academic judgement. The Student Complaints procedure has three stages: Stage 1 (Informal Complaint) - students can raise their concerns with staff locally, or with the Student Casework team. The recipient should consider whether there is evidence of a failure to properly follow University policies, or another failure such as incorrect information given. Where an issue is identified, the individual should take steps to have the situation rectified. The outcome should be communicated to the student within 10 working days. Stage 2 (Formal Complaint) - where an informal resolution is not possible, students can request a formal investigation of their complaint by contacting the Student Casework team. This team will appoint an Investigating Officer to undertake a full review of the issues raised. Stage 3 (Review Stage) - if the student remains unhappy after the completion of the formal investigation, they may apply to the Executive Director of Corporate Governance for a review of the findings and conclusions of that investigation. This procedure is for use by applicants who are unhappy with a decision either to offer or to refuse, a place at the University. This procedure follows the same stages as the Student Complaints Procedure (above), but there is no review stage, and the outcome of the formal investigation is final. This procedure is for anyone who is not a student, applicant or member of staff, who needs to complain about something the Unviersity has/has not done. This procedure follows the same stages as the Student Complaints Procedure, but there is no review stage, and the outcome of the formal investigation is final. This procedure allows students to challenge specific academic decisions if the grounds for appeal are met, which are: The University or End Point Assessment Organisation has made an administrative error/not followed its procedures (which the student can evidence) when arriving at the decision being appealed, or The student was prevented from completing a process/procedure for reasons related to their circumstances, that they were unable to disclose, for a sound and acceptable reason related to the circumstances themselves, before the decision they are appealing was made. The Appeals Procedure has three stages: Stage 1 (Early Resolution Opportunity) - the appeal will be received by the member of staff who made the decision the student is unhappy about. If the member of staff identifies an obvious error on behalf of the University or End Point Assessment Organisation, that individual should take steps to have that error rectified, and where relevant, the effect of this change applied to the student record. Typically, this will relate to an assessment mark or progression decision, and not to a decision arrived at by a panel considering more complex issues. Stage 2 (Formal Appeal) - the appeal will be received by the Student Casework Team in the Academic Registry, who will confirm if the appeal is valid or not. If accepted for formal consideration, the appeal may be reassigned to a senior member of staff depending on the subject of the appeal. Stage 3 (Review of Stage 2) - this appeal will be received by the Academic Registry, which will check that the procedures were followed properly and determine whether the outcome was reasonable in all circumstances. This policy covers three main areas: Academic Misconduct - this process should be followed when there is a suspected breach of academic regulations Behavioural Misconduct - this process should be followed when a student has been reported to have demonstrated unacceptable behaviour (defined as behaviour that could be reasonably perceived to cause upset, distress or harm to another person or an organisation) Serious/Complex Cases - a serious/complex case is defined as the most serious of cases which typically involve harassment or violence of a discriminatory nature, such as hate incidents/crime, or sexual harassment or violence. A trained Sexual Violence Liason Officer (SVLO) is available to assist students involved in serious or complex cases, whether they are making an allegation about another student, or whether an allegation of misconduct has been made against them. This policy applies to the range of regulated programmes offered by the University, which leads to successful students being eligible to seek registration with the relevant professional, statutory and regulatory body (PSRB). A Fitness to Practice panel may be needed if a student on one of these courses breaches the safety of the student and those around them, including members of the public, and to safeguard public confidence in the profession. Fitness to Practice Process Handbook Fitness to Practice Referral Form This policy is designed to support students who may be experiencing challenges that could affect their ability to successfully participate in their academic studies. This policy aims to: Provide support for students struggling with physical or mental health issues Support and enhance student wellbeing Maintain academic standards Create a clear pathway for addressing concerns about a student's ability to study Concerns about a student's fitness for study should, in the first instance, be raised to the Faculty's Fitness to Study Advisor (usually the Associate Dean (Students)), or by contacting the Student Support Services team at studentlife@port.ac.uk. The safety and well-being of all students are our top priority. In some situations (and about any student casework procedure), we may need to take immediate safeguarding measures to protect everyone involved whilst the process is ongoing. These measures are not about assigning blame but ensuring a safe and supportive environment for all parties. Examples of safeguarding measures include: Temporary adjustments: changes to schedules, class attendance, or living arrangements, to minimise contact between the parties No-Contact Agreement: a formal agreement to prevent communications and contact between the reporting and reported parties Interim Suspension: in some circumstances, it may be necessary for a temporary suspension to be put in place to ensure safety, pending the outcome of the investigation and review panel. Suspension or other changes to student status can only be recommended by the Academic Registrar, who is advised by the Student Casework team. These steps, and similar ones, are taken to ensure that both the reporting, and reported, parties feel safe and supported throughout the process. Safeguarding measures are applied fairly and reviewed regularly as the case progresses. Precautionary Measures Guidance (with link to templates) For all queries related to student casework, please contact us at studentcasework@port.ac.uk For all queries related to Fitness to Study and Fitness to Reside, please contact the Student Support Services team at studentlife@port.ac.uk Please refer students to our MyPort web pages, and also invite them to contact us directly at studentcasework@port.ac.uk Academic Roles and Responsibilities Annual Monitoring and Academic Review Apprenticeships Quality Management Assessment Regulations Collaborative Programmes Faculties Quality Assurance Office for Students Publications Policies and Regulations Professional, Statutory and Regulatory Bodies Reasonable Adjustments Student Quality Representatives Work-based and Placement Learning Contact and departments

title: Student Quality Representatives
headings: Academic Registry Explore
body: At the University, we believe a core way to enhance student outcomes and enrich the overall experience is by working in genuine partnership with our students. From January 2026, we are proud to introduce Student Quality Representatives (SQRs). Our SQRs are current students, typically one per Faculty and London Campus, employed on a paid basis to collaborate with faculties and central teams, including Academic Registry, CADI, Academic Services, the Library etc. on Faculty and University-wide initiatives that drive quality enhancement. SQRs play a pivotal role in shaping improvements that matter most to our students. They actively contribute to projects that strengthen teaching, learning, and assessment practices, academic regulations and policies, ensuring that the student voice is embedded in our development activities. By bringing their lived experience and perspective, SQRs help us identify opportunities for innovation and continuous improvement. Working alongside Course Representatives, and in close liaison with the Students’ Union, SQRs act as an important additional quality assurance link between students and the University. For more information about Student Quality Representatives and what they are currently contributing to please contact academicregistry@port.ac.uk Meet our current team of Student Quality Representatives: Academic Roles and Responsibilities Annual Monitoring and Academic Review Apprenticeships Quality Management Assessment Regulations Collaborative Programmes Faculties Quality Assurance Office for Students Publications Policies and Regulations Professional, Statutory and Regulatory Bodies Reasonable Adjustments Student Quality Representatives Work-based and Placement Learning Contact and departments

title: Student Surveys
headings: Academic Registry Student Voice and Surveys Post Arrival Questionnaire (PAQ) – October 2025 What is the PAQ? Why it matters What will students be asked? Next steps for staff Analysis of Open Text Comments on NSS, PTES and Course Level Surveys Internal Feedback and Surveys UPSU Surveys Explore
body: Welcome to the Academic Registry Student Voice and Surveys staff resource page. We are deeply committed to fostering a learning environment where every student’s voice is heard and valued. Our student voice policy reflects this commitment by actively engaging students in shaping their education experience. Through representation, regular feedback and survey opportunities, we ensure that students have a direct impact on decisions that affect their academic journey. With every member of staff actively supporting these principles and initiatives, we can enable students to directly contribute to the continuous improvement of our courses, services and campus life, helping us to create a more inclusive and responsive university community where every student can succeed. This page has been designed for staff, our students have a dedicated MyPort page on student feedback available here: https://myport.port.ac.uk/my-course/student-surveys The University of Portsmouth is taking part in the Post Arrival Questionnaire (PAQ), in collaboration with Advance HE. This survey will run from Monday 6 October to Friday 17 October 2025. The PAQ is an evolution of the Pre-arrival Academic Questionnaire, originally developed to understand students’ prior learning experiences, expectations, concerns, and goals when entering higher education. It provides a structured roadmap of information that supports universities in tailoring both academic and professional services to student needs. The Undergraduate (UG) and Postgraduate Taught (PGT) versions of the PAQ are carefully designed to reflect the distinct characteristics of these student groups while also enabling comparisons across the sector. The PAQ serves three core purposes: Understanding students’ starting points – capturing their learning backgrounds, expectations, and challenges. Encouraging student reflection – helping students think meaningfully about their studies as they begin their academic journey. Informing staff practice – providing valuable real-time insights for academic and professional staff to help close the gap between students’ expectations and their lived experience. The PAQ is designed to be supportive and reflective. Students will be asked about: Previous study qualifications and experiences Motivations and anticipated challenges Expectations of university study Current learning approaches Attitudes towards their level of study Biographical and demographic information In total the survey will take between 20-25 minutes to complete. Be aware that students may mention the PAQ to you during the survey period. Encourage participation if asked about it – the more responses we receive, the stronger the insights. Look out for communications later this year summarising the findings for Portsmouth, including recommendations for practice. By engaging with the PAQ, we are helping to ensure that every student at Portsmouth receives the right support from the very start of their journey. The Student Voice Policy The Student Voice Policy - Valuing Students’ Views and Opinions is the central policy document which sets out our approach to listening to and responding to our students. You can find it here: Student Voice Policy - Valuing Students' Views and Opinions The above policy sets out the University’s student voice principles and expectations for both students and staff. It sets out the arrangements for student representation, and how student feedback is gathered at module, course and faculty level. The Student Voice Committee Please see the Student Voice Policy and Operational Handbook for full details. Student Voice Committees Agenda: November Student Voice Committees Agenda: March These are template agendas and can be adapted to fit and include local requirements as required. All Module Coordinators are expected to provide students with a mid-module feedback session, and ensure the feedback loop is effectively closed. These sessions can be tailored to best meet the needs of the specific cohort and course, taking on various formats to ensure the feedback process is inclusive, accessible and valuable for all students. The importance of closing the feedback loop swiftly is so that those same students who provided the feedback can see first-hand that the University has listened and responded to their thoughts and contribution. Please find here key points and best practice guidance on Mid-Module feedback and evaluation: Supporting Mid Point Evaluation [Acrobat (.pdf) - Thu, 10 Oct 2024 10:22:00 BST] For 2024-25, a review of the survey questions, format and support arrangements took place. As part of this review, in April 2025, a pilot was introduced to trial a new set of questions, specifically for Year 0 and Level 4 students. This pilot survey includes 12 core questions. In addition, course leaders will be able to select up to four sets of bank questions, which allows some flexibility in tailoring the survey to better support students, course evaluation, and continuous improvement. The Level 5 questionnaire contains 26 questions, grouped into the below eight themes (alongside two free-text comment options): The Teaching on my Course Learning Opportunities Careers Opportunities Organisation and Management Once the survey has closed, the final report will be available to the course leader on EvaSys+. The course leader will have 20 working days to close the loop and provide reflections on the free text comments. After the 20 working day period, a report is sent to the students with the overall scores and the course leaders' reflections. This is the Student Unions’ Annual Survey that goes out to all students and is incentivised. It is crucial to gather student feedback on levels of satisfaction with the Union and how we can improve our services. It is usually open from October/November but isn’t promoted until the mid to end of March for a fortnight. The survey is made up of roughly 16 questions and grouped around the following themes: Union Values and Vision, Strategic Direction, Union Services and Annual Priorities. Hot Topic is the Union’s monthly bitesize survey that aims to gather quick feedback from students on topical issues. The surveys typically include 2-3 questions and take no longer than 5 minutes to complete, the UPSU Insights team proceed to write a report based on this feedback at the end of each month and work with Union services and relevant University departments to implement impactful change. StART, an acronym for ‘Student Academic Representation Tool’, is UPSU’s primary mode of collecting Academic Feedback, analysed by the Insights Team (working with the Representation Team) to keep the Union, as well as the University, informed about any current academic issues or successes. StART is open throughout the entire academic year, with the Representation Team responsible for organising a total of three StART Talking Weeks throughout the year, carefully spread out across the year to gather feedback at set points. Once you’ve submitted your feedback, it gets sent to your Course Rep (or student representative) and then a copy also goes to our Insights team for analysis - which means that we can help drive forward the changes that you want. You can submit feedback on any topic you like, from Union activity to something about your course. The StART tool gives you the opportunity to submit neutral and positive feedback too. All insights, reports and next steps gathered by the Student’s Union are published on the Union website. For any queries regarding surveys or research carried out by the Union, please feel free to contact insights@upsu.net Do you wish to survey a group of students? Any proposal to conduct a university-wide or cross-university survey, where the sample size is >250 students, and/or the student sample is taken from more than one School or Central Service, will require the permission of the Monitoring and Evaluation Group. This will ensure that: the survey is scheduled at the most appropriate time; ethical and data protection considerations have been taken into account; the proposers have considered their evaluation and dissemination plan, and have identified which Faculty and/or University committees will consider the outcomes, and how the outcomes will be fed back to students; the results will be made available to the wider University community. Requests to undertake university-wide or cross-university surveys must be made to: surveyrequests@port.ac.uk The University actively engages in a number of pivotal external surveys, the three main surveys supported by the Academic Registry are the NSS, PTES and PRES, see below for further details. These surveys provide our students with a valuable opportunity to provide feedback to two key national bodies; the OfS and Advance HE. This in turn provides us with robust, benchmark data on our students’ experiences and our courses. A key external audience of these results is prospective students who use the outcomes to help inform their choice of institution and course. The data is also widely used in various national league tables. Reviewing and analysing results from our external surveys All NSS, PTES and PRES results are available to staff on our Student Experience and Course Outcomes Dashboard (SECO) Student Experience and Course Outcomes Survey. The data in this dashboard is refreshed on the 1st of each month. If you cannot access the Dashboard please log an access request through Information Services (IS) Hornbill. The National Student Survey (NSS) The NSS is a major national student survey conducted annually, aimed at capturing the experiences and satisfaction levels of final-year undergraduate students in the UK. The NSS provides valuable insights into our students' experiences. Core questions are grouped under the following themes: Teaching on My Course, Learning Opportunities, Assessment and Feedback, Academic Support, Organisation and Management, Learning Resources and Student Voice. The results of this survey play a critical role in shaping our academic practice, student support and university environment, and help inform prospective students’ choice of future university. NSS 2025 Help Card - Inappropriate Influence You can find more information about the NSS on the dedicated Office for Students page: National Student Survey - NSS. The NSS Survey operates between late winter and closes in early spring, with exact start and end dates published by the OfS in the previous autumn each year. The results are typically published in July/August. You can find the University’s latest NSS results on the Student Experience and Course Outcomes Dashboard. Analysis of the NSS results forms a central part of the University’s student feedback system and annual quality assurance processes. Leader's Blog | The National Student Survey Postgraduate Taught Experience Survey (PTES) Operated by Advance HE, the PTES is a national survey conducted annually in the UK to gather feedback from students registered on postgraduate courses, such as Master’s degrees, PgCerts and PgDiPs. The survey focuses on areas relating to teaching quality, assessment and feedback, academic support, organisation and management, resources, and skills development. You can find out more about the PTES survey directly from AdvanceHE Postgraduate Taught Experience Survey (PTES) | Advance HE The survey will be open from 8th April - 9th May 2025. Initial results are made available shortly after the survey has closed and benchmarking reports are published around the end of July. You can find the University's latest PTES results on the Student Experience and Course Outcomes Dashboard Student Experience and Course Outcomes Dashboard. As with the NSS, the PTES results are integral to enhancing our PG provision and are considered through our annual quality assurance mechanisms. Postgraduate Research Experience Survey (PRES) Similar to the PTES, Advance HE also operates the national PRES survey which gathers feedback from students registered on research programmes such as PhDs, MPhils, and other research-based doctorates. The survey focuses on areas such as supervision quality, resources and research environment, progress and assessment, research skills development, professional development, and overall satisfaction. You can find out more about the PRES survey on the Advance HE site Postgraduate Research Experience Survey (PRES) | Advance HE The survey will be open from 8th April - 9th May 2025. Initial results are made available shortly after the survey has closed and benchmarking reports are published around the end of July. You can find the University’s latest course PRES results on the Student Experience and Course Outcomes Dashboard Student Experience and Course Outcomes Dashboard. title: Faculties Administration
headings: Academic Services Explore
body: What do the Faculty Administrative Service Centres do? The Faculty Administrative Service Centres (FASCs) provide a comprehensive service in support of academic administration. They operate across two academic administration centres: Portland Building and the University Learning Centre. Each Centre has a dedicated management team focused on the development and delivery of a high-quality administrative support service. Centre staff work collaboratively to ensure a consistent, efficient and effective approach to student and academic administration. The FASCs handle a wide range of activities, including: Curriculum Management: coordination and administration of course structures and module changes, including the validation of information supplied by academic colleagues and liaison with Academic Registry, Central Timetabling Unit (CTU) and Centre for Academic and Digital Innovation (CADI). Student Records Management: maintaining and validating student information through the University’s student records system (SITS), including updating students details and status i.e. withdrawn, interrupted, transferred and validating student data to confirm eligibility to graduate. Assessment and Examinations: Organising coursework submissions, in person and online. Managing the extenuating circumstances process. Coordinating examinations and assessments, including Examination Boards, confirmation of results, and progression decisions. Monitoring and assisting with marks entry. Compliance: Ensuring adherence to academic and assessment regulations, meeting professional, statutory, and regulatory body requirements. Additional responsibilities In addition, the FASCs play a significant role in responding to student and academic queries, guiding students to key student support services, such as the MyPort Hub and Student Support Services. They provide significant resources in support of University activities such as Graduation and Registration, as well as Confirmation and Clearing and Open Days. title: Internal Audit
headings: Corporate Governance Internal Audit Who are the key contacts? What is the purpose of an Internal Audit? Why it matters? Who are the University internal auditors? What internal audits are taking place this academic year (2025/26)? What is the process for an internal audit? Where can I find guidance on using the Deloitte “DIAmonde” Internal Audit Management System? How can I navigate to the DIAmonde Portal Explore
body: This page has been created to provide a short guide aimed at staff who are hosting or contributing to an audit undertaken by the University’s internal audit function. The guide is intended to explain some background about the audit process, what you can expect during the course of an audit and to advise on how you can make the audit run smoothly and as useful as possible for improving and enhancing the work of your area. In general, the most effective audits are those where the relevant University staff engage most fully. We are committed to making our internal audits a positive and collaborative experience. That means starting early with key stakeholders and keeping communication clear and open every step of the way. The University’s day-to-day relationship with the Internal Audit partners (Deloitte) is managed by the Corporate Governance Department. If you need any support or have queries, feel free to reach out Anita Randell or Sarah Hare from the Risk and Resilience Team at internal-audit@port.ac.uk. Internal Audit provides independent assurance that the University’s risk management, governance and internal control processes are operating effectively. Through systematic evaluations, Internal Audit plays a key role in identifying and mitigating potential risks, ensuring compliance with statutory and regulatory requirements, and safeguarding the University’s assets. It supports informed decision-making, promotes accountability and transparency, and fosters a culture of continuous improvement—helping prevent issues before they arise and enabling the University to achieve its strategic goals. Internal Audit activity at the University is currently delivered by our partners Deloitte. Rayna Chacko serves as our day-to-day account manager, supported by Hannah Preece, who will work alongside Rayna on audit engagements. They may also draw on additional subject matter experts as needed. Oversight of our account is provided by Morag Childs, who acts as the lead partner and Head of Internal Audit for the University. Further information can be found below: Deloitte and the University of Portsmouth Internal Audit CharterDeloitte and the University of Portsmouth Internal Audit Strategy Annual audit areas are selected using a risk-based approach, informed by the Corporate Risk Register and the University’s annual audit risk assessment. During planning, all Audit Sponsors are consulted before the audit plan is approved by the University Executive Board (UEB) and the Audit & Risk Committee. The audit plan for this academic year will consist of the following exercises: • Cash Flow Forecasting and Management • Risk Management • IT Disaster Recovery • Academic Portfolio Management • Transformation Governance • Estates Contract Management A typical audit will consist of four stages: • Initial Contact: Deloitte will contact UEB sponsors or senior operational leads to arrange a planning meeting. This session identifies the audit’s focus areas, considering any known issues, challenges, or areas of concern. • Draft Scope: After the meeting, Deloitte will prepare a draft audit scope. This will be reviewed and agreed upon by the UEB sponsor and Paul Mould, Executive Director of Corporate Governance. • Final Terms: Feedback will be incorporated, and Deloitte will finalize the Terms of Reference along with an indicative timetable for the audit. • Deloitte will provide a list of required information in advance to ensure smooth progress. • Activities typically include reviewing policy and process documentation and conducting interviews with relevant staff Audit Report • A closing meeting is held with the UEB sponsor and senior contacts to discuss observations and recommendations. • Deloitte issues a risk-rated draft report to the UEB sponsor and Corporate Governance team for feedback. The University must identify actions in response to recommendations, including responsible individuals and completion timelines. • After receiving the University’s response, Deloitte issues the final report. • Agreed actions and timelines are recorded in Deloitte’s Internal Audit Management System (DIAmonde). • Responsible individuals are required to upload evidence of progress and will receive periodic reminders. The guidance below outlines examples of evidence typically accepted by auditors. Please connect to VPN to view document Action Wording Guidance • Internal Audit and Corporate Governance monitor progress and may request additional testing or a follow-up audit if needed. • The Audit and Risk Committee’s clear expectation is that actions are normally completed to the schedule agreed in the relevant Audit Report. In exceptional circumstances, those responsible may need to seek an extension to the completion date. Extension requests should be submitted to Anita Randell, Head of Risk and Resilience at internal-audit@port.ac.uk. body: The University of Portsmouth, in its role as a centre of knowledge, research, education and training, holds a great deal of information about the people who study or work here. The way in which the University manages this personal information is governed by the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). These web pages aim to explain how the University manages information in line with the GDPR, and the implications for both staff and students. There is a general explanation of the most relevant parts of the GDPR that are applicable to everyone, followed by information for students and staff on how both groups are affected by the legislation - your rights and responsibilities. title: Freedom of Information
headings: Our publication schemes Freedom of Information requests Complaining about a response to your freedom of information request Appealing a decision We use cookies
body: Freedom of Information (FOI) promotes more openness among public authorities in England, Wales and Northern Ireland. This includes the University. The Freedom of Information Act (2000) gives anyone the right to request to see the information that we hold. You can get information about the University in 2 ways: Our publication schemes hold lots of information about us and our subsidiary companies. Publishing and maintaining publication schemes is a requirements of section 19 of the Freedom of Information Act. Our publication scheme includes information that all Higher and Further Education institutions must make publicly available. View our publication schemes If the information you want isn't covered by our publication schemes, you can make a freedom of information request. We'll usually send you the information you request within 20 working days. If we can't give you some or all of the information you ask for, we'll tell you why. How to make a Freedom of Information request If you're unhappy with our response to your Freedom of Information request, you can complain or request a review of the decision in writing to the Vice-Chancellor. In your request you need to explain why you think we should provide the information you want. Email vice-chancellor@port.ac.uk with your request or send it to: The Vice-Chancellor University of Portsmouth Mercantile House Hampshire Terrace Portsmouth PO1 2EG If you're not satisfied with the response to your complaint, you can contact the Information Commissioner's Office, the regulatory body for information governance matters. We may use cookies to analyse visitor data, improve our website, show personalised content and give you a great website experience. title: Information Governance
headings: Corporate Governance Information Governance Explore
body: Information governance is a framework of policies, procedures and controls to manage information in a joined-up way. It involves knowing: what information is held who is reponsible for it Information governance also involves establishing effective procedures for all aspects of information management, including creation, maintenance, storage, use, retention and destruction. Three areas - namely, Data Protection, Records Management and Information Security - are working together to develop a coherent approach towards information governance across the University. BenefitsThe University's information is its lifeblood: it it the basis on which decisions are made and services provided. The aim of information governance is to ensure that this information - whether in paper or electronic format - is handled efficiently and effectively at all times. In particular, information governance should help to: maintain confidence in the reliability of information protect the privacy of students and staff comply with legal and regulatory requirements support decision-making by ensuring that relevant, accurate information can be readily located increase cost-effectiveness by making sure data is disposed of when no longer needed minimise the risk of information security breaches We can provide advice, guidance and training on all aspects of information management. title: Prevent duty
headings: We use cookies
body: The Counter-Terrorism and Security Act 2015 placed a responsibility upon all universities to have ‘due regard to the need to prevent people from being drawn into terrorism,’ with effect from 28 September 2015. This has become known as the ‘Prevent duty’. The Government’s Prevent duty guidance states that relevant higher education institutions need to balance their legal duties in terms of both freedom of speech and academic freedom, while also protecting student and staff welfare. The approach at Portsmouth is particularly focused on student and staff welfare. The University’s Prevent duty activities are overseen by the Safeguarding Board, which is chaired by the Deputy Vice-Chancellor Global and Student Life – who leads on the Prevent agenda, with the support of Director of Student Services as Prevent Coordinator. For further information please email kevin.child@port.ac.uk We may use cookies to analyse visitor data, improve our website, show personalised content and give you a great website experience. title: Register of interests
headings: Corporate Governance Register of Interests Declarations of Interest Declarations of Corporate Hospitality and Gifts Explore
body: The University encourages its staff to participate in a wide range of external activities that are complementary to its strategic goals and serve the public interest. Such activity brings benefits for the individual, for the University and for the public interest. These may include, for example, governorships, trusteeships and voluntary work. However, such activities may sometimes give rise to actual, potential or perceived conflicts of interest and staff must ensure that these are properly and fully declared to the University. Refer to Declarations of Interest Policy. Declarations should be made via the online form available on the Hornbill System. Declarations of Interest FAQs. It is a requirement of the Financial Regulations that you complete this form if you have received Corporate Hospitality or Gifts. Refer to Corporate Hospitality and Gifts: Procedures for Receipt of Corporate Hospitality and Gifts by Members of Staff and Governors of the University. A declaration should be made where the value of a single gift or hospitality event is above £50, or where a repeated gift cumulatively exceeds £150 in a period of twelve months, or where repeated hospitality cumulatively exceeds £150 in an annual year. Any queries about the policy should be addressed to Adrian Parry, Executive Director of Corporate Governance. Declarations should be made via the online form available on the Hornbill System. body: The Health, Safety and Compliance Team provides help, advice and support to effectively manage health and safety across all departments and activities including teaching, research, staff and students. This Policy has been produced for all staff and others to be aware of both the University's and their own responsibilities under the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act. Our Health and Safety Policy Our Health and Safety Commitment Reporting incidents, accidents, near misses and hazards is essential to enable us to improve the University's health and safety management. Report a Building Issue Reporting a gas leak, electrical / heating failure, or a water leak / flood on university premises. Health and Safety A-Z If you are looking for information or advice relating to a specific health and safety subject, please find more information here. title: DBS Checks
headings: Corporate Governance Frequently Asked Questions 1, What is a DBS Check? 2, Do I need a DBS check? 3, How do I complete a DBS check? 4, What information do I need to complete a DBS check? 5, I do not have the ID/address requirements requested by my department.  What do I do? 6, Do I need to pay for my DBS check? 7, What if I am not using my birth name; can I still get my check? 8, Do you offer private spaces for ID check? 9, Can my department keep a copy of my certificate or ID documents? 10, Are DBS checks transferrable? 11, My check is really old – do I need a new one? 12, My DBS check is taking a very long time to come back – what can I do? 13, What happens if my DBS check comes back with content? 14, I’ve not received my certificate.  What do I do? 15, Who do I contact with queries regarding DBS checks? Explore
body: ‌‌Everything you ever wanted to know about DBS Checks...! A Disclosure and Barring Services Check, DBS check for short, is a check of a person's criminal convictions and cautions to help ensure it is appropriate for a person to work with vulnerable groups, including children. If you are a member of staff and you have queries regarding DBS checks, please contact the HR Service Centre. If you are a student and you have queries regarding DBS checks, please contact your department. The University uses an umbrella company, Due Diligence Checking (DDC), to complete our DBS checks. Should you need a DBS check and you are a member of staff, HR will be able to administer your check for you. If you are a student, your department will be able to administer your check. If a DBS check is required for your role, as either staff or student, you will be sent an email from either HR or your department. This email will contain a list of the information required for the ID check and address checks. If you do not have the information requested by your department, please contact them directly so they can work with you to arrange a suitable alternative. If your DBS check is required for your studies or your role, the University will assume the cost of the DBS check. Of course. If you have changed your name by deed pole, please bring in this information. If you are currently using a different identity to that which you were born with, and do not have official supporting documentation, please click here for more information. If you require a private space to carry out a DBS check, please contact your department. They will be able to provide a suitable space. No. Students or members of staff are required to keep their DBS certificate and produce it upon request. The University will keep a record through our DDC system showing basic information, including applicant name, check required and some applicant details, but we will not keep the result of the check more than 3 months. DBS checks are not transferrable to a new company. If you have a check from a previous role or studies, unless you have signed up for the DBS Update Service*, a new check will be required. If you are moving courses/job roles or wish to use your course DBS for University sponsored volunteering roles, please contact dbs@port.ac.uk. In certain situations, it may be possible to use the same DBS check, but this is on a case-by-case basis. *If you have any queries concerning the Update Service, please contact dbs@port.ac.uk. DBS checks do not currently have an expiration date. Some courses/roles may have external requirements that will mandate a rechecking timeline, but at this time, the University does not require checks to be re-issued. Legally DDC cannot chase a stalled check until it hits certain trigger points – the most common of these is 60 days. If your check has stalled, and you cannot start your course/job role until your check has been returned, please contact dbs@port.ac.uk. If your DBS check comes back with content, more commonly known as convictions or cautions, you will be contacted directly by your department or HR with further instructions. If you have not received your DBS certificate in the post, and it has been issued within the last 93 days, please visit this website for more information. If you have any other queries relating to DBS, please email dbs@port.ac.uk. Freedom of Information Information Governance Modern Slavery Statement Register of interests Contact and departments

title: General information
headings: Estates and Campus Services General Information Definition of Works Repairs and Maintenance Long Term Maintenance Rechargeable Capital Explore
body: Repairs and Maintenance Long Term Maintenance These works comprise all aspects of the built estate that break and wear out, excluding Long Term Maintenance. Currently, anyone in the University can report faults and request repairs and maintenance. The procedure for so doing can be found on the Repairs and Maintenance page. A large proportion of this category is planned maintenance generated by the Estates Department. These works comprise specific maintenance contracts that have been identified by the Estates Department as being required to bring the estate up to an acceptable level of condition. Projects are scheduled over a number of years and prioritized by urgency of need and amount of funding available in any given financial year. These works comprise those items specifically requested by Faculties and/or departments and which they will fund. All types of work are catered for from minor items such as putting up shelves etc, to relatively major adaptions and redecoration. The procedure for requesting such works can be found on the Rechargeable Works page. These are one off contracts generated by the needs of the University strategic plans and funded centrally. They generally comprise new build or major refurbishment projects but can also include a number of smaller projects brought together to enable a series of events to take place. Such events could include departmental relocations requested centrally which would require alteration and adaption works in a number of locations. Details may be found on the Capital Works page. title: Engineering maintenance and management
headings: Estates and Campus Services Engineering, Maintenance and Management Explore
body: Within the mechanical section of the Estates Department, we are responsible for the operation maintenance and development of plant equipment and services associated with the built environment. Our long term aim is to provide the users throughout the University with comfortable working conditions whilst at the same time minimising our impact on the global environment in terms of energy consumed and control of pollution. At the core of our operational strategy is the Satchwell BAS 2800+ Building Energy Management System which incorporates the latest technology and software. This allows us to control and monitor plant operation and status from a central location at our Guildhall Walk offices which ultimately provides an efficient and focussed response to faults and breakdowns. In our approach to maintenance we use a proven combination of in house staff, supplemented by specialist sub contractors and currently have a schedule of maintenance for all plant and equipment throughout the University. title: Electrical
headings: Estates and Campus Services Electrical Explore
body: The aim of the Electrical Section is the safe and reliable provision of the services that the team are responsible for with minimum inconvenience to building users. The Electrical Section is responsible for the design, provision and maintenance as appropriate throughout the estate of: Electricity supply at the correct tarrifs High Voltage 11,000 Volt distribution sytems Low Voltage 415/240 Volt distribution systems Sub circuits to lighting, power and specialist equipment within and externally to building Internal and external lighting and power Electric heating systems using direct and storage heaters Electric passenger, goods and disabled lifts with either hydraulic or traction mechanisms Internal and external cable management systems The installation of structured data wiring Intruder alarm systems with automatic sensors and remote signalling of fire conditions CCTV systems with local and remote control and recording linked to local authority, public and police systems with proper policies for control and distribution of images Central control room for monitoring, recording and management of CCTV,Security and Fire Systems using a computerised alarm handling package Monitoring of electricity supplies to buildings and building consumption using Ease II software and remote connections Consultants, Contractors and Specialists are employed by the electrical team as required due to work load and or the nature of the work to be undertaken. title: Property records and database
headings: Estates and Campus Services Property Records and Database MAXIMO ARCHIBUS Explore
body: The Estates Department operates two corporate systems supporting the maintenance of the University's estate in both Help Desk and Work Order Tracking. This is the maintenance system at the heart of our operation and provides the main support for our Help Desk, Work Order tracking, preventative maintenance, time logging and asset management. Requests to our Help Desk are passed electronically to our supervisors who prioritise and allocate the work to the appropriate tradesmen. When jobs are complete, details of materials and time are entered back into the system for subsequent analysis and financial processing. Details of all space owned by the University are held in the Archibus database, which is linked to digitised plans held in an AutoCAD database. The space database is used to provide statistical returns to the Funding Council and is the basis of calculations for space charging, while the AutoCAD plans are maintained to provide up to date details of room and floor layouts, as well as design changes for refurbishment. Each year a validation exercise is carried out to ensure that space is correctly assigned to departments, and throughout the year amendments are made to reflect changes in ownership, room use, door numbers and areas of rooms. The department's link with Space Management ensure consistency of data between Archibus and Facilities CMIS, the University's timetabling system. title: International Student Advisers
headings: Global International Student Advisers What do we do? Contact information: International Student Advisers: Explore
body: The International Student Advice team provide immigration advice and support to international students studying at the university. They are are the only team in the university who are qualified to give visa advice to students. They also manage the International Orientation Programme and social and cultural events. Advice on Tier 4 student visas, Short-term Study visas and signposting for visa options after studies Lost and stolen visas and passports for students Cultural and social events throughout the year including annual Global Week, Festival of Cultures, and a full programme of coach excursions to cities and attractions in the UK which are open to all students Deliver the Orientation Programme for new arriving International and EU students in September and January Publish a regular newsletter for International and EU students called WeAreGlobal Manage the UoP Global Community Facebook Page Provide general support and signposting for International and EU students We are open to students Monday - Friday 10am -3pm. Students do not need an appointment to see us, they can just drop in. Current students contact: international.student.adviser@port.ac.uk Prospective students/applicants contact: Tier4-visa@port.ac.uk Telephone: 02392 845121 or 3524 International Student Advice Manager alana.holland@port.ac.uk International Student Adviser anna.vaernes@port.ac.uk International Student Adviser (Mon, Weds, Thurs) laura.ford@port.ac.uk International Student Adviser (Tues, Weds, Fri) nicki.gregory@port.ac.uk Current students contact: international.student.adviser@port.ac.uk Prospective students/applicants contact: Tier4-visa@port.ac.uk Telephone: 02392 845121 or 3524 Contact and departments

title: Hybrid Working
headings: Human Resources Hybrid Working Support and Guidance for Staff and Managers Explore
body: The University has agreed to adopt a hybrid approach to working arrangements and the ability for staff in specific roles to be able to work both on campus and at home. The University recognises that some roles require 100% attendance on campus, and this guidance does not apply to those roles. Some roles, whilst partially student facing, may have elements of the role that can be delivered from home. Whether a role is to be worked fully on campus or on a hybrid working basis is determined by the business needs of the University, and will be a decision made by the Head of Department/Faculty Manager/Director of Professional Service. Hybrid working will be evolving as it is implemented over the 2021/22 academic year and it will be reviewed regularly as we learn together how to best work with our new arrangements. Guidance for staff can be found under My responsibilities. Guidance for managers can be found under Ensuring team health, safety and wellbeing. For information, the guidance document links are also linked below: Hybrid Working Guidance Hybrid Working Guidance for Managers Hybrid Working Flowchart HR Business Partnering Team Learning and Development My Rewards and Benefits Occupational Health Service Equality and Diversity Contact and departments

title: Checking an Individual's Right To Work in the UK
headings: Human Resources Checking an Individual's Right to Work in the UK Right to Work Document Checks Guidance on employing Student Visa Sponsored Students Explore
body: As an employer, we have a duty to prevent illegal working. The Immigration, Asylum and Nationality Act 2006 says that employers should conduct document checks which show that your potential employee has the right to work in the UK and is permitted to do the type of work you are offering. You are required to undertake the right to work check at least 5 days before the employment commences. This is to ensure a smooth transition and set up in our HR and IT systems, and to ensure the University remains compliant with Home Office Statutory requirements. You should not make assumptions about a person’s right to work in the UK or their immigration status on the basis of their colour, nationality, ethnic or national origins. The best way to ensure that you do not discriminate is to treat all job applicants fairly and make the same document checks.. The current UKVI checklist provides information and guidance about checking an individual's Right to Work in the UK. If you will be employing students who are in the UK under Student visa Sponsorship, including Undergraduate, Postgraduate Taught and Postgraduate Research students. As part of the visa conditions, there are restrictions on the number of hours sponsored students can work. Sponsored students with restrictions undertaking variable hours/casual seasonal contracts can work up to 20 hours per week during term time. The following should be noted: Undergraduate students are restricted to working 20 hours per week during the academic teaching blocks. Postgraduate Taught students are restricted to working 20 hours per week during their specific course dates. Postgraduate Research students are restricted to working 20 hours per week every week unless they are on an authorised period of annual leave which is recorded on Student Records. Sponsored students still on PTHP contracts in the Department of Professional and Flexible Working) can work up to 8 hours per week because there is 2.5 hours work in total for each 1 hour of teaching (2.5 x 8 = 20). There are sanctions for non-compliance of UKVI Sponsor Licence Policy Guidelines Any member of core staff found not to be adhering to their responsibilities under the UKVI Sponsor Licence policy guidelines will be subject to disciplinary action through the University's formal procedure. Any member of casual staff found not to be adhering to their responsibilities under the UKVI Sponsor Licence policy guidelines will have the offer of paid employment withdrawn. If you believe there has been a breach of visa restrictions, or illegal working has been taking place, notify the Chief People Officer, or their nominated deputy with immediate effect, so the student can be suspended from work until the potential breach is investigated. It is critical that this happens without unnecessary delay. For guidance regarding allowing work outside of the contract dates, please refer to Employing Sponsored Students below and the Right To Work Document Check webpage. title: Employee Assistance Programme (EAP)
headings: Human Resources Employee Assistance Programme (EAP) Accessing the Helpline Is it confidential? Explore
body: Supporting the mental health and wellbeing of all our colleagues is incredibly important to us, and our new Employee Assistance Programme will help us do just that. Whether you’re facing problems at work or at home, you can access impartial, independent and entirely confidential advice from qualified counsellors for any issues you may be facing, such as Anxiety; Bereavement; Stress; Depression; Workplace Issues; Trauma; Relationships; Finances and debt; and Family Difficulties. Confidential 24/7 Helpline: The telephone helpline is available 24/7, 365 days a year and is completely confidential and to anyone needing mental health support. To access the helpline, simply call 0800 023 9324 from any standard UK landline or mobile phone Digital Resources and Self-Help:A wide offering of resources is also available and includes downloadable self-help workbooks, debt advice and more. Visit the EAP Platform to find out more: www.port.ac.uk/myeap The University does not know who uses the service unless the individual personally chooses to tell someone about their contact with Vivup. Vivup do provide statistics to the University to show how many employees use the service and the broad types of issues raised, for example; ‘relationship breakdown at home’ or ‘bullying and harassment in the workplace. No information is ever passed on which could potentially identify an individual. title: Merchandise
headings: Recruitment, Marketing and Communications University Merchandise Personalise your clothes Explore
body: Staff are able to order branded University merchandise via the Students’ Union website to ensure brand quality and provide a more efficient way of ordering products. A full range of branded products can be found in the online shop and bespoke products (that are supported by a strong business case and approved by Marketing and Communications) can be ordered using an online order form. Here are some things to remember: Ordering in bulk may offer you better prices so be sure to think of your future needs to allow you to place larger orders for better value. If you are ordering for a specific event such as an Open Day, make sure to do this enough in advance before the event. The Union strives to keep all core range products in stock, but if you are placing a large order be aware you may need to allow up to three weeks for the stock to be delivered to the Union. Lead times and delivery options vary depending on what’s ordered, but you should leave around 3 working days for small standard orders, 3 weeks (15 working days) for standard orders of over 50 items and 8 weeks (40 working days) for bespoke orders to allow for quality checks and delivery. You can refer to the Merchandise Catalogue for more detailed lead times on standard items. Products that are in stock will have quicker lead times and can be collected from the Students’ Union shop. Design for bespoke products need to be approved by the Brand team through the online order form. The University of Portsmouth has strict quality and ethical standards to ensure merchandise appropriately represents the brand. You will need to either pay online with a credit card or raise a purchase order (PO) for the payment. The Union does not use cost codes and cannot process orders until a credit card payment has been made or a PO number has been received. The Union can post small items in the internal mail but for larger items staff will need to arrange delivery with Estates. Visit UPSU’s website for Official Merchandise for more information or email merchandise@upsu.net A personalisation service is also available in the Students’ Union shop where for £2 per line of text you can add your own text to garments from the existing shop range or touch up clothes from home that you already own. title: Social media
headings: Recruitment, Marketing and Communications Social Media Guidelines Approval to use social media for University of Portsmouth Points to consider Responsibilities Accounts Explore
body: The purpose of the social media guidelines are: to support best practice to protect the University’s reputation to promote effective use of social media as part of the University's marketing strategy The guidelines apply to you if you’re a University of Portsmouth employee or student creating or contributing to any kind of social media which uses the University’s name or department therein. This includes all course, faculty, research and support service accounts. This encompasses Twitter, Facebook, LinkedIn, Instagram, YouTube and Snapchat channels that you use to represent the University for the purpose of information sharing and promotion. Other channels will be assessed as they are released and social media platforms which post-date the creation of these guidelines should be assumed as included between document updates. Students using social media should do so in line with the student code of conduct to not bring the University into disrepute, and are not covered therefore in this document as they are not representing the University directly. The University expects all who participate in social media representing the University of Portsmouth to understand and to follow these guidelines. Failure to do so could put the University’s reputation at risk and put your future participation in social media activity on the behalf of the University at risk. As new technologies and social networking tools emerge, these guidelines will evolve and it is your responsibility to check this information regularly if you contribute to social media as a member of University of Portsmouth staff. Social media is an essential tool for communication for the University and its use has an impact on our reputation and the way we are perceived. In the same way that our web pages and publications are subject to editorial processes we need to know who is using social media on the behalf of the University and ensure accounts remain active and serving a clear purpose within the University social media structure. All requests for new social media presences managed by staff and bearing the name of the University (or one of its departments or groups) and/or logo should be made to marketing.campaigns@port.ac.uk and have the approval of the relevant Head of Department, Head of School or other appropriate person. There need to be clear objectives for using social media. Discussion of these goals allows us to establish whether accounts already exist that can fulfil these objectives or if a new account is required to best meet them, as well as which channels will work best and how to utilise these. Effective use of social media is interactive and consistent, and therefore has resource implications so consideration needs to be given to whom has responsibility for populating accounts on social media. This responsibility should be shared in order to ensure continuity during absence and continued access to accounts in the case of personnel changes. This is to provide a good level of content and ensure continuing support of the accounts. In many cases it may be that an area or service can input to one of the University’s already established channels rather than create new accounts that can fragment our messaging and miss the intended audience. There are billions of words and online conversations - what can you add that’s different and why will anyone want to hear from you? Think about a potential student who might be engaging online with several universities, who are all trying to be an applicant’s first. What can we offer that’s different and is of benefit? Or a researcher looking for places to read about their chosen subject, how can you appeal to them enough to follow your account over a publisher? These types of questions are not meant to deter use, but to encourage consideration of content before beginning so that you have the best chance of success and that time can be spent in the most efficient way. The best way to get your contributions read, watched or listened to, is to write about and share things that people will value. University of Portsmouth social communication should help our customers, partners, and colleagues, and be clear in which of these audiences it is for, even if this varies from post to post. It should be interesting, generous (what can we give in terms of content that people want or will want to share?) and build a sense of community. It is adding value if it helps people improve knowledge; or business activities to grow; staff to feel more confident and informed at work or potential students to understand what makes the University of Portsmouth standout. Personality is important. Social media must be sociable and should retain this element even for corporate accounts. People engage with the personality behind a brand, not the brand face and therefore it’s important to create a mix of content to reflect this. This may be showcasing the caring nature of a support service with shares and engagement with relevant news stories, or another example would be to show the creativity of those representing creative courses. If you need help establishing this email marketing.campaigns@port.ac.uk and we can work to establish what makes your social media presence unique, yet still a clear part of the wider University brand. Only think about social media if you have the time and the material to update it regularly. An unused Facebook group, Twitter account or blog can look worse than none at all. You must have an idea of what types of content can be posted before you begin - what type of updates/info/pictures will you upload? Who will be responsible for keeping it up to date? Can you commit to update it regularly? There is no specific best time or frequency to post to your social media accounts, and advice on this varies depending on the source of the information. You will start to see trends as you develop and experiment on your accounts around what type of content works well and when. There are some common sense principles to be applied however, such as posting during lunch or commuting hours for a corporate audience, or scheduling content for evenings, outside of scheduled lecture times, for the student audience etc. The decision to post should be driven by having something to say, rather than to fulfil a quota, and if there is nothing to say then do not post, it will be likely to have more of a detrimental effect than a positive one. If you’re concerned you don’t have enough to say, try planning a month’s worth of content, if this is a struggle, perhaps there's another account your messaging could add to. Twitter accounts should be weighted 70/30 in favour of retweeting other people’s content, such as industry bodies, trade publications, researchers, news articles etc. This method helps your account become a hub of information on a topic, rather than a self-serving broadcast account, like shouting in a crowd rather than having conversations. Instagram should only be used by those with enough visual content to keep it populated. These images should be on brand, not of posed groups of people, and varied. Facebook pages are restricted to a structure, and areas outside that will need to discuss with the Social Media Coordinator as to why any exceptions. The University should not use profiles instead of pages, but groups tied to pages will sometimes be a better option for an activity. Snapchat is restricted to one University level account, which colleagues are encouraged to add to rather than create fragmented accounts on this channel. Advice and sign off on accounts, their uses and the set up can be obtained by emailing marketing.campaigns@port.ac.uk. Responsibility for the maintenance of a corporate social media channel rests with its creators/administrators and should not misrepresent the University. Accounts other than the core University of Portsmouth accounts should largely consist of content regarding their own area of expertise, rather than the university sector as a whole. The exception to this is when celebrating success of the University as a whole, or where mass communication is required, as this should be amplified as far as possible across all corporate accounts. Managers should consider resources for social media – how much time should a staff member devote? – it is an integral part and highly effective method of communicating with internal and external audiences, and should be allocated the time that reflects this importance. Social media does not always require express written responsibilities in a job description, as it is an extension of existing duties. For example, course promotion or communicating with students are activities which exist outside of social media, and the social media element is simply another route to the audiences here. If a channel/page is no longer relevant, has resulted in very little engagement or there is insufficient resource to maintain it, it should be deleted or made non-public. If personnel changes mean that an account cannot be accessed in order to remove it, all efforts should be made through the help desk of the platform in question (for example contacting the Facebook support team) to have it removed or access given to another administrator to do this. If you are in need of support to do this please contact marketing.campaigns@port.ac.uk. Contact marketing.campaigns@port.ac.uk (put NEW ACCOUNT in the subject) to discuss the creation of new accounts. A decision will be made regarding the account creation based on how it fits within the social account structure of the University, whether an account already exists that can be used for the proposed need, and whether there is sufficient resource and content to fuel the account long term. For branded corporate accounts, engagement must represent the University, or any of its departments or activities. The appropriate manager must approve its creation, more than one administrator must be assigned to maintain it, and details of login information must be stored so lasting access can be maintained to the account should personnel changes occur. Once created, login details must be made available if requested for admin purposes to the University’s Social Media Coordinator and Marketing. Once created advice and training can be requested on the use of the account for the purposes of best practice, to ensure effectiveness. The account must serve and support the University strategy and the social guidelines must be adhered to. Never represent the University in a false or misleading way. Be prepared to provide evidence that claims and statements are true and not misleading. Create a balance between created and curated content - this ensures your account is seen as a credible source of information about your field rather than purely self serving and gives people a reason to follow. This is to be weighted in favour of curated as this enables population of the account even on tight time scales and shows the account to demonstrate varied sources Use your area of expertise and write what you know – your ‘voice’ and perspective will make this unique and interesting. You should not post messages offering advice or opinions on behalf of the University when this is not part of your approved work. You don’t have to be an expert on behalf of other departments or post messages about their areas of work - refer enquiries outside your direct field to the appropriate University website pages or colleague. Write in the first person and be transparent. When you are writing about the University, use your real name, identify that you work for the University, and be clear about your role. If you have a professional or vested interest in what you are discussing, make sure that this is declared. Make sure your efforts to be transparent don't violate the University's privacy, confidentiality, and legal guidelines or bring the University into disrepute. Reply to comments in a timely manner, preferably by the next business day. Respect proprietary information, content, financial disclosure regulations and confidentiality. It is your responsibility to ensure you have sought appropriate copyright permission for third party content and have permission to use any images. Do not publish or report conversations that are meant to be private or internal to the University of Portsmouth without express permission from senior staff. Never comment on anything related to legal matters, litigation, or any parties with whom the University may be in dispute. Do not denigrate our competitors or the University of Portsmouth. Seek guidance before participating in social media when the topic being discussed may be considered sensitive (e.g. a crisis situation, intellectual property, a volatile news story, commercially sensitive material). Refer social media activity around sensitive topics to the Social Media Coordinator or, in their absence, to the Head of Marketing and Campaigns Apologise quickly and honestly for any mistakes you make in online conversations. Remember, you may be personally responsible for your content.
