title: Digital content
headings: Recruitment, Marketing and Communications Digital content Positioning of the website University websites For current students For external audiences For staff Website content standards Using our websites effectively The role of the Digital Content team The role of devolved content/website editors Web management processes Content ownership Timelines for changes Critical and high priority updates Updates to existing content Changing webpage titles, addresses and menu locations Suggesting new content Documents Why don’t we use documents on the websites? Adding necessary documents Drupal forms Redirects Removing webpages Specific processes for port.ac.uk Blogs Retention Course webpages Department webpages Event webpages Eventbrite events Manual event pages News articles Research webpages Staff profile pages Specific processes for MyPort Blogs Updating existing content on MyPort Student events Additional requests Analytics Other websites Resources Explore
body: This webpage defines the processes and responsibilities for updating and maintaining: MyPort (myport.port.ac.uk) Other websites (for example marketing.port.ac.uk) are not maintained by the Digital Content Team and are not covered by this document. The Staff Essentials website (staff.port.ac.uk) is managed by a different team and uses a different model of operation. The Digital Content team are not responsible for the staff website. Website content standards The role of the Digital Content team The role of devolved content/website editors Web management processes A successful website is a tool that enables visitors to find and action information efficiently, with minimal stress. Minimising the stress of accessing and actioning information has many parts, including making sure that: content is findable - so users can reach the information content is findable - so users can reach the information content is understandable - so users know what to do content is understandable - so users know what to do content is actionable - so there are clear next steps for them to complete upon digesting the website information content is actionable - so there are clear next steps for them to complete upon digesting the website information Good webpages are not created in isolation, so all “good” website content must also link to the goal or purpose of the overall website. Our main University websites are divided by audience. Each website’s goal aligns with its audience. Most content will easily fit into one of these categories. MyPort is for current students including undergraduate, postgraduate and PGR students. This website is a primary channel for communicating with current students, and is not for communicating with prospective students, staff, or the general public. Because this website is in the public domain (i.e. findable on search engines and accessible without logging it) it needs to meet all public website standards. port.ac.uk is for external audiences such as prospective students, job seekers, the media, businesses and research funding bodies. Some types of content (course pages, staff profiles, news articles, blogs, and events) have specific update processes. staff.port.ac.uk is for internal staff information, and staff must log in to see the website. The website is maintained by the Corporate Communications team within the RMC department. As a University, we have processes in place to ensure that all content published on our websites is high quality and meets our content standards. Our key content standards include: Accuracy — We are legally responsible for what we say on our website, so it's vital that the information we publish is accurate. Information owners from relevant departments are responsible for ensuring that facts and information published on public websites are accurate — for example, a Student Service is responsible for their information. Accuracy — We are legally responsible for what we say on our website, so it's vital that the information we publish is accurate. Information owners from relevant departments are responsible for ensuring that facts and information published on public websites are accurate — for example, a Student Service is responsible for their information. Information owners from relevant departments are responsible for ensuring that facts and information published on public websites are accurate — for example, a Student Service is responsible for their information. Accessibility — Digital accessibility means building a digital product (like a website) so that its information can be accessed and understood by as many users as possible — including people who use assistive technologies like screen readers, magnifiers, or keyboard navigation. Content editors are responsible for ensuring that the content they produce and publish meets the relevant accessibility requirements; namely, WCAG 2.2 AA standard. Content editors will usually focus on the Perceivable WCAG principle. The Digital Experience team is responsible for non-content related accessibility requirements, has oversight of the general health and accessibility score of the websites, and manages an annual accessibility audit for port.ac.uk and MyPort. Content editors are responsible for ensuring that the content they produce and publish meets the relevant accessibility requirements; namely, WCAG 2.2 AA standard. Content editors will usually focus on the Perceivable WCAG principle. Content editors are responsible for ensuring that the content they produce and publish meets the relevant accessibility requirements; namely, WCAG 2.2 AA standard. Content editors will usually focus on the Perceivable WCAG principle. The Digital Experience team is responsible for non-content related accessibility requirements, has oversight of the general health and accessibility score of the websites, and manages an annual accessibility audit for port.ac.uk and MyPort. The Digital Experience team is responsible for non-content related accessibility requirements, has oversight of the general health and accessibility score of the websites, and manages an annual accessibility audit for port.ac.uk and MyPort. Branding — A consistent tone of voice means that communications from the University are recognisable. When users are confident that they’re hearing from us directly, they’re happier and more likely to engage with what we say. Content editors are responsible for ensuring that the content they produce and publish meets brand guidelines and requirements, as specified in the Marketing Portal. This includes writing in the University tone of voice and using appropriate imagery. The Recruitment, Marketing and Communications department has ultimate responsibility for branding on the website. Search Engine Optimisation (SEO) — Optimising content for search means building webpages to include pointers that tell search engines like Google what our content is about. This boosts our visibility in search engines, and ultimately increases the impressions and visibility of our website. Content editors are responsible for ensuring that the content they produce and publish meets SEO and AIO (AI Optimisation) guidelines and current best practice, where necessary. Responsibility for SEO and AIO performance and reporting sits within the Recruitment, Marketing and Communications department, primarily between the Digital Experience and Digital Content teams. Usability — Usability means building websites and information so that visitors can find the information they need. Content editors are responsible for ensuring that the content they produce and publish meets the UX guidelines. The User Experience (UX) Team is responsible for overall review and recommendations for UX improvements across University websites. Usability — Usability means building websites and information so that visitors can find the information they need. Content editors are responsible for ensuring that the content they produce and publish meets the UX guidelines. Content editors are responsible for ensuring that the content they produce and publish meets the UX guidelines. The User Experience (UX) Team is responsible for overall review and recommendations for UX improvements across University websites. The User Experience (UX) Team is responsible for overall review and recommendations for UX improvements across University websites. Each of our websites has a specific goal that relate to their target audience. Using these sites effectively: streamlines content so visitors can find the most up to date and relevant information streamlines content so visitors can find the most up to date and relevant information ensures public messaging (such as Clearing and campaigns) is not diluted by communications for internal audiences ensures public messaging (such as Clearing and campaigns) is not diluted by communications for internal audiences means that students and staff know where to access information, and don't need to juggle multiple resources unnecessarily means that students and staff know where to access information, and don't need to juggle multiple resources unnecessarily Crucially, information that does not meet a website goal does not belong on that website. Just because something can go online, does not mean it will be published on these sites. The Digital Content team has overall responsibility for all content management across port.ac.uk and MyPort. Page design (i.e. the specific use of components on a webpage) Page design (i.e. the specific use of components on a webpage) Content and design approval Content and design approval In collaboration with other teams in RMC, the Digital Content team also contributes to the following for port.ac.uk and MyPort: Practically, this means that the Digital Content team approves the creation of new webpages or webpage updates and may reject webpage submissions if the content does not meet University webpage requirements, standards, or best practice. We will suggest alternatives where appropriate, such as adding information to an existing webpage rather than creating a new page. The Digital Content team owns the overall content standards and content design of our websites (port and myport), but is not responsible for the accuracy of the information on the website. Accuracy of information is the responsibility of the relevant University department. Department information owners are responsible for ensuring that information on the public websites is correct. For example, details about University services are provided by the teams of those departments. Teams can provide website content suggestions through a Service Desk ticket, or by editing webpages themselves as content editors. The content editor role is specifically to enable editors to update factual information on webpages across port.ac.uk and MyPort. Editors will update information on webpages that have already been designed and published, and submit changes for approval and publishing. This role is not to enable editors from departments outside of RMC to produce or publish new webpages, move pages, change URLs, or make significant webpage changes. Content editors are responsible for ensuring that information is correct and submitting changes to be published. This role is ideal for editors who need to regularly update information on the port.ac.uk website or MyPort for accuracy — for example a student service. If you only need to make updates a handful of times per year, it may be more efficient to submit a Service Desk ticket with your content updates instead. Staff may request to become a website editor by requesting the relevant training. Training is delivered by the Digital Skills team, who will be in touch to book a training session in due course. After training for port.ac.uk or MyPort, editors need to submit an editing and Tone of Voice assessment to the Digital Content team before you account is created. The content of port.ac.uk and Myport is managed by the Digital Content team. The Digital Content team oversees webpage updates and may reject changes if they do not meet University webpage requirements, standards, or best practice. We are not responsible for factual accuracy on the website, and will not change factual information during the editing and publishing process. Accuracy of information published on the website is the responsibility of the relevant University department. Other teams in the Recruitment, Marketing and Communications department are responsible for technical issues and code updates to the website. The exact timeline for changes to the website will depend on the size and priority of the changes, as well as the time of year and team capacity. Most factual changes are actioned within 2-3 weeks of us receiving a complete Service Desk ticket will all relevant information. Complex tickets, such as page redesigns, commonly take longer. During high priority periods (such as Clearing updates) there may be change freezes on the website, so non-priority updates will not be made at this time. The most effective way to request website updates is to provide all relevant information through the Service Desk. Requesting meetings with team members is not usually the most time-efficient way to update your content. Priority updates include: Inaccurate or misleading information Inaccurate or misleading information Concerns about compliance or legal issues Concerns about compliance or legal issues Technical issue or error messages Technical issue or error messages Timely changes that clearly impact student recruitment, our research profile or reputation Timely changes that clearly impact student recruitment, our research profile or reputation For a critical request, provide all relevant information through the Service Desk and email digitalcontent@port.ac.uk to notify us of the incoming ticket. Please only email if the updates are time-sensitive or high priority (as listed above). If you want to update a course page or a staff profile, you should check the relevant guidance below. To update other pages, send us a ticket on the Service Desk. Please include the URL of the page you are referring to, and the changes you suggest. These changes should always be completed by the Digital Content team. Changing page titles impacts how pages appear in menus across the website, in search (in Google and on our own website), and how we link to the page from other areas of the site. If page name or URL change is not added correctly, it can cause broken links, poor user journeys, or even redirect loops where no-one can visit the page and our site appears to be "under maintenance". This can impact the Drupal CMS page too, which only a Website Administrator can resolve. To suggest a new page name, menu location, or URL, submit a Service Desk ticket. Please include the URL of the page you are referring to, and the changes you suggest. The process to request a new webpage may be different depending on what you want to add. You do not need to request webpages for new courses or new researchers with Pure profiles. We have existing processes for these pages. For dated content, such as news, events and blogs, see the specific guidelines for the relevant website below. The Digital Content team builds new pages on the port.ac.uk and MyPort websites. Before proposing a page, please review our guide: Where should my content go? (VPN needed) All new webpages on port.ac.uk and MyPort must have a clear page goal and call to action, enable the target audience/user complete the task they are on the page to do, and contribute to at least one content pillar: recruitment, research, reputation, retention. All non-dated content (long-term pages that are added to our website inside existing sections of the website) need to be reviewed annually by your team to ensure the factual information is still accurate. Use the Service Desk to contact us about a new webpage and let us know what your page is about and who is responsible for it. Your request should include: what kind of page you suggest (e.g. content page) what kind of page you suggest (e.g. content page) who the primary audience of the content is, and what action they will take after visiting the webpage who the primary audience of the content is, and what action they will take after visiting the webpage any rich media you'd like to include (such as photos, videos, social media links) any rich media you'd like to include (such as photos, videos, social media links) where you think the page should sit on the website where you think the page should sit on the website if the page needs to be live before a specific date if the page needs to be live before a specific date who is responsible for maintaining the webpage who is responsible for maintaining the webpage If you do not provide the above information in your initial request, we cannot process your webpage request and it will take longer to publish any approved webpages, or to identify alternative content solutions if a webpage is not suitable. Generally, we do not use documents on the website. You should always consider whether a document is the best way to communicate your information. PDFs used to be considered ideal because they were not editable, kept whatever layout the document publisher saved, and were viewable on a range of devices without needing specific software to open them. These historic PDF benefits now present problems: the unresponsive design means that users on small screens can struggle to view all information the unresponsive design means that users on small screens can struggle to view all information when a user saves a PDF to their device, they won’t see updated information if it changes when a user saves a PDF to their device, they won’t see updated information if it changes users with assistive technologies like screen readers struggle to interpret the information, unless the document is tagged correctly users with assistive technologies like screen readers struggle to interpret the information, unless the document is tagged correctly Webpages now provide these historic benefits — and they can be updated so visitors will see the most recent information when they visit a webpage, instead of saving a potentially out-of-date document to their device. Our website is responsive to the screen size it is being viewed on — which means that users on small screens can still access and use all information. Our webpages are also designed to be accessible for users with assistive technologies. Notably, other documents like .docx files are editable, which is also not best practice and can be difficult to navigate. If the document meets all document requirements below, it must be added to eRecords by the document owner, and then added to the docstore by the eRecords team. The Digital Content team will review your request and advise on next steps. We can build webforms in Drupal to collect information for specific campaigns and purposes. To publish a form on the website we need: specific information from form owners for the privacy notice specific information from form owners for the privacy notice a designated stakeholder to be responsible for managing and deleting any data once the form is live a designated stakeholder to be responsible for managing and deleting any data once the form is live To suggest a new form, submit a Service Desk request. If your form is approved, we’ll build the form, add it to the relevant webpage, and create or update your Drupal account so you can access and manage submissions. Form Managers (you) are responsible for the form and its data, including: accessing form submissions accessing form submissions maintaining and reviewing the data collected in the form maintaining and reviewing the data collected in the form removing data after the period of time set out removing data after the period of time set out See our Form Manager Guidance in Moodle, including how to delete submissions and open and close the webform. To request an update to an existing form or that a form is removed, please request a web content update via the Service Desk. These changes should always be completed by the Digital Content team. You can request a vanity URL, which is a short link used in print material to direct to a page that has a longer URL. For example, port.ac.uk/alumni redirects to www.port.ac.uk/about-us/alumni. To request a vanity URL, submit a Service Desk ticket. Please include the URL of the page you are referring to, and the vanity URL you want. To request that webpages are unpublished and removed from the website, send us a ticket on the Service Desk. Suggest blog posts through the Service Desk. All post requests should relate to an existing blog topic: Building and inclusive and growth led economy and society Building and inclusive and growth led economy and society Developing enhanced technologies Developing enhanced technologies Parents and supporters Parents and supporters Protecting our environment Protecting our environment Research participation Research participation Student and student life Student and student life If your blog post is approved, the Digital Content team will build and publish your blog post. We remove blog posts after 5 years. This is due to the decay of content over time. Search engines and AI searches value recent content, which means that older content (which has a higher chance of containing broken links or incorrect information) does not support our overall website goal. Strategically important or notably well performing blogs may remain live after this period on a case by case basis. To suggest a blog post in this instance, please submit a Service Desk ticket with a link to the blog post and a statement about why the post should stay published. Course pages are not maintained by the Digital Content team. These pages are managed by Faculty Marketing Teams. You do not need to submit requests for the following updates: Accreditation changes Accreditation changes These updates are reviewed by the Faculty Marketing teams at the appropriate time of year, or when they are notified of course changes by the relevant team. If you do need to change this information, it needs to be updated on central records before any changes are made on the website. Please contact the relevant department to action changes to this information, who will notify the Faculty Management Teams when appropriate. To request a change to other content a course webpage, submit a Faculty content request via the Service Desk New and closed courses New courses won't be added to the website until they course have reached the right point in the approval process because certain details need to be confirmed before anything can be published. Faculty Marketing Teams will use the Course Pitch document to draft the course page, which will be shared with you to approve. Similarly, courses that are closed or undergo recruitment or course suspensions need to be updated centrally before these changes can be added to the website. Department webpages represent our schools and faculties. These pages include content on department teaching and research, key accreditations and staff members, and more. To request updates to an existing department webpage, submit a Service Desk request. To request a new webpage for a new department submit a Service Desk request. If your page is approved, we’ll send you a template to complete with the relevant information so we can build the webpage. Pages are built using the existing page structure. Please consider this when completing the content template. To add staff to the full staff listing for your webpage (for example, the School of Film, Media and Creative Technologies), please submit a Faculty content change request via the Service Desk and include all staff members who need adding to the listing and which department you are referring to in your request. There are two kinds of event webpage on port.ac.uk . Most webpages are imported from Eventbrite — these events must be published on the UoP Eventbrite account. To add an event to the port.ac.uk website, choose the 'University of Portsmouth' organiser. Events are automatically added to port.ac.uk overnight. When you change or delete an event from Eventbrite it will be changed or deleted from the website overnight. Users who need an Eventbrite account should speak to Becky Miles. You’ll be asked to sign up with an individual work email rather than a group/team email. Student recruitment and alumni events should use Salesforce CRM. For event information/factual updates, please contact the information owner. For support using Eventbrite, including setting up an account, contact Becky Miles (becky.miles@port.ac.uk). For changes that have been published on Eventbrite but not imported to the website after 48 hours, contact: websupport@port.ac.uk. For specific events, such as Stargazing, the Digital Content team can build manual event pages. These look the same as imported events to website visitors, so are only used when Eventbrite is not suitable. To request a new event page, submit a Service Desk request and include the content for the webpage in your request. Request a public news article on the University website by submit a PR and Media support Service Desk ticket and selecting “I want to promote a story”. The Press team will assess whether the topic works for an external news story and be in touch as needed. Research content on the website includes: Centres, institutes, groups, areas and areas of expertise (Research entities) Centres, institutes, groups, areas and areas of expertise (Research entities) To request updates to an existing research page, submit a Service Desk request. To request a new webpage for a new entity, submit a Service Desk request. If your page is approved, we’ll send you a template to complete with the relevant information so we can build the webpage. Pages are built using the existing page structure — for example a new research area will be built with the same webpage sections as published research areas. Please consider this when completing the content template. Staff profile pages on the website mirror information from the staff profiles on Pure, the Portsmouth Research Portal. More about Pure. The website updates to match Pure each night. So if you made changes to your Pure profile today, they'll be visible on the University website tomorrow. If you can’t see the changes, we suggest clearing your browser cache. To add tags to your profile page on the University website, please submit a Faculty web content change via the Service Desk. This is the only profile update managed by Faculty Marketing teams. Any other changes, including updating your profile email address are managed by the Pure team (pure@port.ac.uk) or HR directly. On the first of each month, the Pure team receives a list of new starters from HR and creates Pure profiles for staff who are eligible. These will automatically import to the website. You're eligible for a Pure profile if you work in an academic department above 0.01 FTE in a teaching and/or research capacity. If your profile is not visible on the website after your first month but you meet the eligibility requirements, email pure@port.ac.uk to ensure your profile is selected for import. Suggest blog posts through the Service Desk. If your blog post is approved, the Digital Content team will build and publish your blog post. We remove blog posts after 5 years. Contact local department editors for updates. For example, a staff member in Academic Services will find their local department editor in the Website Editors spreadsheet and email them directly to ask for webpage updates. If you or someone else will be editing the website in the future, you should complete Drupal CMS training. If you do not have access to publish the site, speak to other editors in your department to identify who can publish your changes within the team. If there are no department editors available, complete a Service Desk form. Student-facing events are added to the UoP Eventbrite account with the 'University of Portsmouth - Student' organiser. Events are automatically added to MyPort overnight. When you change or delete an event from Eventbrite it will be changed or deleted from the website overnight. Users who need an Eventbrite account should speak to Becky Miles becky.miles@port.ac.uk. For event information/factual updates, please contact the information owner. For changes that have been published on Eventbrite but not imported to the website after 48 hours, contact: websupport@port.ac.uk. Depending on your needs you may be directed to either Microsoft Clarity or Google Analytics. For more information, email digitalcontent@port.ac.uk with what information you need and what pages you need to review. title: Institutional statements in response to conflict, crisis or natural disaster
headings: Recruitment, Marketing and Communications Institutional statements in response to conflict, crisis or natural disaster External communication Internal communication Freedom of speech and the right to protest Explore
body: We are proud to welcome a global community of students and staff to the University of Portsmouth; our institution and our city is enriched by this diverse range of cultures, interests and beliefs. With this global outlook comes the responsibility to understand how events around the world can have an impact locally and to respond appropriately. Our first priority will always be to support and uphold the safety and security of our staff and student body and to communicate this at times of crisis. We provide a comprehensive set of well-being and support services that are designed to help at times of individual or collective need. Our challenge is in judging when it is appropriate to issue communication in response to troubling events, and what kind of response to take. To identify and respond to all crises is neither reasonable nor feasible. To respond to none overlooks our position as a global institution that cares about our staff and students. We are also mindful that, especially in times of conflict or political strife, taking a stance on an issue could do more harm than good by unintentionally creating division within our community. Furthermore, the university needs to respect the Charity Commission guidance on political activity and campaigning that applies to universities. The University will not issue an institutional statement, or provide a public comment, on every global, national or local issue either proactively or in response to a request. In most cases, it is not appropriate for the University to take a stance on conflict or crisis, whether at home or abroad. The Vice-Chancellor and/or UEB retain the authority to issue an institutional statement or public comment from the University should they deem this appropriate. Such a decision will take into account the relevance of the issue to our institution and the direct impact on our community. The response will usually be factual in nature, seeking to signpost support services and/or university policy rather than to express a view. This guidance relating to institutional statements or public comment from the University does not preclude our staff from contributing to public discourse on issues relating to their area of academic expertise, and does not infringe on their academic freedom. Further, the University may choose to support an academic to highlight research that is relevant to any ongoing local, national or global issue. There is a distinction between when an academic is speaking as an ‘expert’ and when they are providing the University of Portsmouth’s view/position on a particular topic or situation. We will not issue staff or student communications in response to every global, national or local issue. As our primary concern is staff and student safety and welfare, we will send out proactive targeted communication when we have reason to believe that: the physical safety of student or staff members is directly at risk (in this case, our disaster/emergency communications strategy takes over); staff or student well-being will be severely affected by the event (in this case, the message will focus on the well-being and support services we offer). Communication may take the form of an email to all staff/students, targeted communication just to those who are known to be affected, or the addition of a general message on relevant internal websites. The message may be best coming from line managers and will not always be centrally issued. The decision as to the most appropriate approach will be made by Internal Communications, where appropriate in conjunction with HR and/or Student Support Services. Freedom of speech/expression and the right to protest are fundamental pillars of democracy in the UK, and staff and students can exercise their rights within the boundaries of the law. Members of our community may hold differing views on crises, conflicts and disasters. In such times of heightened emotion, we should all strive to act with kindness and respect, being mindful of how our words and actions may affect others, regardless of intent. title: Vice-Chancellor
headings: Vice-Chancellor's Office Vice-Chancellor - Professor Graham Galbraith Executive Assistant to the Vice-Chancellor - Shani Thirlwell Explore
body: Graham joined the University of Portsmouth in September 2013. Previous to this he had held the sole Deputy Vice-Chancellor role at the University of Hertfordshire since 2008 and the role of Pro Vice-Chancellor (International) at Glasgow Caledonian University which he joined in 1993. He gained a BSc (1st class) in Environmental Engineering, an MSc, Mechanical Engineering and a PhD from the University of Strathclyde. He has a wealth of management experience and a strong entrepreneurial outlook which, combined with a substantial record of academic achievement and a thorough understanding of the international and HE sectors, puts him in an ideal position to lead the University through its next stage of development. Graham has responsibility for the strategic direction of the University working with the Board of Governors and the senior management team which comprises the Provost, Deputy Vice-Chancellor (Education), Deputy Vice-Chancellor (Global Engagement & Student Life), Deputy Vice-Chancellor (Research, Innovation & External Relations), Chief People Officer, Executive Director of Corporate Governance and Chief Financial Officer who all report directly to him. He is passionate about HE and its power to transform the life choices of individuals from a wide variety of backgrounds. He is committed to leading the University to provide the very best student experience underpinned by internationally excellent research and world class business engagement. His primary research interests are within the area of Building Physics and Material Science with emphasis on moisture within building components and the development of appropriate prediction models and measurement techniques. Telephone: 023 9284 3191Email: graham.galbraith@port.ac.uk Address: University House, Winston Churchill Avenue, Portsmouth, PO1 2UP Telephone: 023 9284 3191Email: shani.thirlwell@port.ac.uk Address: University House, Winston Churchill Avenue, Portsmouth, PO1 2UP Vice-Chancellor communication Vice-Chancellor's Awards for Excellence Vice-Chancellor's Thought Pieces Contact and departments

title: Chancellor
headings: Vice-Chancellor's Office Our Chancellor, Karen Blackett OBE, is a former University of Portsmouth student The Chancellor's role Explore
body: A proven and experienced business leader, Karen Blackett has a record of accomplishment in creating vibrant cultures, energising teams, and consistently delivering business growth. Karen is known for her transformative work with large, multi-disciplined and diverse teams delivering consistent commercial success. She began her media career in 1993 at CIA MediaNetwork and was quickly head-hunted by The Media Business Group, where she received many promotions, starting in the Board of Directors in 1999 and moving to Chairwoman of MediaCom UK & Ireland in 2016. While at MediaCom, she created and launched an Apprenticeship Scheme for 18-24-year olds, one of her proudest achievements. Under Karen’s leadership as CEO, MediaCom was voted Agency of the Year by industry magazine Campaign for an unprecedented two years in a row, 2013 and 2014. She was appointed Chancellor of the University of Portsmouth in 2017. She featured among the Top 100 Great Black Britons in 2020 and, in 2021, was chosen to feature in the book The Female Lead Volume II: We Rise by Lifting Others. Karen mentors people across her industry and further afield, including clients, internal staff, other businesspeople and students. She takes pride in helping them and learns a great deal from the process herself. Karen drives inclusion as a key contributor to business success in all the organisations she works with. In her 5 years as CEO of MediaCom, ethnic diversity transformed from 11% to 19%. Women in senior management roles increased to 44%. In September 2021, Karen became one of the founding trustees of a new national charity, Black Equity Organisation (BEO), focused exclusively on dismantling systemic racism across a spectrum of issues affecting Black communities in the UK. Karen was appointed NED for Diageo in June 2022 and in October 2022 she was appointed WPP UK President. Karen’s awards and recognition are extensive, including an OBE for services to Media and Communications in 2014. Since 2008, she has featured five times in the power list, Britain’s 100 Most Influential Black People, coming in at number 1 in 2015, the first businesswoman to do so. In 2017 she was voted Leader Most Admired by Campaign magazine. The Chancellor is the ceremonial head of the University. This is an honorary and unpaid role. The purpose of the role of the Chancellor is to help to raise the profile and prestige of the University by representing us – and our mission, aims and objectives – in the world beyond academia. title: Equality and Diversity
headings: Vice-Chancellor's Office Equality and Diversity How do the Equality and Diversity Policy and other University of Portsmouth policies and procedures affect members of the University? Explore
body: This is the Equality and Diversity mini site. Visit our full website for more detailed information, help and guidance around equality and diversity at the University of Portsmouth. The University of Portsmouth affirms its commitment to fair and equitable treatment for all its students, potential students and members of staff. It seeks to encourage, support and value diversity in the student population. It aims to create the conditions whereby all students and staff are treated on the basis of their merits, abilities and potential, regardless of gender, colour, ethnic or national origin, age, disability, religious or political beliefs, family circumstance or sexual orientation. It is firmly opposed to any form of unfair discrimination based on these attributes and values. Visit the various Equality and Diversity policies and guidelines for more information. To better understand your rights and responsibilities as a manager and/or member of staff use the University Diversity elearning resource. The University considers the effect of its policies and procedures. Find out about the University?s approach to Equality Impact Assessment. Vice-Chancellor communication Vice-Chancellor's Awards for Excellence Vice-Chancellor's Thought Pieces Contact and departments

title: Environmental Information Regulations 2004
headings: Vice-Chancellor's Office Environmental Information Regulations 2004 Definition of Environmental Information: Explore
body: The Environmental Information Regulations came into force on 1 January 2005 and provide a right of access to specific environmental information held by public authorities - the University included - which it is not possible to access under the Freedom of Information Act 2000. Some general environmental information is already available on the University's Publication Scheme under the Physical Resources classification. Requests for any other environmental information should be made to the University's Information Disclosure Officer who has 20 working days after the receipt of a request in which to provide the information. An extension to 40 working days may be possible though, if the complexity and volume of the information requested makes it impracticable to provide the information earlier. Contact the University's Information Disclosure Officer. Specifically, the type of environmental information available under these Regulations falls into the following categories: the state of the elements of the environment such as air, water, soil emissions and discharges such as noise, energy and radiation measures and activities (e.g. policies) that affect or are likely to affect the state of the elements of the environment reports, cost-benefits and economic analyses used within the policies that might affect the state of the elements of the environment the state of human health & safety, including the contamination of the food chain the state of cultural sites and built structures. Vice-Chancellor communication Vice-Chancellor's Awards for Excellence Vice-Chancellor's Thought Pieces Contact and departments

title: Loyalty
headings: Catering Services Explore
body: Foodon Campus Loyalty Scheme launched! Just register online at www.port.ac.uk/loyalty, you can then present your student/staff card to a loyalty scanner at any of the Foodon Campus tills (excluding the Waterhole), and collect points against your purchases. You will collect five points for every £1 you spend, and each point will be worth 1p which you can redeem against your future purchases. The scheme will also provide students and staff the chance of collecting double points, seasonal bonus points, and if you buy any hot drink you can get your ninth one for free! View Food on Campus Loyalty (.pdf). If you have any queries or questions, please see our frequently asked questions (.pdf) or email loyalty@port.ac.uk. Contact and departments

title: One Water
headings: Catering Services One Water Want to see more? You can! Want to do more? You can! Explore Related Links
body: Did you know that 1 billion people in the world don’t have access to clean water? Or that 2 million people die each year as a result of water related diseases? When we found this out we wanted to do something to change those figures. We recognise that it's a massive challenge, but we can change the lives of people – one person, one day at a time. Simply buy a bottle of One Water – all profits, every last drop, go to building unique PlayPump™ water pumping systems overseas which will improve people’s lives by providing free, clean water. With your help it can make a difference. Follow this link to see videos of people's efforts in action Support our just for fun "One Water in the World" competition. All you have to do is: Get yourself a bottle of One Water Go on holiday and take a picture of yourself with One Water somewhere interesting in the world Share your photo by posting it on our competition photo centre on Facebook It's as simple as that! Or just visit the One Water photo centre and give us your inspirational thoughts on how else to promote this excellent project. UoP Catering Services One Water Campaign Contact and departments

title: Events
headings: Catering Services Events Department Explore
body: Here you will find our fantastic new menus. We can provide a variety of solutions to meet your catering needs, whether in the form of hot and cold drinks for a meeting, a finger buffet or a sit down meal. By offering a tailored service we can adapt our menus to suit your needs and budget constraints. Where possible we will accommodate any dietary requirements. Should you have any specific requests, or would like to arrange a meeting to discuss your needs, please feel free to contact us via email catering.events@port.ac.uk or by phone on 3691. To make a booking please use the Online Catering Booking system. For any technical problems with the Online Catering Booking system please email kxsupport@port.ac.uk. For any queries with an order please email catering.events@port.ac.uk. title: Car Parking
headings: Estates and Campus Services Jump to: Latest car park disruptions Disabled bays in Eldon relocated What You Need to Know About Parking Parking Charges How the System Works Frequently Asked Questions Car Parks and Spaces View the full Parking Policy. Explore
body: Latest car parking closures What you need to know about parking Occasionally, we may need to temporarily close car parks, either for essential maintenance or for ground works related to the Estates Masterplan. We'll list upcoming closures here, and communicate this in the weekly Staff Newsletter. From Friday 27 March, the 3 disabled bays parallel with St. James’s Road within Eldon car park will be relocated adjacent to the current plantroom. The bays along the far perimeter edge will be occupied by the contractor relocating the plantroom. Read more. The University's parking system is managed through an online permit system and ANPR enforcement. Here’s what staff need to know: Permit Renewal: Staff must renew their permit annually. Reminders will be sent via the CPP Permits system. Applying & Managing Permits: Staff can apply for or amend their permit details through the CPP Permits portal: CPP Permits System. Payment Setup: Staff must register on Evology to set up their payment method for the ANPR Pay As You Go system: Register on Evology. This site also provides service details and terms & conditions. Vehicle Registration: You can register up to two vehicles on your permit and Evology account for University car parks. Updating Vehicle Details: If you change your vehicle, you must update your permit and Evology accounts to avoid receiving a Parking Charge Notice (PCN). Update your CPP Permit account: Sign in to update Update your Evology account: Sign in to your Evology account, delete and add vehicle details or email permits@carparkingpartnership.co.uk Charges are capped at daily and weekly limits. Payments or payment methods must be preloaded onto the portal; failure to maintain credit may result in a PCN. All permits are virtual—your vehicle registration is added to the ANPR system database. Charges apply per hour, up to a daily/weekly cap. Public parking is not allowed between 7.00 am – 4.00 pm to prioritise staff permit holders. Temporary vehicle changes can be registered via the Estates Helpdesk (ext 6677) or Security Lodge (3418) in an emergency. Who can apply for a permit?Staff and eligible students outside the two-mile exclusion zone (PO1-PO6, excluding Gosport) can apply. Exceptions apply for disabled staff/students, carers, parents with children under the age of 11, and those working unsociable hours - see the Car Parking Policy for more details. What do I need to do if I think I am exempt from the two-mile exclusion rule? You must send an email with all the relevant information to car.parking@port.ac.uk, the parking panel will then review your application for a permit and within 7 days respond with the outcome of your request. Do I need to reapply each year?Yes, all permits must be renewed annually. You will receive a reminder via the CPP Permits system. Is a parking space guaranteed?No. While we aim to provide sufficient spaces for permit holders, availability is not guaranteed. How do I top up my parking credit?Credit can be added via the Evology portal. You can set up notifications for low balances. What happens if I change my vehicle?You must update your permit and Evology accounts to avoid receiving a PCN. Changes to Evology can be made by emailing permits@carparkingpartnership.co.uk. I enter/exit the car parks multiple times per day. Will I be charged every time? Charges will be made on an hourly rate, while you are in the car park and you will be charged for the full hour whether you stay for the full hour or not. You will be charged for the time you park from the point of entry and exit up to the hourly rate, to the maximum daily charge. For example, if you stay for 30 minutes you will be charge or one hour, if you stay for one hour five minutes you will be charged for two hours Will I be able to get a refund if I leave or give up my permit? Yes, this can be requested through the parking portal. Below is a list of University car parks with total spaces, disabled bays, and EV charging points. Anglesea Front Car Park Anglesea Front Car Park Anglesea Rear Car Park Anglesea Rear Car Park Burnaby Terrace (Side of Wall) Burnaby Terrace (Side of Wall) St. George's Car Park St. George's Car Park Ravelin House Car Park Ravelin House Car Park Ravelin Sports Centre Car Park* *For sports centre use only Ravelin Sports Centre Car Park* *For sports centre use only William Beatty Car Park William Beatty Car Park Spinnaker Building Car Park Spinnaker Building Car Park 1* *Disabled bay temporarily unavailable from 9 March 2026 *Disabled bay temporarily unavailable from 9 March 2026 University House Car Park University House Car Park Dorothy Dymond Car Park Dorothy Dymond Car Park Harry Law Underground Car Park Harry Law Underground Car Park Park Building Car Park Park Building Car Park Melbourne Place Open from 16 March 2026 Open from 16 March 2026 St Andrew’s Court car park for visitors and preassigned spaces only and is not available for staff to park in. The car park will be closed from 25 September until early 2026 to allow for the installation of new fire escapes to Neptune Building. For further assistance, contact car.parking@port.ac.uk. title: Waste management
headings: Estates and Campus Services Our Commitment to the Environment Waste Collection Key Improvements we’re working on: How to Sort Your Waste Recycling Bins – What Can Go In? Food Waste Bins – What’s Accepted? General Waste – What Belongs Here? Special Waste Items – What Goes Where? Confidential Waste What Happens to Your Waste? Help Us Reduce Waste Having an Issue? Stay Informed Explore
body: We're committed to continuously improving our environmental impact by reducing the amount of waste sent to landfill. Since 2011, we've increased our recycling rate from 40% to 70%, and we're aiming for better—with your help! We’re reviewing how waste is collected across university buildings to make recycling: Fewer bins – smarter layout Fewer plastic bin liners Clear signage to reduce confusion and contamination Paper (including plain, printed, magazines) Cardboard (flat packed) Envelopes (no plastic windows) Plastic bottles, pots, tubs, trays (clean and empty) Glass bottles and jars Cans (e.g. drinks cans) Tetrapaks (note: check signage for updates) Do not put in: Food, liquids, tissues, or coffee cups Meat, fish, and bones Don’t have a food bin nearby? Let us know at sustainability@port.ac.uk Coffee cups (these can’t be recycled!) Tissues and contaminated packaging Blister packs (e.g. paracetamol) Hard plastics (e.g. spray nozzles) Sandwich packets (e.g. supermarket meal deals) Aerosols, Chemicals, Paint Tins Aerosols, Chemicals, Paint Tins Email: estates.helpdesk@port.ac.uk Email: estates.helpdesk@port.ac.uk Battery buckets in office receptions/caretaker areas Battery buckets in office receptions/caretaker areas Batteries (mobile/non-domestic) Batteries (mobile/non-domestic) Email: estates.helpdesk@port.ac.uk Email: estates.helpdesk@port.ac.uk Computers, Electronics (WEEE) Computers, Electronics (WEEE) Request pickup: servicedesk@port.ac.uk Request pickup: servicedesk@port.ac.uk Use blue-lid bin, then collect sack from estates Use blue-lid bin, then collect sack from estates Request pickup: estates.helpdesk@port.ac.uk Request pickup: estates.helpdesk@port.ac.uk Disposal managed through servicedesk@port.ac.uk Disposal managed through servicedesk@port.ac.uk View the full Detailed Waste List Some materials must be securely destroyed to protect the university and its people. Use blue-lid confidential bins Decant into confidential sacks (available from Estates Helpdesk) Recycling: Sent to a materials recovery facility to be sorted and recycled. General Waste: Incinerated at an energy recovery facility to generate electricity. Food Waste: Anaerobically digested to produce renewable energy. Recycling is great—but reducing waste is even better. Bring your own water bottle Avoid single-use packaging Bring leftovers for lunch Think before you throw! General waste queries: sustainability@port.ac.uk Estates Helpdesk: estates.helpdesk@port.ac.uk Computer/electronic waste: servicedesk@port.ac.uk If a bin looks broken or lacks separate liners, let us know! We’re updating bin signage across campus to make things even clearer. Recycling in Portsmouth city (e.g. at home) may be different from campus rules—so check the local council site for domestic waste guidance. title: Lost/Found Property
headings: Estates and Campus Services Lost/Found Property Explore
body: When items are found on campus they should be transferred to the nearest Lost Property Point within the University. On receiving lost property, staff at the below points will be required to log items onto the shared lost property spreadsheet on Google Drive. This must be done at the time of the item being handed in. Should be sent to Myport in Liongate. Myport should contact the student to let them know where their card can be collected. The staff member should be contacted and asked to collect their card from where it is being held. The card should be destroyed if not claimed within 1 month. Contractor & white cards Should be sent to Adrian Partington in Milldam LC who will endeavour to find out who they belong to so they can be reunited with their owners. The cards will be destroyed if not claimed within 1 month. Bank cards, chequebooksand items containingthese like purses orwallets Bank cards, chequebooksand items containingthese like purses orwallets Should be logged onto the spreadsheet. If the owner can be identified then they should be contacted and asked to retrieve their item. Items containing personal information like bank cards, etc to be destroyed if not claimed within 30 days. Everything else to be given to charity if not claimed within 30 days. Should be returned to the Library. If unclaimed after 1 month should be sent to the Passport Office (HM Passport Office, Globe House , 89 Eccleston Square , London, SW1V 1PN) or the relevant embassy. If unclaimed after 1 month should be sent back to Drivers Customer Services Correspondence Team, DVLA, Swansea, SA6 7JL. The notes should be checked to see if there is a name or student number in them. If yes the student should be contacted to let them know where their notes can be collected. If not they can be destroyed if not claimed within 1 month. Clothes & any other items Should be kept for 1 month and then disposed of if not claimed. There is a charity bin outside the Student Union on Cambridge Road which can be used for this purpose. A sticker will be put on the bicycle warning the user that it will be removed if not taken away within 2 weeks. If it is still there after 2 weeks it will be removed by security/caretakers/Estates Staff. Items will be held at the local property points for a maximum period of 4 weeks. Lost property found in the Sports Hall and Gym will be recorded on a separate spreadsheet which will also be available to view in Google Drive. Contact sport@port.ac.uk/x5555. The spreadsheets will be available to all front facing staff/reception points and lost property points. Lost property left on the University bus should be enquired about by contacting First Bus at this webpage. Lost Property Points are located at: Buckingham, Caretaker’s Office, ext 2532, buckingham@port.ac.uk Burnaby Caretaker’s Office, ext 2531, burnaby@port.ac.uk Burnaby SEES School Office 1.15, ext 2257, sees@port.ac.uk Eldon caretakers, ext 3842, eldon@port.ac.uk King Henry group caretaker’s office, ext 2527, kinghenry@port.ac.uk King Henry fl6 admin office, ext 2222, biology@port.ac.uk King Henry technician’s office, room 2.21, psych-technicians@port.ac.uk Library reception, ext 3231, library@port.ac.uk Liongate Myport, ext 4444, myport@port.ac.uk Ravelin Sports Centre, ext 5555, sport@port.ac.uk Spinnaker 2.01, ext 5111. title: Charters
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Explore
body: As part of our commitment to equality and diversity, we have signed up to two national Higher Education equality charters – Athena SWAN, which focuses on gender equality and the Race Equality Charter. Run by Advance HE, the charters enable us to apply for awards recognising our commitment to, and progress on, equality and diversity. They aim to drive forward the cultural and systemic changes we need to remain competitive and attractive to talented staff and potential students in a global market. They also support our University values and strategies. More information about our approach and progress can be found on the Race Equality Charter page. title: Fire Alarm Digital Messaging Service
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Fire Alarm Digital Messaging Service Information How to sign-up for Digital Messaging Service? Building Codes Explore
body: In our drive to continually improve our Equality, Diversity and Inclusion, Estates and Campus Services have launched a campus wide fire alarm digital messaging service. The Digital Messaging Service (DMS) is a fire alarm warning system which alerts people who are deaf or have hearing loss when the fire alarm sounds. The system uses text messages, Whatsapp messages, phone calls and push notifications to relay the fire alarm signal to the mobile phone user. On the activation of the building's fire alarm, notifications will be sent to your mobile phone immediately, in the following format. The system will ensure that anyone who has difficulty in hearing the existing fire alarms or works in an area without a beacon can be notified by an alternative method. However, anybody can use the system as an alternative method of fire alarm alert. There are three methods for registering to the service: 1. Send a text message with the relevant building code to the DMS number 07480 486966. *Recommendation: Save 07480 486966 as ‘Fire alarm’ in your contacts so you recognise the number in an emergency. 2. Download the free Fireco Insite app for both Android and IOS devices which gives you instant access to your user preferences, without having to use the hyperlink in your confirmation message. 3. Scan the QR code on the DMS signage which will be located in building entrances. For further details, please see the Fireco DMS installation and user guide. Any questions should be directed to the Estates Helpdesk on estates.helpdesk@port.ac.uk. title: EDI Consultative & Steering Group
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion University EDI Consultative Group & Steering Group Explore
body: The University’s EDI Team, in consultation with the EDI Steering Group and the EDI Consultative Group, is responsible for developing policy and best practice in order to support meeting the legal requirements. We commit to sharing the EDI Framework objectives with all new students and colleagues when they arrive at Portsmouth, and to refresh this as required, to underpin our desire to give students and staff the best possible experience whilst working and studying here. The EDI Consultative Group is made of individuals with useful opinions in EDI matters, meanwhile, the EDI Steering Group consists of those at the University with the authority and power to enact changes required to make necessary changes to meet our EDI objectives. For any further questions or queries please don't hesitate to contact us at equality@port.ac.uk. title: EDI Mini-fest (Call for Papers)
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Explore
body: As an international and widening participation University, the University of Portsmouth is committed to ensuring that our diverse student body experience equitable student experience and outcomes. As part of Global Week, an annual event that celebrates the diversity of our staff and students, Academic Development is running an EDI mini-fest on Wednesday 13th March 2024. Global Week is a prime opportunity for colleagues to showcase best practice strategies and research on equity, culturally responsive pedagogies, and the wider challenges impacting student engagement through a global lens. We particularly welcome submissions from international colleagues and students, and submissions that offer practical applications in the learning space. To contribute a case study, research output or discussion piece for this online event, please see the link below to submit an abstract to present a 20 minute session (with 10 minute Q&A). title: Sense of Belonging & Representation Paper
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Sense of Belonging & Representation Paper Explore
body: This paper looks at University of Portsmouth students’ perceptions of culture, representation, and barriers to success in higher education (HE) for People of the Global Majority (PGM). Our students face many challenges whilst at university, and Students of the Global Majority (also referred to as BAME or Black, Asian and minority ethnic) have experiences specific to their culture and ethnicity. Students of the Global Majority find that their experiences are impacted by the level of representation and belonging in their university community. This includes: Their broad university experiences, both inside and outside of the classroom and in their university city; The diversity and representation of the curriculum; Student success metrics such as awards, continuation, and progression; Approaches to learning and teaching. This paper focuses specifically on representation and sense of belonging for Students of the Global Majority at the University of Portsmouth and shares the results of a survey of 441 students, framed by Critical Race Theory (CRT). The paper is part of a larger research project that covers representation of culture in the curriculum; cultural sensitivity in learning environments; the impact of culture and student backgrounds on higher education study; experiences of different teaching methods; and prior educational experiences. Data is available for all of these sections, and has been shared with Associate Deans. All colleagues are welcome to read the paper. If you have any questions about this research, please contact fiona.cook@port.ac.uk. Key words: representation; sense of belonging; PGM; People of the Global Majority; BAME; culture EDI in Action Charters Breastfeeding and expressing facilities Fire Alarm Digital Messaging Service EDI Consultative & Steering Group EDI Mini-fest (Call for Papers) Sense of Belonging & Representation Paper SPICE up your events Breastfeeding and expressing facilities Fire Alarm Digital Messaging Service EDI Consultative & Steering Group EDI Mini-fest (Call for Papers) Sense of Belonging & Representation Paper Inclusion Calendar 2023/24 Equality Impact Assessment (EIA) Equality and Diversity training The Report and Support Tool Contact and departments

title: SPICE up your events
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion SPICE up your events S: Spaces – Check space accessibility and suitability. P: Partnerships – Pick partner organisations that align with our values. I: Inclusion – Diversify the event organisers. C: Communication – Make the most of the event registration process. E: Evaluation – Compare the event against your EDI objectives. Explore
body: Inclusion in events may seem like a no-brainer, but there are always improvements we can make to improve the environments and spaces of our events to invite a broader range of audiences and ideas. Below you’ll find five easily actionable ideas to implement when making events and activities of your own. Just remember to add some SPICE to your events! The University has many resources available to us that allow us to ensure spaces we’re hosting events in are as accessible as possible, and to check for this easily. Two that the Equality, Diversity & Inclusion (EDI) team likes to use include the UoP Room Check tool; and the AccessAble Access Guides. Across these tools, you can get room pictures, details of building access, a view of seating facilities and other general room details. However, if possible, we recommend visiting venues before events to ensure they suit all of your intended audience. Make sure to also consider digital spaces. For example, ensure that there is a live-captioning function and that attendees can spotlight speakers, i.e. for lip reading. Also, you should ensure that meeting materials are in screen-reader friendly fonts. The University has three values that underpin everything we do. We are ambitious, responsible, and open. As such, when organising events with outside collaborators, try to remember these values and pick partners that are compatible with our values. Similar to the acknowledged benefit of a diverse workforce, a diverse group of event organisers will allow for the event handlers to pool from a larger base of knowledge and experience. Allowing you to take into consideration viewpoints that may not have occurred to you, but that may be appreciated by your event attendees. The registration process is likely to be the first time that your attendees will learn about your event, and also what you as the organiser(s) are aiming to achieve. If you have an EDI objective to use an event to promote the recruitment of women students, then capturing attendee gender would be useful. Additionally, it’s a great opportunity to ask for dietary, mobility, and accessibility requirements, allowing you to make the event as inclusive as possible. Also, sending out clear agendas and information in advance can improve the experience for people with neurodiversities. Furthermore, The power of reflection shouldn’t be underestimated. Once your event is complete and feedback received, this is the perfect time to evaluate if you met your EDI objectives. Did you attract the right audience? Were the right voices empowered? Was anybody excluded and why? If you would like support from the EDI team to support you in making the Eventbrite page for your equity-boosting event then complete our Eventbrite Details Form. https://forms.gle/PR7c1ok33hGeKHDFA EDI in Action Charters Breastfeeding and expressing facilities Fire Alarm Digital Messaging Service EDI Consultative & Steering Group EDI Mini-fest (Call for Papers) Sense of Belonging & Representation Paper SPICE up your events Breastfeeding and expressing facilities Fire Alarm Digital Messaging Service EDI Consultative & Steering Group EDI Mini-fest (Call for Papers) Sense of Belonging & Representation Paper Inclusion Calendar 2023/24 Equality Impact Assessment (EIA) Equality and Diversity training The Report and Support Tool Contact and departments

title: Clearing
headings: Clearing 2026 What is Clearing? What is Clearing? UCAS points calculator Useful information Our Clearing Checklist 2026 How to apply through Clearing How to accept a Clearing offer Why choose Clearing at Portsmouth? Connected Degrees® Why choose Portsmouth? Student life Clearing 2025 FAQs Who can apply through Clearing? What times can I call the Clearing hotline? What happens after receiving an offer? When to apply for accommodation? Can I apply through Clearing for courses at University of Portsmouth London campus? Is it too late to apply for a student loan? What to do as an international student? What can't I apply for in Clearing? We use cookies
body: UCAS Clearing 2026 opens 2 July. This video is hosted on YouTube. To show it to you, we need your okay to use their cookies – it’s how the video player works. You need to accept functional cookies for the video to play. You can update your choices using the button below, or anytime on our Cookie Policy page. Clearing gives you another opportunity to find the best course for you - whether you've changed your mind, your results surprised you, or you're just starting to think about going to university. Clearing at Portsmouth could be the perfect option. I'm really glad that I applied for Clearing. It definitely paid off. Clearing is a process of applying to university after the deadline for applying for your uni. So if you decide to change your course or to change your university, Clearing is how you can get into those universities. I had originally applied to Portsmouth to do Sociology with Criminology, and then decided the course probably wasn't for me. So very, very last minute I decided in the summer that I wanted to switch courses and I found International Development and then it was too late to change through the normal application process, so I decided, well, the only option was to go through Clearing. It was very nerve-wracking. I was afraid that I'd end up with nothing. However, that definitely wasn't the case and there was so much support and information online that was given to me. So it was quite straightforward. It wasn't difficult or scary at all. It was surprisingly super, super easy. I was put in touch with my course leader and it was all sorted within the hour, basically, after results. The first thing I would say to somebody applying through Clearing is, don't panic. Keep positive and less worried when applying to the Clearing process. It's actually very quick and very easy. Literally, the whole process of Clearing took me less than 24 hours. Whether it's enrolling you to a course that you wanted or even just providing information for you, the Clearing team will always have your back. I definitely recommend anyone that's worried to just go with their instinct and do it. I'm really happy with the choice I've made. Things don't go according to plan on purpose and it happens for the best. I felt relieved knowing I’d gotten what I wanted and my dream was about to come true. See what grades you need to get into your preferred course at Portsmouth. Use UCAS points calculator If you’re wondering about how to prepare for Clearing, then follow our Clearing checklist to ensure a stress free process. Want a step-by-step guide on how to apply through Clearing? We’ve got you covered! Here’s everything you need to know about accepting your Clearing offer. Portsmouth is the only UK uni to offer a 3 year course with 4th year placement. Discover more about Connected Degrees. There are plenty of great reasons to choose Portsmouth for your university adventure – here are some of the best. Explore our island city and its many attractions, and find out what student life is really like at the University. You can apply through Clearing if: You don't meet the conditions of your offer for your firm (first) or insurance (second) choice courses Your exam results are better than you expected and you want to change your course or university You don't hold any offers You've accepted an offer but changed your mind about the course you want to do You're applying for the first time after 30 June 2025 Find out more on UCAS Phoneline opening times Monday - Thursday 9am - 5pm You can apply online 24/7. After we make you an offer we'll send you a confirmation email. This email will let you know what you need to do next and it will tell you what you need to provide us. In some instances we may ask you to send us copies of certificates or other information. If you've previously applied through UCAS you'll need to use your UCAS Hub to accept our offer by adding us as your Clearing choice. If you're having issues, please contact us on +44 (0)23 9284 8090 or admissions@port.ac.uk Once you've accepted your Clearing course offer, we'll be in touch with details of available accommodation in the area. This will include our latest hall availability and support to find local rented accommodation via portsmouthstudentpad.co.uk See our accommodation page for more information. Yes, our four career-focused undergraduate courses at our growing London campus are available to apply for through Clearing. Find out more about our London campus No, it's not too late and you should make your application for student finance as soon as possible. You don't have to wait for your results. You can make your application now and just amend it when you know where you're going to be. If you've already applied for your student loan, you'll need to log into your account and update details about your new course/university. If you haven't applied for your student loan yet, don't panic. Apply today – it only takes 30 minutes. Find out more in our Student finance for Clearing guide. If you're an EU or international student and you need a visa to study here, you need to start the process quickly as visas can take some time to come through. Get in touch with our visa support team if you have a question or problem. See more on visa advice. If you would like further information or guidance, please contact our international office. You are welcome to apply for our courses in Clearing, please use the International apply form found on each course page. See courses in Clearing. Please note that 2025 applications for the medical degree have now closed and it will not be in Clearing. If you have any questions, contact us at medicalschoolenquiries@port.ac.uk Please be advised that not all courses will enter Clearing and some may only have limited spaces, so it's advisable to call our Clearing Hotline to check the latest vacancies on 023 9284 8090. We may use cookies to analyse visitor data, improve our website, show personalised content and give you a great website experience. For more information about cookies on our site and to tailor the cookies we collect, select ‘Manage settings’.

title: Research and innovation
headings: Our global research Our research Research centres and institutes Research areas Areas of expertise Our global research impact Our research in the media Research blogs Life Solved podcast Research news Join us in our research Find a PhD Supervisor Our research degrees subject areas Postgraduate events Fellowships Our universe-changing space research Collaborate with our researchers Let's collaborate - work with us Explore our research database and staff directory We use cookies
body: Responsible Innovation: solving problems, enriching lives, creating lasting impact We want to help shape the future and know it takes more than words to make a difference. That's exactly what our research aims to do. We're making a significant difference in the world. 77% of our research impact was rated as world-leading or internationally excellent in REF 2021, and we are working on preparations for our REF 2029 submission. Our belief in knowledge-sharing makes our research exceptional and relevant. Our approach is always collaborative. We work with local and global partners in education and industry. Our media ready experts also regularly contribute their knowledge and expertise to print, broadcast and digital media coverage of the issues their research addresses. View our Research and Innovation Strategy to find out how we're delivering globally significant research excellence, and explore below how you could become part of our research community with our PhD and research degree opportunities. Our research centres, institutes and hubs bring together expertise and resources from across the University of Portsmouth to tackle pressing global issues. Working with industry and academic partners, and across our 5 faculties, our researchers are leading their fields and creating impact. Discover our unique, collaborative and multi-disciplinary areas of expertise. Our research responds to challenges, influences policy and helps change the world for the better. Discover the impact of our research If you've already got your own research idea, start looking for a supervisor whose research interests match yours. Take your expertise further by studying for a PhD or other research degree at the University of Portsmouth. Explore the subject areas you can study & get started on your research journey. Come to one of our events to explore your course, find out more about our expert support teams and talk to our current students. Fellowships give you the step-change opportunity to undertake an individual project alongside activities to develop your career. Explore our fellowship opportunities. The University plays a critical role in supporting space industries in the UK and is delivering pioneering research in the space sector. We may use cookies to analyse visitor data, improve our website, show personalised content and give you a great website experience. For more information about cookies on our site and to tailor the cookies we collect, select ‘Manage settings’.

title: About us
headings: We're a new breed of university Welcome to the University of Portsmouth Our story Our Strategy Our facilities Our ambition Structure and governance Key dates Working at Portsmouth Contact us Our London campus You may also be interested in Policies and standards Freedom of Information Publications Our staff Maps and directions We use cookies
body: Find out how we make a positive difference to the world and how you can join us We’re proud to be a breath of fresh air in the academic world – a place where everyone gets the support they need to achieve their best. We’re always discovering. We don’t fit the mould, we break it. Just as the world keeps moving, so do we. We’re closely involved with our local community and we take our ideas into the global marketplace. We partner with business, industry and government to set the course for a better future. And we educate and transform the lives of our students to help them achieve their potential. We stand out, not just in the UK but in the world. We excel in research areas from cosmology and astrophysics to cyber security, forensics and sport science. Since the first day we opened our doors, we've looked towards the future. And we're here to help you shape it. Study in London at the University of Portsmouth, in the heart of Waltham Forest. Our purpose-built, capital city campus puts world-class teaching and industry connections on your doorstep, allowing you to fuel your ambition in one of the world's greatest financial centres. We may use cookies to analyse visitor data, improve our website, show personalised content and give you a great website experience. For more information about cookies on our site and to tailor the cookies we collect, select ‘Manage settings’.
