title: Health, Safety and Compliance
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Explore
body: The Health, Safety and Compliance Team provides help, advice and support to effectively manage health and safety across all departments and activities including teaching, research, staff and students. This Policy has been produced for all staff and others to be aware of both the University's and their own responsibilities under the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act. Our Health and Safety Policy Our Health and Safety Commitment Reporting incidents, accidents, near misses and hazards is essential to enable us to improve the University's health and safety management. Report a Building Issue Reporting a gas leak, electrical / heating failure, or a water leak / flood on university premises. Health and Safety A-Z If you are looking for information or advice relating to a specific health and safety subject, please find more information here. Health and Safety Law Poster Health and Safety at work is governed by the Health and Safety Executive (HSE). To help you better understand your rights and responsibilities for staying safe at work, we encourage all employees to read the HSE Law Poster above. The Health, Safety and Compliance Team provide advice and guidance relating to all health and safety matters within the University. Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Academic Registry
headings: Academic Registry Explore
body: About Us and Who to Contact Welcome to the Academic Registry! The Academic Registry plays a central role in our institution’s commitment to maintaining academic excellence. Our role is to develop and support an academic quality regulatory landscape which supports the highest standards of academic quality, through academic regulations and policies that support a fair, transparent, and effective learning environment. Our Mission is to ensure the University’s academic quality environment complies with mandatory regulatory requirements, upholds academic standards, enhances the student experience, and supports all students, regardless of their background, to succeed. Academic Excellence: Promoting the highest standards of education and quality of student experience Integrity: Ensuring fairness, transparency, and accountability in all our actions. Collaboration: Partnering with students, staff and external stakeholders to uphold and improve academic quality. Responsiveness: Adapting to the changing landscape of higher education through innovation and foresight. Working collaboratively across the University, and informed by sector leading-practice we will: Ensure all academic policies, regulations and procedures meet mandatory regulatory requirements whilst being efficient and maintaining a forward-thinking approach. Provide an assessment regulatory framework which supports student achievement, and eliminates unwarranted barriers to learning and success; Create a student and staff-centric academic quality environment that promotes high standards and continuous improvement; Generate and analyse reports on academic performance, student feedback and outcomes to drive informed decision-making for shaping the education portfolio and enhancing quality; Efficiently manage and compassionately resolve student casework relating to academic quality and regulations, providing support and solutions to minimise the impact on all students involved. Offer expert leadership and advice on academic quality and regulations to support the academic and professional services community. Who We Are and How to Contact Us: Meet the Senior Team: Rebecca Di Pancrazio, Academic Registrar (T: 02392 843436/ E: rebecca.dipancrazio@port.ac.uk) Jazz Lambert, PA to Academic Registrar (T: 02392 843436/ E: jazz.lambert@port.ac.uk) Amy Dymond-Hobbs, Deputy Academic Registrar (T: 02392 843436/ E: amy.dymond-hobbs@port.ac.uk) Nicola Young, Senior Casework Manager (T: 023 92 84 2132 / E: nicola.young@port.ac.uk) Gemma Thompson, Senior Quality Assurance Manager (T: 023 92 84 6100 / E: gemma.thompson@port.ac.uk) Alex Bradley, Senior Student Outcomes Manager (E: alexander.bradley@port.ac.uk) Academic Registry is responsible for, and supports, the following areas, with links to relevant resources: - Academic Regulations - Academic Policies and Procedures - Academic Year Dates - Access and Participation Plan - Monitoring and Evaluation - Applicant complaints - Apprenticeship Policies and Frameworks - Annual Monitoring and Review of Modules and Courses - Behavioural Misconduct - Collaborative Partnerships - Course Modification - Degree Outcomes Statement - Education Committees - Fitness to Practice - Members of the public complaints - Professional, Statutory, and Regulatory Bodies - Postgraduate Research Experience Survey (PRES) - Postgraduate Taught Experience Survey (PTES) - Quality Priority Plans - Recognition of Prior Learning - Teaching Observation Framework Who We Are and How to Contact Us: Meet the Senior Team: Rebecca Di Pancrazio, Academic Registrar (T: 02392 843436/ E: rebecca.dipancrazio@port.ac.uk) Jazz Lambert, PA to Academic Registrar (T: 02392 843436/ E: jazz.lambert@port.ac.uk) Amy Dymond-Hobbs, Deputy Academic Registrar (T: 02392 843436/ E: amy.dymond-hobbs@port.ac.uk) Nicola Young, Senior Casework Manager (T: 023 92 84 2132 / E: nicola.young@port.ac.uk) Gemma Thompson, Senior Quality Assurance Manager (T: 023 92 84 6100 / E: gemma.thompson@port.ac.uk) Generic email accounts: Academicregistry@port.ac.uk - For all Academic Registry general matters. studentcasework@port.ac.uk - For all matters relating to complaints, appeals, student conduct, fitness to study, fitness to practise. Quality Assurance Matters sci.qa@port.ac.uk - For all matters relating to quality assurance within the faculty of Science and Health. tec-qa@port.ac.uk - For all matters relating to quality assurance within the faculty of Technology. qa-hums@port.ac.uk - For all matters relating to quality assurance within the faculty of Humanities. cci-qa@port.ac.uk - For all matters relating to quality assurance within the faculty of Creative and Cultural Industries. pbs-qa@port.ac.uk - For all matters relating to quality assurance within the faculty of Business and Law. london-qa@port.ac.uk - For all matters relating to quality assurance within the University of Portsmouth London Campus. studentsurveys@port.ac.uk - For all internal student survey matters. surveyrequests@port.ac.uk - For all students requesting to conduct surveys. academiccommittees@port.ac.uk - For all Academic Registry committee matters. externalexaminers@port.ac.uk - For all external examiner matters. Courseexemptions@port.ac.uk - For all matters relating to course exemptions. headings: Board of Governors Explore

Information has been summarised as simply and clearly as possible. If you need more detailed information, then please contact the Director of Corporate Governance, Adrian Parry on e-mail adrian.parry@port.ac.uk Any comments or questions about the site would be welcome! title: Pay and Recognition
headings: Human Resources Pay and Recognition Pay / Salary Information Payroll Deadlines Season Ticket Advances Cycle to Work Scheme Our Recognition Ecosystem Holiday Purchase Scheme Relocation Expenses Explore
body: If you have any queries on the information in these pages please contact reward@port.ac.uk Please click here for information on pay scales; salary spine points; salary increases; cost of employment and the University's commitment to the Real Living Wage. Information on Payroll Deadlines can be found here. Please follow this link for eligibility details and guidance on how to apply for Season Ticket Advances. Save money on your commute to work by purchasing a new bike and spreading the cost over a year through payroll deductions. This is a salary sacrifice scheme so you'll make savings by paying less tax and National Insurance contributions. The University's scheme is provided by CycleSolutions. Applications are open throughout the year, with a maximum spend of £1,500 (after discounts have been applied). Visit CycleSolutions for full information. Find out more about the Recognition Ecosystem at the University. The Holiday Purchase Scheme application window for 2025/26 is now closed. The Holiday Purchase Scheme allows eligible employees to apply for up to 5 days (37 hours) additional time off (pro-rata for part-time employees) in a leave year over and above existing entitlements to holidays, public holidays and University closure days. It is only open in August of each year. Provided certain conditions are met, the University will consider paying relocation expenses for newly appointed employees. Expenses are paid in line with HMRC rules that are current at the time the expense claim is made. For full details on eligibility and the process to be followed please read the Reimbursement of Relocation Expenses Guidance. If you are eligible to claim for relocation expenses, you will need to complete the Relocation Personal Declaration Form (including witness signature, who must be a University employee and should not be a relative or somebody affected by the move) and send that through to HR who will then confirm your eligibility to the Payments Team. Claims should be made within 12 months of your start date and you should send the Relocation Expenses form and relevant receipts to your Faculty Manager / Head of Department or the appropriate Faculty/Department Finance Officer for signature. The forms should then be sent to the Payments team, Finance Department at Mercantile House Any queries regarding these claims should be sent to the relevant Faculty/Department Finance Officer or Payments Team (please check with your line manager if unsure). title: Leave and Absence
headings: Human Resources Leave and Absence Leave Holiday Purchase Scheme Absence Family Friendly Leave Other Leave and Absence Flexible Working Explore
body: Annual leave is an integral part of a staff members reward package and a legal requirement. Staff wellbeing is important and it is essential that managers work with their teams to ensure that annual leave is planned and taken on a regular basis for the entire leave year. For more information on annual leave, sickness absence and flexible working, please follow the links below. If you have any queries on the information in these pages please contact reward@port.ac.uk The University provides a generous leave entitlement and additional Christmas closure days. Full details can be found in the Annual Leave policy. The Holiday Purchase Scheme allows eligible employees to apply for up to 5 days (37 hours) additional time off (pro-rata for part-time employees) in a leave year over and above existing entitlements to holidays, public holidays and University closure days. It will open between July and August each year, to take effect in the leave year commencing in the following October. We will advise when the window opens via Staff News. The University is committed to the fair and consistent treatment of staff in relation to sickness absence and to supporting individuals during periods of ill health. Find out more. There are circumstances where members of staff have a statutory entitlement to or can request additional leave. Many of these instances relate to support for families. This includes Adoption Leave, Maternity Leave, Paternity Leave, Shared Parental Leave, Parental leave, Premature Birth and Neonatal Care Leave, Stillbirth and/or Neonatal Loss, Parental Bereavement Leave, Carer's Leave, Compassionate Leave, Time off for Dependants and the Right to Request flexible working. For further information on these guidelines please visit the Family Friendly Entitlements page. There are certain other circumstances where members of staff are entitled to or can request additional leave. This includes Compassionate Leave and Sabbaticals as well as other types of leave. Follow this link to find further information on these circumstances. Employees have a right to request a change to their contractual terms and conditions of employment to work more flexibly. This could be a change in working pattern, or perhaps the number of hours worked. It is important to note this is the right to request a change only, and not an automatic right to actually change your pattern. Find out more. title: Health and Safety Team
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Explore
body: Health, Safety & Compliance The Health, Safety and Compliance Team provides help, advice and support to effectively manage health and safety across all departments and activities, including teaching, research, staff and students. It also ensures that the University understands and carries out their legal and moral responsibilities in relation to the health, safety and welfare of staff, students, contractors and members of the public. Find the contact details for our main offices below, or you can contact a team member directly. title: Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS)
headings: Human Resources Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS) your LGPS TIMELINE - what you need to know about LGPS Want to Know More? Explore
body: (available to University of Portsmouth employees in Support, Administrative, Professional Services roles who started prior to August 2024, and all non-teaching Research roles) The Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS) is one of the largest public sector pension schemes. It is one of the best schemes available in the UK and one of the biggest benefits for being a part of the University of Portsmouth (UoP) and we are proud to offer it to eligible employees. At UoP, the scheme is administered on our behalf by Hampshire Pension Services. It is a Career Average ‘Defined Benefit’ scheme, meaning the benefits you get are guaranteed, based on your earnings, and set out by regulations rather than being dependent on investment risks and financial markets You need to know about your pension, what it is worth to you, and what it means to be a member – this guide aims to point you in the right direction throughout your entire pensions timeline, from choosing Portsmouth to life in retirement… your LGPS TIMELINE - what you need to know about LGPS HOW YOU JOIN ESSENTIAL ACTIONS WHEN YOU JOIN WHAT DO YOU GET AND WHAT DOES IT COST? title: Teachers' Pension Scheme (TPS)
headings: Human Resources Teachers' Pension Scheme (TPS) Retirement - Teachers’ Pension Scheme Types of retirement Estimates How to apply Explore
body: Teachers’ Pensions require a personal email address for their records upon being enrolled into the pension scheme. This is because your Teachers’ Pensions account stays unique to you throughout your working life regardless of your employer. The University will provide Teachers’ Pensions with the personal email address that has been provided on your recruitment application upon your appointment. You can update your personal email address on your My Pension Online account on the Teachers’ Pensions web site. Joining or leaving the scheme Printable Member Guides for New Starters, Active Teachers, Family Benefits and more If you are considering retirement, click for more information. Ill Health Retirement Transitional Protection (the 'McCloud' Remedy) You can find information on types of retirement, how to get an estimate of your pension benefits and how to apply for your pension on our Retirement - Teachers' Pension Scheme page. You can find more information on the Teachers' Pension Scheme website. If you are considering retirement and are, or have been a member of the Teachers' Pension Scheme, you should discuss your retirement options with your Line Manager and Dean or Head of Professional Service. Please see the information below which you may find helpful. I want to retire on or after my normal pension age. What are my options? Information about Age Retirement I am considering retiring before my normal pension age. What are my options? Information about Early Retirement Can I take my pension and continue working without a break in my employment? Phased Retirement was introduced to allow someone to continue working but reduce their earnings due to part time work or a reduction in their responsibilities and be able to withdraw part of their pension to substitute their loss of income. The maximum a person can withdraw is up to 75% of their pensionable benefits. To be eligible to claim Phased Retirement, you must be between the age of 55 and 75. How can I get a pension estimate? A personalised pension estimate is available through the Teachers’ Pension Member Portal. If you have not yet done so, you will need to register for the Member Portal. A calculator is also available to allow you to model your final salary pension & lump sum. How do I apply for my pension benefits on or after my normal pension age? You will need to notify your Line Manager of your intention to retire, in writing, with a copy to HR. Submit your resignation in accordance with your contractual notice period, although a longer period of notice may be appropriate to ensure there is no delay in the payment of pension benefits. You can find further information on our webpage Leaving the University. To apply for your pension, the Age Retirement form will need to be completed. Teachers’ Pensions suggest completing this 3 to 6 months before the date you want to retire to ensure your benefits are paid on time. Click to access the online Age Retirement application form. How do I apply for my pension benefits before my normal pension age? It’s possible to take your pension on or after age 55 but you will suffer a permanent reduction in your pension benefits. The University’s consent is required in order to take Early Retirement, therefore, you will need to discuss this with your Line Manager and, if consent is given, notify your Line Manager of your intention to retire early, in writing, with a copy to HR. Submit your resignation in accordance with your contractual notice period although a longer period of notice may be appropriate to ensure there is no delay in the payment of pension benefits. You can find further information on our webpage Leaving the University. To apply for your pension, the Early Retirement form will need to be completed. Teachers’ Pensions suggest completing this 3 to 6 months before the date you want to retire to ensure your benefits are paid on time. Click to access the online Early Retirement application form. How do I apply for Phased Retirement? If the University consents to your Phased Retirement request, the Phased Retirement application form can be completed online. Teachers' Pension & Absences How leave may affect your pension. title: MyPensionPlus
headings: Human Resources MyPensionPlus - Your Pension and Benefits Scheme your MyPensionPlus Timeline - What you need to know Want to Know More? Explore
body: Eligible members of the University of Portsmouth Academic Services Limited (UASL at Portsmouth) may join our ‘MyPensionPlus’ benefits scheme. This scheme comprises two elements – a workplace pension, and a choice of additional benefits to select from. Please note these pages are not relevant to colleagues based at the London Campus - please see Aviva for London Campus instead. Eligible employees are those who are salaried (not paid via fees or timesheets) and on a permanent/indefinite or fixed-term basis. MyPensionPlus uses Aviva, one of the UK’s largest pension providers, to provide their Workplace Pension to all members. We recognise just how important a good pension is, and are proud to offer a very generous employer contribution of 12%, with options to increase this further. This goes hand-in-hand with a Death in Service payment of three times your salary, and a choice of a flexible benefits that can be picked each January at no cost to yourself. This is one of the biggest benefits you have working at the university and whether retirement is in the distant future or just around the corner, the decisions you make now are equally important and that means you need to know more about what it is, what it is worth to you, and what is means to be a member. These pages aim to point you in the right directions throughout your entire pensions timeline, from choosing University of Portsmouth, to life in retirement… your MyPensionPlus Timeline - What you need to know BECOMING A MEMBER HOW IT WORKS: CONTRIBUTIONS HOW IT WORKS: BUILDING YOUR BENEFITS AFTER YOU JOIN DURING YOUR MEMBERSHIP OPTING OUT RESIGNATION RETIREMENT HOW IT WORKS: CONTRIBUTIONS HOW IT WORKS: BUILDING YOUR BENEFITS DURING YOUR MEMBERSHIP Want to Know More? title: UASL Nest
headings: Human Resources Your UASL Nest Pension Explore
body: UASL Nest is the pension for employees of University of Portsmouth Academic Services Limited (UASL) in casual, variable hours and hourly paid contracts, and all other employees and workers who are not eligible for any alternative scheme. If you aren't certain if this applies to you, your contract will confirm the pension your are eligible for. Nest is a workplace pension scheme for all, set up by the government and administered by a public body according to the interests of its members. It is a 'defined contribution' (also known as 'money purchase') pension scheme where you and the University contribute a percentage of your salary towards a personal 'pension pot', where it will be invested and has a chance to grow over time. UASL Nest Timeline - our policy and guidance on your scheme, covering contribution rates and the benefits of membership Nest Homepage – the scheme webpage to learn more and access to your pension Member Portal & Guidance to use it – update your personal details and beneficiaries, manage your contributions and apply to retire Additional Contributions The Nest scheme offers flexibility in making additional contributions through a one-off payment made directly to the scheme, or by setting up a regular direct debit from your bank. Alternatively, you may request to increase the rate of contributions you pay each month on your salary, by emailing pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk with the details. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: NHS Pension Scheme
headings: Human Resources NHS Pension Scheme Adding to your Pension Explore
body: In limited circumstances some employees are eligible to participate in the NHS Pension Scheme. You can find more information on the areas below by accessing the NHS Pension Scheme website. Upon enrolment into the NHS Pension scheme, the University are required to complete a joiner form detailing your home address and work email address. You can update your home address and personal email address via your NHS Member Portal. The NHS Pension scheme state on their website that if they cannot contact you, they may use the services of a third party to check the address shown on your credit history record to see if the address they hold is more up to date. This will leave a 'soft trace' on your credit record and will be visible to you if you access your credit report. This does not affect your credit rating. Total Reward Statement NHS pension and absences You may increase your annual pension by purchasing an Additional Pension, either by lump sum payment or by regular contributions deducted from your University earnings. Follow the above link for further information, and instructions on how to obtain a quote and apply. Money Purchase Additional Voluntary Contributions (MPAVCs) Alternatively, you can build up a separate pension through NHS Money Purchase Additional Voluntary Contributions. This allows you to build up a flexible pension pot with either Standard Life or Prudential that is linked but separate to your NHS scheme. You’ll make separate contributions from your monthly earnings, the amount you contribute is invested according to your wishes, and its value can go up and down over time depending on how well those investments preform. You decide how much you wish to pay, and can change your contributions if you need to. For more information and instructions on how to apply, follow the above link. Early Retirement Reduction Buy Out (ERRBO) Normal retirement age in the 2015 'Career Average' (which all members are now in even if you previously had a Final Salary pension) has a pension age aligned to the State Pension. However, you can pay extra to allow you to retire earlier without 'early retirement' reductions being applied. This can be for up to 3 years, but no earlier than your 65th birthday. There is further information on the NHS Pension Scheme website. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: Aviva for London Campus
headings: Human Resources Your London Campus Aviva Pension your Aviva Timeline - What you need to know Want to Know More? Explore
body: At University of Portsmouth Academic Services Limited (UASL), we enrol new London Campus employees into the Aviva Company Pension Scheme from their contractual start date. Aviva are one of the UK’s largest pension providers and we have established our London Campus scheme with them to provide you one of the best ways you can invest in your own future. We recognise just how important it is to have a leading company pension, and are proud to offer a generous employer contribution of 10% for you to invest and build upon. This is one of the biggest parts of your reward and benefits at UASL and whether retirement is in the distant future or just around the corner, the decisions you make now are equally important that means you need to know more about what it is, what it is worth to you, and what is means to be a member. title: NEST (Other Subsidiaries)
headings: Human Resources Nest Pension Nest Pension & Absences Explore
body: Nest is the pension available to employees of ASTA Technology UK Ltd, and University of Portsmouth Enterprise Ltd (UPEL). Nest is a workplace pension scheme for all, set up by the government and administered by a public body according to the interests of its members. Nest Homepage – the place to start for information and access to your pension Member Portal & Guidance – update your personal details and beneficiaries, manage your contributions and apply to retire Additional Contributions The Nest scheme offers flexibility in making additional contributions through a one-off payment made directly to the scheme, or by setting up a regular direct debit from your bank. Alternatively, you may request to increase the rate of contributions you pay each month on your salary, by emailing pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk with the details. Family Leave (Maternity, Paternity, Shared Parental and Adoption Leave) During paid family leave you will pay contributions on the actual earnings you receive. If you move to reduced pay or statutory-only pay, the University will contribute based on the ‘normal’ pay you had prior to going on reduced pay. If you move to unpaid leave, neither you nor the University will make further payments until you return to work. However, if you move to unpaid leave for any reason while still in ‘Ordinary’ family leave periods (normally the first 26 weeks), such as by not being entitled to statutory pay, the University will continue making contributions while you don’t have to. Your funds will continue to be invested (including during unpaid periods) so may increase or fall in value depending on how the investment choices you have made perform. Other Family Leave (Antenatal, Premature Birth, Neonatal, Parental Bereavement) You will continue to make pension contributions on any earnings or statutory pay you receive. Keeping in Touch (KIT Days / SPLIT Days) You will pay contributions on payment for any KIT days during your Family Leave. You will continue to pay pension contributions during sickness absence on whatever earnings you are paid, and UoP will do same too. This includes any statutory sick pay you receive. If you move to unpaid sickness, you and UoP will not make contributions. Your funds will continue to be invested (including during unpaid periods) so may increase or fall in value depending on how the investment choices you have made perform. https://www.nestpensions.org.uk/schemeweb/nest.html DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: Pensions News & Updates
headings: Human Resources Pensions News & Updates Changes to member contributions in Teachers’ Pensions and Local Government Pension Scheme from April 2026 Pensions Awareness Week NHS Total Reward Statements Available Now Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS) Annual Benefit Statements  - Out Now Teachers' Pension: 'My Pension Online' Reminder Teachers' Pension:  Deadline For 'Faster Accrual' is Fast Approaching Explore
body: From 1 April 2026, the salary bands used to calculate member contributions in the Teachers’ Pension Scheme (TPS) and Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS) will be updated. These bands are reviewed each year and adjusted in line with the Consumer Price Index (CPI) to reflect changes in earnings and inflation. The contribution percentages themselves are not changing, but the salary thresholds will increase. If your salary remains within the same band, your contribution rate will stay the same. Any changes will be applied automatically through payroll from April, so no action is required from you. LGPS 2026/27 Member Contributions TPS 2026/27 Member Contributions If you are making monthly Additional Pension contributions in TPS, the amount may also change from April. Our Payroll team is currently awaiting the updated figures and will apply any adjustments once this information is received. If you would like more information about how this may affect you, please contact the Pensions Team. September 15th is Pensions Awareness Week 2025, and there’s never been a better time to learn more about your scheme and how it works, check up on the value of pension you’ve been earning, and give a little thought to your future. Whatever scheme you are in – MyPensionPlus Aviva, Teachers’ Pension, LGPS, NHS, London Aviva or NEST – your pension is one of the biggest and best benefits we are proud to offer. Each scheme works differently, so be sure to find some time to learn more by looking through some information and resources. Are you not in a pension scheme, or have you opted out? We always recommend joining up as soon as your circumstances allow it. Just email pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk and we’ll let you know what you need to do. The latest Total Reward Statements (TRS) for all NHS Pension members should now be available. Following the Public Sector Pensions remedy (McCloud) these now show two alternative benefit options for eligible members ‘Option A - standard’ and ‘Option B - alternative’, representing the choice of benefit you will have to make when it comes time to retire. For more information, visit: Employee section | NHSBSA. The latest Annual Benefit Statements for LGPS have now been released. You should take this opportunity to check your LGPS member portal and see the updated value of your benefits. Be sure to double-check your beneficiaries and personal details at the same time. Find out more on our benefit statements page. If you're in TP, MyPensionPlus, Aviva or Nest, you don't need to wait and can track your benefits year-round. If you're in NHS, they'll write out and let you know whenever your 'Total Reward Statement' becomes available. For TP members, the 'My Pension Online' member portal is the only way to keep up-to-date on the value of your pension, and is the best and fastest way to update your details and beneficiaries, communicate with the scheme, and apply for added pension and retirement. If you haven't already done so, register now at My Pension Online. If you are considering adding extra contributions to boost your pension, the window to guarantee Faster Accrual (where you pay a higher percentage of contributions during a single year from April to March) closes 31 January. Please login to your 'My Pension Online' account to find out more and apply. title: Managing Sickness Absence
headings: Human Resources Managing Sickness Absence How do I report my sickness absence? Employee self-service and sickness absence FAQs. Entitlement to Sick Pay for Variable Hours Staff Explore
body: The Sickness Absence and Ill Health Policy sets out the requirements employees and their managers must follow in the event of absence from work due to sickness and where ill health or disability prevents effective performance of an employee’s duties. It also identifies sources of support and guidance for employees and managers, as well as outlining its use with other University procedures. If you have management responsibilities and need to refer a member of your team to Occupational Health, you will need to complete a management referral formin line with the Occupational Health referral process If you are a member of staff who has been referred to Occupational Health, you can download our Staff Guide to OH Referral and Advice. What do I do if I am ill? You are required to notify your line manager that you are unwell and unable to attend work as early as possible on the first day of your absence. You should try to give your line manager an estimation of the length of sickness absence if you think it may last longer than a day. How long can I be off for, before I need a fit note? You can self-certify for up to and including 7 calendar days. If you are ill for longer than this, you will need a fit note from your Doctor. What do I do once I receive a fit note? You need to provide your fit note to your line manager who will then send it to People Operations via the People Hub. The fit note details will then be added to the HR system. How do I record my self-certified sickness absence if I am off for more than 7 calendar days and I have been provided with a fit note? Your line manager is responsible for recording the absence directly in iTrent via MSS, in line with the Sickness Absence and Ill Health Policy. Do I include weekends when entering sickness? Yes. If you were ill from Thursday through to Tuesday, this needs to be recorded as one period of absence to include the weekend. Should I record a sickness absence of less than 1 day (I left work due to sickness)? Yes. All periods of sickness absence must be recorded on the HR system, including part-days. If you leave work due to illness, you must inform your line manager and the absence must be recorded in accordance with the Sickness Absence and Ill Health Policy. What dates do I enter if I don’t work every day? Regardless of your working days, you need to input every day that you were off sick. For example, if you only work Monday and Wednesday but you were sick from Monday until Thursday, then you need to enter the period of Monday to Thursday. Do I include disability related absence? Disability-related absence is managed in line with the Equality Act 2010 and the Sickness Absence and Ill Health Policy. Recording requirements should be discussed with your line manager and HR to ensure appropriate authorisation, handling and support. My period of sickness absence is due to a long term condition, do I still need to record this? Yes. Every period of sick leave you take needs to be recorded on the HR system. It is important that policies for Variable Hours staff reflect fair and consistent Terms and Conditions. Specifically, Variable Hours are entitled to claim Occupational Sick Pay in line with their contract of employment. Full details are available at our page Entitlement to Sick pay for Variable Hours Staff. title: Referral to Occupational Health
headings: Human Resources Referral to Occupational Health (OH) Overview Explore
body: You may be referred to Occupational Health (OH) for advice where your health is affecting your fitness to carry out your role, or your role is adversely affecting your health. A referral may be appropriate when:- Your sickness absence is long–term (four weeks or more) Your sickness absence is short-term, intermittent and/or frequent (five days sickness absence in the last 12 month rolling period) You require help and support with health concerns at work Health surveillance for Health & Safety regulatory purposes as required for specific roles, e.g., working with hazardous materials etc. You have had an accident at work Further information is provided in the University Managing Sickness Absence Procedure. Who does this apply to? Any member of staff employed by the University can be referred to OH because the University has a duty of care to ensure that you are fit for work and that work conditions do not have an adverse effect on your health. You may not refer yourself to OH. Staff who are experiencing health-related conditions or concerns that may be impacting on their fitness for work, should bring this to the attention of their line manager who will be able to complete an OH referral to obtain further advice and guidance. In exceptional circumstances, you may be referred by Human Resources (HR). What do I need to consider? You will be referred for one of the reasons shown above and your manager will explain this to you. Your manager will complete a standard Occupational Health Referral Form giving the reason for the referral and the specific questions related to your work that require OH advice. You are not required to provide approval for the referral or reasons for the referral and your responsibilities are detailed in the Staff Guide to Occupational Health Referral and Advice. The OH practitioner will prepare a report and you have the right to see the report before it is sent to your manager and Human Resources. Informed consent for the report is explained in the referral form. The OH report will provide advice in relation to your fitness to work and may include suggested adjustments. Your manager will discuss the report with you. The report will not include confidential clinical information. What do I need to do? 1. Ensure that you record all your sickness absence on the Employee Self-Service (ESS) system as soon as possible on your return to work after the period of sickness absence. You are entitled to Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) and Occupational Sick Pay (OSP) depending on your length of service. The information recorded on ESS is used to meet the statutory requirement to pay the correct sick pay and maintain accurate records. 2. Familiarise yourself with following documents: a. University Managing Sickness Absence Procedure b. Staff Guide to Occupational Health Referral and Advice c. Occupational Health Referral form 3. Confirm and attend your appointment with OH. If you do not attend or engage with OH, decisions may be taken on the information available and without the benefit of OH advice. 4. You should attend an appointment with OH even if you are signed off sick and are absent from work. There may be follow up appointments which you are expected to attend. 5. Discuss the report with your manager including the suggested adjustments to confirm what actions will be taken. Where can I find further advice? Your designated HR Business Partner team Employee Assistance Programme (EAP) HR Business Partnering Team Learning and Development My Rewards and Benefits Occupational Health Service Equality and Diversity Contact and departments

title: Entitlement to Sick Pay for Variable Hours Staff
headings: Human Resources Entitlement to Sick Pay for Variable Hours Staff Overview Who does this apply to? What do I need to consider? Service Full Pay (calendar days) Half Pay (calendar days) Statutory Sick Pay What do I need to do? What else do I need to know? How do I get help? Explore
body: It is important that policies for Variable Hours staff reflect fair and consistent Terms and Conditions. Specifically, Variable Hours are entitled to claim Occupational Sick Pay in line with their contract of employment. Variable Hours staff and their managers or supervisors. Variable Hours staff are entitled to claim Occupational Sick Pay in line with their contract of employment, as follows: Full Pay (calendar days) Half Pay (calendar days) 61 (after completing 4 months’ service) 61 (after completing 4 months’ service) During 4th & successive years During 4th & successive years An individual’s entitlement is calculated by deducting their absences due to illness during the twelve months immediately preceding the first day of absence from the Occupational Sick Pay entitlement according to their length of service. For example, an individual who has been employed for two years and who has two periods of sickness absence, each of 12 days, during the previous 12 months will have 98 days full pay entitlement remaining (equal to 122 minus 2 lots of 12). Variable Hours staff can claim Occupational Sick Pay only for the hours they would have worked on the days for which they are absent. Variable Hours staff may also have an entitlement to Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) if they satisfy the conditions prescribed by the Department of Work and Pensions. To qualify for SSP, the Government’s Statutory Sick Pay eligibility criteria must be met. Individuals would only need to claim SSP if their Occupational Sick Pay entitlement were to fall below the level of their SSP entitlement. Provided a Variable Hours staff member has contacted their manager or supervisor to say they are unwell and can't come into work, they can claim Occupational Sick Pay for the hours they were asked to work according to their entitlement. The timesheet should include the hours they would have worked on the day they were sick and recorded as 'sickness'. If the absence continues beyond seven calendar days, they must produce a doctor's statement. The links below include additional guidance on sickness entitlement: University of Portsmouth Managing Sickness Absence procedure. Government guidance on Statutory Sick Pay Use this contact form to get in touch. title: Portsmouth Hallmark
headings: Human Resources The Portsmouth Hallmark Explore
body: Through the People Strategy, we will ensure that all our people are supported to acquire the Portsmouth Hallmark. The hallmark is based on the attributes that we wish our people to demonstrate, with the aim of our supporting our students to acquire the Hallmarks of a Portsmouth Graduate (Education Strategy 2016-2020). The characteristics of the Portsmouth Hallmark are that we will be knowledgeable, informed, self-aware, self-motivated and engaged. More specifically, our people will take pride in realising the aims of the University of Portsmouth, as appropriate to their role: Have an excellent understanding of their role and the part they play in ensuring that the University achieves its strategic aims; Have the ability to work independently as well as collaboratively, coming up with creative and better ways of doing things, embracing change and challenges as they arise; Be engaged, with a sense of professional responsibility linked to a culture of valuing equality, diversity, difference, respect and sustainability; Be effective team players, able to communicate clearly and effectively, supporting all members of the University community; Be able to work in a range of environments, responding positively to new situations by being aware, flexible, agile and realistic in their expectations; Be proactively engaged in and proud of working for the University, and striving for excellence in all they do in order to achieve the best outcomes for the University and its students. Be passionate and effective advocates in promoting the University to the wider community. title: Using vehicles on University business
headings: Estates and Campus Services Using Vehicles on University Business Booking a Vehicle Insurance Accidents Explore
body: These can be booked via e-mail to transport.bookings@port.ac.uk Please Note: 48 hours notice is required. A follow up copy of the booking form is no longer required as confirmation. All bookings are processed via a web based system designed especially for the University by the contractual hire company. Prices can be obtained by contacting Transport Section or Jane Sheldrake If you wish to take a vehicle overseas, please let the Transport Section know in advance with at least three weeks notice. Overseas travel will only be arranged with the approval of the Head of Department. Please ring for further information about overseas travel. No minibuses to be taken overseas unless the driver can prove he has a PCV licence and has passed the medical and test required by the University. The University vehicles are covered by a fully comprehensive insurance policy with an excess of £250.00. Please contact the Transport Section for information. If you are involved in an accident while driving or acting as the person in charge of a vehicle, you are responsible for completing an Accident Report Form in respect of any accident to the vehicle or any person, property, or other vehicle. You should submit this form as soon as possible to the Transport Section. If subsequent to a motor accident, the Police prosecute you must inform the Insurance Officer immediately. title: Disclosure and Barring Service  Checks
headings: Human Resources Disclosure and Barring Service Overview Who does this apply to? What do I need to consider? What do I need to do? What else do I need to know? How do I get help? Explore
body: The University is required to carry out criminal record checks with the UK Government’s Disclosure and Barring Service (DBS) on individuals in specific circumstances dictated by UK legislation. The University’s Human Resources (HR) department is responsible for administering DBS checks for staff where they are required for employment purposes. The HR department also administers DBS checks for staff who need to apply for an NHS Research Passport. The University will make it clear if and when individual circumstances require a DBS check to be carried out. Should you be required to be referred to the DBS for a criminal records check, you will need to arrange a time to visit the HR department to complete the online application. HR will not be able to submit the check without sight of appropriate original documents as evidence of your identity. This information will be submitted to the DBS service, who make the necessary checks, and return the completed certificate directly to you. You must then be able to produce this certificate as evidence of the outcome of the referral. In order to prove your identity, you will need to provide original qualifying documents (not copies) to Human Resources. Without these, it will not be possible to proceed with your application. The application for a DBS check for a staff member or a job applicant follows these steps: The University will identify a need for you to be referred to the DBS. As soon as the University informs you that you require a DBS check then book a time with HR to conduct the check. Ensure you have the appropriate original documents to complete your identity check for your DBS application. At the appointed time, report to the location specified in your appointment, bringing with you with the necessary original documents to prove your identity. The HR officer will check your identification documents, and then return them to you. They will then complete the online application form with you and submit it to the DBS. After performing the appropriate checks, the DBS send the certificate directly to you. Human Resources will be notified by the DBS when the certificate is sent. You must be able to produce the original certificate as evidence of the outcome of the referral. You can also view this as a flowchart. Generally, it can take up to 14 working days (3 weeks) to complete a DBS check, from the time the DBS receive the completed online form. The links below give more detailed information on the Disclosure and Barring Service: This link gives more detailed guidance on the UK Government Disclosure and Barring Service. You can personally apply for a Basic Disclosure (administered by Disclosure Scotland) at any time: this does not need to be submitted via the University. The Basic Disclosure will include details of convictions considered unspent under The Rehabilitation of Offenders Act 1974. There are specific requirements for the documents acceptable to provide evidence of your identity. If you have a non-GB driving licence, you need to check whether your licence is valid, and therefore acceptable as proof of identity. Use this contact form to get in touch ( Please note that you need to be logged into your UoP staff email account in order to access this form). title: Repairs and maintenance
headings: Estates and Campus Services Repairs and Maintenance How to report a fault or request repairs and maintenance Definitions Explore
body: We have set up a Helpdesk on ext. 6677 that should be used to report faults and request repairs and maintenance. This service should also be used to request rechargeable works. The Helpdesk is manned from 08.30 until 17.15 Mondays to Thursdays and 08.30 to 16.15 on Friday. Outside of these hours a Voicemail answering service operates directing callers to the Lodge on 3418 for emergency items. Alternatively you can leave a message which will be dealt with on the following working day. An email facility is also available, Helpdesk. Whatever method is used to report a fault etc, please ensure that as much information as possible is supplied to enable a more efficient service to be provided. Specific information that is required is listed below: Who you are and your phone number Where the problem/job is. Please specify building,floor and room/door number and/or estates reference number if known What, if any, access limitations exist What the nature of the problem is. Please provide as much clear information as you can Is the problem an emergency, urgent or routine (see definitions below as a guide) You will be provided with a job reference number that you should note down. You will not recieve written notification of your job request unless it was made by voice or email when you will be notified of the job reference number. Your report will be allocated to the relevant member of the Estates Department team who will either allocate it to a tradesman or contractor as appropriate. If necessary you will be contacted to clarify details or to arrange a convenient time to meet with you. There are currently no agreed target response times for the fault rectification or indeed a strict method of measuring performance. However, as a guide the following categories and times may be used: Emergency: Investigate and make safe as soon as possible, restore or provide temporary facilities within 24 hours Urgent: Investigate and make safe as soon as possible with repairs being carried out within 5 working days Routine: Schedule works into department work programme and normally complete within eight weeks Please note that to enable us to achieve the above we need your support in operating the procedure outlined and providing the most accurate information possible. The intentional incorrect categorisation of problems to achieve an earlier response time will only lead to a general degredation of the service for everyone. The department receives approximately 300 reports/requests each week so, understandably, it is not possible to respond to every call immediately. As mentioned above, it is easy to feel that your particular problem is an emergency but there is the obvious need to be carefull in determining priority in order that we can manage our workload as efficiently as possible. The following guide should be used to categorise your request: Emergency work is resolving a problem that is an immediate threat to the health and safety of people, buildings and equipment. Examples of emergency work are: someone trapped in a lift An urgent job is resolving a problem which is a threat to the general welfare of people, property or equipment, or which causes significant disruption to the business of your department. Examples of urgent work are: loss of lighting and or power loss of sanitary provision Routine work is non-essential and can be planned to occur at any time to suit the client. Examples of routine work are: new keys need cutting repair of minor damage General information Rechargeable works Capital works Repairs and maintenance Repairs and maintenance Engineering maintenance and management Soft Services (Campus Environment) Policies and Procedures Property records and database Building opening times Planon Self-Service (Log-In using your standard desktop details) Contact and departments

title: Research Passports
headings: Human Resources Research Passports Overview Who does this apply to? What do I need to consider? What do I need to do? How do I get help? Explore
body: A Research Passport is the mechanism for non-NHS staff (that is, higher education institution employees who have no contractual arrangement with the NHS) to obtain an Honorary Research Contract or Letter of Access when they propose to carry out research in the NHS. An Honorary Research Contract (HRC) applies to researchers who plan to undertake research which requires access to patients and identifiable patient data and has a direct bearing on the quality of patient care (for example provision of healthcare, personal care and guidance on well-being). By having an honorary research contract the researcher comes under the umbrella of NHS indemnity and is therefore covered by the NHS against potential claims for negligent harm. A Letter of Access (LOA) is required by employees who plan to undertake research which requires access to NHS premises or access to patients and patient data with no direct bearing on the quality of care (for example qualitative interviews, focus groups, on-site analysis of patient data). The Research Passport scheme provides: One set of checks on a researcher conducting research in the NHS. One standard form completed by the researcher and his/her employer, and validated by an NHS organisation. A completed Research Passport which is presented to all the relevant NHS organisations. Faster study start-up. A Research Passport may be project-specific or may be valid for a period of three years for a number of projects. If you are either a member of staff or research student at the University, are proposing to carry out research in the NHS, and have no ongoing contractual relationship with the NHS, you will need a Research Passport. If you are unsure whether you require a Research Passport please contact the Research and Development office at the NHS Trust where you intend to carry out your research for clarification. You will not need a Research Passport if: You are employed by an NHS organisation; or You are an independent contractor (e.g. GP) or employed by anindependent contractor; or You have an honorary clinical contract with an NHS Trust (e.g. clinical academics). In this situation if you wanted to research in another NHS Trust you would need to apply to their R&D department directly for a Letter of Access; or You are a student on a healthcare placement. You may only start your work within an NHS Trust when that Trust has issued you with either an Honorary Research Contract or a Letter of Access. Please note that the Research Passport is only the mechanism to apply for these: it is not in itself permission to work in an NHS Trust. We are implementing the Research Passport initiative in accordance with the arrangements set out in the NIHR ‘Research in the NHS: HR Good Practice Resource Pack’ and the Hampshire & Isle of Wight NIHR guidance notes. To apply for a Research Passport, you must follow these steps: Download the Research Passport Form and read the instructions for completing the form. Contact the R&D department in your lead NHS Trust to agree which Risk Assessment Level is appropriate for your research activity, and confirm whether a Disclosure and Barring Service check is necessary, and the level required. You will need to share these Occupational Health Clearance Levels with them and ask them to advise you which Risk Assessment level you need to be cleared to. If a Disclosure and Barring Service check is necessary then read the Disclosure and Barring Service Guidance and then book a time with the University’s HR department to conduct the check. Contact the University’s Occupational Health (OH) Service and advise them of the agreed Risk Assessment Level, to identify the necessary assessments and immunisations required as detailed in the Occupational Health Clearance Levels document. Complete sections 1–3 of the Research Passport form, and prepare all relevant documents you will need to present to the University HR department for the relevant pre-engagement checks. Ask your University line manager/supervisor to complete section 4 of the form. At the agreed time, attend the University HR department to complete the Disclosure and Barring Service application. Support the University Occupational Health team to carry out the necessary assessments and immunisations required as detailed in the Occupational Health Clearance Levels document. On successful completion of the necessary assessments and immunisations, OH will issue you with a signed clearance certificate. Once you have completed the occupational health assessments and immunisations, received your DBS certificate, and have the original documentation for the Pre-engagement Checks, pass these, with your completed form, to the University HR department for checking. If you are a research student at the University, HR will ask Academic Registry to check the details on the form. On completion of the checks, the University HR department will sign off their part of the form and return it to you, along with the relevant certificates and clearances. If you are a research student at the University, HR will ask Academic Registry to check the details on the form. Take the completed Research Passport form with your CV, original DBS Certificate and Occupational Health Clearance Certificate, together with a synopsis of what your research will involve to the lead NHS organisation. Once the form has been authorised by one NHS organisation it becomes a valid Research Passport that you can provide to other NHS organisations when you require an Honorary Research Contract or Letter of Access. You can also view this as a flowchart. The time taken to complete a Research Passport form is usually governed by the time it takes to receive the Disclosure and Barring Service certificate. Generally, it can take up to 14 working days (3 weeks) to complete a DBS check, from the time the DBS receive the completed online form. What else do I need to know? The links below give more detailed guidance on the Research Passport and the application process. Research Passport form (the link to the form is "Research Passport form", under "Background Information") and completion detailed guidance Guidance on Occupational Health Clearance Levelsand the necessary assessments and immunisations Guidance on the Disclosure and Barring Service (DBS) application Guidance on the Pre-engagement Checks Sample Occupational Health Clearance Certificate You can also refer to our Frequently Asked Questions. Use this contact form to get in touch ( please note that you need to be logged into your UoP staff email account to access this form). title: Information for staff about Brexit
headings: Human Resources Information for Staff about EUSS Overview The Points Based Immigration System University Contact Details Frequently Asked Questions Explore
body: Pages last updated: 12 August 2021 The way we recruit non UK/Irish nationals has changed. On 1 January 2021 UKVI launched a new points based immigration system for workers coming to the UK. This webpage has been established to provide information and support to staff who may be impacted by the changes to free movement between the UK and the EU/EEA. EU,EEA and Swiss citizens had until 30 June 2021 to apply to the EU Settlement Scheme to protect their rights, to continue to live and work in the UK, and access public services like the NHS. Successful applicants obtain settled status or pre-settled status, depending on how long they have lived in the UK. Evidence of this status is provided digitally through an online service. The new points based immigration system came into effect on 1 January 2021 and impacts both EEA and non EEA citizens coming to work in the UK on or after 1 January 2021. Anyone who did not obtain EUSS in time will need to obtain a visa status under the new system to continue living and working in the UK. UKVI will, in some cases consider late applications to the EUSS if there is a genuine reason behind the delay in applying. Email HR-UKVI-Compliance@port.ac.uk if there are any questions or queries related to the EUSS or the new immigration in relation to staff employed by the University. Before emailing please read the FAQs page which may cover your query, giving further information for staff and their family members on the scheme.If international staff or students experience racism or harassment, this should be reported and dealt with under the University’s Dignity and Respect Policy. All staff are encouraged to appropriately challenge inappropriate behaviours and raise concerns with managers so these can be dealt with. The FAQs page gives further information for staff and their family members on the EUSS scheme. If the FAQs do not answer your query please email HR-UKVI-Compliance@port.ac.uk. title: Career Development Framework
headings: Human Resources Your Career Development Framework ‌01 Consider your career, consider your development 02 Plan your development 03 Explore university opportunities 04 Access additional University resources 05 Consider external resources 06 Discuss and agree your development with your line manager‌ Explore
body: People are our most valuable asset at the University of Portsmouth. Providing the right support and development opportunities are crucial to our success. Professional development forms part of your annual Performance and Development Review (PDR). Depending on your role, skills and experience your career will vary. Your development can focus on a number of areas including: Developing in your current role Preparing for new responsibilities Increasing your potential Aspiring to work in new or different areas Hint - Read further information on career development. Professional development is your responsibility. The first step is about taking ownership. Deciding what you would like to achieve, then making it happen. Progressing your career - taking ownership Your skills, your strengths, your experience Identifying your skills Hint - Our training and development plan will help identify development needs and support career discussions. See more opportunities on the Learning Resource Centre webpage. ‌ Development Programmes ‌ Women's development Personal effectiveness ‌ Mentoring and coaching Leadership and Management Research and Innovation Professional Services Learning and Teaching Learning and Teaching Learning resource centre Employee Assistance Programme (EAP) Extra opportunities at UoP Employability support Hint - Contact the EAP for an hours career guidance each year. You may wish to gain external experience, explore wider opportunities, or read more on: Professional associations External career support Hint - Speak to your networks - they may have other resources and contacts to support your development. Your PDR is part of a meaningful conversation with your line manager, that supports you in contributing to the strategic aims of the University. Reviewing your development ideas will help you identify any training needs. title: Research and innovation
headings: Our global research Our research Research centres and institutes Research areas Areas of expertise Our global research impact Our research in the media Research blogs Life Solved podcast Research news Join us in our research Find a PhD Supervisor Our research degrees subject areas Postgraduate events Fellowships Our universe-changing space research Collaborate with our researchers Let's collaborate - work with us Explore our research database and staff directory We use cookies
body: Responsible Innovation: solving problems, enriching lives, creating lasting impact We want to help shape the future and know it takes more than words to make a difference. That's exactly what our research aims to do. We're making a significant difference in the world. 77% of our research impact was rated as world-leading or internationally excellent in REF 2021, and we are working on preparations for our REF 2029 submission. Our belief in knowledge-sharing makes our research exceptional and relevant. Our approach is always collaborative. We work with local and global partners in education and industry. Our media ready experts also regularly contribute their knowledge and expertise to print, broadcast and digital media coverage of the issues their research addresses. View our Research and Innovation Strategy to find out how we're delivering globally significant research excellence, and explore below how you could become part of our research community with our PhD and research degree opportunities. Our research centres, institutes and hubs bring together expertise and resources from across the University of Portsmouth to tackle pressing global issues. Working with industry and academic partners, and across our 5 faculties, our researchers are leading their fields and creating impact. Discover our unique, collaborative and multi-disciplinary areas of expertise. Our research responds to challenges, influences policy and helps change the world for the better. Discover the impact of our research If you've already got your own research idea, start looking for a supervisor whose research interests match yours. Take your expertise further by studying for a PhD or other research degree at the University of Portsmouth. Explore the subject areas you can study & get started on your research journey. Come to one of our events to explore your course, find out more about our expert support teams and talk to our current students. Fellowships give you the step-change opportunity to undertake an individual project alongside activities to develop your career. Explore our fellowship opportunities. The University plays a critical role in supporting space industries in the UK and is delivering pioneering research in the space sector. We may use cookies to analyse visitor data, improve our website, show personalised content and give you a great website experience. For more information about cookies on our site and to tailor the cookies we collect, select ‘Manage settings’.

title: University of Portsmouth Staff Mentoring Programme
headings: Human Resources Staff Mentoring Programme About the Programme Explore
body: The success of our vision, to become the UK’s top modern university and one of the top 100 universities in the world by 2030, relies upon the creativity and innovation of staff and the development of a culture where people feel passionate about their contribution to the University. Mentoring helps us realise the potential of our staff in achieving organisational and professional goals. It also supports the development of a culture of equality and diversity and contributes to our Athena SWAN and HR Excellence in Research award commitment. The University of Portsmouth’s Staff Mentoring Programme is an easy-to-use online platform designed to promote and develop meaningful mentoring partnerships. The scheme supports staff to engage in effective development and embody our drive for excellence. Staff Mentoring at UoP How do I join the Staff Mentoring Scheme? FAQs and case studies If you have any queries regarding the programme please email staffmentoring@port.ac.uk. title: Developing your Supervisory Skills
headings: Human Resources Learning and Development Title Target Audience Outline The aims of the workshop are to: Learning Outcomes Pre-event Preparation Format Presenter(s) Delegate Feedback Explore
body: Developing your Supervisory Skills (2 day programme) This workshop is aimed at staff new to a supervisory role or aspiring to move into one. It is also relevant to more experienced supervisors wishing to refresh their approach. These two, linked one-day workshops cover all the essential aspects of supervisory management and seek to develop practical skills and confidence. Within a confidential environment, participants can share and learn from each others experiences. Outline for participants the basic principles of effective supervision and highlight the skills needed by supervisors in order to succeed Explore in more depth some of the critical areas of skills, ie self-management, team leadership, promoting and supporting team work and tackling poor performance Provide opportunities for participants to apply good practice principles to representative case studies and scenarios Create a supportive environment in which participants can recognise their strengths and identify how to build on these. At the end of the workshops participants will: Have explored practical strategies for dealing with challenging situations by undertaking individual and group exercises Have reflected on their role; assessed their current level of supervisory skill and identified ways in which to improve these. Participants are asked to reflect in a structured way on their supervisory role and to attend the workshops prepared to share their thoughts with other participants. Click to download the Developing your Supervisory Skills pre-course preparation document. Although the workshops include some structured input from the workshop leader about the principles and techniques of effective supervision, much of the time will be spent on individual and small group activities, feedback and discussion. Rayna Baker, Trayna UK, External Consultant "Both workshops were well presented and well explained" "Very interesting and enjoyable" "The variety of activities used was fun and kept it interesting" Click here for the Learning and Development calendar with all available dates. title: Support for Staff With A Disability
headings: Human Resources Support for staff with a disability Human Resources Occupational Health Advisers Equality and Diversity Office Disabled Staff Forum Employee Assistance Programme Explore
body: The University of Portsmouth offers a range of support services for individuals with disabilities and their managers. The HR Business Partnering teams provide professional guidance and support for individuals and managers across all HR policies and procedures across the University, with each team supporting different Faculties and Professional Services. They also provide advice on employment solutions and best practice / legislative advice. The University Occupational Health Service provides impartial advice to employees and managers following a clinical assessment of need. Employees may be formally referred by their line manager or HR. Advice will be provided on adjustments that are recommended in respect of the employee’s fitness to work. The Equality and Diversity (E&D) team is able to give advice, guidance and support, in addition to that available from other business areas, around disability and reasonable adjustments. The University has a Disabled Staff Network for consultation with disabled staff and for their mutual support. Please encourage disabled staff to participate in these sessions. The Disabled Staff Forum exists for all staff 'with a long term illness, disability or infirmity that affects their everyday life or the work that they do'. The University provides an Employee Assistance Programme for staff, which offers a free, confidential helpline (0800 023 9324) that provides unlimited access to information, advice and support, and onward referral to telephone or face-to-face counselling where appropriate. title: Changing Roles within the University
headings: Human Resources Changing Roles within the University Managing the transition Secondments Moving to a management role Explore
body: Leaving your current role If you are transferring between departments or changing roles within the University, it is likely that you will not require access to the same systems or folders in shared drives, or to certain business applications (e.g. Finance). Your Head of Department/Service or their agreed delegate should make arrangements to ensure that access to areas no longer required are withdrawn by Information Services and/or the appropriate Business System Owners and any equipment belonging to the department is returned prior to the date of transfer. Induction into your new role The University of Portsmouth values the importance of providing an effective and timely induction for all new members of staff to ensure they successfully integrate into the University, their department, their team, and to their role. You can find out more information in our Induction section. The line manager of your new role should provide you with an Induction checklist as well as a Training and Development plan. Taking up a secondment Following your induction into your seconded role, it is recommended that there are regular reviews with your new line manager to ensure you are supported, and also there is an opportunity to review performance. Day-to-day line management during the secondment will be the responsibility of your new line manager. Further information can be found in the University's Secondment Policy. Your substantive line manager may wish to keep in touch during your secondment. In addition, any HR matters that may continue beyond the secondment period must be informed to your substantive line manager. Returning from a secondment Prior to the end of the period of secondment, your substantive line manager should arrange a meeting to discuss reorientation to your substantive post and update you on any changes within the department. At the end of your secondment, the Head of Department/Service of your seconded role, or their agreed delegate, should make arrangements to ensure that access to areas no longer required are withdrawn by Information Services and/or the appropriate Business System Owners and any equipment belonging to the department is returned prior to the date of transfer. If your new role has line management responsibility you should should ensure you have completed the core training for managers. title: Leaving the University
headings: Human Resources Leaving the University Resigning from my job Fixed-term contract employees - Core Role Managing my retirement Changing my retirement or resignation plans Redundancy Explore
body: If you are planning on leaving your role at the University, you are required to provide written notice of your intention to leave to your line manager, subject to the terms specified in your Contract of Employment or Letter of Appointment. The University standard notice periods for staff depend on the type of contract. Before submitting your written notice of leaving to your line manager, you will need to consider the following: Leaving the University- Issues to Consider. You will also need to agree with your line manager your last paid day of employment at the University. To prepare for leaving the University you should familiarise yourself with the Staff Leavers Procedures and Checklist. If you are employed on a fixed term contract, HR will write to you as necessary before the end date of your contract and you will be given access to the redeployment module, which will enable you to apply for university vacancies before they are published to the wider audience. If you are retiring from the University, you should bear in mind the guidance in Leaving the University- Issues to Consider. If you intend to return to the University as either a paid employee or visiting staff member, please ensure that HR is advised of the details by your line manager. If you are thinking about retirement and are a member or have previously been a member of one of the pension schemes offered by the University, our Pensions section provides links to the schemes’ websites and member portals where further information about retirement can be found. General information about workplace pensions can be found on the UK Government website where you can also find information and advice about the State pension and pension entitlements. If you have any queries which are not answered above, please email pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk Providing that your line manager is in agreement, your final date of employment can be amended. Authorisation will need to be sent to the People Operations Team via PeopleHub to confirm the change. Should you wish to withdraw your request to leave, please submit your request in writing to your line manager, who will initiate a discussion with you. It is the aim of the University to seek to prevent situations arising which threaten job security. Redundancies will only be considered as a last resort. Further information on the University's policy and details of the redundancy process can be found in the Organisational Change, Redeployment and Redundancy Policy (OCRR).
