title: Recognition
headings: Human Resources Recognition at the University of Portsmouth The Recognition Ecosystem Our Values Ambitious Responsible Open Ecosystem Components Recognition Ecosystem Introduction - supporting you during the launch Explore
body: People are the driving force behind everything we do and each success and accomplishment is a collective achievement, made possible only through the exceptional contributions of individuals and teams across our University. Recognising and celebrating these achievements in a manner that is fair, inclusive, consistent and transparent is essential to fostering an environment where everyone feels valued, supported, and inspired to do their best work. Recognition is not just about rewarding success, it’s about reinforcing the attitudes, actions, and values that will help us realise our ambitions and support a culture where we are all motivated to excel, collaborate, and innovate in pursuit of our strategic goals. Our Recognition Ecosystem is a framework to promote a culture of appreciation and values-led behaviour across the University, embedding Appreciative Management Practice that reflects and reinforces who we are: Ambitious, Responsible, and Open. The Recognition Ecosystem brings together a range of complementary schemes tailored to different contexts and levels of recognition, ensuring that contributions of all kinds are valued and celebrated. Each of these is directly linked to our values, ensuing what we celebrate and reward reflects who we are and what we stand for as a university. By aligning with those values, we recognise the behaviours and mindsets that drive successes, not just the outcomes, and credit how achievement is reached when done so in ways consistent with our identity and aspirations. We pursue bold ideas in an environment where creativity, innovation and success is encouraged and celebrated We nurture and value relationships and partnerships that foster a global outlook We inspire and support staff and students to achieve their potential and meet the challenges of society We never settle for second best We pursue bold ideas in an environment where creativity, innovation and success is encouraged and celebrated We nurture and value relationships and partnerships that foster a global outlook We inspire and support staff and students to achieve their potential and meet the challenges of society We never settle for second best We act with integrity for the greater good We insist on upholding the highest academic and professional standards We respect and celebrate diversity and equal opportunity through an inclusive culture We aim for sustainability across all our activities We act with integrity for the greater good We insist on upholding the highest academic and professional standards We respect and celebrate diversity and equal opportunity through an inclusive culture We aim for sustainability across all our activities We work consultatively and collaboratively to benefit from new perspectives We share ideas and maintain transparency We are trustworthy and do what we say we will do We listen and respond to the needs of our students and staff We work consultatively and collaboratively to benefit from new perspectives We share ideas and maintain transparency We are trustworthy and do what we say we will do We listen and respond to the needs of our students and staff Vice Chancellor’s Awards for Excellence represent our highest form of recognition. This ceremonial celebration honours extraordinary contributions across the University and reinforces our shared purpose through public acknowledgment and the development of institutional pride Vice Chancellor’s Awards for Excellence represent our highest form of recognition. This ceremonial celebration honours extraordinary contributions across the University and reinforces our shared purpose through public acknowledgment and the development of institutional pride Living our Values Awards are a nomination-based recognition scheme that highlights and provides financial reward for exceptional demonstration of our values. Linking recognition to our core values, alongside excellence in contribution and delivery, these help highlight achievements to the University and inspire others Living our Values Awards are a nomination-based recognition scheme that highlights and provides financial reward for exceptional demonstration of our values. Linking recognition to our core values, alongside excellence in contribution and delivery, these help highlight achievements to the University and inspire others ThankUoP is manager-led, giving rapid recognition and an award of retail vouchers to recognise behaviours shown and achievements made. It supports senior managers in embedding a culture of appreciation within their teams and aligns daily leadership with our institutional values ThankUoP is manager-led, giving rapid recognition and an award of retail vouchers to recognise behaviours shown and achievements made. It supports senior managers in embedding a culture of appreciation within their teams and aligns daily leadership with our institutional values Highfive is a peer-to-peer recognition scheme, empowering all colleagues to celebrate everyday contributions and moments of support in real time with a message of thanks. Anyone can use Highfive to acknowledge and celebrate any other colleague Highfive is a peer-to-peer recognition scheme, empowering all colleagues to celebrate everyday contributions and moments of support in real time with a message of thanks. Anyone can use Highfive to acknowledge and celebrate any other colleague Appreciative Management Practice a leadership mindset that values, acknowledges, and builds on the strengths and contributions of individuals and teams and builds a connected community. This is applicable to all, whether a senior leader, manager, colleague or team member and each of us should be encouraged to adopt an appreciative management approach to our interactions with others Appreciative Management Practice a leadership mindset that values, acknowledges, and builds on the strengths and contributions of individuals and teams and builds a connected community. This is applicable to all, whether a senior leader, manager, colleague or team member and each of us should be encouraged to adopt an appreciative management approach to our interactions with others Find out more about each component by following each link above. Recognition Ecosystem Introduction - supporting you during the launch Our new Recognition Ecosystem brings together 3 new tools (accompanying appreciative management practices and the Vice Chancellor's Awards) to help show appreciation and recognise the behaviours and accomplishments of our colleagues at all levels. These 3 new options are introduced in stages over the next few months, with Living our Values Awards launching 1 August, Highfive from 1 September, and ThankUoP on 1 October. Where to Start? We've produced a video introduction to the Recognition Ecosystem to check out first: Please follow this YouTube link, and you can follow each link above to find all the details on each recognition component. We're also running 11 drop-in sessions (open to all and with no need to book) in various locations over the next few months, where you can ask us anything about the new opportunities for reward and how they work - we look forward to seeing you there in person. title: Maps and directions
headings: Getting here and getting around Already here? Train Car Park and ride Parking in the city Parking discounts Parking at the University What you need to know about parking How to pay Parking charges (Pay as you go tariff) Monday - Friday Friday 4.00pm - Monday 7.00am Frequently Asked Questions How can I pay for parking? How long can I park for? When can I use the car park? What if I need to extend my parking time? How many car parks are there? Can I use any of them? What if my car is still parked there outside the designated public parking times? What if I move my car to a different University car park during my pre-paid parking session? What if I have issues paying through the app? Can I use the EV parking spaces? Car parks and spaces Car parking Taxi Coach Air From London From Southampton Sea Local bus services Bike or foot Accessible travel Our locations We use cookies
body: Plan your journey to and from Portsmouth, and explore our campus when you get here Find your way around our campus. Our closest station is Portsmouth and Southsea. It’s a 5-minute walk from the buildings on our Guildhall campus. There are daily direct trains from London Waterloo and London Victoria (2 hours), and regular services from Southampton, Bournemouth, Chichester and Brighton. We’re only a 2-hour journey away from Bristol too. You can plan your route with National Rail or Trainline. Take the M275 into Portsmouth (signed 'Portsmouth West') whether approaching from the east (A3(M)/A27) or the west (M27 Junction 12). Ignore the junctions signed to Southsea and Hilsea. As with any city, parking in Portsmouth is limited. Therefore it's best to use the park-and-ride service at the entrance to the city on the M275. The cost is £4 per vehicle and buses run into Portsmouth every 12 minutes. Up to 5 people can travel using 1 ticket. If you want to park in the city, follow signs for 'Historic Waterfront/Seafront' from the end of the M275. This takes you across 3 roundabouts to traffic lights by Unicorn Gate (entrance to the naval dockyard) and St John's Cathedral. These are the traffic lights at the top of the Guildhall Campus map – the route you take from here depends which part of the campus you're heading to. Car parks and limited on-street parking is available in the city centre for a small charge, but spaces fill up quickly. You’ll need a visitor’s permit if you want to use a University car park. To apply for one, email car.parking@port.ac.uk. Sorry, we can’t guarantee you a space in advance. If you have an electric car, there are a number of charging points around the city that you can use. You can find the locations for all of the points using the carwow interactive tool. Students and University staff can park at Gunwharf Quays Car Park during weekdays for the discounted rate of £5 for up to 12 hours per day. The discounted rate is not available on weekends, during Portsmouth school holidays and one-off events. Please visit the car park office at Gunwharf Quays (G Section of the Upper Basement Level) at the start of the day to get your ticket validated for the discount, you will have to provide valid student or staff ID. Gunwharf Quays can also offer student discount at Wickham Street Car Park - 3 Wickham Street, PO1 3EF on weekdays during school holidays. If you have any questions, please call 02392 836773 or email gunwharf.quays@apcoa.com The University has a number of car parks with spaces available on a first-come first-served basis University car parks are open to the general public and staff/students without permits outside of permit hours (from 4.00pm - 7.00am Mon - Fri and anytime at weekends) using the pay and display tariff. Parking charges can be paid by using the Pay By Phone app or by visiting paybyphone.co.uk. You can also pay by phoning 0330 400 7275. You will need the location number of the car park which will be displayed on signage at your chosen car park. EV charging is also available in selected car parks using the SEE-V app at 55p per KW. You will need to pay for both the car park charge and EV charge separately. All parking charges are paid through Pay By Phone by using the app, website or by phoning 0330 400 7275 Charges apply per hour You will need the location code which you can find on signage within the car park You will need a valid credit or debit card and your vehicle registration number Public parking is not allowed between 7.00am – 4.00pm Monday - Friday to prioritise staff permit holders For more support with payments visit paybyphone.co.uk All Tariffs expire at 7.00am Same charges as above All tariffs expire Monday 7.00am Payment can be made using the Pay By Phone app, website or by phoning 0330 400 7275 Vehicles can be parked for up to 15 hours as long as they are not during permit hours. Please refer to charges above for parking rates. Those without University parking permits are able to park using the pay as you go tariff at the following times: Monday - Friday from 4.00pm to 7.00am the following day Friday from 4.00pm until Monday at 7.00am You can easily extend your parking session by using the same method you used previously to pay for your parking. Simply select ‘extend’ and pay for the additional time you wish to use. There are 17 pay and display car parks as listed below. Car parks available for public parking will have relevant signage. Ravelin Sports Centre car park is for sports centre use only. The car parks are monitored by ANPR and failure to comply with terms and conditions of the car park will result in a £70 parking charge. You will be charged for the full pre-paid session whether you stay for the full session or not. This applies even if you move your car to a different car park as you would still pay for the previous session before it runs out and will need to pay for the new session in the new car park. Support with the app can be found at paybyphone.co.uk. Yes, you are welcome to use the EV parking spaces as long as you park outside of the permit hours listed above. There is an additional charge for using the chargers which is separate to the parking charges. To use the charging stations simply download the SEE-V app, register and set up payment details, plug in and start charging. Charging will cost 55p per KW and drivers will need to move the vehicle once charging is complete to avoid idle fees. Please note that vehicles cannot be left in charging bays for long term or overnight parking. Below is a map of the University car parks with total spaces, disabled bays, and EV charging points. Anglesea Front Car Park Anglesea Rear Car Park Dorothy Dymond Car Park Harry Law Underground Car Park Langstone Car Park E F Langstone Car Park East Langstone Car Park Main Langstone Car Park West Melbourne Place Car Park Park Building Car Park Ravelin House Car Park Ravelin Sports Centre Car Park Spinnaker Building Car Park University House Car Park William Beatty Car Park If you're travelling to Portsmouth from one of the major London airports, taxis are fairly expensive, costing between £100 and £200. For this reason, we don't recommend taking a taxi for your journey. If you do choose to take a taxi, you should book it in advance to get the best offers possible. Once you arrive in Portsmouth, taxis are common and journeys will generally cost no more than £15 to £20. Regular daily direct coach services run to Portsmouth from London, Bristol, Birmingham, Heathrow and other locations around the country. All these services call at The Hard Interchange by Portsmouth Harbour railway station, which is a 15-minute walk from our Guildhall campus. You can find out coach times and routes with National Express and megabus. For all international connections, we’re only 1 hour 30 minutes from both Heathrow and Gatwick airports. Southampton International – which has regular flights to Paris, Cherbourg, the Channel Islands, Belfast, Glasgow and more – is just 30 minutes by car and easy to reach by train too. There are daily sailings between Portsmouth and Caen, Le Havre and St Malo in France. There are also twice-weekly services to Bilbao and Santander in Spain, and regular services to Jersey and Guernsey. There are crossings each day to the Isle of Wight too, by Hovercraft and passenger/car ferry. A passenger ferry also runs across the harbour to Gosport. Our University bus is available during term-time. The circular route runs from Monday to Friday, close to where many students live in Southsea. Portsmouth has excellent city bus services too. You can catch local and intercity buses from the main bus station at The Hard Interchange, next to Portsmouth Harbour railway station. There is also a park and ride just off Junction 1 of the M275. Portsmouth Park and Ride is a convenient and sustainable way to travel into the city. The daily PR1 bus service runs every 15 minutes to The Hard Interchange via the city centre. During summer weekends, an additional route operates to Southsea. Park and ride fares are great value, with flexible ticket options available for frequent travellers. Passengers can hop on and off throughout the day, and tickets can easily be purchased either on the bus or via the First Bus app. Portsmouth is fairly flat and there's plenty to see, so the best way to discover the city is on foot or by bike. You’ll find plenty of footpaths and cycleways to follow across the city. Whether you’re looking for information about local cycle routes, walking maps, or links to public transport you can plan your journey using My Journey Hampshire. Blue badge holders can park free of charge in most Portsmouth City Council car parks and most on-street parking spaces. If you have special requirements, please contact us. Are you looking for a building, facility or service but don't know where to go? Find the addresses and opening hours of our buildings here. We may use cookies to analyse visitor data, improve our website, show personalised content and give you a great website experience. For more information about cookies on our site and to tailor the cookies we collect, select ‘Manage settings’.

body: With students arriving at the University of Portsmouth from over 120 countries each year, and the University's commitment to widening participation in higher education attracting an ever-increasing variety of students - and indeed staff - understanding and accommodating their needs is essential to its continued success. Read more about the wide variety of work the Equality and Diversity team undertakes to achieve this and the members of the Equality and Diversity Team. title: Accident reporting
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Accident and Incident Reporting Introduction Reporting Lifts Further Information Explore
body: An Accident is an unplanned event that results in injury or ill-health. An incident may be a near miss or undesired cirumstances that have the potential to cause injury, ill-health damage or loss. The law requires that certain work-related incidents are reported to the local authority or the Health and Safety Executive. Near Miss: An event that has occurred without causing harm, but it had the potential to cause injury, ill health or damageUndesired Circumstance/Hazard: A set of conditions or circumstances and sources that have the potential to cause injury, ill health and damage. The investigation of near-misses and undesired circumstances are an important step in accident prevention. These terms does not include Dangerous Occurrences which are certain unintended, specified events which may not result in a reportable injury but do have the potential to cause significant harm. These are specifically identified in Schedule 2 of the Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences Regulations (RIDDOR) 2013, and is reportable to the enforcing authority. See the Accident Reporting Policy for details. All accident and incidents , however minor, should be recorded and reported to the Health & Safety Office by submitting a HS1 Form. These are now available on service manager/ hornbill via the Health and Safety pages. If you are unable to access please contact the Helath and safety office by emailing hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk. If there is any concern that health is affecting work or that work is affecting health as a result of any incident at work, please talk to your line manager about a referral to Occupational Health. More information on the referral process can be found here. For more information, please see the University Accident Reporting Policy. In the event of a lift failure staff, students and visitors should follow instructions that are posted inside all lifts for summoning help. Lift failure incidents are not usually classed as an accident or near miss and are reported to Estates and Campus Services via the Estates Helpdesk. However, a HS1 form submission will be necessary if problems arise that either lead to injury or ill health or are considered to have the potential to have done so. title: University Staff Apprenticeship Scheme
headings: Human Resources University Staff Apprenticeship Scheme Explore
body: In support of our training and development of staff, the University provides opportunities for existing staff to undertake apprenticeship programmes, subject to the approval and commitment of their line manager. Apprenticeships are funded by the Apprenticeship Levy and offer University staff the opportunity to work and be paid while learning and working towards completion of a recognised qualification. Intermediate and Advanced level apprenticeships can offer skills and knowledge to support you in your role, developing your existing skills and enhance your effectiveness. These apprenticeships are available in any sector or industry and the University HR Apprenticeship Team will source an appropriate training provider to deliver the training. Degree apprenticeships were launched by the Government in April 2015 and provide the opportunity for staff to gain a degree as part of their apprenticeship training. The University of Portsmouth currently offer a range of Degree Apprenticeship programmes including Master’s level, and plans are underway to increase our offering in future. Find out more Apprenticeships – What are they and how do they work? All apprenticeships are work-based and therefore require the support of your line manager. While working towards an apprenticeship, staff will be required to spend 20% of their working hours on off-the-job learning and line managers will need to facilitate this. Additionally, an appropriate mentor will need to be identified to support staff throughout the apprenticeship programme to ensure learning is effective. UoP Degree ApprenticeshipsNon-Degree ApprenticeshipsAcademic Professional ApprenticeshipApplication process for the University’s Degree Apprenticeship SchemeCourse requirements The HR Apprenticeship Team manage applications for all University staff wishing to undertake any apprenticeships: Apprenticeship Coordinator: Penny Buckmaster Apprenticeship HR Lead: Helen Keetch To contact the team, please email: staff-apprenticeships@port.ac.uk. title: Promotion to Associate Professor and Professor
headings: Human Resources Promotion to Associate Professor and Professor Overview Who does this apply to? What do I need to consider? What do I need to do? What else do I need to know? Explore
body: This guidance provides an overview of the process for preparing and evaluating applications to Associate Professor and Professor, and providing feedback to applicants. The University awards the title of Associate Professor or Professor on the basis of exceptional performance and externally-recognised distinction. The process applies only to Associate Professor and Professor, and not to other grading activity under Higher Education Role Analysis (HERA). The award is made by the Vice-Chancellor who will take advice from Faculty and University Promotions Committees, internal and external assessors, and referees. Professors are banded according to the level at which they are working and their contribution to the University. Band 1 will normally be for both internal and external candidates promoted or appointed to Professors. Band 2 is the appropriate Band for Professors who are able to demonstrate academic standing and distinction that is significantly greater than that required for promotion or appointment to Band 1. Band 3 is for the small number of professors who are able to demonstrate and sustain an unusually highly accomplished track record of academic achievement and leadership. Both Associate Professor and Professors are expected to make a full contribution to all aspects of University activity. Applications for Professorial re-banding are biennial, the application round for re-banding will open again in autumn 2026. Promotion to Associate Professor or Professor is based on the applicant demonstrating evidence across a range of attributes of academic leadership and citizenship. In addition, a successful candidate will have demonstrated evidence of excellence in at least one of enterprise and innovation, research, teaching and education, or across a combination of areas. Both full-time and part-time colleagues are eligible to apply for promotion. The normal expectation is that professorial applications will only come from colleagues who are already Associate Professors. However, if an applicant feels that this normal route is not appropriate in their case they should consult with BOTH their Head of School (or equivalent) and must consult with the Executive Dean of their Faculty. We particularly welcome applications from Women staff and those from ethnically diverse backgrounds. The attributes applicants will be expected to demonstrate and evidence are described in the Promotion to Associate Professor and Professor Policy. Those considering making an application must read and familiarise themselves with the Promotion to Associate Professor and Professor Policy. You should also consult with BOTH your Head of School (or equivalent) and must consult with the ExecutiveDean of your Faculty, failure to do so will result in the application not being progressed further. Non-faculty-based colleagues holding academic contracts should consult with their line manager about the most appropriate faculty through which to submit their application. All applications must conform to the 2025-2026 academic promotions round key dates and deadlines listed below and must be submitted via ESS (current vacancies/internal staff job board), you will need to attach the following documents (which must be converted to PDF): Referee and External Assessor Nomination Form External Income Table (as appropriate) Please ensure that you select the correct link for your faculty and the position that you are applying for e.g. BaL Promotion to Professor. By 23:59 pm Wednesday 7 January 2026 By 23:59 pm Wednesday 7 January 2026 Closing date for applications Closing date for applications By Friday 23 January 2026 By Friday 23 January 2026 Heads of School reviews application and provides and evaluative statement. Heads of School reviews application and provides and evaluative statement. Faculty Promotion Committees: Humanities and Social Sciences – Monday 16 February Technology – Tuesday 17 February Creative and Cultural Industries –Wednesday 18 February Business and Law – Thursday 19 February Science and Health – Wednesday 25 February Faculty Promotion Committees: Humanities and Social Sciences – Monday 16 February Technology – Tuesday 17 February Creative and Cultural Industries –Wednesday 18 February Business and Law – Thursday 19 February Science and Health – Wednesday 25 February February to April 2026 February to April 2026 Assessors and referees reports requested by Provost as agreed with Faculty Promotion Committees Assessors and referees reports requested by Provost as agreed with Faculty Promotion Committees Wednesday 27 May 2026 Wednesday 27 May 2026 University Promotion Committee - Applications reviewed University Promotion Committee - Applications reviewed Professorial Interviews: Tuesday 17 June Friday 19 June Monday 22 June Friday 26 June Professorial Interviews: Successful candidates transfer to their new position Successful candidates transfer to their new position The University recommends that all potential applicants read the Promotion to Associate Professor and Professor Policy before proceeding with their application. What else do I need to know? You may also wish to attend one of the Promotion to Associate Professor or Professor Information Sessions. These sessions are aimed at sharing information and guidance on preparing an application for promotion to Associate Professor or Professor and provide an opportunity to learn more about the role and expectations of Associate Professors and Professors within the University of Portsmouth, directly from the Provost, Professor Sherria Hoskins, who will outline the procedure and process of applying for promotion and highlight any changes to the policy/criteria for 2025/26. We will be running two promotion information sessions, which will be held on: Wednesday 29th October (online - this session will be recorded for anyone unable to attend either session) 10 am - 12 noon Thursday 30th October (in person) 11 am - 1 pm My Role My Responsibilities My Benefits My Pay Leave and Absence Managing My Contract Developing My Career Ensuring My Health, Safety and Wellbeing Dealing With Change Ensuring My Health, Safety and Wellbeing HR Business Partnering Team Learning and Development My Rewards and Benefits Occupational Health Service Equality and Diversity Contact and departments

title: Association of Higher Education Professionals (AHEP)
headings: Association of Higher Education Professionals (AHEP) AHEP at the University of Portsmouth
body: The Association of Higher Education Professionals (AHEP) aims to promote excellence in HE management and administration and to raise the profile of those roles in HE as a profession through the development of best practice and high standards in the area. Not only are they a representative voice for HE Professionals in the sector, they also provide support and guidance for members in developing your their careers. Find out more here. The AHEP core commitment for members are to: Empower your career journey Improving your professional practice Supporting your professional development Championing the vaules of: Credible, Collaborative, Inclusive and Personable They provide a Professional Framework linked to 8 key professional behaviours, which can be used whatever your role or level. Membership is open to everyone, whether you are and existing or aspiring HE Professional. Click here to find out more about why you should join, or here to explore the membership levels available. To find out more check out the National Events and local activities via our Padlet AUA@UoP Community. If you have any questions or would like to find out more, please contact the UoP Branch Advocate: Nikki Sowerby. Nikki is also the South Region Network Deputy Coordinator. Contact and departments

title: Athena Swan
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Athena Swan What is Athena Swan? Our Athena Swan Journey Athena Swan Principles Explore
body: The Athena Swan Charter is a national charter mark that recognises the advancement of gender equality in academia. It encompasses representation, progression, and success for all. Established in 2005, it set out to encourage and recognise commitment to advancing women's careers in science, technology, engineering, maths, and medicine (STEMM) employment in higher education and research. Expanded in 2015, it also covers gender equality in the arts, humanities, social sciences, business, and law (AHSSBL) disciplines. It also focuses on professional and support colleagues working in the higher education sector and on the issues facing all genders, including trans & gender non-conforming colleagues and students. The University initially received an Athena Swan Bronze Award in November 2014. In April 2018, we retained it, recognising our commitment to gender equality. Lastly, we succeeded in receiving our Athena Swan Silver Award in 2022. Our submission was developed across the university at all levels, including academic, research, professional, and support colleagues. This award gives us just a few years to complete the vital work developed in the Athena Swan Silver Action Plan. If you have any questions about Athena Swan please contact the Equity, Wellbeing & Inclusion Team (equality@port.ac.uk). In supporting the Athena Swan Charter, the University of Portsmouth has signed up for the 8 key principles; we embed these principles within our policies, practices, action plans, and culture. We commit to: Adopting robust, transparent and accountable processes for gender equality work, including: Embedding diversity, equity and inclusion in our culture, decision-making and partnerships, and holding ourselves and others in our institution/institute/department accountable Undertaking evidence-based, transparent self-assessment processes to direct our priorities and interventions for gender equality, and evaluating our progress to inform our continuous development Ensuring that gender equality work is distributed appropriately, is recognised and properly rewarded. Embedding diversity, equity and inclusion in our culture, decision-making and partnerships, and holding ourselves and others in our institution/institute/department accountable Undertaking evidence-based, transparent self-assessment processes to direct our priorities and interventions for gender equality, and evaluating our progress to inform our continuous development Ensuring that gender equality work is distributed appropriately, is recognised and properly rewarded. Addressing structural inequalities and social injustices that manifest as differential experiences and outcomes for staff and students. Tackling behaviours and cultures that detract from the safety and collegiality of our work and study environments for people of all genders, including not tolerating gender-based violence, discrimination, bullying, harassment or exploitation. Understanding and addressing intersectional inequalities. Fostering collective understanding that individuals have the right to determine their own gender identity, and tackling the specific issues faced by trans and non-binary people because of their identity. Examining gendered occupational segregation, and elevating the status, voice and career opportunities of any identified under-valued and at-risk groups. Mitigating the gendered impact of caring responsibilities and career breaks, and supporting flexibility and the maintenance of a healthy 'whole life balance'. Mitigating the gendered impact of short-term and casual contracts for staff seeking sustainable careers. title: Driving
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Driving Explore
body: The Driving on University Business Procedure is designed to secure a healthy and safe working environment for everyone who drives a motor vehicle as part of their employment or study and to ensure compliance with current legal requirements. It includes the use of vehicles owned, leased or hired by the University and vehicles owned by staff or students when used to conduct University business. Areas of responsibilities are defined and all staff should make themselves aware of their responsibilities. Managers are responsible for ensuring annual driver declaration - licence verifications are completed in accordance with the procedure. The process in Appendix 1 should be followed. The annual driver declaration - licence verification should be completed and in place where required through the Corporate Health and Safety self Service Portal within Service Manager. On approval and authorisation the driver will be placed on the Record of University Authorised Drivers. Estates and Campus Services will continue to process requests for hire vehicles through transport.bookings@port.ac.uk. Requests will only be processed if an annual driver declaration - licence verification is in place and in date. title: Emeritus Professorships
headings: Human Resources Procedure for conferring the title of Emeritus Professor Introduction Criteria Procedure for award of Emeritus Title Rescission of an Emeritus title Explore
body: The award of Emeritus status is exceptional in nature and is conferred upon someone to recognise their sustained, distinguished service to the University and their field of study and their ability to continue on this trajectory as an Emeritus. The status is considered to be mutually beneficial and facilitates a continuing relationship, on a voluntary basis, between the retiree and their faculty. An Emeritus Professor is not required to exercise any teaching, research or supervisory role or any administrative, executive or representative function. The conferment of the title Emeritus Professor does not imply any continuing status as an employee or officer of the University, but there is an expectation that the Emeritus Professor will wish to continue a voluntary and unpaid association with the teaching and/or research activities of the University and that may include the title ‘Emeritus Professor of the University of Portsmouth’ in publications. Any award of the title of Emeritus Professor is made entirely at the discretion of the University. The title of Emeritus Professor may be awarded to a Professor who is leaving the University to retire and: who has made a substantial contribution to the reputation of the University; who has a sustained and distinguished service to their field of study; who has been in continuous employment with the University for at least five years before retirement ; With whom the University would like a continuing association by participating, on a voluntary basis, in activities of the University. No professor shall be awarded an Emeritus title if any of the following apply to them: At the date they leave the University they have a live disciplinary record or are leaving the University with a disciplinary investigation pending. Are guilty of any fraud or dishonesty or act in any manner which in the University’s opinion brings or is likely to bring the University into disrepute or is materially adverse to the University’s interests or business. There are or have been concerns regarding behaviour contrary to UoP values. Where the Executive determines that it would not be in the interests of the Faculty to have an ongoing relationship with the academic in question. Recommendations can be made by: the Executive Dean of the Faculty in which the Professor worked and the appropriate Head of School or equivalent. the Provost or a Deputy Vice-Chancellor. The recommendation should outline the Professor's key achievements during their time at UoP and be submitted to the Vice Chancellor’s Office six months either side of the individual’s retirement date together with the Professor’s CV. The Vice-Chancellor will give each case careful consideration and will write to the individual to confirm the outcome, if Emeritus status is awarded, the Vice Chancellor’s Office will advise HR who will create an honorary emeritus record. Emeritus status commences the day after a professor retires. The Emeritus title is conferred in perpetuity and is not remunerated. It does not have the status of either a member of staff or student at the University and the University retains the right to suspend or remove the title if: there is serious misconduct. if there is a conflict of interest which arises, or it is considered that damage may be caused to the reputation of the University by continued association. Any conferment of the title will be notified to Academic Council. In accepting the Emeritus title, retirees will agree to observe the University’s policies and procedures, protect the reputation of the University, and conduct themselves in a manner consistent with the University’s values and behaviours. They will be required to comply with the relevant policies and procedures, which relate to their activities. Emeritus professors will retain their existing IT and email accounts and full library membership. The Emeritus Professor may relinquish their emeritus title at any time by writing to their Head of School. The award of (or rescission of) an emeritus title is entirely at the discretion of the University. A decision to rescind an emeritus title will normally be made by the Provost on the recommendation of Head of School, and/or Executive Dean and/or a Deputy Vice-Chancellor. In the event of rescission, the Executive Dean will notify the emeritus academic in writing of the decision to rescind the emeritus title. title: Visa break for Skilled Workers
headings: Human Resources Visa brake for Skilled Workers Explore
body: The UK government is introducing a restriction that refuses visas for people applying for certain UK visas on specific routes and for specific nationalities. This is known as a visa brake. Taking effect on 26 March 2026. This visa brake will apply to Afghan nationals only, applying overseas and intending to apply for a Skilled Worker visa only. If an Afghan national is already in the UK, on a valid visa route, they will be able to apply for an in-country extension or move to another visa route if eligible. The visa brake is not permanent, and will be reviewed by the government regularly. Please refer the the FAQs for more information. If you have any questions or concerns please contact the Compliance Team via the People Hub. title: Biological Safety
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Explore
body: Biological Safety Training Christina Scott, Biological Safety Officer Pre-requisite for anyone with responsibilities in biological laboratories; particularly those working in CL2 laboratories. Aspirational for staff and students who wish to widen their knowledge as part of development. This training will give participants an understanding of: - Why biological safety matters - How to contain biological agents - Risk assessment process relating to biological hazards As a result of attending, participants will be able to: - identify the legislation applicable to the safe use of biological agents - outline the requirements of containment level laboratories - outline the hazards associated with the biological agents they handle - outline the control measures that may be used. title: Biosafety Awareness
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Explore
body: Christina Scott, Biological Safety Officer New staff and post-graduate students working in biological laboratories or with potentially infectious material. title: COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety COSHH - Safe Use of Chemicals Explore
body: Pre-requisite for Technicians who use chemicals in their work and aspirational for Technical Staff who wish to widen their knowledge as part of development. This training will give participants an understanding of: - the legislative requirements for the use of chemicals and substances hazardous to health in the workplace. As a result of attending, participants will: - have awareness of the legislation that applies to the use of chemicals and substances hazardous to health - be able to outline the classifications of hazardous substances and associated ill-health risks - know what to include and where to find the relevant information for your COSHH risk assessments - be able to identify suitable and sufficient control measures and how to maintain them - understand when exposure monitoring and health surveillance is required - understand the requirements for emergency planning Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Genetic Modification Safety Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Safe Use of Compressed Gas Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Training Explore
body: Pre-requisite for Technicians who work with chemicals. This training will give participants an understanding of: - Impact of chemical spillages on the University - Overview of the relevant legislation - Risk assessment which includes identifying chemical hazards, routes of entry and people at risk - Procedure for dealing with chemical spillages - Control measures for chemical spillages As a result of attending, participants will be able to: - Explain why dealing with spillages correctly is important and the consequences of not doing so - Identify the legislation relevant to spillages of hazardous materials. - Outline the hazards associated with chemicals that are used on campus. - Outline the procedures for dealing with spillages. title: Drone Training
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Explore
body: Uncrewed Aerial Systems (UAS) Training Martin Schaefer, UAS Safety Officer Toby Meredith, UAS Safety Officer - Staff and PhD students wishing to operate Uncrewed Aerial Systems on University business - Staff who manage staff likely to operate Uncrewed Aerial Systems on University business - Staff who are required to sign off risk assessments This course is a prerequisite to be allowed to operate Uncrewed Aerial Systems (UAS) on University business under the UAS Arrangement. It is designed to give participants the required knowledge to operate UAS within the scope defined by legislation and the UAS arrangement and to operate UAS safely. Please note that this does not constitute training as defined by the CAA, and additional external qualifications may be required depending on the Uncrewed Aerial Vehicle and environment of operation. As a result of attending, participants will be able to: - Identify different types of UAS and what they can be used for - Identify current UK laws and regulations on operating UAS - Identify where you can and can't fly a UAS - Outline the requirements to operate a UAS in the UK - Outline the University structures that manage UAS operations in the UK and abroad - Outline the University staff and student policies on operating UAS on University business - Outline record keeping requirements as set out in the policies - Outline UAS incident reporting - Plan a UAS operation in the Open Category This course is based on independent online learning (Moodle), including a quiz, and a 3 hour in person workshop. Completing the online component is a prerequisite for the workshop. title: DSE Online training
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Display Screen Equipment Online Training and Risk Assessment Explore
body: The Health and Safety Office provide online Display Screen Equipment training and risk assessment. The objective is to provide computer users with training in accordance with Health and Safety legislation, Approved Codes of Practice and HSE guidelines. The target audience is all University of Portsmouth members of staff who are computer users. The duration to carry out the online training is approximately 20 minutes in total and a quiz then needs to be completed. The online DSE training can be accessed on Docebo, as part of your Core Training Learning Plan. This training needs to be completed every 3 years or sooner if your circumstances change. i.e: new computer equipment, new furniture (desk/chair), change in location/building, change in desk setup. If you have any queries or require any further information, please email hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk. Please also see this Occupational Health webpage containing health and wellbeing resources for staff working at home. University of Portsmouth Eyecare Information Ext 3075 Email hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk Computer problems, software settings for mouse, etc University of Portsmouth Information Services Ext 7777 Email servicedesk@port.ac.uk Concerns relating to your DSE work Ext 3264 Email hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk Concerns/health issues relating to your DSE work University of Portsmouth Occupational Health Service Queries regarding this training package Email hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk Comments and Concerns Charlotte Downs in Health and Safety is the administrator for this package. You, as the individual need to take responsibility for resolving your Display Screen Equipment concerns. If you are unable to resolve any concerns yourself you should then discuss them with your immediate line manager in the first instance. If your manager is unable to resolve concerns then you or they may contact the next relevant department, eg Information Services, Health and Safety or Occupational Health. Charlotte Downs in Health and Safety is the administrator for this package. You, as the individual need to take responsibility for resolving your Display Screen Equipment concerns. If you are unable to resolve any concerns yourself you should then discuss them with your immediate line manager in the first instance. If your manager is unable to resolve concerns then you or they may contact the next relevant department, eg Information Services, Health and Safety or Occupational Health. title: Fire Awareness
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Online Fire Training How often should the on-line Fire Safety training be completed? Explore
body: It is a legal requirement (Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005) that employers must provide appropriate information, instruction and training to employees, during their normal working hours, about the fire precautions and the fire equipment in the workplace. Confirmation of how the University responds to these specific requirements is shown below in our FAQ section. For access to the Online Fire Safety Training please follow this link to Docebo and select My Learning Plans. You will then be asked to enrol. This training comprises of a course and an assessment. The training will not be registered as complete unless both elements have been completed. For assistance with the Online Training please contact hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk. Frequently Asked Questions How often should the on-line Fire Safety training be completed? The on-line Fire Safety training should be refreshed every 3 years or sooner if your circumstances change. i.e: if you move buildings/area within the building. title: First Aid
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety First Aid Training Programme Explore
body: Refresher Training Workshops - Refresher training sessions are open to all First Aiders who wish to practice unused skills or discuss relevant First Aid topics. We advise First Aiders to attend at least one workshop within the 3 year period between courses as part of the Annual Refresher Training, but these are optional. A certificate will be issued on completion of the workshop. title: Manual Handling
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Explore
body: - Members of staff who carry out manual handling tasks This training will give participants an understanding of: - The Manual Handling Operations Regulations and other relevant Health & Safety legislation - The University’s Policy and Procedures in relation to manual handling - Risk Factors, Assessment and Control Measures On completion of this course, candidates should be able to: - understand the legislation covering manual handling operations at work - understand manual handling risk factors - appreciate the need for a risk assessment to be completed - comply with the control measures - be aware of the mechanical aids available - understand efficient movement principles Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Genetic Modification Safety Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Safe Use of Compressed Gas Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Mental Health First Aid
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Explore
body: Health, Safety and Compliance Team Members of staff interested in delivering mental health first aid support to University colleagues and students As an MHFAider you will be able to: Recognise those that may be experiencing poor mental health and provide them with first-level support and early intervention Encourage a person to identify and access sources of professional help and other supports Practise active listening and empathy Have a conversation with improved mental health literacy around language and stigma Discuss the MHFAider role in depth, including boundaries and confidentiality Know how to use the MHFAider Support App Know how to access a dedicated text service provided by Shout and ongoing learning opportunities with MHFA England As a result of attending, participants will be qualified Mental Health First Aiders. title: Pressure Systems Safety
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Pressure Systems Safety Explore
body: Pre-requisite for Technicians who work with pressure systems Aspirational for Technical staff who wish to widen their knowledge as part of development To provide candidates with an understanding in the following subject areas: - Overview of the Pressure Equipment Regulations 1999 & the Pressure Systems Safety Regulations 2000 - Types of failure in pressure systems - Inspection and maintenance of pressure systems - Outline the main requirements for relevant legislation - Outline the main features and safety accessories associated with pressure systems - Identify pressure systems that can be found within the University - Outline how the legislation applies to the purchasing of new and pre owned equipment - Outline procedures for operating, maintaining and testing of pressure systems and equipment - Outline the modes of failure for pressure systems and equipment - Outline methods to prevent failure Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Genetic Modification Safety Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Safe Use of Compressed Gas Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Risk Assessment
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Introduction Template and Guidance New and Expectant Mothers Risk Assessment Young Persons Risk Assessment Risk Assessment Training Further Risk Assessment Guidance Explore
body: Risk assessments are an important step in protecting the University and its employees, as well as complying with the law. The process involves identifying the risks in the workplace with the potential to cause real harm, to evaluate whether enough precautions have been taken to prevent this harm, or whether more needs to be done. Accidents and ill health can ruin lives and affect the University as well if output is lost, equipment is damaged, insurance costs increase, or the University is taken to court. It is a legal requirement to assess risks in the workplace to protect staff, students and others that can be affected, from the work undertaken by the University. The Health, Safety and Compliance Team have created the following risk assessment template and guidance to help with the risk assessment process: Risk Assessment Template Risk Assessment Guidance Method Statement Form New and expectant mothers are defined as those who are pregnant, breastfeeding, or have given birth in the previous 6 months. Under regulation 16 of the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations, a specific risk assessment may be required to ensure the safety of new and expectant mothers whilst at work. Consider the following: Review existing risk assessments and controls Consult with the individual to identify specific conditions and circumstances If further controls are required, a risk assessment will need to be created Young persons are defined as those under the age of 18. Under regulation 19 of the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations, a specific risk assessment may be required to ensure the safety of young persons whilst at work. Consider the following: The individual may have a greater lack of experience and awareness of risks Young persons may require additional health and safety training for certain activities In some cases, it may not be suitable for young persons to take part in work involving particular risks Everyone involved in the risk assessment process should receive suitable training. To book onto a course, please see our training calendar below for dates, and email hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk to book a space. If you require further guidance completing a risk assessment from the University's Health, Safety and Compliance Team, please contact hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk. The Health and Safety Executive has also produced the following guidance documents for risk assessments: HSE - What is a risk assessment? HSE - Managing risks in the workplace This information can also be found via the following link: Please contact the Health and Safety Office if you require further information Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Genetic Modification Safety Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Safe Use of Compressed Gas Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Safe Use of Compressed Gas
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Safe Use of Compressed Gas – Theory and Practical Explore
body: Technical, Academic and Research staff who use Compressed Gases. To give members of staff a greater understanding of the hazards associated with compressed gases and how to comply with the Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations (DSEAR) 2002 and the Control of Substances Hazardous to Health 2002 (COSHH). The practical session will give candidates the opportunity to install gas handling equipment, such as 2 stage pressure reduction heads and flame arresters. This training will give participants an understanding of: - A legislative overview - The properties of gases - Types of cylinders and how to identify them - Hazards associated with compressed gases - Principles of combustion, including oxygen enrichment - Requirements of relevant legislation The practical session will include manual handling of gas cylinders, connection and testing of gas handling equipment, maintenance and use of personal protective equipment. As a result of attending, participants will be able to: - Know the properties and classification of compressed gas - Identify the different types of compressed gas and cylinders - Identify the health hazards associated with compressed gases - Identify potential fuel and ignition sources - Select suitable control measures - Outline the requirements of legislation, including DSEAR and COSHH in relation to compressed gases - Safely install and test gas handling equipment Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Genetic Modification Safety Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Safe Use of Compressed Gas Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Working at Height
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Explore
body: All University staff in particular members of staff who routinely work at height. The Health and Safety Executive (HSE) definition of working at height: "...a place is 'at height' if a person could be injured falling from it..." Statistics show that two thirds of all major injuries are caused by low falls (below 2m), therefore this course is aimed at people that also routinely work at low heights, such as using footstools and step ladders. This course is aimed at enabling those responsible for such work to understand what is required when carrying out such an assessment, i.e. legislative requirements and best practice. This training will give participants an understanding of: - working at heights and height awareness including the Legal Duties imposed by the Work at Height Regulations 2005. - the Risk Assessment process required by law for all work at height. - at the safe systems of work for working at height using the BS 8437 'Hierarchy of Protective Measures', include avoidance, safe places of work and appropriate work equipment. - the most common items of work equipment used within the University campus... their selection, classification and inspection, including ladders, step ladders and footstools. As a result of attending, participants will: - understand their responsibilities and duties under the Work at Height Regulations 2005. - be competent to work safely at height with strong hazard awareness and a sound theoretical knowledge and practical experience of assessing ladders, step ladders and footstools. - have the ability to assess the risks and requirements needed for safely working at height in their scope of work. - be able to select their job specific fall protection equipment with confidence. title: Intention to apply
headings: Research and Innovation Services Intention to apply Explore
body: This form should be completed by the Principal Investigator (PI) or similar, for all applications for external funding at the University of Portsmouth. Please provide as much information as you can. This information will be held in a database accessible by colleagues supporting research and innovation across the University and will be used to manage a smooth development and application process. After submitting you will receive an automated email with a copy of your responses. Intention to apply form Contact and departments

title: Information
headings: Estates and Campus Services Information Explore
body: To ensure that your internal and external post is dealt with quickly and efficiently, please make sure that the following points are observed: Indicate class/service on the top left hand corner of the envelope. Unless otherwise indicated mail will always be sent by the cheapest option. Be sure to imprint your departmental stamp on the reverse of the envelope. This will assist us should correspondence be returned to the University. To save time in the Post Room and to prevent any unnecessary work, please use an elastic band to separate 1st and 2nd class letters, ensuring that all of the envelopes are facing in the same direction. The earlier you get your external mail to the Post Room, the sooner it will be dealt with. Large postings delivered at the end of the day may not meet with the Royal Mail collection at 4.30 pm. Please inform the Post Room of any bulk posting (internal or external mail) at the earliest possible opportunity. Always use a name, department and building on internal mail. Failure to do so can result in delay. Bulk posting for internal mail should be sorted by department not building. Deliveries to Halls of Residence are made once a day. Please ensure that any correspondence for the Halls are placed in the internal mail and sorted into separate addresses. Do not send cash via the mail. Departments should make their own arrangements for all other courier services, however, please feel free to enquire about Parcelforce prices for comparative costs. PLEASE NOTE: In exceptional circumstances the Post room Supervisor will open 'returned' mail if unable to find address of original sender. If you have any questions concerning any of the above mentioned services, please do not hesitate to contact the Post Room. title: Parcelforce and target services information
headings: Estates and Campus Services Parcelforce Explore
body: We now have three methods of despatch for International Items. 1. GLOBAL EXPRESS (Replaces International Datapost) A premiun rate service with items being delivered the next working day by 10.30 to the U.S, 12 noon to Canada and from two working days to the rest of the world. Items for this service MUST be in the Post Room by 14.30 each day and MUST include recipients telephone number and details of contents. N.B This service also attracts a premium rate. If your department does not currently have a 13.00 pick-up, please contact the Post Room for an additional collection. 2. GLOBAL PRIORITY (New Service) From two working days to Europe and the U.S, and three working days to most other destinations. 3. EUROPRIORITY BUSINESS (Replaces Euro 48) A two day service to most European Countries. Please contact the Post room for prices and/or any restrictions on the above services All the above items must be placed in your UK Mailbags and NOT in the white Royal Mail polylopes, which are for STANDARD airmail items only. In addition, please include a 'Notification of Despatch Form' for each parcel. Please contact the Post Room to ensure your items are collected ready for same day despatch. title: Degree apprenticeships
headings: Learn and Earn Would you like to earn a salary while you get a Bachelor's or Master's degree, without paying tuition fees? Learn skills in high demand Types of employer you could work with Why do a degree apprenticeship? How to apply for degree apprenticeships Ask us anything about degree apprenticeships We use cookies
body: Earn a salary, get a degree, and pay no tuition fees Degree apprenticeships allow you to work as an employee while you study part time. They combine academic study from a traditional university degree with practical on-the-job experience and employment skills you need for a successful career. Browse our degree apprenticeships Degree apprenticeships are developed between the Government, employers and universities, so you’ll learn skills that will be in high demand in the future. They're suitable for anyone over 18, whether you've just finished school or college, or you're already well into your career. The Government and your employer pay your tuition fees, so you won’t need to take out a tuition fee loan. Degree apprenticeships take 1–5 years to complete, depending on the course you take. You'll typically go to university 1 day a week for around 30 weeks every year. On the weeks you don't go to university, you'll spend a minimum of 6 hours per week studying outside of work. The Government's Office for Students website provides further guidance on degree apprentices for students, potential apprentices and employers. We provide degree apprenticeships with various types of employer, from large multinational organisations to small local companies Previous employers include the Royal Navy, Portsmouth City Council, Hampshire County Council, Blanchard Wells, Estee Lauder, Mace, Osborne, Apollo Fire, Neilcott Constructions, Pfizer and Hampshire Chamber of Commerce. We regularly discuss new degree apprenticeships with employers. If you're interested in a role or subject not listed, get in touch with us and we can let you know if other programmes are on the horizon. Please contact us with your questions. Once we've received your message a member of our friendly team will get back to you as soon as possible. Email: degree-apprenticeship-office@port.ac.uk Phone: +44(0)23 9284 3865 We may use cookies to analyse visitor data, improve our website, show personalised content and give you a great website experience. For more information about cookies on our site and to tailor the cookies we collect, select ‘Manage settings’.

title: Accessibility
headings: Accessibility Standards compliance Access keys Text resizing Exceptions Accessibility software Explore
body: The University of Portsmouth is committed to increasing the accessibility of information to all users, including visitors with visual or motor impairments. This statement outlines our activities and achievements so far in increasing accessibility. Should you encounter any problems in accessing these pages, or you would like to suggest improvements, please contact interact@port.ac.uk. Our aim is for all pages within the domain www.port.ac.uk to comply with priority 1 and 2 guidelines of the World Wide Web Consortium’s (W3C) Web Accessibility Initiative (WAI). We are striving towards validating all pages as XHTML 1.0 Transitional, whilst using structured semantic markup, with CSS 2.1 for page design. The use of tables for non-tabular information is being phased out, however some may remain in the transitional period of changeover from immediately after site launch to early 2008. Nearly all modern browsers support jumping to key links by typing keys defined on the web site. On Windows, this is done by pressing ALT + an access key, on Macintosh, press Control + an access key. All pages on this site define the following access keys: Access key 1 - Home page. Access key 2 - Skip to content. Access key 4 - Put cursor in the search box. Access key 0 - Help/Accessibility details (this page). The text on this website is built to resize in order to be as flexible as possible to users with different needs. Text can be increased or decreased from your browser tool bar, or on Windows, if you have a mouse with a scroll wheel, you can hold down ALT and scroll the wheel backwards or forwards to alter the text size. Within these pages there are links to external sites that are not part of the www.port.ac.uk domain. The University of Portsmouth cannot guarantee the level of compliance of these external pages. We are currently undertaking a project to increase the accessibility of all our pages. At this moment in time some of our pages have not been improved and are therefore not as accessible as we would like. These pages include the University Library pages at www.port.ac.uk/Library and our Research groups under www.port.ac.uk/research. Browse Aloud free software that works with your browser to read aloud on Browse Aloud enabled websites (including this site) JAWS, a screen reader for Windows. Opera, a web browser with enhanced accessibility-related features which include text zooming, and the use of customised user stylesheets. Windows, Macintosh, Linux, versions available. Contact and departments
links: interact@port.ac.uk www.port.ac.uk www.port.ac.uk/Library www.port.ac.uk/research Browse Aloud JAWS Opera Accessibility Copyright Disclaimer Feedback Privacy and cookies Site Map UniversityofPortsmouth Home Students Staff Contact and departments Home Staff services Departments Resources © University of Portsmouth Cookie policy Modern Slavery Statement Privacy policy Website terms and conditions

title: Why choose Portsmouth?
headings: Discover all Portsmouth has to offer Portsmouth is a great place to live and study. Here’s the proof. More for your money Module choices Unique placements Wellbeing support Outstanding teaching Global opportunities We use cookies
body: We're a top 10 most affordable UK city for students Save on rent – rent in Portsmouth is 54% lower than London and 25% lower than Brighton. (Numbeo, 2025) Save on living costs – NatWest ranks Portsmouth as one of the top 10 most affordable student cities. (NatWest Student Living Index 2024) Save on transport – you can walk to any of our uni buildings in under 15 minutes. Even better, there’s a free uni bus to take you to all the popular student spots. See our student accommodation Our wide range of module options means you can choose what interests you the most You asked, we listened - module choice rated the highest factor in choosing Portsmouth Module choices are available on the majority of our undergraduate and postgraduate taught courses Portsmouth is the only UK university offering Connected Degrees We offer you the flexibility to choose when to take your placement: either before your final year or after you’ve completed your degree 3 years learning + 1 year earning, to suit you Explore Connected Degrees If you need it, our Student Wellbeing team is on hand to provide personal and emotional support, mental health support and counselling Our Residence Life team are always on hand to manage any welfare and concerns about living in halls of residence "The services that the uni offer are so valuable and I would recommend utilising them if you ever need to - everyone is really friendly, helpful and impartial and have a vested interest in your wellbeing." (Roxanne Smith, MSc Health Psychology student) "The services that the uni offer are so valuable and I would recommend utilising them if you ever need to - everyone is really friendly, helpful and impartial and have a vested interest in your wellbeing." (Roxanne Smith, MSc Health Psychology student) Discover our health and wellbeing support We're rated Gold in the latest Teaching Excellence Framework (TEF 2023) We're in the Top 20 in England for student satisfaction in academic support and learning resources (NSS 2023) 75 percent of our undergraduates are studying courses that are accredited by professional, statutory and regulatory bodies Our outstanding teaching We've welcomed over 5000 international students from over 150 countries QS World University Rankings give us 5 stars for teaching, employability, internationalisation, facilities, arts and culture, and inclusiveness (QS World University Rankings 2025: Top global universities) With International College Portsmouth (ICP) and the Navitas Ltd Australia group, we’ve established a pathway for students abroad to get a University of Portsmouth degree Learn more about our Global community We may use cookies to analyse visitor data, improve our website, show personalised content and give you a great website experience. For more information about cookies on our site and to tailor the cookies we collect, select ‘Manage settings’.
links: Skip to main content See our student accommodation Find your course Explore Connected Degrees Discover our health and wellbeing support Our outstanding teaching Learn more about our Global community Book an Open Day Chat to our students Life outside study

title: Email signature guidance
headings: Recruitment, Marketing and Communications How to create an email signature Example layout and content of university signature Example signature Explore
body: This document supports the Staff Email policy in providing information on what to include within an email signature and also how to add the signature and image. Open your Gmail in a new browser window and access your account settings by clicking on the gear image in the top righthand corner. Select 'See all settings'. Scroll down to the 'Signature' section and enter your new signature text in the box. You can format your text using the buttons directly below the text box. To add the image, click the 'Insert Image' icon below the signature text box. Insert the image file for the version of the signature you wish to use. If you wish to use the most up-to-date University footer, you can do so by selecting the 'Web Address (URL)' insert option and entering the following URL: https://storage.googleapis.com/edm-email-content/email-signature1.png and clicking 'Select'. Once the image is pasted into your signature, click the image and select 'original size'. This will ensure that when the image is updated in the future the image will not stretch. Click 'Save Changes' at the bottom of the ‘general settings’ page. title: Music
headings: Music Explore
body: We offer lots of ways to get involved in music-making at the University of Portsmouth. Our aim is to provide accessible and enjoyable learning opportunities for students, staff and members of the local community. We bring diverse groups of people together to participate in music in a number of different settings. We also help individuals to realise their musical potential. We embrace all levels from beginners to advanced performers and provide a supportive, friendly and socially vibrant environment to engage with music. We offer specialist facilities, a range of different ensembles, numerous performing and listening opportunities, links with the curriculum and specialist staff to support musical learning. We also offer a range of musical activities for students who are interested in music, regardless of which degree they are studying for. Groups on offer include Student Chorus, Choir, Orchestra, Concert Band and Big Band. In addition, some instruments are available for hire, and private tuition can be arranged for most instruments, as well as voice and music theory. We can also organise access to individual practice rooms. For more information visit our What We Offer page. To book tickets for all of our concerts please visit the University's online store. If you are interested in musical activities, please register online. Contact and departments

title: Mental Health
headings: Human Resources E-Learning package for staff to support students who may have mental health issues Thu, 25 May 2017 12:49:00 BST Explore
body: For students experiencing depression, anxiety, homesickness or low self-esteem, that first conversation can make all the difference. Developed by the Charlie Waller Memorial Trust, this new e-learning package is designed to give non-specialist staff the skills, knowledge and confidence to offer a first line of support to students who may have mental health issues. Introduced by well-known TV presenter Alexander Armstrong, the package consists of six, easy-to-follow 20-minute sessions, including: Signs to look out for Knowing who else to involve Supporting students at risk or in crisis HR Business Partnering Team Learning and Development My Rewards and Benefits Occupational Health Service Role of Occupational Health Referral Sickness absence Risk Assessment for New and Expectant Mothers Mental Health Bullying & Harassment Information Job Hazard Information Protocols and Guidance notes Stress information Job Functional Assessment - Information for Managers Research Passports Disability Guidance Access to Work PGCE applicants Tailored Adjustments Plan/Agreement Role of Occupational Health Risk Assessment for New and Expectant Mothers Bullying & Harassment Information Job Hazard Information Protocols and Guidance notes Job Functional Assessment - Information for Managers Tailored Adjustments Plan/Agreement Equality and Diversity Contact and departments
