title: Information Matters!
headings: Corporate Governance Information Matters IG eLearning Package - Core Training Forthcoming Events Google Housekeeping Update Webinars 2025 Records Management: What's the Point? eRecords - 6 simple steps to migrate your records Digital Housekeeping - Network Storage Edition Our Policies IG Group Further Info Posters Fun and Games Explore
body: That's the theme and the message of our awareness campaign. The value of well managed, accurate, timely and secure information can be overlooked, but it underpins so many of the services we provide and activities we undertake. Often, just a small improvement in the way information is treated can have a significant, positive effect on a student's experience of the University, or on the working lives of ourselves and colleagues. For more information, visit our Information Governance Homepage. Information Governance is now part of the Core Training Learning Plan on Docebo: Leave us feedback... help to improve the course Staff Induction Conferences Find us in the marketplace on 23rd April 2025 LIS have introduced a new process for requesting shared Google Drives. If you require a new shared drive, please complete this form. If you have any questions about clearing out your Google storage, we are still able to offer: Face to face meetings A guide containing hints and tips on clearing Google and a list of quick win emails Retention Schedule advice A walk through of the digital tools available in Google and Windows Explorer If you would like more information on any of the above, please contact us at recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk Please remember, LIS also offer guidance for clearing out your Google accounts and their page can be found here. Overview: When we are constantly being asked to do more with less, sometimes things get dropped... so we are going back to basics with this webinar that seeks to explain why records management is something you and your colleagues should still be taking seriously and answer the question "what's the point of records management?". What is it? Why should you do it? What happens when record keeping goes wrong? When we are constantly being asked to do more with less, sometimes things get dropped... so we are going back to basics with this webinar that seeks to explain why records management is something you and your colleagues should still be taking seriously and answer the question "what's the point of records management?". Why should you do it? What happens when record keeping goes wrong? Duration: 1hr Format: Zoom webinar Audience: All staff Presenter: Sarah Arnold, Stacey Langford Sessions: Join the wait list via the booking email below. Once we have sufficient numbers, further sessions will be set up. To Book: email recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk Presenter: Sarah Arnold, Stacey Langford Join the wait list via the booking email below. Once we have sufficient numbers, further sessions will be set up. To Book: email recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk Overview: As you housekeep your Google Drive, K:drive or N:Drives, you may identify University records that need to be migrated into the eRecords system. This session will outline an easy to follow approach to migrating records, to help staff understand what’s involved. This is to support you so that you can complete records migration with confidence. We will take you through a 6-step process to provide you with the skills and knowledge required to complete this necessary task. Following the session we will provide access to the presentation slides together with a migration checklist that you may find helpful. As you housekeep your Google Drive, K:drive or N:Drives, you may identify University records that need to be migrated into the eRecords system. This session will outline an easy to follow approach to migrating records, to help staff understand what’s involved. This is to support you so that you can complete records migration with confidence. We will take you through a 6-step process to provide you with the skills and knowledge required to complete this necessary task. Following the session we will provide access to the presentation slides together with a migration checklist that you may find helpful. Duration: 1hr Format: Google Meet Audience: All staff Presenter: Magda Gruszecka, Martin Talbot Sessions: Thursday 3rd April, 10:00-11:00 To Book: email erecords@port.ac.uk Presenter: Magda Gruszecka, Martin Talbot Thursday 3rd April, 10:00-11:00 To Book: email erecords@port.ac.uk Overview: Congratulations! We made it under the Google storage cap. Now we need to work at keeping ourselves under that limit and start turning our attention to our network storage, more commonly known as our K: and N:drives. This session will briefly recap tips on continue to clear email and Google drive but will be focusing on our network storage areas: The best ways to review our information, Identifying information that must be kept, Highlighting areas of quick wins to help see the wood through the trees, Recognising areas that may have been abandoned or inherited due to organisational change. We will also be discussing the tools at our disposal and some hints and tips to try to make the process a bit easier for everyone. Congratulations! We made it under the Google storage cap. title: eRecords
headings: Corporate Governance Electronic Records Management - eRecords Who has an eRecords account? eRecords access levels How do I access eRecords? On Campus Working From Home on a UoP Laptop Working From Home on a Personal PC or Mac: Viewing Documents Editing Documents Benefits of using eRecords Training eRecords Online Navigator and Super User training Scanning Paper Records Further Information Process Guides Other Trouble-shooting Timeout Error Objective Director Re-authentication Contact Us... eRecords Team contact details: Explore
body: Electronic Records Management is the process of managing the storage, organisation, access and retention of records using computer based systems. To support this process University of Portsmouth is currently using the eRecords system provided by Objective. It consists of two main components: a web client used by the majority of users and a desktop client called Navigator, both of which allow access to the eRecords system itself. eRecords provides the University with a structured, auditable method of managing its electronic records and ensuring we are using our electronic storage space in the most effective way. Prior to July 2025 all permanent and fixed-term contract staff were provided with an eRecords account as part of their overall University account profile. From July 2025 onwards, eRecords accounts are now only created upon request to the LIS Service Desk. Line managers can request an account for new starters prior to the joining date, so that it is available from the day they join the University. New and existing staff members requiring access can also request an account by contacting LIS Service Desk directly. Read-only access - provided by default upon eRecords account creation Edit access - this can be provided upon successful completion of eRecords online training. Super User access - this level of access is provided to staff who require to undertake additional responsibilities and/or tasks within the system. This access is only provided after prior discussion with the eRecords team. If you are on campus, but only connected via Eduroam, please ensure you are connected to the University VPN before you sign in to eRecords. Web browser apphttps://erecords.port.ac.ukNavigator (desktop client)Information Sheet If you experience difficulties, please refer to our "Troubleshooting" section below before you contact IS Service Desk. If you or your colleagues are working from home, you can still access the eRecords system. Ensure you are connected to the internet. Sign in to the University's VPN. You can now use eRecords exactly as you would on campus. Ensure you are connected to the internet. Sign in to the University's VPN. Go to https://erecords.port.ac.uk/ and sign in using your standard UoP username and password. You can now view eRecords. This solution will work on any device (including mobile devices) capable of having the VPN installed, however, please note that if you do not have MS Office installed on your device, the view provided may look a little different to expected, as it may not include the original formatting of the document. If you have edit access to eRecords and need to edit anything in the system, this can be done from a personal PC or Mac by following the advice in this guide. Please note: This guidance provides a technical solution for those without access to a UoP laptop PC. We strongly urge colleagues to pay very close attention to the security measures/housekeeping outlined in the guidance. If you have any further questions about using eRecords from home, please contact erecords@port.ac.uk. Easy to update records and always find the definitive version - when your document is updated, all existing links will automatically point to the new version. Provides Better Security - greater protection of sensitive records is guaranteed. This is supported by a comprehensive audit trail and robust version control. Ensures legal compliance - helps to ensure we are compliant with laws and regulations, such as GDPR and the FoI Act. Saves time and space - powerful search tools allow you to find records quickly and frees up valuable office space as compared to paper. eRecords is available as an eRecords online training course hosted by Moodle. Completing this course will enable you to become an eRecords Edit User. You can revisit the training at any time as a refresher. If you have difficulty accessing any of the content, please contact the eRecords team. Please contact eRecords team for details. If you have a large volume of paper records to be scanned and uploaded into the eRecords system, we can help make the scanning process quicker and easier! The eRecords scanning capability (EzeScan service) can be set up on your MFD, which will enable bulk scanning directly into eRecords, as well as OCR of typed paper records. Please contact the eRecords team to find out more. General information and best practice advice on scanning can be found in Records Management Factsheet 06 - Scanning. You are strongly advised to read this factsheet before beginning any scanning project. We have created guides for specific processes using both the web browser interface and the desktop client. Click here to access the full list of available guides. Factsheet 10 - eRecords The most common reason for anyone receiving a 'system timeout error' when attempting to sign in to eRecords is that you have forgotten to activate your VPN access. If you are in an environment that requires the VPN to be avalable (see "How do I access eRecords" above), please check the VPN is active on your device. If you are unable to open a document using the eRecords web browser, it may well be that you need to re-authenticate your Objective Director credentials. The first indicator will be this icon on your task bar: A window will pop up (please note this may be hidden behind other windows you have open) and you'll be asked to re-enter your eRecords account login details again. The document will then continue to download as expected. Email: erecords@port.ac.uk Magdalena Gruszecka x5495 For technical support and assistance, please contact the IS Service Desk on x7777 or via the IS Self-Service portal. title: FileStore
headings: Corporate Governance Key Account Contacts Packing Guidelines *** IMPORTANT *** ReStoreWeb Storage Management System Collection & Delivery Explore
body: ‌Departments that need to retain physical records, but do not have sufficient storage space, can transfer them off-site to ReStore Records Management (aka FileStore). FileStore is based in Southampton and provides a range of services, including: secure and confidential storage retrieval and delivery service confidential destruction For more information about using FileStore, please contact Sarah Arnold (University Records Manager) via recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk. Every University of Portsmouth account with FileStore has a designated key account contact. That person is responsible for: informing the University Records Manager when staff who have access to their department's FileStore account leave, or when new staff join who will need access disseminating FileStore-related communications from the University Records Manager to their colleagues as appropriate keeping account details (such as collection/delivery address(es) and log-ins) up to date with the University Records Manager keeping their account's holdings well managed and GDPR compliant To request that a new member of staff be given access to one of UoP's FileStore accounts, the key contact must email recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk with the person's name and UoP email address. If the account has more than one collection/delivery address associated with it, the preferred address for the new member of staff should also be stated. Under the terms of our contract with ReStore, all stored property must be packaged in accordance with good industry practice and good health & safety practice. The below link will take you to the packing guidelines in the eRecords System. You may be asked to enter your standard username and password: The below information is for internal University use only!(ReStore do not permit the dissemination of this information to third parties) ReStoreWeb is the name of the online system provided by FileStore which allows users to: submit / retrieve files manage files in storage To access ReStoreWeb, go to https://online5.restore.co.uk/RestoreWeb A full user guide is available on the home screen once you have logged in to ReStoreWeb. Please note: for security purposes ReStore will deactivate the account of any individual who has not logged into the system for 60 calendar days. If this happens to you, you will need to contact the Business Support team (contact details are linked from the ReStoreWeb log in screen) to get your account reactivated. If, after that, you still cannot log in, you may have to reset you password using the "forgot password" option, also linked from the ReStoreWeb log in screen. In order to save money and reduce our carbon footprint, UoP has agreed a weekly collection/delivery slot with ReStore. From the week commencing 13th November 2023: Any orders for collection/delivery placed before 3pm on the Wednesday will be actioned on the Thursday of the same week Any orders for collection/delivery placed after 3pm on the Wednesday will be actioned on the Thursday of the following week Any request for collection/delivery outside of this weekly slot will incur a significant charge and therefore can only be authorised by either Sarah Arnold or Stacey Langford. All requests must be made to recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk and include: the barcode(s) of the item(s) in question; the ReStore work order number (if you have it) and full justification for the request. title: Weeding Starter Pack
headings: Corporate Governance Why weed? Paper or electronic? Your weeding project - step by step Step 1 - Define your scope Step 2 - Get support and set a date Step 3 - Work out your retention periods Step 4 - Get set Step 5 - Go for it! Step 6 - Evaluate your success Step 7 - What happens next? Explore
body: ‌‌Weeding Starter Pack This is an intranet only page aimed at staff planning a local weeding exercise in the near future. If you require assistance with this, please contact recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk From March 2020 guidance links on this page will now take you into the eRecords system. If you do not yet have an eRecords account, this can be requested from IS Service Desk, ext-7777. Click here to read our paper on the benefits of weeding your files. Most of this starter pack relates to the weeding of paper files. However, the logic and principles also apply to weeding your K:drive, N:drive and other electronic storage locations, including email. Try to get a rough idea of the problem. What volume of files do you have? What type(s) of files are they? Where are they currently stored? Not only will this help you estimate how long the weeding will take and what resources you will need; but it can also help you to predict savings and benefits. A template for scoping is available from recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk Take note of lessons learnt from other teams who've already carried out a weeding project. A list of lessons learnt is available in the eRecords system and can be accessed here. You will probably need assistance from your colleagues to carry out the weeding. Although it is good for everyone to lend a hand, weeding files does require a specific mind set and some staff may find the disposal of any documentation a difficult and stressful task. Be mindful of this and try to avoid causing undue anxiety by ensuring that tasks are assigned to play to the strengths of your team. It is always best to try to set a designated period of time for a concerted weeding effort. This time should be chosen to minimise the impact on your team's routine activities. Thought should be given to how you will provide any routine services during this time. Once time has been set aside, it needs to be kept free by everyone involved. It may take some strong leadership to prevent other tasks and appointments encroaching on your weeding time. Be aware of the effect that annual leave may have on your weeding time. The University Retention Schedule covers all University records. However, it can be quite complex to interpret the schedules, so the records management team has produced some simplified guides to the most common types of records: These files are an aid to applying the Retention Schedule, but staff should be aware that the University Retention Schedule remains the definitive source. If you have any queries about how much transcript data is held in what was the old HEMIS database for your archived students (and therefore does not have to be held in paper), Stacey Langford and Sarah Arnold now have access to search the HEMIS data for records management purposes. To request a search, please email recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk. Please note we cannot provide transcripts, we can only tell you what level of transcript data is on the system for an individual. When you are weeding out your files, you may come across items (records, photos, A/V, artefacts) which, whilst no longer required by the University Retention Schedule, may have some value to the University Archive. Here is a brief guide to the sorts of things the University Archivist would like you to look out for. Confidential waste bags - Unless you have an industrial shredder, you are likely to require a large number of confidential waste bags during your weeding. Contact your local caretaker/post room in advance to let them know what you are planning. That way there should be no issues with either the provision of large quanities of bags, or the regular removal of filled bags. Remember, not everything needs to go into the confidential waste. A guide to dealing with confidential waste can be found here. Make space - You and your colleagues will require room to sort through the files. If you are sorting boxes held at Filestore, remember that these will have to be delivered back to you (unless you go to FileStore and hire their reading room, which is an option) and stored securely throughout the weeding. Manual Handling - Paper is heavy. If you are expecting staff to lift and/or move large quantities of files or boxes of files, please make sure they are aware of manual handling. Dress appropriately - Paperwork, particularly old paperwork, is usually quite dusty and sometimes dirty. You may find spiders' nests and other bugs! Also, staples in old files can snag clothing. Wear comfortable, old clothing that allows free movement and you are happy getting mucked up... bring a change of work clothes if you know you have to go to a formal meeting during the day. First aid - Paper cuts are guaranteed! Make sure your first aid box is well stocked with plasters. Indexes - Don't forget to allow time to update any indexes (or create them, if you don't already have any!) that will be used to locate the remaining files within your filing system. This is particularly important if you use FileStore because without accurate indexes it will be impossible to know which box to recall. Make sure you index and store files in a way which both aids retrieval when you need the file, and efficient disposal when it reaches the end of its life as a record. For more information see our Guide to Basic Indexing During your project... Don't be afraid to throw things away in line with the Univesity Retention Schedule. However, you should be mindful of any requirements from professional accrediting bodies that may be specific to your department. The retention schedule should indicate where these may apply and override the schedule. If the University Retention Schedule, or the age of your record, requires you to carry out a review to justify disposal or further retention, our handy guide to Reviewing your Records will help ensure you consider all the main criteria. If you think that something in the University Retention Schedule is missing or incorrect, please email recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk If you have records that need to be retained in paper format for more than 30yrs from the date of your weeding project, it would be best practice to consider replacing the materials used to store them (e.g. wallets, folders, paperclips) with archival standard materials. However, archival materials can be expensive, so should be used thoughtfully and sparingly. For more information on archival supplies, contact the University Archivist (Anna Delaney). If you have any questions or issues during your weeding project, please contact Sarah Arnold or Stacey Langford... or email recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk Did you achieve what you set out to do? What benefits have you achieved... storage space? money? risk? In order to calculate the cost of your weeding project it is a good idea to collect the following information: How many staff (at what grade) worked on the project for how many hours? How many bags of confidential waste did you fill during the project? How many and what type of FileStore transactions did you make? (include: recalls, destructions and re-submission of boxes to be retained) What other expenses were incurred? This can then be off-set against the savings made by reducing your paper holdings. The reduction in paperwork can be calculated by: Comparing the total number of boxes at FileStore before and after the project Counting the number of confidential waste bags filled Counting the number of shelves / filing cabinets etc emptied The savings should be calculated over the average remaining life of the records. Retention periods vary by record type, which is why it is important to calculate roughly how many records of each type you have at the start (Step 1). The records management team has some average figures they use for such retention calculations. For assistance with turning your statistics into savings, please contact recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk It is also good practice to compile a Lessons Learnt Report. What went well? What could have gone better? What would you do differently next time?... and in all cases: Why? If you share your lessons learnt report with the records management team, we can ensure that the whole University benefits from your experience. So, now you have completed your paper weeding project, you need to make sure that your efforts do not go to waste. It is important to put in place on-going measures to keep on top the efficient disposal of your paper files. Also, if you have noticed any filing practices that could be improved to make the management of paper files smoother and easier long term, these should be recorded and implemented. You could now turn your attention to the weeding and maintenance of your electronic files (K:drive, Google Drive etc). Guidance on weeding Google Drive Such files that need to be retained after weeding should ideally be transferred to the eRecords system. Talk to the eRecords team for guidance on this. You may also be wondering if you can save even more space/money on physical storage by undertaking a scanning project to digitise your files. Before embarking on any sort of scanning project, please read Records Management Factsheet 06 - Scanning as there are some really important points to consider. title: Email Hints & Tips
headings: Corporate Governance ‌Hints and tips for managing email using Google 1         Start Again… 2         Labels 2.1       Nest Labels 2.2       Label upon Sending 2.3       Add Retention Periods to your Labels 2.4       Create Retention Labels 2.5       Hide Labels 3         Advanced Search 4         Filters 5         Stars 6         Make Time! 7         Putting it all together… a real world example Explore
body: Google no longer provides limitless storage for emails. Staff still have a legal responsibility to manage their emails as efficiently and effectively as any other University records. Some emails will need to be retained for a specific length of time, whilst others containing personal data must be kept only so long as they are necessary. However, we all receive vast numbers of email messages every day. Finding time to sort through and manage these emails is often difficult and, left untackled, the problem only grows. This page aims to give staff hints and tips on how they might harness the functionality of Google Apps Mail to help manage their mailboxes and save themselves time in doing so. Ultimately the management of a staff member’s mailbox is the responsibility of the individual. This page is designed to be read in conjunction with the Staff Email Policy (available in the Document Warehouse), Records Management Factsheet 4: Managing Email and Google’s own user guides (http://learn.googleapps.com/). Training in the use of Google Apps Mail is stongly recommended and available from the University’s IT Training team. The Records Management team also offer webinars to help you sort your email and keep UoP within the Google storage cap. If you have a personal Gmail account, all these hints and tips can be used to manage your personal email as well. If your email has been unmanaged for some time, the task of sorting all your existing mail may seem too much to contemplate. Sometimes it is simply easier to start again. Create a Label called ZZ and then nest your old email structure underneath this new Label. You now have an archived structure, sitting neatly under just one Label, and accessible to you whenever you need it. You can now begin setting up a new Label structure which is better suited to the way Google works, without it getting lost and confused amongst your old structure. As and when you have time, you can slowly sort through your ZZ archive, keeping only what you need to keep. Alternatively, you can use the 6yr rule, whereby you periodically run a search for label:ZZ older_than:6y and delete the results. This latter is a risk based approach and not suitable if you are in a role where it is critical that you retain records for more than 6 years. That said, any email that consitutes a record requiring 6yr+ retention, should not be held in a personal email account. It should be exported as a PDF (in Google choose Print, then change the printer to Save as PDF) and stored with the file to which it relates, be that on EDM, the K:drive, or printed out in hard copy. By default, Google puts all emails into its “All Mail” archive. Whilst this may seem like a good solution, such a vast pot of emails will be almost impossible to manage effectively in the long term. If you apply Labels to your messages and group them logically it will make it easier to locate your emails and to apply retention rules in the future. Logical groupings include: This list is not exhaustive and any combination can be used to suit your personal needs. You can apply more than one Label to a single message. Any Labels you create will display on the left-hand-side of your Google mailbox. If you nest them, you can effectively create the appearance of a folder structure (similar to Groupwise or Outlook). Most people are aware of the messages in their Inbox (those received from others), but it is equally important to manage the emails in your Sent Items (the ones you write). Within the Compose function of Google, it is possible to apply a Label to an email before you send it. If you are using Conversation View, this will have the added benefit that any responses arriving in your inbox will automatically be tagged with the same Label, saving you effort. Where all the email you place under a given Label will have the same retention period, include that retention period in the Label title. This will act as a prompt for clearing them out on a regular basis. If you manage you emails by student, you might want to edit your Label once the student graduates/withdraws (at which point you can specify a review/disposal date). This also works well for closed case or project Labels. Where the emails under a given Label will need different retention periods depending on the content of the email, each message will need to be reviewed in turn. This is a labour intensive process, but one which should only need to be done once for each message. Create a series of Labels with relevant retention periods (e.g. 2yrs) and once you have reviewed you message, apply the appropriate retention Label to it. You can later perform advanced searches (see section 3) to identify emails which can then be deleted in bulk (e.g. any mail with a 2yrs Label, which is over 2 years old) without the need for further review. Complex, nested hierarchies of Labels are great for grouping your email, but can look messy on the screen. Keep the left of your screen clear by hiding Labels. The full structure will still be immediately displayed if you click the More button. You can also chose to show hidden Labels if they contain unread email (show if unread). This function is particularly useful if you set up filters to automatically Label some of your incoming emails, as it will flag up the arrival of new mail meeting that filter’s requirements. As well as the free-text search and basic search criteria in the drop-down box, Google Apps Mail also gives users the ability to build advanced searches using key phrases, known as operators, and Boolean logic (AND, OR, NOT). You can use this advanced searching to look for emails that can be confidently be deleted in bulk. If you leave out the Boolean logic, Google will assume you mean AND as the default. label:2yrs older_than:2y – This search will find any emails with the label 2yrs, AND which are over 2 years old. has:attachment AND larger:10M - This search will find any emails with an attachment, where the total size of the email is over 10Mb. (i.e. this could be 1 large attachment or multiple small attachments that add up to over 10Mb) has:spreadsheet OR filename:xlsx - This search will find any emails with either a Google Sheet OR an Excel spreadsheet. older_than:3m NOT has:purple-star - This search will find any email over 3 months old, which has NOT been given a purple star. Full details of the advanced search operators can be found on the Google help pages: https://support.google.com/mail/answer/7190?hl=en Filters are a good way of automating the application of Labels to some email. If you are managing your emails by student, you could set up filters that automatically tag emails from a specific student with the corresponding Label. If your inbox is prone to clutter, set filters up make some emails skip your inbox and archive themselves directly under the appropriate Label. This should be always be used in conjunction with the show if unread facility (see section 2.5), or you risk missing incoming mail. This is great for managing regular emails such as JISCmail digests or Staff Essentials. Google stars can be applied very quickly to emails and used to denote all sorts of things. If you don’t currently use stars, consider applying a star to emails that contain important information that will need to be retained for longer than usual (see RM Factsheet 4: Managing Email, section 4). That way, when you come to apply retention Labels later, you won’t have to re-read all of your email first, just the starred ones. Remove the star once the retention label is applied. Alternatively use stars to denote emails that don't need review and can be deleted quickly. There are up to 12 star symbols to choose from in Google, so you can be quite prescriptive in their application... just remember to make a note of what you have decided each one will mean for your inbox. All of the above can help minimise the time and effort you spend managing your emails, but the system still requires your input. Aim to clear your inbox weekly or fortnightly by labelling and archiving your emails. Searching for archived email to delete in bulk should be done 2-3 times a year. Choose times that will give you the best returns on your efforts (e.g. just after the end of the academic year, calendar year or financial year). Staff Essentials, discussion board digests (e.g. JISCmail) and subscription alerts are all very useful, but they generate lots of email which will have little value a month or so down the line. If we can minimise the effort required to manage these emails, we should have more time to devote to more important emails. Let’s take the example of Staff Essentials emails. Step 1 – Set up a Label called Staff Essentials Step 2 – Decide how long you need to keep these emails and edit your Label to include that information. For example: Staff Essentials 1mth Step 3 – Set the Label to hide itself unless there is unread content (show if unread) Step 4 – Set up a filter to automatically make any new emails from the Staff Essentials email address skip the Inbox and apply the Label Staff Essentials 1mth Google will automatically label and archive all of your Staff Essential emails from now on. Google will also alert you when new Staff Essential emails arrive by displaying the Label on the left of your mailbox screen. Step 5 – Periodically run an advanced search to identify any Staff Essentials emails that are over your chosen retention period and then bulk delete the search results. For example: label:Staff Essentials older_than:1m Freedom of Information Information Governance Modern Slavery Statement Records Management Fact sheets Meet the Team FAQs Email Hints & Tips Destruction eRecords FileStore Weeding Starter Pack Register of interests Contact and departments

title: FAQs
headings: Corporate Governance Frequently Asked Questions General FAQs Q: Why are records important? Q: What does records management involve? Q: How will records management help me? Record Creation Record Storage Q: Where should I save my electronic records? Q: Where can I keep my older paper records? Q: What's the life expectancy of a CD or DVD? Retention/Disposal Q: How long do I need to keep my records? Q: Can I instantly discard my records once the retention period has expired? Q: How do I declare documents from Google Drive as records into a corporate system? Q: How long do I need to keep my emails? Weeding Q: What staff file information should my school or department be keeping locally? Scanning Q: Can I scan all my records and discard the originals? Filing & Retrieval Q: How should I organise my department's records? Q: How can I find my information more quickly? Explore
body: ‌‌Everything you ever wanted to know about records management...! General FAQs Record Storage Weeding Filing & Retrieval Personal Info Disclosure FAQs Record Creation Retention/Disposal Scanning Email FAQs A: Without records the University would not be able to conduct business, to account for what has happened in the past, and to make decisions about the future. The University's records are a vital, corporate asset, and are required: to provide evidence of actions and decisions; to support accountability and transparency; to comply with legal and regulatory obligations, including employment, contract and financial law, as well as Data Protection legislation and the Freedom of Information Act; to support decision making; to protect the interests of staff, students and other stakeholders. A: Records management involves controlling all records (e.g. paper files, electronic records, emails) throughout their lifecycle from their creation or receipt until the time of their disposal. The controls should cover every aspect of record-keeping, including classification, access, storage, retention and disposal. For a summary of the main elements of records management, see Records Management Factsheet 1: Introduction A: Records management will make it easier for you to carry out your work because you will be able to find the information you need whenever you need it. It will also: Save time by ensuring your records can be found easily and quickly; Save space by keeping your data no longer than necessary; Save money by reducing your storage and maintenance costs; Protect your department by maintaining your information in line with relevant legislation (e.g. employment, contract and financial law, as well as the Data Protection legislation and the Freedom of Information Act). Help the environment by reducing the carbon footprint of digital record storage A: Electronic records should be held within a Corporate System (e.g. Student Records system, HR system etc) or the eRecords system. Electronic documents may be held on networked storage (e.g. K:drive) or Google Drive. With so many choices for electronic storage available to staff, it can be difficult to know when to use which storage area. Information Services have produced a handy two-page guide to your options for electronic storage a) whilst you are working on a document and b) when it is finished. The below link will take you to the guidance which is held in the eRecords System, so you may be asked to enter your standard username and password. Where Should I Save My Files? The RM team also offers webinars on the pros & cons of our various storage options. A: Your immediate office space should be reserved for the records that you need to consult frequently in order to carry out day-to-day work. Your older material should ideally be kept within a separate store room within your department. More information about what to consider when choosing and equipping a store room can be found in Records Management Factsheet 7: Preservation. Alternatively, you can transfer your older records to off-site storage. This is a secure record storage, retrieval and delivery service provided by a third party for the University. Please note we currently operate a single collection/delivery per week (Thursdays). For more details about using this facility please see the FileStore page. A: Do not keep data that you cannot afford to lose on portable media. The life-span of a CD or DVD is determined by several factors, including its manufacturing quality, its handling, and environmental conditions. If it is of low quality or is subjected to careless handling or high levels of humidity and heat, it may last only a couple of years and, in some cases, no more than a number of months. For more information see Records Management Factsheet 7: Preservation It is always advisable to store critical data on the network, so that it will be protected by appropriate back up and disaster recovery procedures. A: You should retain your records in line with the University Retention Schedule. A: Not quite, before discarding any records listed in the University Retention Schedule, you should carry out the following actions: Check whether they have been requested under Data Protection or Freedom of Information legislation. If there is an outstanding enquiry, delay the destruction. The Information Commissioner's Office recommends postponing the disposal of material requested under FOI for at least six months from the date of the last communication concerning the request. Check whether there is any ongoing or pending litigation/investigation and, if so, retain the records as evidence and review them at a later date. Once the documents are no longer required destruction can be carried out, but the act of destructions should be recorded. This will ensure that there are transparent, auditable procedures. If someone requests the information following destruction, you will be able to prove that the retention schedule has been implemented and the records have not simply been mislaid. For more guidance, see Records Management Factsheet 9: Destruction A: A guide to weeding Google Drive, which covers the act of record declaration, can be accessed here. A: Emails should not be treated as a single series with a single retention period: the length of their retention should be determined by their subject matter or business purpose, as is the case with any other electronic or paper record. For further guidance on email retention, please see Records Management Factsheet 4: Managing Emails and Other Modern Media A: Local HR files are important for the management of staff within a school or department; but if too much information is retained locally, this can begin to duplicate the information held centrally by HR. Not only could this constitute over-processing (as defined by the Data Protection Act 1998), but it is also costly to manage and store local files, particularly if off-site storage (e.g. FileStore) is used. Below is a handy guide to what schools and departments should be holding on their local HR files and what is held centrally by the HR department. The link will take you through to the eRecords System, so you will be asked to enter your standard username and password. Guidance Notes - Weeding Staff Files A: There is much to consider before embarking on a bulk scanning exercise, regardless of whether you intend to dispose of the originals afterwards. It is recommended that you read Records Management Factsheet 6: Scanning. If you do decide to dispose of the originals, please remember that it is the responsibility of the business owner of the records to ensure that the relevant professional, statutory and regulatory bodies are happy to accept a scanned copy. This agreement must be obtained prior to disposal of the originals. A: All records (e.g. paper files, electronic documents, emails) need to be organised systematically, so that you and other members of staff can find information easily and quickly. For more information please see sections on classification within the Records Management Factsheets. Factsheet 2: Managing Paper Records and Factsheet 3: Managing Electronic Records. A: There are various ways of reducing the time you spend searching for information, such as: a. Systematic arrangement Ensuring all your records have been arranged systematically. Ideally you should use the same structure for all your information (e.g. paper files, electronic records, emails), and then you will only need to learn one system. Labelling your paper files consistently and helpfully. c. Naming conventions Developing naming conventions for your electronic records to make the retrieval of information faster and simpler. d. Version control procedures Applying version control procedures to records that are regularly revised, so the current versions can be readily identified. Not keeping any of your information longer than necessary - the more you keep, the longer it will take to locate individual items. Establish for how long your major categories of records need to be held, and make sure you discard them promptly once the retention period has expired. Avoiding unnecessary duplication: make sure information to which several members of staff require access is held within a shared folder, so there is no need for each person to retain a personal copy. title: Rechargeable works
headings: Estates and Campus Services Rechargeable Works Explore
body: Historically, when rechargeable works have been required direct contact has been made with any one of a number of the Estates Department staff. However, in an effort to provide a more efficient service please lodge initial requests with the helpdesk on ext: 6677 or any of the other methods outlined on the Repairs and Maintenance pages. This will then enable the appropriate member of staff to be allocated the job. On most occasions the relevant member of staff will contact you to obtain further and more detailed information. For minor jobs such as putting up shelves or notice boards etc, an estimate is not normally provided unless specifically requested. A cost code will be required against which the works can be charged. For more major works such as redecoration or revised office layouts etc, an estimate will be provided and you will be asked to provide a written (or e-mail) instruction together with an appropriate cost code. It should be borne in mind that some works may require planning permission or building control/fire officer approval and therefore sufficient time should be allowed for this in your planning process. Advice regarding this can be obtained from Carolina Spoor. It is important to remember that for health and safety and consistency reasons no works can be undertaken direct by departments. All works must be carried out via the Estates Department. title: Capital works
headings: Estates and Campus Services Capital Works Explore
body: Capital works are major new build or refurbishment projects generally driven by a strategic need of the University. In most circumstances an external design team will be appointed by the Estates Department to undertake the design and contract administration of the project. A member of the Estates Department team will be appointed as Client Representative who will act as the liaison between the design team and the University. An internal Client Representative will need to be appointed by the Faculty or Support Service for whom the works are being undertaken. Responsibilities of the Internal Client Representative are: This representative will need to be given the authority to take decisions on behalf of the Internal Client and act as a single point of contact for the Client Representative and design team. He/She will also participate in the preparation of the Design Brief. This is a key document that states all the requirements of the end users and is therefore a key document to the design team and the ongoing success of the project. To attend design team meetings throughout the design phase and liaise with colleagues to ensure that the ongoing design fully meets your brief. Take an active part in the financial management of the project including any value engineering exercises that may be undertaken. Sign off the drawings and specification upon completion of the design phase. User changes cannot be considered after this point except in exceptional circumstances. Atend site progress meetings during the construction phase of the project. Prepare for the task of occupation of the new/refurbished facility. This will require detailed planning and organisation amongst your colleagues with specific attention to fit out and removals. title: Right To Work Document Check
headings: Human Resources Right to Work Document Checks Right to Work checks for British/Irish nationals For British applicants: For Irish applicants: Right to Work checks for international applicants (including EU/EEA nationals) Sanctions for non-compliance Explore
body: As an employer, we have a duty to prevent illegal working. The Immigration, Asylum and Nationality Act 2006 says that employers should conduct document checks which show that your potential employee has the right to work in the UK and is permitted to do the type of work you are offering. You are required to undertake the right to work check within a reasonable time period before the employment commences. You should not make assumptions about a person’s right to work in the UK or their immigration status on the basis of their colour, nationality, ethnic or national origins. The best way to ensure that you do not discriminate is to treat all job applicants fairly and make the same document checks. Please note the temporary adjusted right to work checks that were introduced during the pandemic were withdrawn on 30 September 2022. Adjusted checking of UK and Irish passports is no longer a suitable UKVI right to work check. On 1 October 2022 UKVI introduced changes to the way we check right to work for UK/Irish nationals to that of international workers. For all British/Irish nationals you must obtain original documents, check validity and make clear copies to send to the People Hub for retention BEFORE someone starts working for the University. We are required by the Home Office to have seen and copied the original documents as follows: A British passport card (current or expired) A British passport card (current or expired) Please copy the personal details (biodata) page of the passport. An Irish passport or passport card (current or expired) An Irish passport or passport card (current or expired) Please copy the personal details (biodata) page of the passport or both sides of the card. If a passport is not available please refer to the Right to work checklist for alternative documents and seek advice from the People Hub if you are unsure if the documents are acceptable. Original documents of British and Irish nationals should be verified in the presence of the holder. Once you have copied/scanned the appropriate original documents, you must either stamp or write on each page of the copy ‘This is to verify that this is a true copy of the original document’, then print your name, job title, department, sign and date (Stamps are available in all departments for this). Remote working UK/Irish nationals and right to work checks If the UK/Irish National is a remote worker, or will find it difficult to come the University with their original documents before they take up post, then a remote checking service can be accessed using a third party provider called Yoti. Yoti are UKVI recognised as compliant providers of right to work checking software. Checks cost £1.80 per completed check, these costs are met at Faculty/Department level. The service is now live and initial requests for checks will be undertaken by the People Hub. The service allows the individual to upload a photo of their current passport and take a photo of themselves, either via a computer or a smart phone. Yoti uses identity verification technology to determine the passport is a genuine document, and belongs to the person submitting the check. The University will be able to access a report from Yoti, confirming the check has been successful, and follow up with a video right to work meeting to fully complete a valid right to work check. UKVI rules related to the documents international workers can provide changed on 6 April 2022 and BRP cards can no longer be used as a right to work document. Instead, most international workers can generate a share code online, allowing you to access their information through the UKVI online portal. A PDF of the right to work confirmation page must be downloaded and saved, and then be verified either face to face or via video call, in line with UKVI rules. Full details on how to undertake the online share code check can be found in the UoP Right to Work Document Checklist. In some instances, for example where the worker has only recently arrived in the UK, they may not yet be able to access the share code system, and in these instances a valid entry vignette in their current passport can confirm right to work whilst UKVI active their information. If you are unsure that you have completed the correct check please contact HR-UKVI-Compliance@port.ac.uk for further advice. Other documents may, in some circumstances be acceptable. Please contact the People Hub for advice or refer to the UKVI document Guide to Acceptable Right to Work Documents for detailed information .You may find it useful to use the UoP Right to Work Document Checklist. With all right to work checks, you must satisfy yourself that the documents are genuine and that the person presenting them is both the rightful holder and allowed to do the type of work you are offering. Employment activities should not commence until at least one working day following the date of the document check. The People Hub are unable to add anyone to the HR system without the correct evidence of the applicant’s right to work in the UK. Employees may not be paid for activities undertaken prior to the completed right to work check. Please note that failure to carry out these checks renders the University liable for a civil penalty of up to £20,000 per breach Any member of core staff found not to be adhering to their responsibilities under the UKVI Sponsor Licence policy guidelines will be subject to disciplinary action through the University's formal procedure. Any member of casual staff found not to be adhering to their responsibilities under the UKVI Sponsor Licence policy guidelines will have the offer of paid employment withdrawn. Examples of non-compliance with University procedure may include: Offering or allowing work prior to the Right to Work document check being completed. Offering or allowing work prior to the Right to Work document check being completed. Working or allowing work in excess of the employee’s visa conditions. Working or allowing work in excess of the employee’s visa conditions. Allowing work outside of the contract dates. Allowing work outside of the contract dates. title: Being a Website Editor
headings: Recruitment, Marketing and Communications Responsibilities Information accuracy Website editing Making updates Website content editor training After training Explore
body: Most factual information on the website, such as details about University services, is updated by the teams of those departments. This means that teams can update relevant information efficiently, without relying on the capacity of central website editors. To make these kinds of updates, you need to become a website content editor. Department information owners are responsible for ensuring that information is correct. For example, details about University services are provided by the teams of those departments. The content editor role is specifically to enable editors to update factual information on existing webpages across http://port.ac.uk and MyPort. This role is ideal for editors who need to regularly update information on the http://port.ac.uk website or MyPort for accuracy — for example a Student Service. This role is not to enable editors from departments outside of RMC to produce or publish new webpages, move pages, change URLs, or make significant webpage changes. Staff may request to become a website editor by requesting the relevant training. If you don’t wish to edit your own pages, you can suggest content updates through a Service Desk ticket for the Digital Content team to review. This option is best of you only need small updates once or twice a year. You'll send us your changes to review by saving your page as ‘Ready for Approval’ in Drupal. The Digital Content team will review your page against our content requirements and publish the page if everything is okay. As these webpages have been published before, information updates (such as small copy changes) generally do not cause issues. If the page needs further changes to meet the content requirements, we’ll reach out to you and let you know what needs changing before we can publish your updates. We review pages that are ready for approval daily. On MyPort your changes may be published by local editors and publishers, or by the Digital Content team. You should speak to other editors in your department to identify who can publish your changes within the team. If there are no publishers in your department, you should submit your changes for publishing by saving your page in Drupal as ‘Ready for Approval’. Additional resources: Drupal Training website — for Drupal CMS users (VPN needed) Connect to / install VPN (MyPort) To become a website editor for any of the core University websites you should request the relevant training. To update http://www.port.ac.uk or http://myport.port.ac.uk you need to request Drupal CMS training. Training is delivered by the Digital Skills team, who will be in touch with you to book a training session after you submit the form. After completing the Drupal CMS training session, you'll take a few extra steps before we create your account. You'll complete a Drupal and Tone of Voice assessment, and email digitalcontent@port.ac.uk for approval. Once we’ve built your account in the relevant CMS, we’ll add you to the Website Editors list — so you may be contacted by colleagues about making updates to the website. To make sure that you only receive requests relevant to your area, please let us know your department and sections of interest when you email us to submit your assessment. title: Menus
headings: Catering Services Food on Campus External bookings Explore
body: ‌Food on Campus plays an important part in the success of your event, and the enjoyment of your guests. Our reputation for great food at competitive prices is outstanding, and you'll be delighted with the presentation. For our full food and drink range, please refer to our 'Food on Campus' brochure. For bookings please complete an online booking form. Bookings can only be made via the online booking system with a valid cost code. Please contact the Events Office on extension 3691 or email catering.events@port.ac.uk to discuss any dietary requirements and to make bookings for over 50 Guests. Organisations outside the University wishing to use our services should contact the Conference and Lettings office via email at Conferences@port.ac.uk or by telephone 02392 844 884. Please note that the prices quoted on these menus apply to internal events only, and that external events will be subject to V.A.T at the current rate. title: Online Catering Terms and Conditions
headings: Catering Services Food on Campus Terms and Conditions for the provision of food and beverages from the University of Portsmouth Events Department. Explore
body: Confirmation of booking Bookings are either secured via Online Catering Booking or by emailing catering.events@port.ac.uk. On written confirmation that your booking is secured and numbers/prices/dates agreed, a contract between us will be formed and you agree to be bound by these terms and conditions. We will supply the catering service for the event on the date set out in the confirmation of booking. We shall provide the catering services: In accordance with food catering industry standards for general hygiene and general cleanliness concerning: The preparation or cooking of food The handling and service of food, beverages, utensils and tableware and The preparation or cooking of food The handling and service of food, beverages, utensils and tableware and In accordance with statutory and regulatory requirements affecting the preparation, cooking, handling and service of food. Allergens and dietary requirements Dishes may contain allergens, if you or any of the customers, who you are booking food for, have any dietary requirements it is your responsibility that we (UoP Events) are made aware of this. We will endeavour to produce dishes for the dietary requirements and label them accordingly. If you have any questions regarding allergens or dietary foodstuffs please contact a member of the Events team: Email – catering.events@port.ac.uk Events Manager – Kevin Goldstone – kevin.goldstone@port.ac.uk / +44(0)23 9284 3733 Events Chef – Mike Williams – michael.williams2@port.ac.uk / +44(0)23 9284 3820 Event Administrator (Allergen Champion) – Tracy de Buriatte – tracy.deburiatte@port.ac.uk /+44(0)23 9284 3691 Cancellation of booking If you require to cancel the booking for any reason whatsoever less than 48 hours prior to the planned event date payments totalling 50% of the anticipated invoice total must be paid. For cancellation within 24 hours of the function, 100% of the anticipated invoice total must be paid. We recommend that you contact us at your earliest convenience when cancelling a booking. Booking staff – numbers Final booking and numbers required must be received at least 48 hours in advance, bookings made less than 48 hours in advance excluding weekends, may be accepted at the discretion of the events department. Late bookings will incur an additional cost: Less than 48 hours = 10% of total booking cost – less than 24 hours = 20% of total booking cost. Out of hours ‘0800-1700’ Monday to Friday events requires 10 working days’ notice to the events department. Out of hours ‘0800-1700’ Monday to Friday late bookings may incur an additional late booking fee. Out of hours ‘0800-1700’ Monday to Friday events requires 10 working days’ notice to the events department. Out of hours ‘0800-1700’ Monday to Friday late bookings may incur an additional late booking fee. Out of normal business hours ‘0800-1700’ Monday to Friday, if staff are required for a function the cost will be; Monday to Friday £15.00 per hour – full or part thereof. Saturday £20.00 per hour – Staff fee is a minimum charge of 4 hours. Sunday £25.00 per hour – Staff fee is a minimum charge of 4 hours. Monday to Friday £15.00 per hour – full or part thereof. Saturday £20.00 per hour – Staff fee is a minimum charge of 4 hours. Sunday £25.00 per hour – Staff fee is a minimum charge of 4 hours. PLEASE NOTE, it is our standard practice to remove all leftovers food from site, unless by specific and prior agreement. This is in the interest of food safety, once we are off site we can have no control over the safe storage or consumption of said leftover foodstuffs, or the removal of foodstuffs to another area of the UOP, and therefore will have no liability for any consequential incidences of food poisoning or other incidences. By agreeing to these T&C’s you indemnify us in respect of all liability, claims and damages which may arise should you wish leftover foodstuffs to be left on site. Rubbish removal, set up and rearrangement of site It is our standard practice to remove any food waste or packaging from site. Any empty bottles / cans resulting from the purchase of drinks from UOP Events will also be removed as a matter of course, however empty bottles/cans resulting from clients own purchase of drinks will not be removed from site unless specifically agreed beforehand. Any waste removal WILL be chargeable pending the quantities involved. Set up and rearrangement of site is the responsibility of the client, UOP Events will not move furniture or any equipment pre or post an event. Damage/loss of equipment You will be notified in writing of any table linen or equipment missing or damaged post event. A charge will be incurred for items damaged or missing post event. If children are to be present at the event, you should ensure that there are a sufficient number of adults present to properly supervise them and take care of them. You acknowledge that childcare is not the responsibility of the UOP Events Team. title: Electric Vehicle (EV) charging
headings: Estates and Campus Services Electric Vehicle Charging How to Use the EV Chargers 1. Download the SEE-V App 2. Register and Set Up Payment 3. Locate a Charging Station 4. Start Charging 5. Monitor Your Session 6. Stop Charging & Payment Pricing Information Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) 1. Who can use the EV chargers? 2. What are the permit hours in the car parks? 3. Do I still need to pay for using the car park if I am paying for the EV charger? 4. Can students use the chargers? 5. What if my car is fully charged but still plugged in? 4. What if I experience an issue with a charger? 5. Can I reserve a charger in advance? 6. When can the general public use the chargers? 7. Can staff use the chargers after permit hours? 8. Why have I been charged £30? 9. Why is my car not charging at 7KW? Further guidance Explore
body: The University provides electric vehicle (EV) charging stations for staff members with University car parking permits. Staff and students will be able to use the chargers at anytime during the day and the general public will have access to them Monday - Friday between 4.00pm - 7.00am and all day at weekends. Below, you’ll find all the details on how to use the chargers, pricing information, and frequently asked questions. To access and pay for charging, download the SEE-V app: Android Users: Google Play Store iOS Users: Apple App Store Open the SEE-V app and sign up with your university email. Agree to the University’s terms and conditions of use. Add a payment method in the app settings. Open the SEE-V app and use the map feature to find available charging stations on campus. Check real-time availability and pricing before arriving. The University has charging points located within the car parks listed below: Burnaby Car Park, Burnaby Road, Portsmouth, PO1 3AE (4 spaces) Eldon Car Park, Middle Street, Portsmouth, PO5 4BT (4 spaces) Milldam Car Park, Burnaby Road, Portsmouth, PO1 3RF (4 spaces) Portland Car Park, St James Street, Portsmouth, PO1 3AE (4 spaces) Ravelin Sports Centre, Cambridge Rd, Portsmouth, PO1 2SE (4 spaces) William Beatty Car Park, Hampshire Terrace, Portsmouth, PO1 2UP (4 spaces) Burnaby Car Park, Burnaby Road, Portsmouth, PO1 3AE (4 spaces) Eldon Car Park, Middle Street, Portsmouth, PO5 4BT (4 spaces) Milldam Car Park, Burnaby Road, Portsmouth, PO1 3RF (4 spaces) Portland Car Park, St James Street, Portsmouth, PO1 3AE (4 spaces) Ravelin Sports Centre, Cambridge Rd, Portsmouth, PO1 2SE (4 spaces) William Beatty Car Park, Hampshire Terrace, Portsmouth, PO1 2UP (4 spaces) Park in a designated EV charging bay. Plug the charger into your vehicle. Open the SEE-V app, select your charger, and tap "Start Charging". Track your charging status in the app, including the cost and estimated completion time. Enable notifications for updates when charging is complete. You’ll need to move your vehicle once it is fully charged to avoid an idle fee of £1 per hour. Tap "Stop Charging" in the app when ready to disconnect. Unplug the charging cable and return it to the station. Payment is processed automatically through the app, and you’ll receive a receipt. The cost of charging is as follows: Staff & student permit holders Staff & student permit holders General public (outside permit hours) General public (outside permit hours) University-owned or leased vehicles (excl. salary-sacrifice cars) University-owned or leased vehicles (excl. salary-sacrifice cars) £1 per hour after 15 minutes of inactivity post-charge £1 per hour after 15 minutes of inactivity post-charge Note: Prices are subject to change. Check the SEE-V app for the latest rates. The chargers are available for university staff and students with valid car parking permits only. Members of the public may use the chargers outside of permit hours (4pm - 7am and anytime at weekends) Only vehicles with University parking permits may use the car parks Monday - Friday between 7.00am and 4.00pm. Outside of these hours, including at the weekend, they can be used by the general public using pay and display tariff. Yes, the use of the car park is a separate charge to the cost of charging the vehicle. Yes, if the student has a valid University parking permit. An idle fee of £1 per hour will apply if you do not move your vehicle within 15 minutes of completing your charge. Try restarting the charging session via the SEE-V app. Contact SEE-V customer support through the app or call 0208 502 6900. Reservations are not available, but real-time availability can be checked in the SEE-V app. The chargers will be available to the public during evenings (after 4.00pm until 7.00am the next day) and anytime during the weekends. Users will need to pay to use the car park in addition to the cost of the charger. Yes, staff with parking permits can use the chargers at any time, as they can with the car parks in general. Staff without parking permits can use the chargers outside of permit hours and pay the same parking charges as the general public. Charging spaces should only be used while their vehicle is actively charging and are not suitable for long term or overnight parking. Don't worry - you haven't! This is just an authorisation charge to ensure that you have money available to charge your vehicle. Some banks may show this money as ‘pending’, but it won’t come out of your bank account. This process can sometimes take up to 7 days to return, but it varies per bank. Tip: You can top-up your See-V account directly from your debit or credit card. This means you won't be charged the £30 pre-authorisation charge. There could be several reasons why you are not getting the maximum charging rate. We have installed dynamic chargers, which only pull through what is currently available within the network. This might mean you receive 3KW to 7.4KW to ensure we do not exceed our available power. You might not get 7kW on a public charger because your car's onboard charger can only handle a lower power level, multiple cars are drawing power from the same circuit, or your car's battery is already nearly full, limiting the charging rate. The charger might be capable of delivering 7kW, but your car or the charging station's limitations prevent it from reaching that full power. We will continually monitor the chargers, so please do not report slow charging as a fault. Further information on the use of the chargers can be found in the EV and charging policy or you can download the SEE-VE app How To Guide. For further assistance, contact the Support Telephone Number 0800 0584291 or email support.see.ev@see-services.com. title: Race Equality Charter
headings: Equity, Diversity & Inclusion Race Equality Charter and the University of Portsmouth Explore
body: In July 2018 the University signed up to a national scheme which aims to tackle race inequality across the HE Sector. Run by Advance HE, and launched nationally in 2016, the Race Equality Charter aims to improve the representation, progression and success of minority ethnic staff and students within higher education. Although similar to Athena SWAN, the national HE gender equality scheme, it is more comprehensive covering professional and support staff and academic staff; student progress and attainment and the diversity of the curriculum. Signing up to the Charter is a significant long-term commitment which will involve long-term institutional culture change across the University. We aim to build a culture of openness and trust that allows for honest reflection and difficult conversations about race and acknowledges that racial inequalities exist. Our membership of the Charter demonstrates our commitment to tackling race inequality for staff and students across the University. The Race Equality Charter principles are: Racial inequalities are a significant issue within higher education. Racial inequalities are not necessarily overt, isolated incidents. Racism is an everyday facet of UK society and racial inequalities manifest themselves in everyday situations, processes and behaviours. UK higher education cannot reach its full potential unless it can benefit from the talents of the whole population and until individuals from all ethnic backgrounds can benefit equally from the opportunities it affords. In developing solutions to racial inequalities, it is important that they are aimed at achieving long-term institutional culture change, avoiding a deficit model where solutions are aimed at changing the individual. Minority ethnic staff and students are not a homogenous group. People from different ethnic backgrounds have different experiences of and outcomes from/within higher education, and that complexity needs to be considered in analysing data and developing actions. All individuals have multiple identities, and the intersection of those different identities should be considered wherever possible. More information about the Race Equality Charter can be found here. The potential benefits are significant – it gives us a comprehensive framework for race equality work, a tool to pull together work being done across the University to tackle the key issues facing our staff and students. Systematically tackling race inequality links to our strategic aims and values and will support delivery of the University Strategy, the Global Strategy and the People Strategy. If you have any questions about the University’s commitment to the Race Equality Charter please contact Yvonne Howard (yvonne.howard@port.ac.uk) or Emilie Smith (emilie.smith@port.ac.uk). title: DCQE Director
headings: Department for Curriculum and Quality Enhancement Lucy Sharp - Interim Director of Curriculum and Quality Enhancement Profile: Aaron Sayers - Departmental Manager Profile: Sophie Carmichael - Senior Finance Officer Profile: Explore DCQE Sections
body: Lucy Sharp - Interim Director of Curriculum and Quality Enhancement Email: lucy.sharp@port.ac.uk Ext: 3568 Email: lucy.sharp@port.ac.uk Lucy leads DCQE, which underpins the strategic aims of the University through the provision of psychological support and academic mentorship to students, strengthening academic skills, innovative pedagogy and the enhancement of learning and teaching, improving digital capabilities and expanding technology enhanced learning, and increasing data analytics through the student voice with internal and external surveys. Before joining the University in 2014, Lucy held senior leadership roles in Third Sector Organisations, Local Authorities and NHS Trusts where she led and transformed Integrated Community Mental Health Services. Lucy has also worked as an Approved Mental Health Professional, with interests in Forensic Mental Health and Veteran’s Mental Health. Lucy studied Sports Science and Theology at St Mary’s University College, and completed her postgraduate and professional studies at the University of Portsmouth. Aaron Sayers - Departmental Manager Email: aaron.sayers@port.ac.uk Ext: 3232 Email: aaron.sayers@port.ac.uk Aaron has worked at the University since 2008 when he joined the HR department as a temporary Admin Assistant. Since then he has worked in administrative and management positions in faculty and professional services and has been in his current role as Department Manager for DCQE since 2011. Aaron has a BA in Film Production, MA in Business Management and is a professionally certified PRINCE2 Practitioner. Aaron's experience and qualifications in HE, administration and management offers creativity, innovation and a safe pair of hands for the operational requirements of the department. Outside of the University Aaron remains active as an Independent Filmmaker and has enjoyed success at a national level. Sophie Carmichael - Senior Finance Officer Email: sophie.carmichael@port.ac.uk Ext: 3162 Email: sophie.carmichael@port.ac.uk As Senior Finance Officer, I am responsible for the budgets of DCQE and Chaplaincy, detailing the expenses and income generated by our services. Reporting to the Director and Department Manager, my daily tasks consist of purchase requisition, receipting, invoicing, inventory maintenance, budget forecasting and reporting monthly and annually, amongst other services. I am also heavily involved with building logistics and operations. Meet the rest of AcDev and TEL team Contact and departments
