title: Who is who on the board of governors
headings: Current membership External Governors Professor Sandy Pepper (Chair of the Board of Governors) Helen Astle Bahram Bekhradnia Madeline Denmead Penny Emerit ‌‌Justin Fanstone Alan Lees David Madoc-Jones Stacey Parrinder-Johnson Mark Readman Sukkanya Siva Elected staff governors Harriet Parr Dr Michelle Hale Academic Council Nominated Teaching Staff ‌Dr Marius Kwint Student Governor ‌To be appointed. UPSU Elected Officer ‌Verity Knight Vice-Chancellor of the University – ex-officio Professor Graham Galbraith We use cookies
body: The current membership of the Board consists of the following: 11 external governors 1 staff governor elected by and from professional services staff 1 staff governor elected by and from academic staff 1 Academic Council Staff governor Further information about each member of the Board can be seen below. Sandy joined the Board in October 2022 and is a member of the Senior Postholders' and Vice-Chancellor's Remuneration Committees. He is currently Emeritus Professor of Management Practice at the London School of Economics, where he worked full-time from 2008 until September 2022. Prior to this he had a long career at PricewaterhouseCoopers, where he held various senior management roles, including as Global Leader of PwC's Human Resource Services consulting practice from 2002 until 2006. He trained as a chartered accountant with Coopers & Lybrand (now PwC) and spent most of his career in practice working in tax and human resource services. His academic background is in organisational behaviour, management theory and new institutional economics. His research interests include executive pay, corporate governance and business ethics. Helen joined the Board in August 2025. She is an experienced pension trustee and non-executive director, currently serving as a trustee director of a large multi-employer pension fund/master trust and as a board member of a legal regulator. She is a seasoned executive-level lawyer and corporate governance leader, with 16 years’ experience advising boards within regulated organisations across financial services (including pensions), housing, and healthcare. She has successfully led and overseen legal services, corporate governance, risk management, compliance, strategy and business planning, HR, customer service, member engagement, and operational performance. She is a practising solicitor and Chartered Governance Professional. Bahram joined the Board in August 2020 and is a member of the Audit and Risk Committee. Currently, Bahram is President of the Higher Education Policy Institute, a member of the Higher Education Commission and a member of the Higher Education Authority of Ireland. Formerly, Bahram was the founder and Director of the Higher Education Policy Institute and Director of Policy at the Higher Education Funding Council for England (HEFCE). Madeline joined the Board in October 2022 and was elected Chair of the Audit and Risk Committee from August 2025. She has some thirty years’ experience in leading organisations to deliver substantial benefits as part of complex strategic transformation programmes. Madeline is a Fellow of the Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales (FCA) and trained as an auditor. She has held a variety of board level executive roles in the not-for-profit sector, including at the BBC and Cancer Research UK. Her current non-executive roles include being an External Adviser to the Royal College of Nursing and the National Lottery Community Fund. Penny joined the Board in August 2022. She is the Chief Executive of both Portsmouth Hospitals University NHS Trust and Isle of Wight NHS Trust, and has worked at director level in the NHS for over ten years. Her areas of expertise focus on strategy and performance management where she is passionate about embedding continuous improvement methodologies to ensure delivery of the best possible health care for local people. Prior to her roles at PHU, Penny held national and regional roles in London and the South East. Justin joined the Board in August 2025. He is a commercially focussed technologist who has spent his career helping organisations implement technology to deliver business benefit. Most recently his digital transformation work at Google has focussed on leveraging data analytics and artificial intelligence to develop insights that deliver quality improvements and business efficiencies. Justin has a keen interest in how technology and artificial intelligence will transform the future of society. He serves as an Independent Board Advisor at the disability equality charity Scope and as a Non-Executive Director at Raven Housing Trust and flexible workspace app Reef. Justin graduated from the University in 1993 with a Batchelor of Arts Degree in Geography. Alan joined the Board in March 2024 and is a member of the Audit and Risk Committee. He is a Fellow of the ICAEW, qualifying in 1983. He has held senior positions in major accounting firms, including as Senior IT Audit Manager at Coopers & Lybrand (now PWC), and as Partner at both Robson Rhodes and Grant Thornton where he led the not for profit and public sector internal audit teams. Alan has worked with higher education sector clients for over 25 years. His current role, as Chief Executive of KCG Audit Limited, which he has held since 2015, extends his involvement in the sector. The company provides internal audit, risk and governance services to over 20 institutions ranging from small, specialist schools and colleges to multi-faculty universities. He is trustee of the National Registers of Communication Professionals working with Deaf and Deafblind People (NRCPD) and has previously worked as a trustee at two other organisations. He is a parish councillor. David joined the Board in August 2020 and was elected Chair of the Finance and Infrastructure Committee in October 2022. David is a former Partner at KPMG, where he spent 20 years as a corporate debt adviser working with a wide range of public and private companies and not for profit organisations. Prior to KPMG he had a number of roles in retail and corporate banking. He is a Trustee of The National Foundation for Educational Research (NFER) and is a member of the NFER Audit Committee. Stacey joined the Board in November 2023 and is the Chair of Nominations Committee and a member of the Finance and Infrastructure Committee. She has spent the past two decades working in the Financial Services industry, most recently serving as Chief Investment Officer of Investec's UK Wealth & Investment division. As well as an investment focus, she brings significant experience working with sustainability and risk management, and has a particular interest in building collaborative and diverse partnerships. She has also worked as a pension trustee, and is a graduate of the University (Business, 2002). Mark joined the Board in November 2023 and is Deputy Chair of the Board of Governors and a member of Audit and Risk Committee. He is CFO of a global technology company in the media sector. He has worked previously at IBM, Misys and Worldpay in various senior finance roles. He is married with two daughters and has lived in the Portsmouth area for over 40 years. Sukkanya joined the Board in March 2024 and is a member of the Finance and Infrastructure Committee. She has worked in residential development for 20 years. She began her career in social housing in Kent and London and went on to work for private residential developers in London. Having worked in both development and construction she has experience of estate regeneration and the delivery of mixed-use developments. Harriet joined the Board in November 2025 and is the Professional Services Staff Governor. She is the Research and Innovation Development Manager in the Department of Research and Innovation. Michelle joined the Board in March 2024 and is the Academic Staff Governor, elected by and from University of Portsmouth academic staff. She attends the Audit and Risk Committee and is the Head of the School of the Environment and Life Sciences at the University. Marius joined the Board in November 2025 and is a member of Finance and Infrastructure Committee. He is a Reader in Visual Culture in the School of Architecture, Art and Design. Verity joined the Board in July 2025 and attends Nominations Committee and the Finance and Infrastructure Committee. Graham has been the Vice-Chancellor of the University since September 2013. He is an ex-officio governor of the University. Graham sits on the Finance and Infrastructure Committee, the Audit and Risk Committee, People Committee, Nominations Committee, and the Senior Postholders Remuneration Committee. title: Make a Payment
headings: Make a payment online We accept payments from: Debit or credit card International and UK bank transfer Make a payment Tuition fees Full payment Sponsorship Instalment plans and recurring card payments Home and Distance Learning Student Options Self Funding Students Postgraduate Loan Funded Students Doctoral Loan Funded Students International and EU Student Options Students have 3 options per course start month: International admission fees Full payment and deposits Deposit conditions - important, please read Accommodation Full payment Termly instalments English language courses Full payment International College Portsmouth Full payment Scams and frauds Keep away from targeted scams and frauds Sanctions Get in touch We use cookies
body: Pay your tuition, accommodation or language course fees If you're paying your student tuition or accommodation fees in full or part payments, or have sponsorship support, read the information here to make sure your online payments are made correctly. You can also find details on fees for our English language courses, international admission fees, how to stay on track with your payments and who to ask when you need help. University of Portsmouth now welcomes UnionPay Cards for safe and convenient payment online. Once you register or re-register online, a tuition fee invoice will be generated and an email will be sent advising you of a new transaction on your account. You can view your account and pay your invoice by logging in to your Student View and accessing payment links via the My Finance tab. Pre-payments such as deposits aren't shown on your invoice; remember to deduct these before making the payment. You can check your balance in the My Finance tab. You can pay via American Express, Mastercard, Visa, Visa Debit or Paypal. You can pay by Flywire international bank transfer. If you are a sponsor and have been issued with an invoice you will be asked to log-in to the Webpay portal using your account number (found at the top left of your invoice). Payment can be made online 24 hours after the invoice has been issued using American Express, Mastercard, Visa, Visa Debit and Paypal. Instalment plans are set up by registering payment details during online registration and need to be completed in the Financial Registration section, this applies to all plan options (apart from the *International Student plan for 2 instalments, where you are required to make a payment directly to the University for both instalments). Once you're set up, we'll create your plan, and send you a payment schedule detailing instalment dates and amounts which we will automatically collect. This will be sent out after your fee invoices have been raised. See terms and conditions. This payment is required before your Confirmation of Acceptance for Studies (CAS) statement can be produced. Alternatively, you may provide a letter from your financial sponsor (this cannot be a friend or family member). Details of sponsorship would be required at least 3 weeks prior to the start date of your course and is required before the CAS statement can be produced. The letter should be emailed to international.admissions@port.ac.uk. All offers made by the University of Portsmouth are subject to checks for criminal conviction, tuition fee status, regulations for students under 18 years of age, Academic Technology Approval Scheme (ATAS), fraudulent materials, and UKVI regulations. Once you register or re-register online, a tuition fee invoice will be generated and an email will be sent advising you of a new transaction on your account. You can view your account and pay your invoice by logging into your Student View and accessing payment links via the My Finance tab. If you require a proforma invoice prior to this please email feeenquiries@port.ac.uk. Pre-payments such as deposits aren't shown on your invoice; remember to deduct these before making the payment. You can pay using American Express, Mastercard, Visa, Visa Debit or Paypal You can also pay your fees via Flywire, read our Tuition Fees for International Students page to find out more. A tuition fee deposit is required of which GBP 1000 is non-refundable (deposits will be refunded in full to students who have their visa refused, unless the refusal is due to a fraudulent application. More details about deposit refunds can be found in the Tuition Fee Policy). Applicants should not pay this deposit until they have received an offer from us. Once the academic conditions and the financial conditions have been fulfilled and the deposit payment has cleared, the Confirmation of Acceptance of Studies (CAS) will be sent out by email. Please note that the CAS won’t be sent earlier than 3 months before the course start date. The deposit will contribute towards the tuition fee. Some students are exempt from paying the tuition fee deposit. For more details, please see International fees and funding. Once you register or re-register online, an invoice will be generated and an email will be sent advising you of a new transaction on your account. You can view your account and pay your invoice by logging in to your Student View and accessing payment links via the My Finance tab. Pre-payments such as deposits aren't shown on your invoice; remember to deduct these before making the payment. You can check your balance in the My Finance tab. You can pay via American Express, Mastercard, Visa, Visa Debit or Paypal. You can pay by Flywire international bank transfer. You can pay your accommodation fees in termly instalments. Instalment can be paid via our Webpay portal by American Express, Mastercard, Visa or Visa Debit. If you wish to pay the termly instalments via Direct Debit or Recurring card instruction this can be set up via your My Finance pages when you register. Get started by signing up on the Webpay portal. You will then be taken to a customer validation screen. You, or the person paying, will enter your student ID, student name and student date of birth. If a third party (for example, friend or family member) is completing the form, they will need to verify their relationship to you If you are paying your own fees, select “self”. An invoice will be generated and an email will be sent advising you of a new transaction on your account. You can view your account and pay your invoice by logging in to your Student View and accessing payment links via the My Finance tab. Pre-payments such as deposits aren't shown on your invoice; remember to deduct these before making the payment. You can check your balance in the My Finance tab. You can pay via American Express, Mastercard, Visa, Visa Debit or Paypal You can pay by Flywire international bank transfer. Learn more about English language courses. For payment methods please visit the ICP web page Students, especially international students, are particularly vulnerable of falling victim to fraudsters when paying their university fees. Online and telephone financial fraud is on the rise and often, fraudsters will offer significant discounts, incentives, or attractive exchange rates to encourage students to use these methods of payments. Please remember that if it sounds too good to be true, then it probably is. The University of Portsmouth does not offer any incentives for payment via a third party. Tuition and accommodation fees should always be paid directly to the University of Portsmouth, preferably through SITS applicant view or student view. Students should never give their log in details to anyone else, especially if that person is offering to pay fees on their behalf. The most common scams to look out for include: Fee payment scams: This is where an external third-party agent claims to make a payment on the student’s behalf. Students can also be approached by a fraudster and/or another student either in person or via social media. They will offer to provide help to pay tuition fees. The fraudster will pay the tuition fee using stolen bank or, debit/credit card details. However, when the genuine cardholder reports this, the funds are recalled leaving the tuition fee unpaid. Another example includes where the fraudster will appear to have made a successful payment by sending you a copy of the receipt showing the full payment made. In reality, all they have done is made a successful £1 payment using a stolen debit/credit card and then altered the receipt to make it seem like they have paid the tuition fees in full. Money muling: the ‘money mule’ trap involves students being offered payment in exchange for receiving money temporarily into their bank account. Students will then be asked to withdraw the cash or to transfer it on to another account. This type of scam is on the increase, targeting students who are short of cash and who may be tempted by offers to make ‘easy money’ on job search or social media websites. Losing money on tuition fees can have serious consequences. Money Laundering is a serious criminal offence and if the University is informed of, or suspects, fraudulent payment activity, it will take appropriate action in accordance with its Anti Money Laundering Policy. In circumstances of payment fraud, be it wittingly, or unwittingly, the University reserves the right to reject an application, withdraw a Confirmation of Acceptance for Studies (CAS) or de-register a student from the course without further recourse from the student. Be mindful of ongoing scams, Always use the University’s approved payment routes www.port.ac.uk/about-us/ways-to-pay, Ask for a number to call back. Usually, a fraudster will never give out their details. Do not share anything over the phone. Do not share your bank account, credit or debit card details, with anyone. Your bank will never ask for full details over the phone, like the PIN, neither will the University. Do not share your University login details with anyone, especially on social media with strangers or any third-party agent. If in doubt, or if you have any questions about payment options, contact our friendly Income team directly by email at any time via income@port.ac.uk, or by calling +44 (0) 23 9284 5533 Monday to Thursday between 10:00 and 4:00pm or Fridays between 10:00am and 3:00pm. Be cautious of offers of easy money or discounts. If it sounds too good to be true, it probably is. The University doesn’t offer discounts on tuition fees. Beware of favourable exchange rates provided by unsolicited agents. Do not feel pressurised to transfer the money to an unknown individual. For further guidance please see the report fraud website. If you or your appointed third party is found to have made a fake/fraudulent payment, the University will hold you responsible and take disciplinary actions against you which may result in your immediate de-registration from the course. We strongly advise that payments on your account are either made by yourself or an immediate trust-worthy member of family and not by an unknown third party. The University is not responsible for any financial loss to any student as a result of fraudulent activity If you are making a payment from one of the following sanctioned countries we will require further information from you confirming the source of funding: For more information, please contact the Head of Treasury and Banking at sanctions@port.ac.uk. Our friendly team can help you with all your questions about fees, payments and invoices. You can contact us by email at any time via income@port.ac.uk, or call us on +44 (0) 23 9284 5533 at the following times: Monday to Thursday – 10.00am–4.00pm Fridays – 10.00am–3.00pm We may use cookies to analyse visitor data, improve our website, show personalised content and give you a great website experience. title: crime/incident reporting
headings: Security and Surveillance Crime/Incident Reporting Explore
body: It is important that reports on all incidents of crime or suspect persons in buildings are made to the Security Control Room as soon as possible. It should be understood that although security will take a report of crime from an aggrieved person, it is important that the person also reports the matter to the police. This is necessary for insurance purposes. It is essential that enough information on the crime and suspect is passed to the Control Room Operator when reporting. Brief description of incident Description of offender Names of persons involved This will ensure that the most effective use can be made of the University security resources in manpower and CCTV. title: Pay / Salary Information
headings: Human Resources Pay / Salary Information Salary Scales Salary Changes for Staff on the Main Pay Scale Cost of Employment The Real Living Wage Timesheets, claims and expenses Explore
body: Your salary will be paid monthly in arrears on the last working day of each month by credit transfer to your bank account. The University issues electronic payslips to staff which can be viewed on Employee Self Service (ESS). If you need to change the details of the bank account your salary is paid into, you are able to do this within Employee Self Service. For support on how to complete a bank account change visit the HR System - Employee Self Service site. You can view the University of Portsmouth current Salary Scales listing all spine points and grades. Progression to the top of your grade is by annual increment. There are two potential increases made to your salary in each calendar year: Your salary increases by one spine point until it reaches the maximum spine point for your grade. After this point, no spine point increments will be made. The nationally negotiated increase to your salary is implemented where the whole salary structure is uplifted. This is usually implemented in August. Follow this link to the table showing the actual cost to the University of employment by spine point. The University has made a commitment to the Real Living Wage set by the Living Wage Foundation. Posts paid at Spinal Points 6 to 14 (Grades 1 and 2) will receive a discretionary supplement to their salary to increase them to the living wage, if appropriate. This supplement is included within the spine point pay scales. The Living Wage increase will be reviewed when the annual National Pay Award is published and may be varied or withdrawn if the Pay Award matches or exceeds the current real Living Wage rate. All hourly paid employees including Casual employees, Zero Hours Workers and Academic employees must claim for their worked hours via Paid Time which is accessible through Employee Self Service. You will be paid on a monthly basis on the last working day of the month and all claims should be submitted as soon as the work has taken place. All staff members should be reimbursed in full for all types of properly authorised expenses incurred in the course of the University’s business. The regulations and procedure for claiming expenses are described in the University’s Expenses Manual. Each month, forms and claims must be submitted by the relevant Payroll deadlines. title: Season Ticket Advances
headings: Human Resources Season Ticket Advances How it works Eligibility How to apply Important details Explore
body: The University of Portsmouth offers employees an interest-free season ticket loan to cover the cost of public travel between home and the workplace. The scheme allows employees to make financial savings whilst encouraging the use of public transport wherever practicable. Buying a season ticket is generally cheaper than purchasing weekly or monthly tickets with some operators offering an annual ticket for the equivalent price of 40 weekly tickets. University Staff may also benefit from an additional discount via the easitPORTSMOUTH website. Please find detailed below a list of general conditions applicable to all season ticket loans. The University will fund the purchase of a season ticket (up to a maximum value of £6,000) by way of an interest free loan. You then pay back this loan within the lifetime of the season ticket by equal monthly instalments from your net salary (that is, after tax and National Insurance have been deducted). The season ticket must be valid for travel between your two chosen stations and can be used at any time, including weekends, although the loan is approved on the understanding that it is mainly intended for travel between home and the workplace. Loans cannot be granted for monthly tickets or for tickets which cost less than £100 per annum. Loans are made in the form of a salary advance, which will be paid at the same time as your usual monthly salary. In the following month, Payroll will commence the regular deductions from your pay. In no circumstances will the repayment period be allowed to extend beyond or exceed the life of the season ticket or your contract of employment. To qualify, you must be a salaried employee and your contract of employment must be for a period which exceeds the expiry date of the ticket. Your net monthly pay must be sufficient to cover the deduction of the loan. The season ticket will cover relevant public transport costs to and from your workplace but not any other costs of travel, for example it will not cover any costs associated with car use or parking. The University will assess each application for season ticket loans on an individual basis and reserves the right to delay or refuse an application. Employees should obtain a quotation from the travel provider to confirm the cost of the season ticket and attach it to a completed season ticket loan application form. All completed applications should be submitted at least four weeks before the loan is required. Please note, applications will need to be submitted and validated before payroll cut-off (usually around the 18th of each month) to be paid in the same month. The Pay and Reward team will confirm eligibility and that they are satisfied that the quotation provided is genuine. Once Human Resources have validated the request it will be sent to Payroll for processing. A loan payment will then be made to your bank account so that you can purchase your season ticket on-line or at the ticket office. Arrangements will then be made for the monthly deductions from your net salary to commence, usually beginning the month after the payment was made. Evidence of the season ticket purchase must be provided to Human Resources within 2 weeks of the loan being paid. Failure to produce this will result in the immediate repayment of any outstanding element in full. Any suspected abuse of the season ticket loan arrangements (for example evidence that the loan has been used for another purpose) will be investigated using the University’s Disciplinary Policy and Procedure. You must request a loan before purchasing the season ticket. The loan must only be used to purchase a standard class season ticket and a copy of the ticket must be provided to Human Resources. The season ticket will not renew automatically at the end of the period: you will need to apply for a new ticket using the same process. Care of the season ticket is your responsibility. In the event of loss of ticket, it is your responsibility to arrange for a replacement from the travel provider. Should the travel provider decide not to replace the ticket for any reason, you will still be liable for the loan and deductions from salary payments will continue unabated until the whole loan has been cleared. If you leave the University before the advance has been fully repaid, the balance outstanding will be recovered from your final salary. If you return your ticket before the expiry date you will be required to pay the outstanding balance to the University. title: Sickness Absence
headings: Human Resources Sickness Absence Managing sickness absence Entitlement to Sick Pay for Variable Hours Staff Explore
body: The University is committed to the fair and consistent treatment of staff in relation to sickness absence and to supporting individuals during periods of ill health. Our Sickness Absence and Ill Health Policy page provides information you may find useful. Managers are required to conduct a return to work form after every period of sickness absence, in an appropriate confidential setting, to support the employee’s return to work. Following each discussion, managers must complete and submit the return to work form via iTrent to ensure accurate documentation and appropriate follow up. Guidance on how to complete this form can be found on Moodle. Your manager can refer you to Occupational Health (OH) for advice if your health is affecting your fitness to carry out your role, or if your role is adversely affecting your health. For more information, visit the Referral to Occupational Health webpage. You can also download the Staff Guide to Occupational Health Referral and Advice. It is important that policies for Variable Hours staff reflect fair and consistent Terms and Conditions. Specifically, Variable Hours staff are entitled to claim Occupational Sick Pay in line with their contract of employment. Full details are available at our page Entitlement to Sick pay for Variable Hours Staff. title: Other Leave and Absence
headings: Human Resources Other Leave and Absence Family-friendly entitlements Leave for religious festivals Compassionate leave Sabbaticals Other absence and leave Adverse weather and travel disruption Unauthorised absence Explore
body: The University provides a wide range of Family Friendly entitlements including information and guidance regarding Adoption/Maternity/ Paternity Leave and Pay, Parental Leave, the Right to Request Flexible Working and Time-off for Dependants. Short term and extended leave for religious or cultural holidays/festivals will need to be booked as annual leave. No reasonable request for annual leave should be refused by a manager. However, in deciding what is reasonable, a manager will need to look at all the implications on the team/department or service they deliver when authorising leave. See the University's Religion and Belief Policy for further details. Line managers are authorised to grant up to five days' leave on compassionate grounds in any rolling 12 month period. Requests for leave of more than five days should be made to the Head of School/Professional Service, who is authorised to grant up to 10 days leave in any one year. In exceptional circumstances, leave in excess of 10 days may be granted. Line managers must notify HR of any compassionate leave taken by their staff via the People Hub. Full details can be found in the Guidance on taking Compassionate Leave. Any academic member of staff, on an open-ended contract to undertake research, innovation and teaching, and who has three years’ continuous service with the University of Portsmouth at the time of commencing the sabbatical, is normally eligible to apply for a sabbatical. Periods of maternity, paternity, adoption leave or long-term sickness absence do count towards the three years’ continuous service. Our Sabbatical Leave Policy provides full details. Information and guidance is available regarding other absence and leave including: Time off for public duties The University recognises that adverse weather conditions can prevent employees from reaching their normal place of employment. All employees are expected to make every reasonable effort to attend for work, but without compromising their health and safety. Where employees are unable to attend for work because of adverse weather, they should report this to their manager as early as possible. If member of staff reports that they are unable to get to work because of adverse weather, and the University is open, then the individual should be offered to take the time as either unpaid, annual leave or flexi-time that they will need to work back at a later date, or where appropriate, work from home on agreed areas of work. If the University takes the decision to close because of adverse weather, casual and PTHP staff who were scheduled to be in work should be paid for the hours they would have been expected to work whilst the University was closed. Timesheets should be completed in the normal way. Staff taking booked annual leave for all or part of the time when the University was closed will not get these days credited back. Unauthorised absence is not acceptable and may lead to disciplinary action including dismissal. title: Flexible Working
headings: Human Resources Flexible Working Explore
body: Flexible working is a way of working that suits an employee's needs. All employees have the legal right to request a change to their contractual terms and conditions of employment to work more flexibly, not just parents and carers. This could be a change in working pattern, the number of hours worked or a request to work from home. It is important to note that this is the right to request a change only, and not the automatic right to actually change your pattern. The government provide guidance on requesting flexible working. Please follow the links below on the process for requesting flexible working at the University of Portsmouth: Right to Request Flexible Working Guidance Guidance on Flexible Working Requests for restrictions of timetabled hours (for Academic Staff) Flexible Working Application form Photo by Luca Bravo on Unsplash HR Business Partnering Team Learning and Development My Rewards and Benefits Health and Wellbeing Retail Discounts Events and New or Limited Time Offers Financial Wellbeing Travel Discounts Leave and Absence Annual Leave Sickness Absence Other Leave and Absence Flexible Working Leisure, Food and Drink Discounts Pay and Recognition Personal Development My Reward and Benefits menu Life Insurance Hardware and Software Recognition Events and New or Limited Time Offers Leave and Absence Annual Leave Sickness Absence Other Leave and Absence Flexible Working Other Leave and Absence Leisure, Food and Drink Discounts My Reward and Benefits menu Hardware and Software Occupational Health Service Equality and Diversity Contact and departments

title: Annual Leave
headings: Human Resources Annual Leave Planning and Booking Annual Leave Rearranging annual leave Sick leave at the same time as annual leave Saving leave until the end of the leave year in September Carrying leave forward Half-day working start/finish times Christmas closure dates Adverse weather and travel disruption Explore
body: The information below provides information on leave options, with links to relevant policies and guidance. Some may be paid/unpaid/annual leave or combination thereof; and some also may be subject to eligibility/qualifying periods, therefore the Guidance on Annual Leave should be referred to for actual details. The annual leave year for all staff runs from 1 October to 30 September in any year and staff are normally expected to take their entitlement within this timeframe. Academic and Research staff receive an annual leave entitlement of 259 hours per year (equivalent to 35 standard full-time days), pro rata for part-time or part-year service. This includes: Deans, Heads, Professors, Readers, Researchers, University Tutors and Graduate Teaching Assistants Professional Services staff receive an annual leave entitlement of 236.8 hours per year (32 days equivalent), pro rata for part-time or part-year service Staff on casual contracts and variable hours contracts have an entitlement calculated in proportion to the number of hours worked Academic and Research staff receive an annual leave entitlement of 259 hours per year (equivalent to 35 standard full-time days), pro rata for part-time or part-year service. This includes: Deans, Heads, Professors, Readers, Researchers, University Tutors and Graduate Teaching Assistants Professional Services staff receive an annual leave entitlement of 236.8 hours per year (32 days equivalent), pro rata for part-time or part-year service Staff on casual contracts and variable hours contracts have an entitlement calculated in proportion to the number of hours worked All University of Portsmouth London staff have an annual leave entitlement of 222 hours (equivalent to 30 standard full-time days) Staff on casual contracts and variable hours contracts have an entitlement calculated in proportion to the number of hours worked All University of Portsmouth London staff have an annual leave entitlement of 222 hours (equivalent to 30 standard full-time days) Staff on casual contracts and variable hours contracts have an entitlement calculated in proportion to the number of hours worked You can check your leave entitlement, balance and leave taken by viewing your Self Service record, which you can access directly via Employee Self-service (ESS). You can also access Self Service from the Staff Essentials page. Joining or leaving the University - leave entitlement If your employment starts or finishes part way through the annual leave year, your annual leave entitlement during that year shall be calculated on a pro-rata basis, rounded up to the nearest hour. On leaving employment at the University, any outstanding leave entitlement to date of leaving should be taken. If this is not possible, outstanding leave entitlement will be paid with the final salary. Leave already taken that is in excess of entitlement will be deducted from the final payments made to you. Please see the Guidance on Annual Leave for full details. Bank Holiday entitlement All staff benefit from a proportional entitlement to Bank Holiday and Christmas closure days off, which is included within the annual leave entitlement. The entitlement period runs from 1 October to 30 September each year. Staff wellbeing is important and it is essential that managers work with their teams to ensure that annual leave is planned and taken on a regular basis for the entire leave year. Please continue to book annual leave in the normal way, via ESS for approval by your line manager, when you wish to take time off, even though you may not be physically working from the University’s premises. If your holiday plans have changed and you would like to cancel your holiday booking, please ensure that you discuss any alterations to annual leave requests with your manager. Once agreed with your manager, changes to annual leave arrangements should be confirmed online via ESS in the usual way. If you are sick or injured during an annual leave period and would have been incapable of work, you may choose to treat the period of incapacity as sick leave and reclaim the affected days of annual leave. The normal policy on sickness absence will apply – you must notify your manager on the first day of sickness and again once you are fit to work. You must then have a return to work discussion with your manager. The period of illness will then be recorded as sickness absence rather than annual leave. You will then either continue the remainder of your annual leave period or return to work. A fit note from your G.P. will be required if the sickness absence is for 8 days or more. It is important that leave is taken throughout the year to support health and wellbeing. We're asking managers to work with their teams to plan annual leave with their staff and to ensure that there is sufficient staffing cover throughout the year. This will be a collaborative process and every attempt will be made to accommodate leave requests. We need to be mindful of other team members who will be looking to take their annual leave before the end of the leave year. It may not be possible to accommodate all requests for leave if the number of requests are high and cannot be balanced with the need to maintain sufficient cover. It is possible that it may be necessary for managers to ask staff to take leave on alternative dates. By booking hours with Friday being a shorter working day for many staff, there will be occasions when odd hours of leave are created which cannot immediately be booked if less than half a day. When these accumulate to half a day, they can be booked but there is a chance that at the end of each Scheme year there will be a few odd hours above the current permitted carry over of 5 days (37 hours), up to an additional 4 hours will be carried over, for a total of 41 hours. Carry over in excess of 5 days (41 hours) The policy is still that a maximum of 5 days or 41 hours annual leave outstanding at 30 September is automatically carried forward into the next leave year, and may be taken at any time during that leave year. The automatic carry forward of up to 41 hours of annual leave allowance includes part-time staff and is not pro rated. Managers have been advised that there may be some unusual and exceptional circumstances (generally maternity, adoption or shared parental leave) which prevent staff from taking their full annual leave entitlement, even taking into account the fact that up to a maximum of 5 days or 41 hours outstanding annual leave will be automatically carried forward into the next leave year. Where an individual’s absence due to one of these reasons for a continuous period of at least four weeks has been recorded on the HR System, their full remaining leave entitlement will automatically be carried over into the next leave year. It is recognised, however, that some staff have been required to undertake additional work to support our students and colleagues, which for some makes the taking of all leave a challenge. You are urged to work with your line manager to find ways to continue to take annual leave where at all possible, as everyone needs a time to rest and rejuvenate. If there are staff who are considered by the University to be business critical and where it is not possible for them to take leave as a result of work requirements, leave carry forward can be extended up to a total of 10 days (74 hours) for the current leave year. In these exceptional circumstances, the employee, line manager or Head of Service will need to complete the carry over request form to request approval. Please note that any approved leave carry forward requests will not be visible on ESS until after the end of the relevant leave year. Annual leave can be taken only in half day or full day entitlements. Your specific work pattern will dictate the number of hours taken for a half day or full day annual leave. For example, if you work a 7 hour day, it will deduct 3.5 hours for a half days holiday. The 'work pattern' for each employee will dictate the number of half or full day hours. The majority of the University is closed over the Christmas period. Staff are not expected to take annual leave to cover this time: working days within the period are allocated as a combination of statutory and customary holidays. Exact dates of the Christmas closure dates can be found here: University Christmas Closure dates. The University recognises that adverse weather conditions can prevent employees from reaching their normal place of employment. All employees are expected to make every reasonable effort to attend for work, but without compromising their health and safety. Where employees are unable to attend for work because of adverse weather, they should report this to their manager as early as possible. If member of staff reports that they are unable to get to work because of adverse weather, and the University is open, then the individual should be offered to take the time as either unpaid, annual leave or flexi-time that they will need to work back at a later date, or where appropriate, work from home on agreed areas of work. If the University takes the decision to close because of adverse weather, casual and PTHP staff who were scheduled to be in work should be paid for the hours they would have been expected to work whilst the University was closed. Timesheets should be completed in the normal way. Staff taking booked annual leave for all or part of the time when the University was closed will not get these days credited back. title: COSHH
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations (COSHH) 2002 2020 COSHH Policy and Chemical Inventory Procedure COSHH Frequently Asked Questions Explore
body: Using chemicals or other hazardous substances at work can put people's health at risk, so the law requires employers to control exposure to hazardous substances to prevent ill health. Employers have to protect both employees and others who may be exposed by complying with the Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations (COSHH) 2002 (as amended). Substances hazardous to health are: Generally those which are classified as being: toxic to reproduction (teratogenic) 2. Gases which act as simple asphyxiants 3. Biological agents and micro-organisms 4. Preparations and mixtures 6. Substances which have been assigned a Workplace Exposure Limit (WEL) 7. Any substance not mentioned above which creates a hazard to health COSHH does not apply to... COSHH applies to virtually all substances hazardous to health except: Flammable and explosive substances Lead and lead salts (Contact Health & Safety Office) Radioactive substances which are only harmful by nature of their radioactivity. These substances are subject to separate pieces of legislation and University policies and procedures. Information on controlling these substances can be found by following the above links. The COSHH Policy has been reviewed and updated and a new Chemical Inventory Procedure has been introduced. The expectation for the Chemical Inventory Procedure is that a chemical inventory should exist for each Laboratory or area that stores chemicals and that a member of staff is identified to be responsible for its upkeep. This inventory will be subject to audit via the local workplace inspection and independently by the Health & Safety Team and/or Fire Risk Assessment process. Examples of Chemical Inventory forms are provided if Departments have not already got one in place. The procedure requests that chemicals are categorised by hazard, eg explosive, flammable, oxidising, corrosive, toxic, health hazard (warning/irritant/sensitiser) and where possible that expiry dates are included. The expiry information together with usage will determine whether chemicals should be disposed of. HSG71 – HSE Guidance - The storage of packaged dangerous substances HSG71 is guidance for anyone with responsibility for storing dangerous substances, regardless of facility size. The guidance contains a segregation matrix for substances and is useful to keep near to Chemical Storage Cabinets. The guidance can be downloaded, free of charge from the HSE website: https://www.hse.gov.uk/pubns/books/hsg71.htm For more information about COSHH and how what you need to do, please see our COSHH FAQs. The Health & Safety Office provides training to support members of staff to work safely with hazardous substances. These include: COSHH - Working Safely with Chemicals Dealing with Spills & Emergency Preparedness for the training flow chart: Flow Chart; Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Prepardness Fire Safety in Laboratories Safe Use of Compressed Gas Genetic Modification Safety UoP-CGHS-P-08 COSHH Policy [Acrobat (.pdf) - 240KB Tue, 06 Oct 2020 16:05:00 BST] Technical Guidance Note - CHM05: Basic Procedures for Handling, Storing and Using Chemicals [Acrobat (.pdf) - 67.2KB Mon, 02 Jul 2018 16:00:00 BST] Technical Guidance Note - CHM09 - Globally Harmonised System of Labelling [Acrobat (.pdf) - 295kb Mon, 27 Mar 2017 09:00:00 BST] Technical Guidance Notes -CHM10 - Hazards Codes [Acrobat (.pdf) - 228kb Mon, 27 Mar 2017 09:00:00 BST] Technical Guidance Notes CHM11 - Precautionary Codes [Acrobat (.pdf) - 143kb Mon, 27 Mar 2017 09:00:00 BST] COSHH Assessment Form - Multiple Chemicals and Biological Agents [Word (.doc) - 99.8KB Tue, 27 Mar 2018 14:00:00 BST] COSHH Assessment Form - One Chemical [Word (.doc) - 268KB Tue, 06 Oct 2020 15:28:00 BST] Chemical Inventory Procedure [Word (.doc) - 54KB Tue, 06 Oct 2020 15:30:00 BST] Chemical Inventory [Word (.doc) - 27KB Tue, 06 Oct 2020 16:01:00 BST] Chemical Inventory Form [Excel (.xls, .csv) - 15KB Tue, 06 Oct 2020 16:01:00 BST] Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: DSEAR
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations 2002 (DSEAR) Explore
body: The Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations 2002 (DSEAR) requires the University to make a suitable and sufficient assessment of the risks to his employees, where a dangerous substance is or is liable to be present in the workplace. Any requirement for a DSEAR assessment is identified through the fire risk assessment process and a specific assessment is completed by the Health & Safety Office. Additional information on DSEAR can be found on the HSE webpages Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Estates
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Estates and Campus Services Explore
body: Estates & Campus Services are responsible for the construction and maintenance of the University's estate. The estate is, broadly speaking, based on two main campuses, these being Guildhall and Langstone Student Village and extends to approximately 1.5 million sq ft of buildings together with associated playing fields and open parkland. The building stock extends to some 70 main buildings together with sundry additional minor structures. title: Working at height
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Explore
body: The Work at Height Regulations 2005 came into force on 6 April 2005. Working at height is defined as "work in any place, including a place at or below ground level, or obtaining access to or egress from such a place, while at work, except by a staircase where, if suitable measures were not taken, a person could fall a distance likely to cause personal injury". Where work at height is required a risk assessment must be completed in order to identify the risk control measures required. Our risk assessment process takes into consideration the hierarchy of control for work at height as set out within the Work at Height Regulations and those in control of any work at height activity must make sure work is properly planned, supervised and carried out by competent people in a manner which is so far as is reasonably practicable safe. Only use ladders and step ladders for work at height where the low risk and short duration makes this acceptable and where the nature of the work enables three points of contact to be maintained throughout. The University has produced a Working at Height Procedure which provides more information. Working at Height Equipment All working at height equipment must be suitable for the purpose for which it is being used and comply with the requirements of the Regulations: Schedule 2 Requirements for guard-rails, toe-boards, barriers and similar collective means of protection Schedule 3Requirements for working platforms Schedule 4 Requirements for collective safeguards for arresting falls Schedule 5Requirements for personal fall protection systems Schedule 6Requirements for ladders Maintenance of Working at Height Equipment All working at height equipment must be inspected regularly by a competent person and inspections recorded locally by the department. Please use the following documents: Main Points to Look for When Inspecting Working at Height Equipment and Working at Height Equipment Register and Inspection Form. All staff and students who use working at height equipment must have received suitable and sufficient training to enable them to use the equipment safety, not endangering themselves or others. For details on our training courses see the training page. If you need any further information or assistance, please contact the Health and Safety Office on ext 3075 or hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk. http://www.hse.gov.uk/work-at-height/index.htm Working at Height Equipment Register and Inspection [Word (.doc) - 32.3 KB Mon, 12 Feb 2018 09:00:00 GMT] Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Lifting equipment
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Lifting equipment Explore
body: The deployment of suitable lifting equipment/transport aids and their use in accordance with safe systems of work will reduce the need for manual handling and can help eliminate poor manual handling practices. Any equipment used at work that lifts or lowers a load is regulated by the Lifting Operations and Lifting Equipment Regulations (LOLER) 1998. These regulations deal with both the lifting equipment and the way it is used. As well as regular passenger lifts, LOLER also applies to equipment such as forklift trucks and reach trucks, tail-lifts on vans and trucks, scissor lifts, cherry-pickers, certain parts of conveyors, and dumb waiters. Currently, the statutory requirements for lifting equipment and accessories are that they must be: Marked with the safe working load, this may take the form of a maximum load on hoists and jacks Checked regularly (see requirement for thorough examination and inspection of lifting equipment). Statutory inspections must be performed by a competent person (usually an external inspection body). Inspections should also be performed by the user prior to use (a simple visual inspection is usually sufficient). Before lifting equipment is used for the first time, it must be thoroughly examined Only used by persons that have had suitable and sufficient information and training on their safe operation and use and any precautions or safeguards required Used under adequate supervision, the extent of the supervision being dependent on the competence level of the operator Immediately removed from service if found to be defective (disposed of or quarantined until repaired, inspected and proved to be safe for further use) Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Microbiological safety
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Microbiological Safety Legislation Risks from Micro-organisms Control Measures Microbiological Hazards in Non Laboratory Situations Explore
body: The main areas of Health and Safety law relevant to micro-organisms used in the University of Portsmouth are: The Health and Safety at Work etc Act 1974 Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations (COSHH) 2002 (as amended) The Genetically Modified Organisms (Contained Use) (Amendment) Regulations 2005 (.pdf) This site deals with the health hazards presented by bacteria, fungi and viruses and the prevention of laboratory and non-laboratory acquired infections. A specific area of responsibility for the University of Portsmouth is the control of legionella bacteria in water systems. Assessment of the relative risk of handling infectious agents has led to the adoption of a scale consisting of four Risk (or Hazard) Groups, ranging from the least (Group 1) to the most (Group 4) hazardous to laboratory workers and the community. The classification of micro-organisms on the basis of risk is: Risk Group 1 (no or very low individual and community risk): A micro-organism that is unlikely to cause human or animal disease. Risk Group 2 (moderate individual risk, low community risk): A pathogen that can cause human or animal disease but is unlikely to be a serious hazard to laboratory workers, the community, livestock or the environment. Laboratory exposures may cause serious infection, but effective treatment and preventive measures are available and the risk of spread of infection is limited. Risk Group 3 (high individual and community risk): A pathogen that usually causes serious human or animal disease but does not ordinarily spread from one infected individual to another. Effective treatment and preventive measures are available. Risk Group 4 (high individual and community risk): A pathogen that usually causes serious human or animal disease and can be readily transmitted from one individual to another, directly or indirectly. Effective treatment and preventive measures are usually not available. Only micro-organisms in Risk Groups 1 and 2 are used in the Laboratories of the University of Portsmouth. The key control measures in microbiological safety are designed to stop entry of micro-organisms into the body. The routes of entry are through: Cuts and abrasions; including those that are not visible to the naked eye The thin mucous membranes of the mouth and eyes Accidental puncturing of the skin by infected "Sharps" such as needles. This is often referred to as a "Needlestick Injury" Inhalation of infected aerosols and airborne particles All these risks can be minimized, if not prevented, by good laboratory practice, the correct use of equipment and a high standard of personal hygiene, as detailed in the University's policies and Technical Guidance Notes. (Source: World Health Organisation Laboratory Biosafety Manual). Microbiological hazards can be encountered in many work place situations and in order to ensure the safety of staff, the Health and Safety Office have produced the Technical Guidance Note - BH02: Dealing with biological hazards from blood, tissues and secretions in non laboratory situations. This also covers "needlestick injuries" resulting from "Sharps". Spills Inside a Centrifuge The Health and Safety Office have created a procedure for spills inside a centrifuge. title: Personal Protective Equipment
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Personal Protective Equipment Explore
body: Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) is all equipment (including clothing affording protection against the weather), which is intended to be worn to protect the user against risks to their health or safety. Examples include: Eye protection and face-shields Respiratory protective equipment Safety boots or other footwear Warm and weatherproof clothing High-visibility clothing Safety harnesses or fall arrest systems Under the Personal Protective Equipment at Work Regulations 1992 (as amended): The University must ensure that suitable personal protective equipment is provided to university staff and students; who may be exposed to a risk to their health or safety, except where and to the extent that such risk has been adequately controlled by other means which are equally or more effective. The Health and Safety at Work, etc Act 1974 (HSWA) section 9, places a general duty of care on the University for ensuring the safety of their workers and requires that no charge is made for providing PPE. Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) plus PPE Issue Form Your risk assessment needs to show that you have considered PPE as a last resort. This form has been designed to show that due consideration has been given to the Principles of Prevent/Hierarchy of Controls and PPE is a last resort and the selection of the PPE is appropriate. The PPE Assessment Checklist is for all PPE, including disposable PPE. It can be used to cover several risk assessments e.g. If the School/Faculty uses one type of disposable gloves for a wide range of tasks. For PPE that has been issued to staff the PPE Issue Record Form will need to be completed. It is recommended that the form be for students were PPE has been issued for long-term tasks/projects. It does not apply to disposable PPE. When choosing PPE make sure: It is appropriate for the risks and the working environment. For example, chemical eye protection where there is a risk of chemicals entering the eyes, or impact resistant eye protection where there is a risk of debris entering the eyes. Takes into account the requirements of the task and the demands it places on the wearer; such as the length of time it needs to be worn, the physical effort required to do the task and any restrictions on visibility and communication. Adequately controls the risk presented by the hazard without increasing overall risk experienced by the user. (eg. ear-defenders that reduce the level of noise impacting on the user may make it difficult to hear audible alarms.) Takes into account any pre-existing health issues of those who will be wearing it. Can be fully adjusted to fit the wearer correctly. Is compatible when more than one item of PPE is being worn. For example, a particular type of respirator may make it difficult for eye protection to be fitted properly The University will supply appropriate PPE free of charge when it is required in order to carry out tasks that have been risk assessed as requiring further controls. The University will provide instruction, information, training and supervision on the use of PPE, which will include: The circumstances in which PPE is required How to use it effectively Why PPE is needed and its limitations Maintenance, storage and replacement PPE must be used when health and safety risks cannot be controlled in other ways. Please note the Personal Protective Equipment Policy is currently being reviewed. title: Placements
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Placements UK Placements Clinical Placements Crown Placements Self-Employed Placements International Placements Students on Placement in Home Country Students Travelling from the UK Abroad for a Placement Explore
body: Placement Offices carry out checks and collate information for placements such as a Health and Safety Checklist (see below) and a Student Overseas Travel Risk Assessment. As per the normal procedure for placements, Placement Offices can escalate issues to Corporate Health and Safety or Insurance for guidance. Placement providers will need to complete the Health and Safety Checklist for UK Placements - Word Version or Google Form. Clinical placements are separated into two categories: placements with the NHS and placements with private healthcare providers. For placements provided by the NHS, it will not be necessary to send a H&S Checklist. For placements provided by private healthcare providers - placement providers will need to complete the Health and Safety Checklist for Clinical Placements - Word version or Google Form. Placement providers will need to complete the Health and Safety Checklist for Crown Placements - Word Version or Google Form. All self-employed placements should have a Risk Assessment in place for the activities involved in their placement. Self-employed risk assessments are no longer required to be sent to Corporate Health and Safety for approval. If any assistance is required to ensure an appropriate risk assessment is in place for higher risk activities please contact Health and Safety at hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk. Self-employed placements should have arrangements in place for their own public liability insurance. The following paperwork must be completed by the student and placement provider and sent to their Faculty Liaison for approval. Overseas Travel Risk Assessment - Where the form requests information regarding international travel, this can be left blank. Health and Safety Checklist for Overseas Placements - Word Version or Google Form. For students undertaking a placement in their home country, as there is no travel involved, the student will not be covered by the University travel insurance. The University would not be able to offer any emergency assistance. The student must be made aware of this information. The student must provide evidence that they have personal arrangements in place to cover healthcare costs (including emergency medical treatment) during the course of their work placement. If the placement provider advises that they do not have insurance, approval should be esclated to the Head of School. The reason being that without travel insurance, there will be no personal accident cover in place in the event that the student is injured whilst on placement. The following paperwork must be completed by the student and placement provider and sent to the Faculty Liaison for approval. Overseas Travel Risk Assessment Health and Safety Checklist for Overseas Placements - Word Version or Google Form. Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Workplace inspections
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Workplace inspections Explore
body: Workplace inspections are one of the primary methods of identifying and eliminating actual and potential hazards. These hazards can include problems with equipment, the workplace environment, the building, as well as with work practices. There are many different kinds of workplace inspections including daily "walkabout" inspections when you enter your laboratory or office, "pre-use" inspections of equipment you are going to run, and "spot-checks" by your work supervisor or advisors. Other inspections are more formal, and involve documenting the fact that inspections have been done on a regular basis as part of "due diligence". The Workplace must be inspected reguarly using the University's Workplace Inspection procedure. Please email hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk if you have any queries or require assistance to complete inspections in your area. 'The role of the Unison Health & Safety team is to work with the University in the interest of reducing injuries and ill health in the work place. This is achieved in consultation with management on new H&S policies and procedures, by ensuring accidents and incidents have been appropriately investigated and by overseeing workplace inspections and audits. Our aim is to promote and encourage involvement of all staff in Health & Safety matters and therefore reduce the likelihood of injuries or accidents at work occurring'. 'Workplace inspections help to identify hazards and potential risks and therefore play an important role in prevention. Unison Health and Safety Rep’s are therefore pleased to have had the opportunity to be involved in the design and launch of the new workplace Inspection forms and to encourage and support staff in carrying out these inspections'. Important Note: This is a link to a Hazard Warning Sign to be used in incidences where a hazard has been noticed and there is an imminent need to highlight the hazard to others. Please print off and place strategically where it is clearly visible to people. Please then follow the accident reporting procedure by filling in a HS1 form. If an incident needs immediate attention, please call the Health & Safety Office on ext. 3075 and if appropriate, the Estates Helpdesk on 6677. title: LGPS Timeline: How you Join
headings: Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: HOW TO JOIN Explore
body: If you are an eligible employee in a Support, Administrative, Professional Services or Research role with a contract due to last at least 3 months, we’ll contractually enrol you in the Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS) from your first day of employment. You need to remain a member for 2 years to qualify to keep your pension – otherwise your contributions may have to be refunded or transferred to another scheme, or you may be able to 'freeze' them on the expectation that you could return to LGPS later. If you have an employment lasting less than 3 months you won’t be contractually enrolled but can still choose to opt in by emailing us at pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. If you are aged 75 or over, you are not able to join the scheme. You may also be automatically enrolled, where you were not in any pension scheme but meet the government’s criteria to be compulsorily enrolled. This happens where you: Were not, at time of assessment, in a pension scheme for this employment Have not previously opted-out of a pension in this employment, or opted-out more than 12 months before the University’s auto enrolment ‘staging date’, which happens every 3 years Earned over a minimum amount in a single month (£833 at time of writing) Are aged between 22 and your State Pension Age We’ll let you know if you are ever automatically enrolled. Opted out previously but now want to re-join? Just let us know by emailing pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: LGPS Timeline: Essential Action when you Join
headings: Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: ESSENTIAL ACTIONS WHEN YOU JOIN Explore
body: Visit the Hants LGPS website as soon as you can to learn about all the benefits of being a member and read the Starter Information Booklet. There are video guides covering the basics, and the employee guide covering most of the detail and technical information you may need. You’ll be sent a welcome pack by email (normally a month or so after paying your first contributions) to invite you to register for the LGPS Member Portal. This is the best way to view and manage your pension and personal details, talk with the scheme administrators, and access your Annual Benefit Statement updating you on the value of your pension each summer. Follow the Just Joined pages to complete your details. You’ll want to check your name, address and marital/cohabiting status is up to date. IMMEDIATE ACTION: Update your ‘Expression of Wish’ on the portal as soon as possible to nominate a beneficiary for your pension and death in service payments. If you are nominating someone you live with who is not a civil partner or married to you, you may need to submit a Cohabiting Partner form. Returning to LGPS? See Re-joining for how this works. Got another pension? You may be able to transfer this to LGPS but have to complete within your first 12 months of membership. Want to add even more value to your pension? It’s never too early to do this. title: LGPS Timeline: What do you get?
headings: Human Resources LGPS – WHAT DO YOU GET & WHAT DOES IT COST? Explore
body: The LGPS Benefits page details the many benefits of being a member: A pension set out in law making it very safe and secure An option for a tax-free lump sum when you retire Tax relief on all your pension contributions Option to pay 50% of contributions for 50% of benefits in the 50-50 scheme Increase pension value by paying extra contributions Massive employer contributions towards your pension of 20.6% A monthly pension payment from your retirement for the rest of your life Flexible (or 'partial') and early retirement options Annual cost of living adjustments to your pension Pension protections during long-term absence Ill health pensions (qualifying after 2 years) Death in Service lump sum of 3 times your pensionable pay Partner’s pension for life, in event of your death, generally based on 30.6% of your benefits (or higher for service prior to 2014) Children’s pension for eligible dependants in event of your death A pension set out in law making it very safe and secure An option for a tax-free lump sum when you retire Tax relief on all your pension contributions Option to pay 50% of contributions for 50% of benefits in the 50-50 scheme Increase pension value by paying extra contributions Massive employer contributions towards your pension of 20.6% A monthly pension payment from your retirement for the rest of your life Flexible (or 'partial') and early retirement options Annual cost of living adjustments to your pension Pension protections during long-term absence Ill health pensions (qualifying after 2 years) Death in Service lump sum of 3 times your pensionable pay Partner’s pension for life, in event of your death, generally based on 30.6% of your benefits (or higher for service prior to 2014) Children’s pension for eligible dependants in event of your death You’ll pay a percentage contribution on all your pensionable earnings (normally including salary, bonuses and overtime), at a rate depending on how much you earn which is often 3 or more times less than we pay as your employer - you’ll see all these contributions on your monthly payslips The good news is that you don't pay tax on contributions you make. For example, £100 of contributions for a 20% tax payer would only cost £80 from your take-home pay. This works for higher rate tax payers too, so you'd get the full 40% saving 'up front' without having to claim it from HMRC at the end of the year You’ll pay a percentage contribution on all your pensionable earnings (normally including salary, bonuses and overtime), at a rate depending on how much you earn which is often 3 or more times less than we pay as your employer - you’ll see all these contributions on your monthly payslips The good news is that you don't pay tax on contributions you make. title: LGPS Timeline: How does it Work?
headings: Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: HOW DOES IT WORK? Explore
body: The ‘How is my pension calculated’ section of LGPS Benefits provides details and examples of how your pension builds up value. Remember, LGPS is a ‘defined benefit’ scheme, meaning that unlike most workplace and personal pensions available, it increases in value according to knowable and defined rules, rather than being dependent on investment and market performance. The current scheme for all members is a CARE (or ‘Career Average Revalued Earnings’) pension: Each year you build up an Annual Pension (paid every year of your life after retirement) equivalent to 1/49th of your pensionable earnings. This is adjusted with cost of living changes so what you contribute today shouldn’t be left behind by inflation. This isn’t an easy thing to understand upon first reading – we recommend the ‘How is my pension worked out – an example’ section of the employee scheme guide for a better illustration. If you were a member prior to 1 April 2014 you may also have a Final Salary pension based on ‘60ths’, and if before 1 April 2008 may have one based on ‘80ths’: You build up a pension based on the total number of years’ membership you have (pro-rated for part time), divided by 60 or 80 (see dates above). This is multiplied by your best salary in the last 3 years at the point you retire. If you have the ‘80ths’ scheme you’ll get an automatic tax free lump sum of 3 times your annual pension too. All Final Salary pensions are now closed - you can’t earn any more years but its value will still increase as your salary goes up. Why Do I pay a higher percentage than I earn? It may look like you’re paying 6% or more to your pension but only adding 1/49th (or ~2%). That might not seem good value at first reading, but remember that the 2% is what you’ll get each year you are retired – that means it only takes a few years to catch up and break even, not to mention all the tax relief from contributions, increases with cost of living, other scheme benefits, and options to take a tax free lump sum... title: LGPS Timeline: Transitional Protections & McCloud
headings: Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: TRANSITIONAL PROTECTIONS & 'McCLOUD' Explore
body: What does ‘McCloud’ mean for me?The age discrimination case (known as ‘McCloud’) against the government for moving from Final Salary to CARE (Career Average) schemes based on age means that members who joined before 31 March 2012 with both Final Salary and CARE pensions can qualify for protection to stay in their Final Salary schemes between April 2015 to March 2022. Hants LGPS will provide details on this when you decide to retire. Find out more on the Hants LGPS website (we recommend reading through the government's LGPS McCloud Factsheet first to see if you need to find out more). title: LGPS Timeline: Opting Out
headings: Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: OPTING OUT Explore
body: You may feel you need to opt out of your pension, however we would always recommend you remain in scheme whenever you can. Please consider this carefully and make sure you choose the right option for your circumstances. First consider the LGPS 50/50 Scheme: This lets you to stay in your pension, paying only 50% of contributions in return for accruing 50% of the benefits. You can later re-join the ‘main’ scheme at any time. If you still wish to opt out you can complete an Opt Out form and attach this to pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. You can only request this on or after the first day you join and forms sent before this must be rejected. Opting out within 3 months of joining the scheme: UoP will refund all contributions through your pay, less tax and National Insurance deductions. This will be the case even if you have another deferred LGPS pension or if an LGPS pension is being paid to you. Opting out after 3 months but less than 2 years in the scheme: You will stop paying contributions from the month after submitting the form and may either claim a refund of contributions you have made, transfer your contributions to another scheme, or defer your choice and keep your service with LGPS in the expectation that you may return to membership later. You will be contacted by the scheme within several weeks of opting out with the options available to you, or you may contact them in advance at pensions@hants.gov.uk or 01962 845588 (please have your National Insurance number to hand to help identify yourself). Opting out after 2 or more years’ membership: You cease paying contributions in the following month. You cannot claim a refund of contributions and will become a ‘deferred’ member until you retire. You may be able to transfer your pension to another scheme by contacting pensions@hants.gov.uk or 01962 845588. Opt back in at any time by contacting pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk.
