title: LGPS Timeline: During your Membership
headings: Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: DURING YOUR MEMBERSHIP Explore
body: With your LGPS pension, you don’t need to worry about investment options or market performance - you’ll get 1/49th of your pensionable earnings added to your annual pension while you work. However, you must still keep track of your pension value and keep personal details (including your beneficiaries) up to date using your Online Member Portal. Each year (sometime before 31st August) you will receive an ‘Annual Benefit Statement’ via your Member Portal. This is the best way for you to track the value of your pension. It will show the benefits you’ve earned so far and also give a forecast of what it would be worth if you continued at your current pay up until your normal retirement date, and indicate the tax-free lump sum you can choose to take. Consider options to add to your pension when you are able to do so. Having a Baby or Adopting? During paid occupational and statutory parental leave your pension will continue to build up as if you were working normally. However, you won’t build up pension at all during unpaid leave. This includes parental leave during the unpaid stages of Maternity, Adoption or Shared Parental (normally months 9 to 12). To top this up again, take a look at the 'Unpaid Leave' section below. Having a Baby or Adopting? During paid occupational and statutory parental leave your pension will continue to build up as if you were working normally. However, you won’t build up pension at all during unpaid leave. This includes parental leave during the unpaid stages of Maternity, Adoption or Shared Parental (normally months 9 to 12). To top this up again, take a look at the 'Unpaid Leave' section below. Unpaid Leave Your won’t build up pension during unpaid leave but may choose to top this up again with a Shared Cost Additional Pension Contribution or 'standard' Additional Pension Contribution. If you submit a request within 30 days of your unpaid leave ending, you are entitled to a 'Shared Cost Additional Pension' where you pay 1/3 of the cost, and the University contributes 2/3. Note that this 30 day window will not be extended, and it starts at the point you cease being on unpaid leave (even if you booked additional paid leave before coming back to work). To apply, first send a Hornbill Pay Enquiry to ask for a calculation of your lost earnings. You may then use this figure and apply using the LGPS 'Buy Lost Pension' calculator and application, which you should complete, download, and send again via a further Pay Enquiry. If you are outside the 30 day window, you may still purchase additional pension via normal methods, but the University will not contribute to the cost. Your won’t build up pension during unpaid leave but may choose to top this up again with a Shared Cost Additional Pension Contribution or 'standard' Additional Pension Contribution. If you submit a request within 30 days of your unpaid leave ending, you are entitled to a 'Shared Cost Additional Pension' where you pay 1/3 of the cost, and the University contributes 2/3. Note that this 30 day window will not be extended, and it starts at the point you cease being on unpaid leave (even if you booked additional paid leave before coming back to work). To apply, first send a Hornbill Pay Enquiry to ask for a calculation of your lost earnings. You may then use this figure and apply using the LGPS 'Buy Lost Pension' calculator and application, which you should complete, download, and send again via a further Pay Enquiry. If you are outside the 30 day window, you may still purchase additional pension via normal methods, but the University will not contribute to the cost. Membership Types While being in employment and contributing to the scheme, you are a ‘Current’ or ‘Active’ member. If you have left the scheme with more than 2 years’ service but are not in receipt of a pension then you are a ‘Past’ or ‘Deferred’ member. This could be due to leaving eligible employment or by opting out. If you are receiving an LGPS pension then you are a ‘Pensioner’, but could become ‘Active’ too if you come back to work later. While being in employment and contributing to the scheme, you are a ‘Current’ or ‘Active’ member. If you have left the scheme with more than 2 years’ service but are not in receipt of a pension then you are a ‘Past’ or ‘Deferred’ member. This could be due to leaving eligible employment or by opting out. If you are receiving an LGPS pension then you are a ‘Pensioner’, but could become ‘Active’ too if you come back to work later. title: LGPS Timeline: Adding to your Pension
headings: Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: ADDING TO YOUR PENSION Explore
body: You have the option to further increase the value of your pension by making additional payments when you feel able to do so. You can do this at any stage of your membership and it will often benefit you to do this as early as possible. Are you approaching retirement and want to boost your pension while you are still earning? Are you thinking about retiring early and want to offset some of the reductions you take when doing so? Are you looking to benefit even more from the tax savings granted on pension contributions? Do you have a projected pension you're aiming for and want to ensure you stay on track? Pensions are incredibly tax efficient, meaning that standard rate tax payers may save 20% on tax the contributions they make, and higher-rate payers can save 40%. When selecting added pension through our 'Salary Sacrifice' AVCs you can also add up to 8% in National Insurance savings on top of that. Of course, as with any investment you have to wait to see the return on investments, and with pensions the downside is having to wait until you retire (which for some will be a long time away!). However, for those that are in a position to do so, paying even more towards your pension is a great way to invest in your future. There are a few different options you have to consider for doing this. Additional Pension Contributions (APCs): Make extra contributions to your LGPS pension to increase your annual payments when you retire. Arrange them as regular or one-off payments, via monthly payroll or with direct payments to the scheme Additional Voluntary Contributions (AVCs): Invest in a separate flexible Prudential pension associated with LGPS Salary Sacrifice Shared Cost Additional Voluntary Contributions (AVCs): Invest in a separate flexible Prudential pension associated with LGPS and benefit from both tax and National Insurance savings too through 'My Money Matters' (formerly AVC Wise) DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: LGPS Timeline: Leaving the University
headings: Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: LEAVING THE UNIVERSITY Explore
body: If you are looking at employment options outside of the University, make sure you add Reward and Pensions to the equation. LGPS is one of the best schemes available, with the University paying over 20% of your salary in employer contributions towards your pension and all the other LGPS benefits you automatically receive as a member. If you do leave us before taking your pension, what happens next will depend on how long you were a member. If you have less than 2 years membership, this may mean your contributions could be refunded, transferred to another scheme or if you have over 2 years in the scheme your pension will become ‘deferred’ until you decide to retire and take your pension later. Find out what will happen in your circumstances on the Hants LGPS website. If you have been a member for long enough to stay in the scheme, after leaving you must still keep your personal details up to date (including your beneficiaries), and you’ll continue receiving an Annual Benefit Statement for your ‘deferred’ pension each year via the membership portal. Thinking of Transferring Out? after you leave?LGPS is one of the best (and safest) pension schemes available. Do some research, get some advice, know the risks, and make sure you understand what option is best for you. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: LGPS Timeline: Retirement
headings: Human Resources LGPS TIMELINE: RETIREMENT Explore
body: Deciding to retire is one of the biggest decisions you’ll make. Thankfully, LGPS offers different options to suit your circumstances. Age Retirement – your full pension paid when you retire and leave work on or after your normal pension age. If you choose to keep working, your pension will continue to increase but you must start taking it before turning 75. If you retire and decide you want to, you can still apply to return to UoP later under a new contract. Early Retirement – leave work and take the pension you’ve earned so far before your normal pension age. Because your pension is being paid to you earlier than expected, the annual amount will be reduced as it’s being paid earlier and for a longer period. You may retire as early as age 55, increasing to age 57 in April 2028. If you have membership in an LGPS Final Salary scheme, this part of your pension has a retirement age of 65, which may be earlier than your Career Average section 'normal pension age'. Where this is the case, reductions would be worked out separately based on each scheme age, so it's possible to take your Final Salary at 65 or later without reduction, with only the Career Average part reduced if you haven't reached the Career Average date at that time. If you have been in LGPS a long time, part of your pension may be protected from these early retirement reductions under what is called the '85-year rule'. If you were a member before 1 October 2006, have reached age 60, and your combined age and length of membership (in whole years) add up to 85 or more you should speak with LGPS to confirm what this means for you. Flexible Retirement – some or all of the pension you’ve earned is paid from age 55 or older when you continue to work while reducing salary by 40% (by dropping hours and/or grade) for a period of 12 or more months. This is a form of ‘Partial’ or ‘Phased’ retirement and, as you are changing your contract to be able to do it, you must have management agreement as well. You’ll stay in LGPS as you carry on working, building up extra pension to be taken when you retire again. Retirement due to Ill-Health or Redundancy? – if this may be relevant to you, HR will be in touch to let you know what it means in your circumstances and guide you through the process. So what is the best for me?Unfortunately, this is not a question the University or LGPS is able to answer. However, there is a lot of information available which should mean you can make an informed decision in almost all circumstances. If your situation isn't clear, or you want to make absolutely certain you are maximising the best available options, we would always recommend you seek an Independent Financial Advisor. The first step in the retirement process is the hardest – deciding when you want to retire and knowing that it’s affordable and the right choice for your circumstances. It’s made a little easier knowing you are in one of the best workplace pensions available, but there is still a lot to consider: Make sure you understand the scheme, how it works, and what options are available. You can use these pages as a place to start and follow the links to find out more, and there are many other resources to help with pensions. Keep track of your LGPS value using your Annual Benefit Statements each September. Once you are thinking of retiring within the following 18 months, you can also request a mid-year estimate but you won’t get a guaranteed final quote until you actually apply to retire. Review your options for a Tax-Free Lump Sum. You can exchange £1 of Annual Pension for £12 as a lump sum, up to the maximum shown on your Annual Benefit Statement (the maximum is typically 4.28 times your annual pension amount but this may change if you also have final salary or added pension - your Annual Benefit Statements should give a prediction for you). An estimate will give you one quote for taking the minimum lump sum (which will be £0 unless you have LGPS membership prior to 2008) and the maximum amount you can exchange. If you want to look at options between the minimum and maximum, LGPS provide a helpful calculator here. Think about tax. The pension you’ll be paid each month is taxable like normal income (but you won’t pay NI on it). This means that if you have other sources of income at the same time you could be paying tax on everything you get from your pension. Understand your state pension and prior workplace and personal pensions too, and that they could potentially be taken at different dates than LGPS. If you have added pensions with LGPS, such as AVCs, you should know the value and your options for these too. Try to get a good idea of how much money you need and how much you want for your life in retirement. You can read information on this online, such as unbiased or Retirement Living Standards, or talk to an Independent Financial Advisor. Once you have made your decision to retire, you should consider the following processes: Inform Your Management If you are retiring and leaving work, you are resigning too. You should give at least your contractual notice for your retirement and ideally more so we can initiate your pensions process and prepare for your departure. If you are seeking Flexible Retirement where you’ll reduce your grade/hours but carry on working, you’ll need management agreement first to change your contract. You should confirm your retirement date in writing to your Line Manager. Line Manager Action: Your manager should follow all the normal exit actions for a leaver, submitting a copy of your notice to retire and informing HR via the People Hub (using 'Advice & Requests - Managers' then 'Notification of Leavers'), and making sure any balance of annual leave is settled in payroll. Line Manager Action (Flexible Retirement Only): Line Managers must agree new contractual hours and/or grade which reduce your pensionable pay by at least 40%. This must then be forwarded to HR Pensions (pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk) by the line manager for review, and the new hours, grade and working patterns must be sent by the manager to HR via the People Hub. Decide on Added Pension AVCs (if you have them) If you do have an AVC, in order to pay your pension LGPS must communicate with Prudential and receive all money being used to top up your pension. As you’ll contribute AVCs in your payslip up until your last paid day, moving the money around can take up to 8 weeks after leaving which can cause a delay to your pension being finalised. That’s not a problem if you don’t mind the delay and wish to maximise your AVCs, but if you want or need pension or lump sum payments sooner, one potential way to speed this up is to choose to end your AVC payments one month before your last month so this part can be finalised with LGPS earlier. Submit Retirement Declaration Form to LGPS Use the member portal to submit your application to LGPS once you are within 3 months of retirement, confirming your personal details and the pension options you’ve chosen. Payroll will then update LGPS with your final confirmed earnings so they have the latest information available to them. LGPS will send you your statement and confirm payment dates for your lump sum and monthly pension. You’ll get these as soon as possible but there can be unforeseen delays – however, even where payment is delayed, it should still be fully backdated to your retirement date so you won’t lose out. If you have any issues or wish updates on the progress of your retirement, you should enquire with LGPS directly. Retain a copy of the UoP Pensions Team email (pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk) for after you leave. If there's anything we need to do or if anything goes wrong, you can still get in touch. title: LGPS Annual Benefit Statements
headings: Human Resources LGPS Annual Benefit Statements - Checking and Understanding your Pension Benefits Explore
body: LGPS members can check a summary of their pension growth each year with the Annual Benefit Statement. This is the best way for you to track the value of your pension. It will show the benefits you’ve earned so far and also give a forecast of what it would be worth if you continued your membership until your normal retirement date, and indicates the maximum tax-free lump sum you could choose to take. These are published online through your online Member Portal by 31 August each year, covering the previous financial year (April through March). If you didn't join the scheme until after 31 March, you won't have a statement until next year. Our LGPS Administrator Hampshire Pension Services offer instructions and support for accessing and understanding your statement so be sure to use the below links if there's anything you aren't familiar with: Sign in or Register for your Member Portal (queries can be sent using the “My Message” system once logged in) Help logging in or to request your statement in an alternative format - pensions@hants.gov.uk Understanding the numbers and FAQ - https://www.hants.gov.uk/hampshire-services/pensions/local-government/about-the-scheme/annual-benefit-statements PowerPoint guide and walkthrough - https://documents.hants.gov.uk/pensions/Yourpension-yourfuture-ABS-presentation2025.pptx We'd encourage you to look through and compare with your statement for the previous year, if you have one. Remember that any additional earnings or changes to pay you've had since 31 March won't be accounted for, nor any pension increase for cost-of-living inflation. Additionally, give some thought to the projection of your pension at your expected retirement date (whether that's soon or in the distant future) and consider whether you wish to boost your benefits by purchasing extra pension. title: LGPS & Absences
headings: Human Resources LGPS & Absences Explore
body: Family Leave (Maternity, Paternity, Shared Parental, Adoption, Parental Bereavement) During paid or statutory paid family leave, you will continue to earn pension at your usual (unreduced) rate. You will still pay contributions on whatever pay you do receive. Periods of unpaid Family does not count as pensionable service and pension will not accrue during while you are unpaid. However, you may elect to buy back your ‘Lost Pension’ during this period (see below). If you are in the 50/50 arrangement (where you pay half contributions for half the pension accrual) and move to nil pay for any reason while still in ‘Ordinary’ family leave periods (normally the first 26 weeks), you’ll automatically be moved back to the main (full) arrangement even though you won’t be making contributions. You may, however, choose to elect back into the 50/50 arrangement again following your return to work. Other Family Leave (Antenatal, Premature Birth, Neonatal) Paid period for time off as a result of Antenatal, Premature Birth, or Neonatal care will be pensionable, while unpaid periods will not be pensionable. You may elect to buy back any ‘Lost Pension’ during the unpaid period (see below). Keeping in Touch (KIT Days / SPLIT Days) 'KIT Days' and 'Shared Parental Leave In Touch Days' are credited as pensionable service. You will pay pension contributions on whatever you earn. While you are on authorised sick leave, your pension will keep accruing at normal rate and will count as pensionable service – even while you are not being paid. You will still pay contributions on whatever pay you do receive. If you are in the 50/50 arrangement (where you pay half contributions for half the pension accrual) when you move to nil-pay leave, you’ll automatically be moved back to the main (full) arrangement. You may, however, choose to elect back into the 50/50 arrangement again following your return to work. Reserved Forces Leave You will continue to build pension during periods of reserved forces leave. Contributions will continue to be paid by you based on your normal or ‘assumed’ University earnings but they will be deducted from the pay you receive from the Ministry of Defence while deployed. Any unpaid period while on jury service will not count toward your pension. However, you may elect to buy back your ‘Lost Pension’ during this period (see below). Time spent on strike does not count as pensionable service. Where the day is unpaid or at reduced pay, pensionable earnings, contributions, and accruals will be reduced accordingly. You may ‘buy back’ the lost pension with the purchase of Additional Pension Contributions (APCs) (see below). Authorised unpaid leave will not count towards your pension. However, you may elect to buy back your ‘Lost Pension’ during this period (see below). Buying Back Lost Pension following Unpaid Absence You can purchase Additional Pension Contributions (APCs) to buy back any lost pension during unpaid leave. If you are buying back ‘authorised’ absence (such as Family leave or authorised unpaid leave), the University will share the cost of buy-back, providing you apply within 30 days of the unpaid period ending. You will pay only the lost member contributions, while we pay the employer part. If you buy back other absences (such as Strike Days) you will have to pay both the member and employer parts yourself, so will be significantly more expensive. To apply for this, submit a People Hub ‘Pay Query’ request, where you will be provided the ‘Lost Pensionable Pay’ you have missed due to absence. You then use the online calculator to work out what it would cost you, and at the end of the calculation, you have an opportunity to complete and application form which should be sent again by People Hub ‘Pay Query’. You extra contributions will be set up in the next available pay month. Unpaid Leave while you are buying extra pension benefits Your extra pension arrangements may be affected by unpaid leave. This may include losing their benefits, extending payment periods, or reducing their value. You should read more about your specific arrangement (see ‘Away from work and paying extra’) and talk with Hants LGPS directly if you have further questions. https://www.hants.gov.uk/hampshire-services/pensions/local-government/current-members/change-to-member-circumstances/absences https://www.lgpsmember.org/your-pension/paying-in/if-you-are-away-from-work/ DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: LGPS Ill Health Retirement
headings: Human Resources LGPS Ill Health Retirement LGPS Official Guidance and Policy Ill Health Retirement Application Process Ill Health Retirement - Tiers of Payment Ill Health Retirement Estimates Payment if Approved Explore
body: Employees with at least 2 years of membership in the Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS) who become unable to continue working due to illness may qualify for ‘Ill Health Retirement’ (IHR). Assessment for IHR is done by an independent occupational health physician appointed by our occupational health provider, who considers medical evidence and assesses your immediate and future prospects to undertake work. The independent assessor will issue a report to UoP, who make a final decision on IHR in accordance with pension scheme rules. If approved, the next step will be agreeing a final day of service and your ill-health pension will start the day after you leave. You should read through the Hants LGPS and LGPS Member pages, which contain information on eligibility and terms, appeals and the official scheme guidance. It is likely that those considering IHR will already be in discussion with management, HR, and our Occupational Health provider about their health and attendance. If you think this might apply to you, you should raise this with them in the first instance, and you will be guided through the process by a member of HR. To be approved for IHR, you have to be assessed as: Being permanently incapable of carrying out your current job Having a reduced likelihood of being able to take gainful employment (working 30 hours a week in any job for at least a year) before your normal pension age How much ‘reduced likelihood’ there is will inform a ‘Tier’ of IHR which determines how much and for how long your pension may be paid. Assessment: Unable to work in your current role again with no reasonable prospect to be capable of other gainful employment before Normal Pension Age Annual Pension Value: The pension you would have had if you'd continued working and earning pension up to your Normal Pension Age, with no reductions for being paid early Duration: Paid for the rest of your life Assessment: Unable to work in your current role again and unlikely to be capable of other gainful employment until three or more years after leaving Annual Pension Value: The pension you’ve earned in employment, plus 25% of what you would have had if you worked up to your Normal Pension Age, with no reductions for being paid early Duration: Paid for the rest of your life Assessment: Unable to work in your UoP role(s) again but likely to be capable of other gainful employment within three years of leaving Annual Pension Value: The pension you've earned in employment, with no reductions for being paid early Duration: Paid temporarily until your become capable of gainful employment, or for a maximum of three years No pension entitlement, but members who have reached their minimum pension age (55, increasing to 57 in April 2028) can still take normal voluntary early retirement, subject to usual reductions due to taking it early (contact pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk to request an estimate). If you haven’t reached minimum pension age yet or don’t wish to take normal early retirement, your LGPS pension will be deferred until you are eligible to retire and choose to do so. If your condition worsens after you leave and need to apply again you can do so by contacting Hants LGPS for guidance on how to do this It is not possible for members to request their own IHR estimates as it is an employer decision to assess the eligibility and tier of an ill-health retirement. This means UoP will not typically be able to request formal estimates of pension value until these have been determined. However, if you are early in the process or still considering whether you need to pursue ill health retirement, you can still get a possible indication of what each tier would mean for you by using your latest Annual Benefit Statement from LGPS. These statements get updated annually before the end of August, and give a statement of your pension value up to 31 March of that year. If you haven’t looked at these yet or want help understanding what it means for you, Hants LGPS provide guidance on their website including ‘Your pension – your future. Your Annual Benefit Statement explained’ which you should read through first. On page 1 of the statement, find the section below and note the value for 'Estimated benefits at 31 March 20xx': Next, find the 'Projection of your benefits to your Normal Pension Age (NPA)' figures in the section below and note the ‘Benefits with no pension exchanged’ value as at your NPA: To make a rough calculation for the annual pension value for each Tier of IHR, do as follows: Estimated at the 'Projection of your benefits to your Normal Pension Age (NPA)' - ‘Standard annual pension’ value Take the ‘Estimated benefits at 31 March 20xx’ – ‘Standard annual pension’ value then add one quarter of the difference between the values of 'Projection of your benefits to your Normal Pension Age (NPA)' and ‘Estimated benefits at 31 March 20xx’ Use the ‘Estimated benefits at 31 March 20xx’ – ‘Standard annual pension’ value only Please bear in mind that the amounts quoted Annual Benefit Statements are from 31 March of the quoted year only so will be slightly be out of date. Further, LGPS advise these values are not ‘formal’ estimates and should not be used to make financial decisions. However, they should still provide a sufficient early indication of what each IHR tier could mean for you were it to be approved. If IHR is approved at any Tier, the next step is to determine a leaving date – HR will discuss this with you. Your Ill Health pension will be paid with effect from the next day after you leave, but be aware that Hants LGPS advise it can take around 6 weeks to get that payment to you. However, if it arrives later than your retirement date they will backdate the payments to that day in the first payment. When a last day is agreed, you’ll need to complete a Retirement Declaration form on your LGPS Member Portal and we’ll send them your IHR confirmation and final pay details. title: Buying Extra Pension
headings: Human Resources Buying Extra Pension Explore
body: Teachers' Pension offer a few different ways to add extra value to your pension benefits. Read through the options below to see if any suit your circumstances. Faster Accrual allows you to accrue pension at a higher proportion of your salary by increasing the contributions you pay each month. This is done over a whole year between April and March, and you typically need to apply no later than January to guarantee it will be processed in time for the coming year. Buying Additional Pension allows you to boost your Annual Pension value. You can buy Additional Pension by either paying a lump sum or regular contributions deducted from your earnings. You have to purchase a minimum of a £250 increase to Annual Pension (paid every year of retirement), so bear in mind that this will cost multiple times that amount to purchase, but thankfully you can split the cost over multiple years if you need to. The cost will also vary depending on how old you are at point of purchase. Teachers’ provide a calculator online to help work this out. Additional Voluntary Contributions (AVC) The Teachers’ Pension AVC scheme allows you to build up a separate flexible pension pot with Prudential. You’ll make separate contributions from your monthly earnings, the amount you contribute is invested according to your choices and its value can go up and down over time depending on how well those investments preform. These AVCs are linked to TP, but you don’t have to take them at the same time as your normal benefits, and can even take them flexibly while continuing to work. You may also vary how much you pay in each month to suit your circumstances. Find further information and the Prudential scheme guidance via the Teachers’ Pension website. Buy Out (First 6 months of membership only) ‘Buy Out’ allows you to take your ‘Career Average’ pension (which everyone is now in, even if you had a Final Salary pension previously) earlier, without any reductions due for Early Retirement. The normal retirement date for Career Average is linked to your state pension age, but purchasing Buy Out allows you to draw it up to three years earlier (but no sooner than your 65th birthday). You have only one opportunity to purchase a Buy Out, and it must be done within six months of first joining the career average scheme. This is a long-term decision, as you’ll pay the cost with extra contributions until you reach that retirement age. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: TPS Ill Health Retirement
headings: Human Resources Explore
body: Teachers’ Pension Ill Health Retirement Members with at least 2 years of qualifying service in the scheme who may be permanently unable to continuing teaching due to illness may be eligible for Ill Health Retirement (IHR). Members with between one and two years of service are not eligible for IHR but may apply for the benefits earned so far as a lump sum payment if they are in ‘serious ill health’ (see below). It is important you check you have access to your Teachers’ Pension ‘MyPensionOnline’ account and that all your personal details and beneficiaries are up-to-date as you begin this process. Scheme Guidance and Policy You should read through the Teachers’ Pension IHR pages, which contain the official information on eligibility and terms, forms to complete, appeals and the scheme guide. It is likely that those considering IHR will already be in discussion with management, HR, or our Occupational Health provider about their health and attendance. If you think IHR might apply to you, you should raise this with them in the first instance, so you can be supported through the process. Applications must be based on medical evidence. This may be from assessment with the University’s occupational health provider, or from your own personal doctors or qualified assessors. If you have decided to apply for IHR, you are required to fill in two PDF forms to send to us and your medical assessor(s). We recommend reading the forms through entirely as they contain helpful instructions and guidance that isn’t easily found in the other policy documents. If necessary, an appropriate representative may complete the member sections on your behalf but you must still sign the form yourself unless they have power of attorney or court of protection. Medical Information Form – You complete Part A and send Part B to our independent occupational health practitioner and/or any other medical assessor(s) who will evidence their assessment. If you are including evidence from multiple assessors, you will need to send each one a separate copy of the form. They will send the form(s) directly back to you. Ill Health Retirement Application Form – You complete Part A and B then send a scanned copy to pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk or paper copy by post to University of Portsmouth Pensions, Floor 6 Mercantile House, Hampshire Terrance, Portsmouth, PO1 2EG. We then add your employment details in part C. It is important you send the entire form to us, not just Part C. TP require all forms to be sent to them in one package, so once the forms are complete, you can either send the Medical Information Form(s) to us and we’ll send them on your behalf, or you can ask us to return the completed Ill Health Retirement Application Form and you can send them yourself. If you choose to send us your Medical Information Form(s), it must be in paper copy and is important you do so in a clearly marked sealed envelope as the Pensions team should not view your personal medical data. It may be helpful to bundle any paperwork in a sealed envelope enclosed within another envelope, with a covering letter to avoid anything being opened accidentally. We send IHR correspondence to the scheme by recorded or special delivery, and would recommend you do so too, keeping personal copies of everything you send to us or them. Ill Health Retirement Outcomes Teachers’ Pension Scheme’s own medical advisers will consider the medical information and evidence provided by you and determine an outcome and, if appropriate, a ‘tier’ of IHR that will be awarded. You will be informed of the outcome directly by the scheme. The University cannot influence this decision but will support you wherever we can with any next steps. Tier 1 (Partial Incapacity): You are assessed as being unable to carry out any further teaching work but are expected to be able to undertake other types of work up to your normal retirement age. Your pension will be paid from the date of leaving employment, calculated the on the pension you’ve earned without any reductions applied for it being paid early (known as ‘actuarial reduction’). Payment will cease if you return to teaching employment before your normal retirement age. If approved, any form of teaching you were still doing must cease immediately and a leaving date arranged with HR. Tier 2 (Total Incapacity): You are assessed as being unable to carry out any teaching or any other ‘gainful employment’ (meaning your ability to work at all is likely to be permanently impaired by at least 90%) up to your normal retirement age. Your pension will be paid from the date of leaving employment, calculated the on the pension you’ve earned in employment plus 50% of the predicted pension you may have received if you’d been able to continue teaching until your normal pension age. No reductions are applied for it being paid early (known as ‘actuarial reduction’). If approved, any form of teaching you were still doing must cease immediately and a leaving date arranged with HR. Ill Health Retirement Not Approved: If IHR is not approved, the scheme’s Medical Advisors will notify you in writing and may propose further treatments, adjustments or other considerations to try. You may choose to apply again later following further assessment, or if your condition worsens. You may appeal the decision. Details on the appeals process are contained within the scheme guidance. Serious Ill Health: Life Expectancy of less than 12 months You may request to have your pension converted to a one-off final tax-free lump sum payment. We can request a Serious Ill Health Estimate to enable you take the best decision. Read more on serious ill health commutation. Less than 2 Years’ Membership with Serious Ill Health You may qualify for a short service serious ill health grant if you have between one and two qualifying years of service and a life expectancy of less than 12 months. If you have less than 1 year’s membership, you are not yet eligible. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: TPS & Absences
headings: Human Resources Teachers' Pension & Absences Explore
body: Family Leave (Maternity, Paternity, Shared Parental and Adoption) The period when you are on family leave remains pensionable as long as you receive at least half pay or statutory pay. Pension contributions will be deducted using the same contribution rate as your usual salary, but the percentage only applies to your actual pensionable earnings in the period. Should your pay drop below half pay and you aren't receiving statutory pay, it is deemed you are no longer be in pensionable service. You won’t accrue further pension during the ‘not in pensionable service’ periods but you should still count as ‘in service’ for applications of Ill Health or Retirement and Death in Service calculations providing you have been ‘not in pensionable service’ for less than 12 months. Other Family Leave (Antenatal, Premature Birth, Neonatal, Parental Bereavement) Paid period for time off as a result of Antenatal, Premature Birth, Neonatal, or Parental Bereavement will be pensionable, while unpaid periods will not be pensionable. Sick leave is deemed pensionable service as long as you are paid at least half of your salary relating to that employment. Your usual contribution rate is used but this applies to actual pensionable earnings in the period only. Sick leave will no longer be pensionable service once your pay falls below half of your usual salary (including statutory-only periods). You won’t pay contributions on less-than-half pay either. You won’t accrue further pension during the ‘not in service’ periods but you should still count as ‘in service’ for applications of Ill Health or Retirement and Death in Service calculations providing you have not been ‘not in service’ for less than 12 months. Keeping in Touch (KIT Days / SPLIT Days) Where you are paid at full or at statutory parental pay, KIT days will be pensionable. If you do not receive payment, or receive a nominal payment not related to your salary, these are not pensionable. Time spent on strike does not count as pensionable service. Where the day is unpaid or at reduced pay, pensionable earnings, contributions, and accruals will be reduced accordingly. However, Death Grant payments will remain at ‘in service’ rates if you die while on strike. Unpaid Special Leave (emergency family leave or sabbaticals) The period of unpaid authorised leave is not pensionable. The period you are away from work will be recorded as being out of service, and will not count towards your pension. title: MyPensionPlus - Benefit Options
headings: Human Resources MyPensionPlus - Flexible Benefit Option Modules Eligibility to select benefits Selecting Your Benefits Flexible Benefits Guide Briefing Sessions Options Form Consider Your Pension Payments Looking for last year's guidance? Need Help Or Have Something To Say? Explore
body: This page is for current members of the MyPensionPlus (MPP) pension and benefits scheme. If you are not sure whether this applies to you, check your contract and payslip to see what scheme you are paying into - if you see contributions for 'MPP' or 'MyPensionPlus' on your latest payslip, this page is for you. Where you are a member of another scheme, please instead return to our Reward front page to see the many other benefit options available to you. This page is for current members of the MyPensionPlus (MPP) pension and benefits scheme. If you are not sure whether this applies to you, check your contract and payslip to see what scheme you are paying into - if you see contributions for 'MPP' or 'MyPensionPlus' on your latest payslip, this page is for you. Where you are a member of another scheme, please instead return to our Reward front page to see the many other benefit options available to you. If you are an active member of MyPensionPlus, you must select one of a range of additional benefit options each year, at no cost to yourself, to personalise your scheme as suits you best. These benefits take effect from 1 April and apply for a full year while you remain a member. You will be asked to make your selection in an annual Options Window, opening each January for all members who joined the scheme on or before 31 December, and who are expected to remain in post beyond 1 April. If you don't make a choice, or if you joined the scheme outside the eligibility for the Options Window, then you will remain in our default benefit option (see latest guidance below) until the following year's Options Window opens. These options may change each year as new benefits may become available, so be sure to read the latest guide as it becomes available. During this window, you also have an opportunity to make changes to how, and how much, you pay into another big part of MyPensionPlus - the pension itself. We'll confirm your selection after the Options Window closes, and be in touch later to provide any necessary details or instructions about your particular benefit when it goes live 1 April. We will email all eligible members each January, letting you know when the window will open. You should read through our 2026-27 Flexible Benefit Options Guide. The Briefing Sessions for 2026-27 have concluded. For those still seeking further information, please use our full guidance document. You may also view the slides from this session here. Once you've made your decision, fill out our Options Form to confirm your selection. Don't want to change anything? Don't ignore this step - you must still complete a form to make your selection each year, even if you don't want to change anything. The decision you make only covers a single year, so if you don't make a new one you'll be moved to the default option and won't be able to change it later. Don't want to change anything? Don't ignore this step - you must still complete a form to make your selection each year, even if you don't want to change anything. The decision you make only covers a single year, so if you don't make a new one you'll be moved to the default option and won't be able to change it later. The Options Form will also provide an opportunity to increase or reduce the monthly payments you make to your pension for the coming year. Note that MyPensionPlus offers a matching arrangement for contributions too, so increasing or decreasing your payments may mean we will increase or decrease ours. You may also switch the way you pay pension contributions from Salary Sacrifice to Basic Deductions, or from Basic to Salary Sacrifice (so long as you earn enough to be legally able to do so). Generally, paying via Salary Sacrifice will be beneficial for most as you save on National Insurance (as well as tax) on whatever you contribute. If you are considering changes to your pension be sure to find out more about the MPP pension on our intranet pages, and by reading and watching the pensions sections of our Option Guides. For reference, last year's full guidance document is still available here. For last year's Health Check documents, see Members and Friends & Family. If you have any queries, suggestions or feedback along the way, contact reward@port.ac.uk, or if you have a question specifically about the pension elements of your scheme email pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. title: MyPensionPlus Timeline: Becoming a Member
headings: Human Resources MyPensionPlus Timeline: Becoming a Member Opting In: Not yet in the scheme or opted out previously and now want to join? Explore
body: New Employment Contracts: MyPensionPlus is our benefits and pension scheme for University of Portsmouth Academic Services Limited (UASL) employees who are salaried (not paid via fees or timesheets) on a permanent/indefinite or fixed-term basis. If you are not sure if you are eligible, check your contract and look at the section for ‘Pension’. The terms of enrolment will depend on the duration of your contract. If your employment contract is for more than six months durationYou will be contractually enrolled in the scheme from your contract start date If your employment contract is for a duration of less than six monthsYou will not be contractually enrolled when you start, but may voluntarily opt into the pension scheme at any time If you start on a contract for less than six months but are later extended beyond thisIf you are not already in the scheme at this point, you will be contractually enrolled from the 1st day of the month following extension You are not eligible to join if you are aged 75 or over, or if you are not normally resident in the UK. You may alternatively be automatically enrolled, where you were not in a pension but met the government’s assessment to be auto-enrolled. This happens where you: were not, at time of assessment, in a pension scheme for this employment have not previously opted-out of a pension in this employment, or opted-out more than 12 months before an auto-enrolment ‘staging date’, which happens every 3 years earned over a minimum amount (currently £833) in a single month are aged between 22 and your State Pension Age We assess people for auto enrolment from the third month of employment. We'll always let you know if you are ever auto enrolled. Additional Day 1 Benefits Death Benefit: When you join the scheme, we'll automatically enrol all members who are younger than State Pension Age into a company 'Death Benefit' life insurance scheme, paying three times your annual salary to your beneficiaries in the event of your death. You'll receive a letter detailing this and instructing you to declare you beneficiaries for this scheme at the earliest opportunity. This scheme is not subject to medical assessment and there are no exclusions for pre-existing conditions. Extra Pension: When joining the scheme, you will be enrolled by default into the 'Extra Pension' flexible benefit, where we pay an additional pension payment into your scheme each month at no charge to yourself. You may keep this or select another 'Flexible Option Module' at each annual selection windows (January / February). See our Benefit Options for further detail on the flexible benefits available to you. Under UK pension regulations you can't choose not to be enrolled when you first become eligible, and you can't opt out before you take up a new contract with us. We would always recommend you stay in a pension scheme wherever possible as it's one of the best ways you can invest in your future, but if you do need to consider opt outing after joining, you can. When you join the scheme, you will make contributions to your pension via one of two options - 'Salary Sacrifice' or 'Basic Deductions'. The default and most beneficial option for most is to pay via Salary Sacrifice, but you can choose to switch to the Basic model if you wish. Find out more about what these options mean for you on our Contributions page. Opting In: Not yet in the scheme or opted out previously and now want to join? Just let us know you want to opt in by emailing pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. You can start as soon as the first day of the next calendar month. Just let us know you want to opt in by emailing pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. You can start as soon as the first day of the next calendar month. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: MyPensionPlus Timeline: Contributions
headings: Human Resources MyPensionPlus Timeline: Pension Contributions Paying your Contributions Explore
body: We have partnered with Aviva to provide their ‘Workplace Pension’ to all ‘MyPensionPlus’ members. You will contribute a percentage of your salary towards your pension, and we do the same. These funds are then invested according to your choices. From the first day you join, we will pay employer contributions of 12% of your pensionable salary to your pension. You'll pay a contribution on your salary too, which will default to 6%. You'll benefit from an immediate tax saving on whatever you contribute, and your contributions have the chance to grow over time with investments. Changing how much you contribute and 'Employer Matching' You may choose to contribute as little as 3% of your pensionable salary each month. Where you can contribute more, we will match your extra contributions to a maximum employer amount of 15%. What counts as pensionable pay? Pensionable pay includes salary and London Allowance only. All other allowances, performance payments (including Living Our Values Awards), supplements and claimable elements are not pensionable. When you join the scheme, you will make contributions to your pension via one of two options - 'Salary Sacrifice' or 'Basic Deductions'. The default (and a beneficial option for most) is to pay via Salary Sacrifice, but you can choose to switch to the Basic model if you wish. Many members would benefit from the 'Salary Sacrifice' method by saving both tax and National Insurance (as opposed to just tax) on the amount they contribute to their pension. However, for some individuals (mostly those contracted to who work fewer hours per week), it may not be possible to make these savings under salary sacrifice tax rules, so they must be started on 'Basic Deductions' instead. Where applicable, you will be asked to choose an option when agreeing the terms of your contract, or when requesting to opt in. Where you join due to auto enrolment rules (see Becoming a Member for more details), you'll be started in our default Salary Sacrifice method if you are eligible to do so, but can choose to switch to Basic Deduction if you wish. Your choice will normally last for the duration of the scheme year (running April through March) but you may switch contribution options during each annual benefit selection window (January / February). We may exceptionally agree to permit changes at other times, such as following a significant contract or lifestyle change - contact pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. Contribution Option 1: Salary Sacrifice (Default) With salary sacrifice contributions, you don’t pay tax or national insurance on the amount you pay into your pension, resulting in higher take-home pay for most people. These savings happen automatically, whether you are a basic, higher, or additional rate taxpayer, with no need to make any claims on a self-assessment tax return to HMRC. Salary Sacrifice represents a contractual arrangement between you and UASL, where you agree to reduce your salary in return for an equal payment into your pension scheme. Your ‘pre-sacrificed’ salary will be used to determine pay awards, overtime, and other salary-related payments, as well as our employer pension contribution, so you won’t be disadvantaged. Varying your salary in this way may affect your eligibility for some statutory payments and state benefits, as your assessable earnings may be considered as being lower. If you receive credits or benefits from the government or expect you may apply for them, you should contact a relevant authority to clarify if these may be affected. Your salary may also be seen as lower for the purposes of loans and mortgages, although most vendors will credit this as a positive if you explain that you are paying pension via salary sacrifice terms. If you do not pay National Insurance (NI) due to being over State Pension Age, you may still use Salary Sacrifice but will not make additional savings from reduced NI contributions. Where Contribution Option 1: Salary Sacrifice is not available to you You cannot make contributions via the salary sacrifice option if doing so would reduce your salary below statutory minimums (such as National Minimum Wage, National Living Wage, Statutory Sick Pay, or Statutory Family Leave payments). You cannot make contributions via salary sacrifice if doing so reduces your pay below the HMRC 0% tax band (the earnings threshold where most workers start to pay tax), as you won't be able to benefit from increased savings. Where these circumstances apply to you, the salary sacrifice arrangement will cease (or will not be offered) from the beginning of the relevant month, and you will be compulsorily moved to the Basic Deductions contribution option (see below). You may be able to opt back into Salary Sacrifice contributions later if changes to your earnings, contributions or the tax threshold mean this no longer applies. To request this, please contact pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. You also may not be able to use Salary Sacrifice if you wish to pay a very large percentage of your earnings into your salary, such that it would reduce your salary below statutory minimums. Contribution Option 2: Basic Deductions Where Salary Sacrifice is not possible, or you elect not to use it, you will make contributions under the ‘Basic Deductions’ option. Your salary remains unsacrificed, and deductions are taken after tax. Unlike the Salary Sacrifice option, you must still pay national insurance (where applicable) on the amount you contribute. Tax relief is not provided at the point of payroll, but instead you’ll pay a contribution rate 20% lower than the agreed, then the pension scheme provider and HMRC provide the 20% tax relief as an additional payment direct to your pension. For example, if you pay 6% pension contributions, you’ll see only 4.8% (6 less 20%) paid on your payslip, and then HMRC and Aviva top up the remaining 1.2% to your account. You are granted this extra 20% whether you normally pay tax or not - giving benefit even for those with lower earnings or working fewer hours per week. This means for every £100 you contribute to your pension, you’ll get an extra £25 tax relief added by the HMRC. If you are a higher or additional rate taxpayer, you should also claim additional tax relief directly from HMRC via a self-assessment tax return. You have the flexibility to pay up to £3,600 or 100% of your taxable salary (whichever is higher) into your pension each year, but if you are paying very large amounts into your pension other limits may apply, such as the annual allowance. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: MyPensionPlus Timeline: Building Benefits
headings: Human Resources MyPensionPlus Timeline: Building your Pension Explore
body: The 'MyPensionPlus' Aviva Company Pension is a ‘defined contributions’ or 'money purchase' scheme. That means your own pot of money is invested according to choices you make, and has a chance to grow (and keep growing) until you choose to retire. The earlier you invest, the longer it has with a chance to grow – that means decisions you make near the start of your career will normally have even more impact than those nearer the end… You choose, from a range of options, how your funds are invested. This is an important decision, although you can change your options later. The fund or funds you invest in may have different aims and levels of ‘risk verses reward’, or may align with different ethics or beliefs. Aviva explains some background on how investment works in their scheme guide and their investment guide gives information on the different types and aims of investment funds and the levels of risk involved. A little confused? Don’t worry, you are in good company! Aviva aims to make these decisions as simple and transparent as possible. If you aren't ready to make a choice you can begin in a ‘default’ investment plan, which can be a good place to start until you want to consider further. The next most straightforward choice is to select one of Aviva’s ‘Investment Approaches’. These are a blend of investments which intend to align with broad intentions of a pension, such as short or long term focus, high or low risk, lump sums or regular monthly payments, and can automatically adapt over time depending on how close you are to retirement. Finally, more experienced investors may build their own profile by combining investments across different funds. Even if you do nothing at all, you can be confident you are in a default plan, good for most, in a very well regarded pension scheme, with a very sizable 12% employer contribution from us (with many employers offering the UK minimum of only 3%). However, we implore you to take the time to read the guidance - there's valuable information you should know, particularly if you don't have as much experience with pensions and investments, and the choices you make can really make a difference to your future. The ‘RISK’ versus the reward… A pension can be the best, safest and most tax-efficient way to save for your future. You'll already have benefited by tax savings (and possibly National Insurance too) just by making contributions. However, with all investments comes a risk that they can go down as well as up. Aviva gives its investment plans a ‘Risk Rating’ (detailed in the Investment Guide) which you should use to make your choices fit your needs and your own appetite for risk. Generally, if you are close to retirement you may wish to be in lower-risk investments, as you won't want a last-minute drop in the fund at the worst time. For those with many years to go, you may wish to consider a slightly higher risk plan as, even though they can fluctuate in value more, they tend to have a chance to increase more overall over a longer period of time. There are other places you can read more about pension basics and general information - we link to a few of these on our Pensions Awareness page - and if you're making significant or risky financial decisions, we'd always recommend you consider independent financial advice. Aviva take a small amount of your funds as charge(s) for managing your investments. These can change based on your investment choices but will be clear when you set them up. Aviva will let you know what these are and if they ever change. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: MyPensionPlus Timeline: After you Join
headings: Human Resources MyPensionPlus Timeline: After you Join Explore
body: First, check your onboarding correspondence or contract to see whether you'll start paying pension contributions via the Salary Sacrifice (the default for most) or Basic Deductions payments. Read our Contributions page to understand what this means and to consider whether you need to switch. Next, make sure you find some time to read through the Aviva Workplace Pension guide to understand the benefits of your Aviva scheme and how it works, and the Investment Guide to see what options you have to invest your pension. Registering Online We will set you up as a new member towards the end of the first month you'll be paying contributions in Aviva will normally send you two ‘welcome packs’ by post to your home address with your pension plan information, including instructions on how to opt out, if this is something you feel you need to consider (note that if you wish to opt out and want a refund, you’ll need to do this within 30 days of Aviva posting you the ‘welcome pack’ so will need to act quickly) Once you have your welcome packs, go to Aviva’s MyAccount to register for online access to your pension and follow the instructions on screen. Enter the Policy Number you have been provided, so it matches you up with our workplace scheme. It is recommended to use a personal email so access is readily available at home, and after you leave or retire Some of your personal details should be pre-filled, such as name, NI number and address. Aviva’s terms and conditions are that you must check these are correct when you first sign in You will be required to verify your account by providing a selfie-style photograph and a scan or photograph of a passport or driving licence You should nominate your beneficiaries as soon as possible to ensure your loved ones may be paid your benefits in the event of your death If you have any issues registering, you can contact Aviva's customer support helpline on 0800 145 5744. We will set you up as a new member towards the end of the first month you'll be paying contributions in Aviva will normally send you two ‘welcome packs’ by post to your home address with your pension plan information, including instructions on how to opt out, if this is something you feel you need to consider (note that if you wish to opt out and want a refund, you’ll need to do this within 30 days of Aviva posting you the ‘welcome pack’ so will need to act quickly) Once you have your welcome packs, go to Aviva’s MyAccount to register for online access to your pension and follow the instructions on screen. Enter the Policy Number you have been provided, so it matches you up with our workplace scheme. It is recommended to use a personal email so access is readily available at home, and after you leave or retire Some of your personal details should be pre-filled, such as name, NI number and address. Aviva’s terms and conditions are that you must check these are correct when you first sign in You will be required to verify your account by providing a selfie-style photograph and a scan or photograph of a passport or driving licence You should nominate your beneficiaries as soon as possible to ensure your loved ones may be paid your benefits in the event of your death If you have any issues registering, you can contact Aviva's customer support helpline on 0800 145 5744. Next, choose your initial investment options (or stay in the ‘default’ fund). You may also be able to transfer in previous pensions you have to Aviva, find out more on the scheme guidance and by checking with Aviva directly (you can ask a question through the website after logging in). If you are considering opting out and want a refund, you’ll need to do this within 30 days of Aviva posting you the ‘welcome pack’. Make sure you are aware of all the benefits and advantages of contributing to a pension before you decide what to do, which will be covered in your welcome pack. Meanwhile, you can read through Opting Out to find out more. title: MyPensionPlus Timeline: During your Membership
headings: Human Resources MyPensionPlus Timeline: During your Membership Explore
body: You should keep logging in regularly to see your contributions and investments, track how these perform and grow over time, make fund changes if you choose to, and always keep personal details up to date whenever they change. You’ll also get an Annual Statement to review each year summarising your contributions and investments. Flexible Benefit Options Your membership of MyPensionPlus affords you an additional Flexible Benefit Option at no cost to yourself. In January / February each year, you'll get to review the options available and pick the one that interests you most, to take effect from April to March. If you don't pick an option, or if you join outside the Options window, then you'll be enrolled in whatever our 'default' option is until the next year. Find out more here. Consider additional contributions If you are in a position to contribute more to your pension, it is one of the best ways to invest in your future. You can make one-off or regular payments directly to Aviva and immediately benefit from 20% tax relief added on top. You’ll pay these directly to Aviva from your personal savings or bank account. As you pay them separately, they won’t show up on your University payslip. Alternatively, you can increase the percentage of your earnings you contribute each month (saving NI costs as well as tax if you use Salary Sacrifice), directly from your salary. Depending on the rate you choose, we may pay extra to your account when you do too (see Contributions and Employer Matching). To do so, email pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk with your request and let us know the percentage you wish to pay. You can start this from as soon as start of the following month. There are some limits on the amount of tax you can save if you are making very significant additional pension payments - find out more on our Tax & Pensions page. Higher Rate Taxpayer using Basic Deduction contributions? If you pay income tax on your earnings at a Higher Rate or Additional Rate, and are making contributions via the Basic Deduction method, you should complete an annual self-assessment tax return with HMRC each year to claim back any additional tax you paid on your contributions. For contributions paid at 40% tax, 20% will have been added to your pension pot already, and you can claim an additional 20% back. There will not normally be a need to do this if you are paying contributions via the Salary Sacrifice method instead. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: MyPensionPlus Timeline: Opting Out
headings: Human Resources MyPensionPlus Timeline: Opting Out Explore
body: We recommend staying in a workplace pension wherever your circumstances allow it, as it’s one of the best ways to invest in your future. However, should you need to, you may choose to opt out of the scheme. If you do opt out, you will stop paying into your pension and we won’t pay in either, and you will lose access to all other benefits and selectable benefit options in the scheme, including ‘Death in Service’ Life Insurance. Please consider this carefully and make sure you choose the right option for your circumstances. Opting out within 30 days of registration: If you inform Aviva you wish to opt out within 30 days of registration, they’ll refund us any contributions you paid, and we’ll refund you in your next payslip. Note that the 30-day period starts on the date your second Aviva 'Welcome Pack' was sent (check the date on the letter), not when you receive it, so you should do this as quickly as possible to receive a refund (see After you Join for more details on this welcome pack, registering online, and finding any other details you need to opt out). This letter should normally be posted to the home address you have given us within a week or so of the first payroll you make contributions in (for example, if you are enrolled on the first of the month but paid on the 28th, you should normally get the letter shortly after the 28th, and it may even be dated on the 26th or 27th). If you are urgently seeking to opt out with a refund and haven't had this letter within the expected time, let us know at pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. Requests must be submitted via Aviva's opt out page or by calling 0800 145 5744 once you have your confirmed plan number (found on your 'Welcome Pack'). Please be aware we cannot do this on your behalf so you must go directly to Aviva. Exiting the scheme after 30 days: After 30 days, you aren’t technically ‘opting out’ but are leaving the scheme instead. You can request by submitting a People Hub ‘Pay Enquiry’ and can stop making contributions from as early as the following month. You won’t get a refund and past contributions will stay invested and continue to have the chance to grow over time. You may be able to ‘transfer out’ past contributions to another scheme, but should contact Aviva directly to do this. Re-joining the Scheme You can request to opt back in at any time by submitting a request to pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk, and your membership can restart from the following month. Additionally, we may be required to ‘auto-enrol’ you approximately every 3 years (see Becoming a Member for more details). Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: MyPensionPlus Timeline: Resignation
headings: Human Resources MyPensionPlus Timeline: Resignation Explore
body: If you are looking at employment options outside of the University, make sure you add Reward and Pensions to the equation. We are proud of our MyPensionPlus scheme, with us paying a starting rate of 12% employer contributions towards your pension with a Salary Sacrifice option, 3 times salary Death Benefit, a choice of flexible rewards at no additional cost, on top of all the other benefits you receive as an employee. If you do leave us before taking your pension, your employee and employer contributions will remain in your Aviva pension pot, and will continue to be invested with the same opportunity to keep growing until your retirement. You will not be able to pay new contributions via salary, and of course we will not contribute further either. However, you may convert our workplace pension to a ‘personal one’ and continue to make contributions directly to Aviva. There are some restrictions and minimum limits on this so check your options directly with Aviva. You may also wish to consider changing your investment options now your circumstances have changed – particularly if you had selected an Investment Plan focused on longer-term membership. You should still log in regularly after you leave to keep track of your pension value, update your personal details and beneficiaries, and to review your Annual Statements. Thinking of transferring from Aviva to another scheme after you leave?Do some research, know the risks, and we strongly advise you seek financial advice to make sure you understand what option is best for you. There are some restrictions on what schemes you may transfer to so check with Aviva first to see what is possible. title: MyPensionPlus Timeline: Retirement
headings: Human Resources MyPensionPlus Timeline: Retirement Explore
body: With your MyPensionPlus Aviva pension, you may choose to take retirement benefits whether you are intending to leave work or not, and even while you continue working full time (although this may mean a higher proportion of pension income could be taxable). The ‘Minimum Pension Age’ to be able to take your pension is currently 55 years old but due to increase to 57 on 5 April 2028. You must take your Aviva pension no later than your 75th birthday. There is no default retirement age under this scheme; however, pension scheme investment modelling will, by default, assume you intend to draw your pension at your state pension age. You will have several options when taking your pension, and may wish to seek free impartial advice from Pension Wise (part of the government-funded Money Helper service), from Aviva, or from an alternative advice service. You may draw all or only some of your pension, and you can decide how to do that by choosing a lump sum, taking individual payments as you need them, or by exchanging for regular income for life (known as an annuity). 25% of the total value of your pension(s) can be accessed ‘tax free’ when drawing lump sums or individual payments. If you purchase an annuity, then normal income tax applies. Let us know your intended retirement date (whether your are leaving employment or not) via a People Hub ‘Pay Enquiry’, and we’ll make sure everything gets done on our side. More information on your options and how this works is contained in the scheme guide or via the Aviva Help Line. title: MyPensionPlus & Absences
headings: Human Resources MyPensionPlus: Aviva Pension & Absences Explore
body: Family Leave (Maternity, Paternity, Shared Parental and Adoption Leave) During paid family leave you will pay contributions on the actual earnings you receive. While you are eligible for Statutory Family Leave pay, the University will contribute based on the ‘normal’ pay you had prior to going on reduced pay. If you move to unpaid leave, neither you nor the University will make further payments until you return to work. However, if you move to unpaid leave for any reason while still in ‘Ordinary’ family leave periods (normally the first 26 weeks), such as by not being entitled to statutory pay, the University will continue making its contributions while you don’t have to. Your funds will continue to be invested (including during unpaid periods) so may increase or fall in value depending on how the investment choices you have made perform. If you move to statutory pay only while using a 'Salary Sacrifice' contributions arrangement, this will cease and you'll move to 'Basic Deductions' instead. See below for further details. Other Family Leave (Antenatal, Premature Birth, Neonatal, Parental Bereavement) You will continue to make pension contributions on any earnings or statutory pay you receive. Keeping in Touch (KIT Days / SPLIT Days) You will pay contributions on payment for any KIT days during your Family Leave. You will continue to pay pension contributions during sickness absence on whatever earnings you are paid, and we will do same too. This includes any statutory sick pay you receive. If you move to unpaid sickness, you and we will not make contributions. Your funds will continue to be invested (including during unpaid periods) so may increase or fall in value depending on how the investment choices you have made perform.
