title: UASL Nest Timeline
headings: Human Resources Your UASL Nest TIMELINE - what you need to know about your pension How you Join - Starting your Contract How you Join - By Request How you Join - Auto-Enrolment Once you Join Leaving the Scheme Leaving the Scheme - Opting Out within One Month Leaving the Scheme - Stopping Further Contributions Leaving the Scheme - Re-Joining Later Scheme Features and Benefits What Counts as Pensionable Pay? What is the Annual Pension Allowance? Explore
body: You will not join Nest automatically from your start date; however, you can request to join at any time. Additionally, as you enter the third calendar month of a contract, we'll start assessing you under the government’s pension ‘auto-enrolment’ criteria and you will join Nest automatically if/when you qualify for it (see ‘Auto-Enrolment’ below). If you are eligible to join but are not in the scheme yet, you can ask us to enrol you. Please email your request to pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk, and you may join the scheme as soon as the first day of the following month. We will assess you for Automatic Enrolment in the third calendar month of your contract and will automatically enrol you if you qualify. Find out more at UASL Auto-Enrolment. You will receive a welcome pack in the post inviting you to sign up for NEST’s online member account. This pack will explain how the pension works and the options you have with your membership. It will normally be sent to your home address by post within five working days of them setting up your account. It will also provide details on how you can opt-out or leave the scheme, if you choose to. We would always recommend staying in a workplace pension if your circumstances allow it - it’s one of the best ways to invest in your future. However, if you can't or don’t want to be in a pension, what happens when you request to leave depends on how long you have been a member. There is an initial 'Opt-Out' period, which starts three working days after your enrolment date and last one month, during which time you may elect to opt-out. Your Welcome Pack from Nest will confirm the window you have to opt-out of the scheme and provide instructions on how to do so. You must notify Nest directly – we can’t do this for you. They will then close your account as if you were never a member and instruct us to refund of any contributions you already paid. You may still choose to stop contributing to your pension after the opt-out window has ended. Nest refers to this as a ‘break’ or ‘pause,’ but you’ll stop all contributions unless you join again later. You won’t receive a refund; all contributions will stay invested until you retire. If you opt-out or withdraw from Nest, you can re-join at any time while you are still working here. You can choose to start and stop payments as you wish by sending us an email at pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. When your Nest ‘welcome pack’ arrives, you should read through it carefully; it will contain most of the additional information you’ll need. You can also get all the details on your pension scheme, including investment and retirement options, from their website, including the section on guidance and advice, or contact them directly via their support and contact. Nest Homepage – the scheme webpage to learn more and access to your pension Member Portal & Guidance to use it – update your personal details and beneficiaries, manage your contributions and apply to retire The core features of the UASL Nest scheme, including what you and we pay towards your pension, is shown below: Standard (Default) Contributions Rates Your Contribution: 3% (you pay 2.4% of your salary, then NEST add on 0.6% tax relief. Your Contribution: 3% Increasing Contribution Rates You may voluntarily increase the rate of your salary contributions by emailing your request to pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. You’ll still get 20% tax relief (subject to HMRC limits) on whatever extra you pay, or more if you are a higher earner (see ‘Additional Tax Relief’ below). In addition to contributing through your salary, Nest offers flexibility to make additional contributions through one-off payments directly to the scheme, or by setting up a regular direct debit from your bank. You may need to consider HMRC's limits on tax relief (see Member contributions and Annual Allowance), particularly if you are making very large additional contributions or are paying in more than you earn. You may voluntarily increase the rate of your salary contributions by emailing your request to pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. You’ll still get 20% tax relief (subject to HMRC limits) on whatever extra you pay, or more if you are a higher earner (see ‘Additional Tax Relief’ below). In addition to contributing through your salary, Nest offers flexibility to make additional contributions through one-off payments directly to the scheme, or by setting up a regular direct debit from your bank. You may need to consider HMRC's limits on tax relief (see Member contributions and Annual Allowance), particularly if you are making very large additional contributions or are paying in more than you earn. Our contribution fixed at 6% Our contribution fixed at 6% Additional Tax Relief Additional Tax Relief If you are paying 40% income tax or more on any of your earnings), then you can claim additional tax relief as part of a self-assessment tax return. See the section ‘Claiming tax relief yourself’ on gov.uk. You can take pension benefits from age 55 (increasing to 57 on 6 April 2028). You have flexible retirement options, such as lump sums and regular payments. You don’t have to stop working when you claim them. When making these decisions, you can get free impartial advice from Pension Wise (part of the government-funded Money Helper service) or look for an alternative or paid service instead. The scheme does not include death in service or life insurance cover. You may wish to consider taking out a private policy. Nest allows members to move money from other pensions into the scheme. If you are considering transferring funds between pension schemes, you are strongly advised to seek independent professional advice. Your pension contributions and those we pay will be based on your monthly pensionable pay multiplied by the relevant contribution rate. Pensionable pay includes salary (basic pay) and London Allowance only. If you receive automatic payments for annual leave as part of your hourly claims (known as ‘rolled-up holiday pay’), instead of booking time off separately, this is also pensionable. All other allowances, supplements and claimable elements are not pensionable. The Annual Pension Allowance limits how much money can be contributed to all your pension in a tax year while still benefiting from tax relief. This comprises your contributions, those we made, and any other personal or workplace pensions you have. Any contributions made above this allowance will be taxable. HMRC sets the annual allowance, and the value for the current tax year is available here. If you use all of your annual allowance for the current tax year, you may be able to carry over any unused annual allowance from the previous three tax years. If you think the Annual Allowance may apply to you, you should seek advice from HMRC or an Independent Financial Advisor. title: UASL Nest Auto Enrolment
headings: Human Resources Nest (except UASL Nest) Auto Enrolment Explore
body: You have been enrolled in the Nest Pension Scheme, as you have been assessed as meeting the government’s criteria for ‘automatic enrolment’. UASL Nest is a ‘Defined Contribution’ or ‘Money Purchase’ workplace pension. The contributions you and the University make are paid to your personal ‘pension pot’, and are invested according to your wishes. You may find out more information about what options you have and the benefits of being in this scheme on our scheme guide, and on the Nest website. Nest will send you a new joiner welcome pack by post, normally within a few days after you first get paid. This will invite you to register online and also provide details on how to opt out, if you choose to (see below for more information on opting out). The default rate of contributions for members is 3%, with a 2.4% deduction from your monthly basic salary and an extra 0.6% added to your pension pot as tax relief. If you wish, you may increase the amount you pay. The employer contribution is 6%. To find out more about contributions and tax relief, please refer to the scheme guide. We would encourage you to think carefully before deciding to opt out of a pension and ensure that it is the right decision for you. There is an initial Opt-Out period, which lasts one month starting three working days after your enrolment, during which time you may elect to opt-out. You must apply to NEST directly. Your Welcome Pack from Nest will confirm the window you have to opt-out of the scheme and provide instructions on how to do so. Nest will then close your account and instruct us to refund of any contributions you paid in the following payroll. Stopping Contributions / Leaving the Scheme If you are outside of the Opting Out period, you may still choose to stop contributing to your pension after the opt-out window has ended. Nest refers to this as a ‘break’ or ‘pause,’ but you’ll stop all contributions unless you join again later. You won’t receive a refund and all contributions will stay invested until you retire. You can request re-enrolment at any time by submitting a People Hub pay enquiry request, and you’ll be re-enrolled from the start of the following month. title: UASL Nest & Absences
headings: Human Resources Nest Pension & Absences Explore
body: Family Leave (Maternity, Paternity, Shared Parental and Adoption Leave) During paid family leave you will pay contributions on the actual earnings you receive. If you move to reduced pay or statutory-only pay, the University will contribute based on the ‘normal’ pay you had prior to going on reduced pay. If you move to unpaid leave, neither you nor the University will make further payments until you return to work. However, if you move to unpaid leave for any reason while still in ‘Ordinary’ family leave periods (normally the first 26 weeks), such as by not being entitled to statutory pay, the University will continue making contributions while you don’t have to. Your funds will continue to be invested (including during unpaid periods) so may increase or fall in value depending on how the investment choices you have made perform. Other Family Leave (Antenatal, Premature Birth, Neonatal, Parental Bereavement) You will continue to make pension contributions on any earnings or statutory pay you receive. Keeping in Touch (KIT Days / SPLIT Days) You will pay contributions on payment for any KIT days during your Family Leave. You will continue to pay pension contributions during sickness absence on whatever earnings you are paid, and UoP will do same too. This includes any statutory sick pay you receive. If you move to unpaid sickness, you and UoP will not make contributions. Your funds will continue to be invested (including during unpaid periods) so may increase or fall in value depending on how the investment choices you have made perform. title: NHS & Absence
headings: Human Resources NHS & Absence Explore
body: You will continue to earn NHS pension at your normal rate while on paid, reduced pay, or statutory pay sickness absence. Contributions will be deducted at the normal rate on whatever pay you earn. If you move to nil-pay absence, you won’t earn further pension but will still be considered ‘in service’ for any ‘Death in Service’ benefits. Family Leave (Maternity, Paternity, Shared Parental and Adoption Leave) When you are on Family leave, you continue to be in pensionable service and will accrue pension at your normal rates. You will continue to pay contributions on whatever you earn. If you move to nil-pay Family Leave, you will continue to earn pension providing you continue to pay contributions based on the rate of pay immediately before the period of unpaid leave begins. That means you may need to pay these backdated contributions when you return to paid work. You may instead request not to pay these, but would be considered out of service for the unpaid period. Other Family Leave (Antenatal, Premature Birth, Neonatal, Parental Bereavement) Paid period for time off as a result of Antenatal care, Premature Birth, Neonatal, or Parental Bereavement will be pensionable while any unpaid period will not be pensionable. Keeping in Touch (KIT Days / SPLIT Days) You will pay pension contributions for any KIT days during your Family Leave. This will be credited as pensionable earnings. Other Authorised Unpaid Leave For other types of authorised unpaid leave, you can choose whether to continue pension payments and have the period count as pensionable service. If you do, you need to notify us via People Hub of your wish to do this and agree arrangements to pay us the contributions during the unpaid period. You will earn a pension at the same rate you have when the absence starts. The University will pay employer contributions for up to 6 months of absence. You may continue payments beyond this date, to a maximum of 2 years, but will have to pay both member and employer contributions so it will become more expensive to do so. Unpaid Leave while you are buying extra pension benefits Your extra pension purchases may be affected by unpaid leave. This may include losing their benefits, extending payment periods, or reducing their value. If unpaid sickness absence exceeds 12 months, you may be unable to resume your extra pension arrangement upon returning to work and may need to enter a separate new agreement. You may wish to contact the scheme directly to see how you could be affected. https://www.nhsbsa.nhs.uk/member-hub DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. title: London Aviva Timeline: Becoming a Member
headings: Human Resources Aviva for London Campus Timeline: Becoming a Member Opted out previously but now want to re-join? Explore
body: New Employment Contracts: If you’ve just started with us, don’t worry! So long as you are eligible, you will have been contractually enrolled into Aviva and don’t need to do anything else to join. If you are not sure if you are eligible, check your contract and look at the section for ‘Pension’. You are not eligible to join if you are aged 75 or over. You may alternatively have been automatically enrolled, where you were not in a pension but meet the government’s assessment to be auto-enrolled. This happens where you: were not, at time of assessment, in a pension scheme for this employment have not previously opted-out of a pension in this employment, or opted-out more than 12 months before an auto-enrolment ‘staging date’, which happens every 3 years earned over a minimum amount (currently £833) in a single month are aged between 22 and your State Pension Age We'll always let you know if you are ever auto enrolled. Opted out previously but now want to re-join? Just let us know by submitting a People Hub 'Pay Enquiry'. You can start from as soon as the first of the next month. Just let us know by submitting a People Hub 'Pay Enquiry'. You can start from as soon as the first of the next month. title: London Aviva Timeline: Contributions
headings: Human Resources Aviva for London Campus Timeline: Contributions Explore
body: From the first day you join, we will pay contributions of 10% of your salary towards your Aviva Company Pension account. This is calculated on your gross monthly salary (meaning before tax and National Insurance deductions). You'll pay a contribution on your salary too and Aviva then automatically refund 20% of the tax you paid straight to your pension account. When you join, you’ll be set up to contribute 4.8% of your pay to your pension, so another 1.2% will be added as the refund of tax to give a total of 6%, or 16% when combined with the employer payment. You’ll be able to check these contributions each month on your payslip. …so what counts as salary? We apply your percentage contributions to the whole of your salary each month, before deductions of tax and National Insurance. If you have a London Allowance, that is pensionable too. Contributions from backdated pay following promotions or pay rises will be made in the month they are paid. All other payments are non-pensionable, and will not result in any additional contributions being made. title: London Aviva Timeline: Building Benefits
headings: Human Resources Aviva for London Campus Timeline: Building your Pension Explore
body: We contribute 10% of your salary, and you make your contributions with an extra 20% relief from tax put straight in to your own pension pot too. So what happens next? The Aviva Company Pension is a ‘defined contributions’ or 'money purchase' scheme. That means your own pot of money is invested according to choices you make, and has a chance to grow (and keep growing) until you choose to retire. The earlier you invest, the longer it has with a chance to grow – that means decisions you make near the start of your career will normally have even more impact than those nearer the end… You choose, from a range of options, how your funds are invested. This is an important decision, although you can change your options later. The fund or funds you invest in may have different aims and levels of ‘risk verses reward’, or may align with different ethics or beliefs. A little confused? Don’t worry, you are in good company! Aviva aims to make these decisions as simple and transparent as possible. You can begin in a ‘default’ investment plan, and that can be a good place to start until you want to consider further The next most straightforward choice is to select one of Aviva’s ‘Investment Approaches’. These are a blend of investments which intend to align with broad intentions of a pension, such as short or long term focus, high or low risk, lump sums or regular monthly payments. Finally, more experienced investors may build their own profile from hundreds of different funds. The ‘RISK’ versus the reward… A pension can be the best, safest and most tax-efficient way to save for your future. You'll already have benefited by 20% just by making contributions. However, with all investments comes a risk that they can go down as well as up Aviva gives its investment plans a ‘Risk Rating’ which you should use to make your choices fit your needs and your own appetite for risk Aviva take a small amount of your funds as charge(s) for managing your investments. These can change based on your investment choices but will be clear when you set them up. Aviva will let you know what these are and if they ever change. title: London Aviva Timeline: After you Join
headings: Human Resources Aviva for London Campus Timeline: After you Join Explore
body: First read through the scheme guide to understand the benefits of your Aviva scheme and how it works. Registering Online We will set you up as a new member towards the end of the first month you pay contributions in Aviva will send you a ‘welcome pack’ by post to your home address with scheme information and instructions on how to join Once you have the welcome pack, go to Aviva’s MyAccount to register for online access to your pension and follow the instructions on screen. Enter the Activation Code or Policy Number you have been provided, so it matches you up with UASL London Campus. It’s recommended to use a personal email so access is readily available at home, and after you leave or retire Some of your personal details will be pre-filled, such as name, NI number and address. Aviva’s terms and conditions are that you must log in and check these are correct when you join Nominate your beneficiaries as soon as possible to ensure your loved ones are considered in the event of your death We will set you up as a new member towards the end of the first month you pay contributions in Aviva will send you a ‘welcome pack’ by post to your home address with scheme information and instructions on how to join Once you have the welcome pack, go to Aviva’s MyAccount to register for online access to your pension and follow the instructions on screen. Enter the Activation Code or Policy Number you have been provided, so it matches you up with UASL London Campus. It’s recommended to use a personal email so access is readily available at home, and after you leave or retire Some of your personal details will be pre-filled, such as name, NI number and address. Aviva’s terms and conditions are that you must log in and check these are correct when you join Nominate your beneficiaries as soon as possible to ensure your loved ones are considered in the event of your death Next, choose your initial investment options (or stay in the ‘default’ fund). You may also be able to transfer in previous pensions you have to Aviva, find out more on the scheme guidance and by checking with Aviva directly (you can ask a question through the website after logging in). If you are considering opting out and want a refund, you’ll need to do this within 30 days of Aviva posting you the ‘welcome pack’. Make sure you are aware of all the benefits and advantages of contributing to a pension before you decide what to do, which will be covered in your welcome pack. You can read through Opting Out to find out more. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: London Aviva Timeline: During your Membership
headings: Human Resources Aviva for London Campus Timeline: During your Membership Explore
body: You should keep logging in regularly to see your contributions and investments, track how these perform and grow over time, make fund changes if you choose to, and always keep personal details up to date whenever they change. You’ll also get an Annual Statement to review each year summarising your contributions and investments. Consider additional contributions If you are in a position to contribute more to your pension, it is one of the best options to invest in your future. You may make a one-off payment to your pension at any time, and immediately benefit from 20% tax relief when while your overall pension contributions remain below 100% of your relevant UK earnings. There are some additional limits on tax - find out more on our Tax & Pensions page. You can make one-off or regular payments to your pension. You’ll pay these directly to Aviva from your personal savings or bank account. As you pay them separately, they won’t show up on your University payslip. Alternatively, you can request to increase the percentage you pay each month on your Portsmouth earnings. You must email pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk with your request and let us know the percentage you wish to pay. You can do this from as soon as start of the following month. Higher Rate Taxpayer? Do you pay income tax on your UASL London earnings at a Higher Rate? If so, you should complete an annual self-assessment tax return with HMRC each year to claim back any additional tax you paid on your contributions. For contributions paid at 40% tax, 20% will have been added to your pension pot already, and you can claim an additional 20% back. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: London Aviva Timeline: Opting Out
headings: Human Resources Aviva for London Campus Timeline: Opting Out Explore
body: You may feel you need to opt out of your pension, however we would always recommend you remain in scheme whenever you can. Please consider this carefully and make sure you choose the right option for your circumstances. Opting out within 30 days of registration: If you inform Aviva you wish to opt out within 30 days of registration, they’ll refund us any contributions you paid, and we’ll refund you in your next payslip. Note that the 30-day period starts on the date your second Aviva 'Welcome Pack' was sent (check the date on the letter), not when you receive it, so you should do this as quickly as possible to receive a refund (see After you Join for more details on this welcome pack, registering online, and finding any other details you need to opt out). This letter should normally be posted to the home address you have given within a week or so of the first payroll you make contributions in (for example, if you are enrolled on the first of the month but paid on the 28th, you should normally get the letter shortly after the 28th, and it may even be dated on the 26th or 27th). If you are urgently seeking to opt out with a refund and haven't had this letter within the expected time, let us know at pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. Requests must be submitted via Aviva's opt out page or by calling 0800 145 5744 once you have your confirmed plan number (found on your 'Welcome Pack'). Please be aware we cannot do this on your behalf so you must go directly to Aviva. Exiting the scheme after 30 days: After 30 days, you aren’t technically ‘opting out’ but are leaving the scheme instead. You can request by submitting a People Hub ‘Pay Enquiry’ and can stop making contributions from as early as the following month. You won’t get a refund and past contributions will stay invested and continue to have the chance to grow over time. You may be able to ‘transfer out’ past contributions to another scheme, but should contact Aviva directly to do this. Re-joining the Scheme You can request to opt back in at any time by submitting a People Hub ‘Pay Enquiry’, and your membership can restart from the following month. Additionally, we may be required to ‘auto-enrol’ you approximately every 3 years (see Becoming a Member for more details). title: London Aviva Timeline: Resignation
headings: Human Resources Aviva for London Campus Timeline: Resignation Explore
body: If you are looking at employment options outside of the University, make sure you add Reward and Pensions to the equation. We are proud of our London Aviva scheme, with us paying 10% employer contributions towards your pension on top of all the other benefits you receive as an employee. If you do leave us before taking your pension, your employee and employer contributions will remain in your Aviva pension pot, and will continue to be invested with the same opportunity to keep growing until your retirement. You will not be able to pay new contributions via salary, and of course we will not contribute further either. However, you may convert our workplace pension to a ‘personal one’ and continue to make contributions directly to Aviva. There are some restrictions and minimum limits on this so check your options directly with Aviva. You may also wish to consider changing your investment options now your circumstances have changed – particularly if you had selected an Investment Plan focused on longer-term membership. You should still log in regularly after you leave to keep track of your pension value, update your personal details and beneficiaries, and to review your Annual Statements. Thinking of transferring from Aviva to another scheme after you leave?Do some research, know the risks, and we strongly advise you seek financial advice to make sure you understand what option is best for you. There are some restrictions on what schemes you may transfer to so check with Aviva first to see what is possible. title: London Aviva Timeline: Retirement
headings: Human Resources Aviva for London Campus Timeline: Retirement Explore
body: With Aviva, you may choose to take retirement benefits whether you are intending to leave work or not, and even while you continue working full time (although this may mean a higher proportion of pension income could be taxable). The ‘Minimum Pension Age’ to take your pension is currently 55 years old but it is due to increase to 57 on 5 April 2028. You must take your pension no later than your 75th birthday. You may draw all or only some of your pension, and you can decide how to do that by choosing a lump sum, taking individual payments as you need them, or by exchanging for regular income for life (known as an annuity). 25% of the total value of your pension(s) can be accessed ‘tax free’ when drawing lump sums or individual payments. If you purchase an annuity, then normal income tax applies. Let us know your intended retirement date (whether your are leaving employment or not) via a People Hub ‘Pay Enquiry’, and we’ll make sure everything gets done on our side. More information on your options and how this works is contained in the scheme guide or via the Aviva Help Line. title: London Aviva & Absences
headings: Human Resources Aviva for London Campus Pension & Absences Explore
body: Family Leave (Maternity, Paternity, Shared Parental and Adoption Leave) During paid family leave you will pay contributions on the actual earnings you receive. If you move to reduced pay or statutory-only pay, the University will contribute based on the ‘normal’ pay you had prior to going on reduced pay. If you move to unpaid leave, neither you nor the University will make further payments until you return to work. However, if you move to unpaid leave for any reason while still in ‘Ordinary’ family leave periods (normally the first 26 weeks), such as by not being entitled to statutory pay, the University will continue making contributions while you don’t have to. Your funds will continue to be invested (including during unpaid periods) so may increase or fall in value depending on how the investment choices you have made perform. Other Family Leave (Antenatal, Premature Birth, Neonatal, Parental Bereavement) You will continue to make pension contributions on any earnings or statutory pay you receive. Keeping in Touch (KIT Days / SPLIT Days) You will pay contributions on payment for any KIT days during your Family Leave. You will continue to pay pension contributions during sickness absence on whatever earnings you are paid, and UoP will do same too. This includes any statutory sick pay you receive. If you move to unpaid sickness, you and UoP will not make contributions. Your funds will continue to be invested (including during unpaid periods) so may increase or fall in value depending on how the investment choices you have made perform. title: Pensions Awareness Week
headings: Human Resources Pensions Awareness Week Pensions Awareness Week 2025 - starting Monday 15 September All Members Teachers’ Pension Scheme (TPS) Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS) MyPensionPlus & Aviva NHS Pension Scheme NEST Explore
body: Pensions Awareness Week occurs each year, normally in the second or third week of September. Schemes, advocates and advisors across the industry come together to provide free events and learning opportunities so there’s never a better time to learn more about your pension and how it works, check up on the value you've earned, and give some thought to your future. Whatever scheme you are in at Portsmouth – Teachers’ Pension, LGPS, MyPensionPlus, NHS, Aviva London or NEST – your pension is one of the biggest and best benefits we are proud to offer. Each scheme works differently, so be sure to find some time to learn more by looking through the latest information and resources on offer. Are you not in a pension scheme yet, or have you opted out? We would always recommend joining up as soon as your circumstances allow it. Just email pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk and we’ll let you know what you need to do. Pensions Awareness – book free-to-all pension talks running Monday through Wednesday, although these sessions won’t cover specific information for any schemes you are a member of. If you can’t attend them live, they are available to catch-up later. They also offer some articles, FAQs and links, covering the basics and beyond Don’t forget your State Pension, which most will be earning alongside the workplace schemes. Register for access via HMRC online services, and view your forecast. Learn how it works and if there are any gaps in national insurance contributions, you may be able to make a voluntary top up for a big boost to your future state pension. MoneySavingExpert have an article to learn more Bookmark the university 'My Pensions' intranet page for information on the schemes we offer and helpful links to find out more Locate old pensions you may have lost track of If you have an old pension you're no longer paying into, get in touch with them and check all your details and beneficiaries are up to date What value of pension should you be aiming for? This can be a difficult question, particularly if it still feels far away. Check out Picture your Future from the Pensions and Lifetime Savings Association and ‘How much will you need to retire‘ on which.co.uk (or search for alternative sites) and compare it to the ‘forecast’ value of your latest pension statement or estimate, and with the moneyhelper.org.uk Pension Calculator that predicts what you may need for retirement Think you might need financial advice? Consider starting at MoneyHelper PensionsWise (where you may be able to get free and impartial guidance) and unbiased.co.uk for an Independent Financial Advisor (IFA). If you're considering an IFA for choices about pensions, be sure to find one with specific pensions expertise, and also experience with your public sector 'defined benefit' schemes if you have one (for example, LGPS, NHS, Teachers' Pension, or USS) Follow this link for an explanation of pensions jargon and terms If you are ever considering moving funds from one scheme to another, or to an investment plan, you need to be aware of how to spot pension scams and fraud. Scams related to pensions are on the rise, and the consequences can be life changing. Member Homepage and your My Pension Online login Not signed up for My Pension Online yet? You need to do this immediately, so register here. It’s the only way to track how your pension grows and the best way to run estimates, ask questions, and keep your personal details and beneficiaries up to date. title: Tax & Pensions
headings: Human Resources Tax & Pensions Annual Allowance Lifetime Allowance (LTA) Explore
body: The ‘Annual Allowance’ is the maximum that can be saved across all your pensions in a tax year (6th April to 5th April) without a tax change being applied. This combines your UoP pension(s), any added pension and AVCs, along with any other pensions you contribute to personally or through other employments or contracts. HMRC establish the value of the Annual Allowance, and it can change each year. Where this limit is exceeded, unused allowances from the previous 3 years can be applied first, but the remaining amount will be taxed at your income tax rate. Generally, this will be very unlikely to affect you unless you are a very high earner (more than £100,000) or have very high (more than £1,000 monthly) in extra contributions throughout the year but it is always your own responsibility to check and report this (or to do so through taking Financial Advice). What do you need to do? Your pension provider will normally tell you if you exceed the annual allowance with their pensions alone, but they won’t know if you will exceed it only after combining your AVCs and/or payments in other schemes. If you think there is any possibility this could affect you, you must find out more via the links below, ask your pension(s) for this value, consider Financial Advice, and if necessary report and pay the tax charges to HMRC. If you haven’t had any contact about Annual Allowance from your schemes or AVCs you paid into, you can request a pensions saving statement from them to get the amount of Annual Allowance used. How the value is determined is different depending on what type of pension it is, and you should always use the values provided by your pension provider. However, we provide some general information about how these are calculated below: For many ‘Money Purchase’ or ‘Defined Contribution’ pensions the value used can be as simple as adding up the cash amounts paid by the member and employer over the year, along with the extra 20% tax relief applied to employee contributions. You do not add in increases or decreases due investment performance. Generally, this covers most pensions (including UoP NEST and AVIVA options) but not Final Salary and Public Sector Defined Benefit schemes, or our AVCs. For AVCs at UoP, this will normally be only the value of contributions you paid – tax relief is included in the cash amount you pay, and there are no additional employer contributions being made. For Teachers’ Pension, LGPS or NHS (which are public sector ‘Defined Benefits’ schemes) this is works very differently and is not based on payments at all. There are details on how you might calculate this yourself contained within the links below, but we’d always recommended getting a confirmed value from your provider(s) if it looks like you could be affected. Affected individuals can arrange payment through a tax return (see HMRC Guidance linked below). It may also be possible to request a ‘Scheme Pays’ arrangement with your pension provider where they recover the charges on your behalf through reductions to your pension itself, so check with them if this becomes relevant. All Schemes: HMRC Guidance; MoneyHelper.org; unbiased.co.uk LGPS: Hants Pension Fund; LGPS Scheme Info; Quick Check Calculator TPS: Guidance; Calculator The Lifetime Allowance determined a threshold where an individual’s total lifetime pension value across all their schemes (except State Pensions) became taxable. Lifetime Allowance is abolished with effect from 6 April 2024. For pensions taken on or after 6 April 2023, the Lifetime Allowance charge does not apply but there may still be additional income tax due on some payments. Most individuals will not be affected by this, unless they have accrued a very high pension over many years of service. Those who have will normally have been written to by their schemes advising them of options available. You should be able to see how much of your Lifetime Allowance you have used up in benefits statements provided by each of your pension schemes and AVCs, or you can ask them to confirm this if necessary. If you may be affected in any way by the Lifetime Allowance, we would strongly advise you seek independent financial advice. Further information is available via HMRC guidance. title: Auto Enrolment
headings: Human Resources Workplace Pension Auto Enrolment Explore
body: We will contact you if you are ever enrolled in a pension scheme under the government’s criteria for ‘automatic enrolment’ in your role. Earned over a minimum amount (currently £833) in a single month Are aged between 22 and your State Pension Age Were not, at time of assessment, in a pension scheme for this contract Have not previously opted-out of a pension in this contract, or opted-out before the University’s latest organisational re-enrolment date, which happens approximately every 3 years The correspondence will make clear what workplace pension scheme you have joined, and date you have been enrolled from (normally the start of the month you qualified in), and you will see employee and employer contributions on your payslips from this date. You may be enrolled even if you already have a deferred pension, or if a pension is already being paid to you. If you want to know more about automatic enrolment, there is some further information on moneyhelper.org.uk. Depending on the pension you have been enrolled in, contributions rates (how much you and we will pay), benefits, effect on tax, and what you need to know will vary. Please follow the relevant link below for the pension scheme you have joined. Aviva for London Campus Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS) Teachers' Pension Scheme (TPS) Nest (Other Subsidiaries except UASL Nest) Opting Out (leaving the scheme) We would encourage you to think carefully before deciding to opt out of a pension and ensure that it is the right decision for you. If you do still wish to opt out the above links will provide instructions on how to do this. Opting Out requests may be processed differently depending on how long you are in the scheme before requesting to opt out, and this may affect how you may receive a refund of prior contributions, or if you are even eligible to one. You should pay close attention to any time limited actions for your particular scheme to understand your position. If you ever opt out, you can change your mind and opt back in again later to re-join the scheme from the start of the following month. title: Purchasing Additional Holiday & Your Pension
headings: Human Resources Purchasing Additional Holiday & Pensions Explore
body: If you are considering applying for additional time off under the Holiday Purchase scheme, you’ll have a choice to pay for it using a ‘salary sacrifice’ arrangement. Under salary sacrifice, your salary is temporarily reduced by the cost of the Holiday, allowing you to save money by not paying Income Tax or National Insurance on the amount sacrificed. There is more detail on this arrangement in the Holiday Purchase Scheme guide, which you should ensure you have read and understood first. One other impact of using salary sacrifice to purchase Holiday is that you won’t pay pension contributions on the amount reduced either. That does mean you’ll pay less, but that may not be what you are seeking if you wish to keep your pension accruing at the same rate. Thankfully, no matter what scheme you are in, there are voluntary options to pay extra so you can ‘top up’ your pension back to where it was, or by as much as you can afford. These aren’t limited to Additional Holiday Purchase either – you can look into them whether or not you buy time off with a salary sacrifice arrangement. Each scheme has different options and processes, so take a read through the relevant section for you below. MyPensionPlus Aviva and London Aviva pensions You can make one-off or regular payments to your pension directly to Aviva. Alternatively, you can request to increase the percentage you pay each month on your earnings by letting us know at pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. See the section on ‘Consider additional contributions’ on our Aviva ‘During your Membership’ page for more details. Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS) You can make Additional Pension Contributions (APCs) or Additional Voluntary Contributions (AVCs) to your pension. With LGPS, you can also choose to pay AVCs (but not APCs) under salary sacrifice (know as Salary Sacrifice Shared Cost AVCs), which can reduce National Insurance payments even further. You should read more about each option on our LGPS ‘Adding to your Pension’ page. The Nest scheme offers flexibility in making additional contributions through a one-off payment made directly to the scheme, or by setting up a regular direct debit from your bank. Alternatively, you may request to increase the rate of contributions you pay each month on your salary, by emailing pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk with the details. Additional information for the scheme may be found from our Nest scheme page. NHS allow extra pension purchase through either ‘Additional Pension’ or ‘Money Purchase Additional Voluntary Contributions (MPAVCs)’. ‘Additional Pension’ is where you contribute either a lump sum or a regular added payment from your earnings to increase your NHS Annual Pension that you'll get each year of retirement. ‘Money Purchase Additional Voluntary Contributions (MPAVCs)’ allow you to build up a separate flexible pension pot with either Standard Life or Prudential that is linked to your NHS scheme. You’ll make separate contributions from your monthly earnings, the amount you contribute is invested according to your choices and its value can go up and down over time depending on how well those investments preform. Follow the link to our NHS Pension Scheme pages for further information. Teachers’ Pension Scheme Teachers’ Pension allow you to buy ‘Additional Pension’ to boost your Annual Pension value, using either a lump sum payment or regular contributions. You have to purchase a minimum increase of £250 to your Annual Pension (which is paid to you every year of retirement), so bear in mind that this can be quite costly – thankfully, you can split the payments over multiple years if you need to. Alternatively, you can start ‘Additional Voluntary Contributions’ (AVCs) where you build up a separate flexible pension pot with Prudential who partner with TP. With this, you’ll choose how much you pay, and the amount you contribute is invested accorded to your fund choices. Its value can go up and down over time depending on how well those investments perform. These AVCs are linked to TP, but you don’t have to take them at the same time as your normal benefits, and can even take them flexibly while continuing to work. Find out more on these options, as well as Faster Accrual and Buy Out, on the Teachers’ Pension ‘Buying Extra Pension’ page. title: Our ambition
headings: We're planning for the future We exist to be a world-class university; one that makes a difference to the world, through transformational education, research and innovation Our Strategy Sustainability at Portsmouth Our Estate Masterplan We are Global Civic University Equality, Diversity and Inclusion We use cookies
body: Read the strategies shaping how we take the University forward But to do great things, it takes more than good intentions. Three things tie together our plan for the future: Our ambitious mission Our vision for bringing it to life The values that define the way we work, collaborate and behave We've already made giant leaps towards our goals – we're developing our campus and becoming a more sustainable university, and the strategies and plans we have in place are ready to be explored below. Discover what we're aiming to achieve by 2030 to make our Vision a reality. Find out how we're becoming more sustainable by improving energy efficiency, minimising waste, cutting back on plastic and reducing carbon emissions. Read more about our ambitious campus development project. We're based in Portsmouth, but our reach and ambitions are global. Learn more about our international vision and activities, including our international research collaborations and our plans to grow further. Explore how we're collaborating with local partners to widen opportunities, strengthen the economy and support our local communities. Find out how we're ensuring equality of opportunity for all by creating a working, learning, cultural and social environment based on dignity and respect. We may use cookies to analyse visitor data, improve our website, show personalised content and give you a great website experience. For more information about cookies on our site and to tailor the cookies we collect, select ‘Manage settings’.

title: Minibuses
headings: Estates and Campus Services Minibuses Driving Minibuses on University Business (UK) Only People Carriers Foreign licences Small Bus Permits (Section 19) Driving outside of the UK Passenger Safety Trailers/roof racks Defects Reporting Explore
body: Every driver of a minibus used on University business must have completed the Driver Declaration to be lodged with the Transport Section. Only those members of staff over 25 years of age, 2 years driving experience and with a category D1 licence entitlement can drive minibuses on University business. All new minibus drivers are required to undergo a medical examination to be repeated at five-year intervals (unless otherwise stated). Upon successful completion of the medical, it is a requirement that minibus drivers are assessed with the Assessor of the Campus Environment Office. Paperwork for the assessment test is available from the Transport Section. The total cost of each assessment is £50.00 and this takes place subsequently at five yearly intervals (unless otherwise stated). All staff are required to take a medical if wishing to drive a 7/8/9 seater people carrier. Please contact the Transport Section. Employees who hold a valid ?full? driving licence issued in the European Community or Iceland, Liechtenstein and Norway can be included under the Universities insurance policy. Licences issued by any other country need to be referred to the Transport Section prior to approval being given. The Section 19 permit scheme enables the University to make a nominal charge to passengers for providing transport without the need to obtain a PSV Operators License. Contributions must not exceed a level to recover the costs of running the minibus, including an allowance for vehicle depreciation and drivers wages. All University owned minibuses and hired in minibuses should clearly display the permit disc on the vehicle. Discs should be displayed on the inside of the windscreen so that they can be easily seen from outside the vehicle but do not obstruct the driver?s view of the road. If the permit is missing do not drive the vehicle but contact the Transport Section immediately. No minibus may be taken overseas as we are not insured to do so. If a number of passengers are being taken overseas one suggestion would be to hire a MPV vehicle through the Transport Section. For example a Ford Tourneo can carry 9 passengers but is still classed as an MPV. When planning a trip, work out journey times and distances in advance in order to structure your journey and avoid the risks associated with driver fatigue. Whenever possible it is strongly recommended that another authorised driver is taken to share the driving. Please bear in mind that if you only driver a minibus occasionally you are likely to find driving more stressful than professionals. It is therefore important to plan more frequent rest breaks than those laid down in the EC Regulations for driver hours. Where a journey is expected to exceed fours hours, you should ensure that you have allowed a rest period of two hours prior to your journey. Breaks should be built into journey planning, aim to have a break from driving of at least 15 minutes every 2 hours with a 45 minute break after 4.5 hours. Under no circumstances should the total period of driving exceed 9 hours. Under no circumstances should you drive for longer hours than you feel capable of undertaking safely. It is a condition of travel that all drivers and passengers wear seat belts. Any person refusing to wear a safety belt will be refused travel. In accordance with the University Smoking policy, smoking is not permitted on any of our vehicles. Legislation came into force on 1 December 2003, which prohibits the use of hand-held mobile telephones whilst driving a vehicle. Hand held mobile telephones may therefore only be used by the driver when the minibus is parked and engine switched off. Our insurers do not permit roof racks to be fitted to minibuses nor trailers to be towed. It is an offence to use an un-roadworthy vehicle on the road; both the University and the driver have a legal responsibility to ensure roadworthiness. Before using the vehicle as walk around check should be made and any minor defects noted on the defect sheet. Before starting a journey check that items such as lights, brakes, warning instruments, windscreen washer and wipers are in good working order and that side mirrors are set at the correct angle for you. If at all concerned please contact the Transport Section. If the defect is in respect of a hired vehicle, the hire company should be contacted immediately Engineering maintenance and management Soft Services (Campus Environment) What do we do? title: Vehicles owned by employees
headings: Estates and Campus Services Vehicles owned by Employees Explore
body: It is the responsibility of every driver to make sure they have adequate insurance for their own private cars used for University business. This includes travelling between different sites. Most private motor policies restrict use to social, domestic and pleasure purposes only. Employees using their own vehicles on University business will need to extend their personal insurance to include business use (including passenger cover). When completing your expenses claim (FIN 1) you are required to sign a declaration confirming appropriate cover is in place. There is often no charge for this extension. However, please be warned: Some insurers provide quotations for social, domestic and pleasure purposes only, plus commuting to and from the employee’s normal place of work. This is not the same as “business use” and will not cover the occasional trip to a meeting elsewhere. If you are named as a driver on someone else’s motor insurance (e.g. spouse), please check that business use is available to the appropriate person. The University is unable to accept any liability in the event of an insurance claim being made. Further advice can be obtained from the Insurance Officer: Anita Randell, Insurance Officer University of Portsmouth Finance Department, University House, Winston Churchill Avenue, Portsmouth, PO1 2UP. University of Portsmouth Finance Department, University House, Winston Churchill Avenue, Portsmouth, PO1 2UP. title: NHS Pre-engagement Checks
headings: Human Resources Research Passports Pre-Engagement Checklist 1. Employment screening checks 1a. ID with a photograph 1b. Two references & 1c Verification of permission to work/study in the UK 1d. Exploration of gaps in employment 2. Evidence of current professional registration 3. Evidence of qualifications Explore
body: These notes should be read in conjunction with the “Research in the NHS: HR Good Practice Resource Pack”. If the HR department is able to confirm that these pre-employment checks have already been completed at an earlier date, the pre-engagement process does not normally need to be repeated. In order to complete Section 5 of the Research Passport (RP) application, the HR department will need to confirm that an identity check has been completed. Acceptable documentation to support this are: photographic evidence supported by originals of either a passport or UK driving licence, and proof of the researcher’s current address, such as a utility bill, a bank statement or the most recent council tax bill. Utility bills and/or bank statements must be within the past three months. In addition, where possible, electronic databases such as electoral roll information may be searched to verify identity. Once HR confirms that these checks have been conducted, this stage does not need to be repeated. As the University routinely obtains two references and verification of permission to work/study in the UK as part of the recruitment process, these checks may be confirmed as having been conducted and do not need to be repeated. The NHS’ expectation is, as part of the RP application, that the University will check for gaps in employment/ study of more than six months in the past three years. The University will use the CV currently on file, submitted as part of the recruitment process, to identify any gaps. If necessary, the HR Department may request a current CV. Please supply original documentation to the HR Department to be certified as a true copy to be included with your Research Passport application. Please supply original certificates (if these are not held on file) to the HR Department to be certified as a true copy to be included with your RP application. title: Research Passports FAQs
headings: Human Resources Research Passport FAQs Eligibility How does the Research Passport apply to visiting international researchers that are not formally employed by the University of Portsmouth? How to complete the form If I am unsure which section(s) on the pre-engagement checklist/Role Assessment form my work falls under, who should I ask? Does the person named under ‘Manager in NHS organisation‘(Section 2.5) have to be an NHS employee? Who fills in Section 5 of the form? When should I apply for a research passport? When is a Research Passport Necessary? Can I apply for a research passport in case I need to use Trust facilities at some point in the future? What if I only need a Letter of Access rather than an Honorary Research Contract? Supporting Documents Can I bring my form in if I haven't got all the necessary evidence yet? How long does a DBS check take? Health/Risk Assessment I had a University OH check before I started my post here, do I need another check? How long will a research passport level OH assessment take? Amendments to your Research Passport Explore
body: In this situation, the University would need to complete the Research Passport form. Where there is no formal employment contract in place between the researcher and the University, a suitable status, (e.g., guest/visiting/honorary) should be put in place to ensure that there is clear accountability between the University and the researcher. We would then undertake the necessary pre-engagement checks to support the completion of the Research Passport. Any associated cost would be allocated to the relevant Institute/School/department. In the first instance ask your line manager/supervisor/PI. If they do not know then contact the R&D office in the relevant Trust who will be able to look up the project details on their database. Yes. They should either be an NHS employee in the Trust in which you applying to conduct research or they should be a University of Portsmouth employee with an Honorary Clinical or Research Contract with the Trust in which you wish to conduct your research. Your line manager/supervisor/PI should know who this is. What do I write under ‘NHS organisation(s) (Section 2.5) if my research will involve work with various departments? Write down the main department(s) where the responsibility lies and then put ‘various’ in brackets afterwards. This is for the University’s HR Department to fill in. Please do not ask your departmental administrator to fill this section in. As soon as you know that you will need one so that all the relevant checks can be started, however, if you are not yet an employee student of the University of Portsmouth then we will not be able to sign off your research passport form until we have confirmation of your employment status. Useful link: Instructions for Completing the Research Passport Form No. There has to be at least one identifiable study that needs Trust facilities in order to know which Trust should authorise the passport. You will need to complete the research passport form whether you need an Honorary Research Contract or a Letter of Access. The Trust will assess the details on the passport application and issue whichever document is appropriate for your work. The exception to this is if you hold an Honorary Clinical Contract with a Trust and wish to apply to carry out research in a different Trust, in this circumstance you should contact the R&D Department of the Trust in which you wish to conduct research and they will provide you with the relevant form. Please gather all the evidence together before arranging to bring in your form - the University's HR department will not be able to complete the form without all relevant original documents. Usually the DBS aims to process 90% of Standard checks in 10 days and 90% of Enhanced checks in 4 weeks but there have been some cases that have taken over 2 months. Therefore it is best to apply for the DBS check as soon as possible as it could take a while to come through. To comply with NHS occupational health (OH) assessment requirements, and if it is identified that you require OH clearance for your research passport, then an additional OH assessment may be required. You will therefore need to contact the Occupational Health Service to ask if you have been cleared to research passport level. This will generally only take a few days, but is dependent on your immunisation status and which vaccinations and/or blood test results are missing and need completing to meet the necessary NHS criteria for the particular post. It also depends on available OH appointments, as well as the speed that the pathology lab turns around the blood test results, and the time to obtain an up to date vaccination history from your GP (which will be required for clearance to level 2 or 3). In other words it is best to apply for the OH assessment as soon as possible as it could take a while to come through. Amendments to the Research Passport usually take the form of the following: Additional research projects, Changes to research activity on the original project(s), Personal details (including changes to DBS or OH status). Researchers who change substantive employer cannot use the amendment process even if they are working on the same research project. In this situation, a new Research Passport application must be made (exceptions may be made in relation to TUPE transfers into the University), but this should be discussed with the HR Department. You will need to send a copy of your original Research passport application form with any amendments annotated on the appendix page (please state the NHS REC number(s) if known) directly to the relevant Trust. If your line manager changes and/or the research project changes, please ask your new line manager to also complete Section 4 of a new RP application form. If your research project has changed you will need to check whether a different OH clearance level is required, and then contact the University’s Occupational Health team so they can identify the necessary Occupational Health assessments and immunisations required and reissue the appropriate certificate. The Trust reserves the right to ask for additional pre-engagement checks where this is deemed to be appropriate. Where amendments are approved, the Appendix page will be signed by the Trust's HR Manager and returned to you. The standard ‘Acceptance of Amendment Letter will be sent to you, along with copies sent to your line manager, the University’s HR Department and nominated NHS Manager. A new LoA/Honorary Research Contract will not be issued. Where amendments are not approved, you will need to submit a new RP application. title: EUSS FAQs
headings: Human Resources Staff FAQs – EU Settlement Scheme I am a non-UK EU national. Do I need a visa to continue working at the University? I am a Norwegian, Icelandic, Liechtenstein or Swiss national, how am I affected? Will I have to pay for medical care? What support services are available for employees? Where can employees go for further information about visas and immigration? What is the difference between settled and pre-settled status? I am an Irish citizen and I didn’t apply, is this a problem? Does my employer need to keep a record of outcomes on their HR systems? Will I hold a BRP similar to that of non-EU individuals in the UK? How will I prove my status in the future? I have been resident in the UK for 4 years,  if I have pre-settled status now will I automatically obtain settled status when I have been here 5 years? I have been living in the UK for 6 years, and hold settled status now, will I still need to apply for citizenship if I want it? Can my settled status lapse if I leave the UK? I am going to a conference/seminar or undertaking a secondment in the EU/EEA do I need to do anything? What support will be available? Explore
body: Page last updated - 12 August 2021 The rights and status of EU citizens living in the UK who have obtained EUSS remain the same. If you were living in the UK on or before 31 December 2020 you should have applied for the EU Settlement Scheme. If you have not applied through the Settlement Scheme during this time you will need to apply for immigration status under the new Immigration system, or apply for EUSS with a justification for the reason for a late application. If UKVI does not grant you EUSS you will have to obtain an alternative visa or leave the UK, and this may lead to dismissal from your role at the University. EEA and Swiss nationals should also have applied for EUSS. If you have not applied through the Settlement Scheme you will need to apply for immigration status under the new Immigration system, or apply for EUSS with a justification for the reason for a late application. If UKVI does not grant you EUSS you will have to obtain an alternative visa or leave the UK, and this may lead to dismissal from your role at the University. For those who have Settled Status through the scheme there will be no change in relation to your ability to receive NHS medical care in the UK. If you have personal concerns related to the changes employees can access personal support from the University’s Employee Assistance Programme (EAP) on a confidential basis. Support is available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week and 365 days a year for staff. Full details of all visa routes can be found at Gov.uk. The HR Compliance Team will be able to provide general information via the following email address: HR-UKVI-compliance@port.ac.uk, but cannot provide detailed immigration advice. The University will continue to assess what further support can be made available. Settled status – for those who have been in the UK for 5 years or more (equivalent of indefinite leave to remain) Settled status – for those who have been in the UK for 5 years or more (equivalent of indefinite leave to remain) Pre-settled status – for those who have been in the UK for less than 5 years (equivalent of limited leave to Pre-settled status – for those who have been in the UK for less than 5 years (equivalent of limited leave to No, there is no requirement for Irish citizens to apply for EUSS and existing arrangements regarding free movement remain in place. No, there is no requirement for employers to hold records on settled/Pre-settled status of EU citizens who were employed on or before 31 December 2020. However, if you are happy to share that information with HR they can hold it on the HR system. If your employment started on or after 1 January 2021 you will have to evidence EUSS or an alternative visa. If you would like to evidence your EUSS to HR please generate a share code and forward to HR-UKVI-Compliance@port.ac.uk where a member of the team will organise a check with you. No, this is a digital system and process. In most cases you will receive an email with a letter confirming your status and access to an online record. As this is a digital status you will be able to give employers / landlords access to your digital information to prove your status by generating a share code though the UKVI online system. No, if you have been granted pre-settled status UKVI cannot assume citizens are still here or still want settled status after 5 years, therefore if you want to apply for settled status you will need to apply again once you have been here long enough. There is no charge for moving from pre-settled status to settled status. Yes, there is no assumption you are eligible, or will want to obtain citizenship. If you want citizenship you will need to apply for it. Yes, current immigration rules state your status will lapse if you are out of the UK for 2 consecutive years. Once you obtain settled status this will increase to 5 years being out of the country before settled status lapses for EU citizens. It would be advisable to check Government essential information on travel, and check you have enough time on your passport. If you will be working abroad and leaving the UK from January 2020 onwards, UKVI have outlined advice and information for UK citizens, however you should check with the Country you plan to travel to in advance. The EU Settlement Scheme Resolution Centre will be able to support and advise applicants. They can be contacted by phone on 0300 123 7379 (inside the UK) or +44 (0) 203 080 0010 (outside the UK) or via an online submission form. Please note some calls to UKVI may incur a charge. Our HR UKVI Compliance Adviser is happy to help staff where required. Please email HR-UKVI-Compliance@port.ac.uk for further support. title: Learning and Development Toolkit
headings: Human Resources UoP Learning and Development Toolkit Explore
body: What is the Learning and Development Toolkit? Whatever your role or grade, this innovative online toolkit is packed with professional development materials designed to assist you in dealing with the day-to-day challenges of your job. Whether you are looking to develop your skills and performance, prepare for a PDR or source development opportunities which support personal reflection and Continuing Professional Development then the Learning and Development Toolkit is the place to look.The resources are sub-divided into key themes including UoP specific initiatives to help inspire you. Alternatively if you’re looking for something in particular such as contributing effectively in meetings, problem solving or tips on how to motivate others you can use the search facility which will source all associated resources.These resources are fully referenced, kept up to date and can be viewed on-line or printed off for your ease.How it works The wide range of resources is accessible directly from this welcome page, making the Learning and Development Toolkit both quick and easy to use.Using the intuitive navigation, staff can quickly and simply find the tools and resources they require. The Toolkit features relevant, streamlined content, written by a team of experts and editors, making them easy for staff to both read and understand.Each resource has a short description to ensure the user selects the right resource. Once selected, the complete document can be viewed online, or downloaded in Microsoft Word or PDF format.All resources can be edited and adapted by the user. Such customisation is particularly useful for staff looking for tools they can quickly adapt for an internal audience.Benefits• 24/7 learning and support – available whenever you need it at work or on the move• a wide range of practical resources designed to empower and develop you and your staff • templates, resources, checklists, questionnaires – all fully downloadable and adaptable • cost-effective on-demand learning• key areas covering Personal Skills Development, Coaching and Mentoring, Policies and Procedures• complements a blended learning approach and supports the University's events programmes• intuitive, easy to use system featuring simple navigation• download documents or read online, whatever your preference• no copyright, can top and tail documents and used for teachingAbout the provider: GoodPractice Set up over ten years ago, GoodPractice originally provided an online ‘Knowledge Bank’ for HR managers and trainers. GoodPractice then used their expertise to develop online learning solutions for leaders and managers. Today, the company provides support to over one million leaders, managers and employees throughout the UK and beyond.As the learning landscape has changed so too has GoodPractice’s approach. As well as providing online toolkits, they now provide high quality learning resources across a range of different media, including e-learning, audio, video and mobile. title: Who is Responsible?
headings: Human Resources Progressing Your Career - Taking Ownership Progression top tips 1. Exploit opportunities 2. Increase your visibility 3. Discuss your career… 4. Network 5. Develop your knowledge 6. Create a toolkit Explore
body: Careers rarely move forward by themselves. While your employer may take an active interest in developing your skills and knowledge, it’s ultimately your responsibility to achieve your long-term career goals. Everyone has different career ambitions. However, whether you want to become the next Chief Executive or just fancy a change of scenery, the following tips should help you make progress. If you feel that your talents are not being fully utilised or developed, look for opportunities where you can apply your skills or gain experience. For example, volunteer to organise an event or be part of a working party, join a project team, offer to help induct new team members or overhaul ineffective systems or processes. Keep your eye on internal bulletins for vacancies or secondment opportunities. Ask colleagues in other departments about any future opportunities and ask them how best to make your interest known. Ask your manager about job rotation or shadowing programmes. Not all career development opportunities are easy to spot or create. So, in order to make sure you are considered for any that arise, you will need to increase your visibility within the organisation through self-promotion. Self-promotion is about making others aware of your abilities and ambitions. In order to build this awareness, you will need to market yourself and demonstrate your talents wherever possible. Exploit the opportunities that come your way, as mentioned above, and try to create your own. Think especially about promoting your ambition and potential in order to find opportunities to develop your skills and knowledge, e.g. if you want to manage others, you’ll need to demonstrate that you are responsible and have good interpersonal skills. Some additional suggestions are: participate actively and positively in meetings get involved in events and team activities, e.g. exhibitions, social events, charity work, staff council meetings, etc offer to buddy, coach or mentor other team members get to know people at all levels in other departments write articles for inhouse newsletters and magazines … with your manager. A career discussion with your manager can help you work out the best ways of bringing the opportunities you are looking for into your current role. Be clear in your own mind what it is you want to achieve and present some suggestions or options. You should work in partnership with your manager to discover solutions that are mutually beneficial. Be realistic and open to feedback and advice. Remember your manager will have objectives to meet and boundaries to work within. … with your colleagues/friends. If you’re struggling to find any direction or answers to your career questions, ask your friends. If you feel frustrated and not sure how to take the initiative, ask the people whose opinion you value what they think you are good at or should be pursuing. … with your family/partner/etc. Your career will impact on the people in your life outside your job. Their opinions will be valid and they often have a very good insight into your true needs. Networking is a method of self-promotion and can be done both internally within an organisation, and externally. It involves building a directory of contacts with whom you can build productive, long-term, mutually beneficial relationships. Your contacts are people you can call upon for advice, information, help or support. Active networking involves targeting certain individuals or environments to plug gaps. For example, if you want to know more about marketing, you’ll need to find people who work in that field by either asking your existing contacts or attending events where marketing people are likely to be. Some networking top tips: Make the most of any networking opportunities in order to find new contacts to add to your network. For example, join a professional association, keep in touch with former colleagues, participate in web forums or networking groups and attend events. Talk to your contacts about yourself, your career and your skills in a way that is clear and memorable. Find out as much as you can about your contacts so that you can do your best to help them in return. Work at maintaining the relationship. Get to know your contacts on a personal basis and keep in touch regularly. Don’t just contact them when you need their help. The more you know about yourself the easier it is to identify the right career opportunity. The more you know about what’s available the greater choice you’ll have. It’s important, therefore, to be knowledgeable about your field of interest and to research other areas to explore any potential opportunities. When considering your career options and choices, do you know what skills and experience you need? Do you know enough about the industry or sector to gauge whether opportunities are easy to find and the operating environment will suit you? Do this by: looking at the job market and noting the requirements for the type of job you would like to do tapping into your network for contacts in your field of interest attending events, meetings, exhibitions, etc. reading relevant journals, magazines and publications Being able to seize an opportunity when it presents itself is crucial. Whether the opportunity is a job that’s been advertised or a chance meeting with someone who thinks they can help you, you must be ready to respond in the most impressive and professional way. Consider the following as essential career progression tools: CV and covering letter. Keep your CV up to date. Submitting a CV is often the first step in the recruitment process and yours must stand out from the crowd. A good, well-presented CV is therefore key to getting an interview and is your first opportunity to make a good impression. Always tailor your CV to the role you’re applying for. Employers are looking for a match between your abilities and their requirements. Always send your CV with a covering letter in which you should introduce yourself, briefly explain why you’re interested in the role and why you think you are a strong candidate. Interview technique. Interviews can be nerve-racking but are the most common way of assessing candidates. Remember, an interview is an exchange of information and is your opportunity to evaluate the role against your career criteria so ask questions when invited to do so. Ask a colleague to interview you so you can practise talking about yourself and articulating your talents. Journaling. Maintain a record of the achievements, skills and other competencies you develop throughout your career. Note all relevant development activities and events, responsibilities and achievements so you can update your CV accordingly.
