title: Course requirements
headings: Human Resources Course requirements Off - the - job training Manager’s support Mentoring English and Maths requirement Explore
body: To be eligible for an apprenticeship, the individual must require significant new learning to take place. An apprenticeship requires staff to spend 20% of their paid working time on “off-the-job training”. This is learning that takes place outside of their normal day-to-day work and leads towards achievement of the apprenticeship. Some courses require weekly attendance and others are block release, but across the entire programme staff must have spent 20% of their paid hours on off-the-job training. Off-the-job training must take place within the person’s paid working hours, and therefore applicants would need to have their manager’s endorsement and agreement that their workload will be adjusted. Apprenticeships are based on the work that you do in your employed hours, and therefore it is critical that your manager supports your application. This is not only important for the off-the-job training time and the implications on the individual’s workload, but also to ensure the staff are able to apply new learning in the workplace and facilitate opportunities for taking on new tasks, as appropriate. Course delivery may require line managers to have some involvement in certain assessments (for example being interviewed or attending presentations delivered by their member of staff). Before making an application, a written proposal should be submitted to your line manager. All staff will be required to have an allocated mentor while they are studying for their apprenticeship. This arrangement facilitates more individualised reflection on learning and exploration of specific themes relevant to their workplace and role. The mentor may be the line manager, or it may be another person with some knowledge of the job role and experience in management. The mentor may change during the programme due to changes in staffing, mentor commitments or the needs of the individual. In order to raise the level of skills in the UK workforce, the government has stipulated that English and Maths must be a mandatory part of every Apprenticeship. Applicants are required to show evidence of having achieved GCSE grade A* to C (or 9 to 4) in English and maths (or approved equivalent). Applicants without level 2 English and/or Maths will need to achieve these in order to complete their apprenticeship. International qualifications will be acceptable where there is clear evidence from UK NARIC (via a Statement of Comparability) that the qualification is an equivalent level and grade to the minimum requirements for English and maths. (It is worth noting that English must have been studied as a primary language in order to meet these requirements; English language proficiency tests, such as IELTS and TOEFL, are not accepted as GCSE equivalent for apprenticeship funding. Equally, qualifications studied in the medium of English are not acceptable; qualifications must be studied in the precise subject. E.g. a degree in English Literature would be acceptable for meeting the English requirements, but a masters in Civil Engineering studied at a UK institution would not be suitable for meeting the maths or English requirements.) Any staff members who are not able to provide evidence that they hold suitable qualifications to meet the Level 2 requirements of the apprenticeship will be required to take Functional Skills Level 2 qualifications in English and/or maths. Depending on the apprenticeship, the training provider and the skills level of the individual, this can be offered either before starting the apprenticeship or alongside studies. We must comply with these rules in order for applicants to be able to access apprenticeship funding. title: Recruitment guidance for temporary roles
headings: Human Resources Recruitment guidance and the contract process for employing temporary staff The Careers and Employability Service offers 2 options: Suitable candidate already identified Explore
body: If you haven't recruited a temporary member of staff before and require some advice on the process, please raise a ticket via People Hub. If you have recruited before and understand the process, you just need to complete a Temporary Employee Requisition Form, add a job description/person specification and send to jobsboard@port.ac.uk. The advert will be used to promote your temporary role through the Careers and Employability Service (previously known as Purple Door) Online Jobs Board- an advert will be placed online for students and / or graduates depending on the role and as requested by managers. Applicants will apply directly to managers as detailed in the advert. Graduate Recruitment Consultancy (GRC)- for graduate-level roles, an email will be sent out to final-year students & graduates who are registered with the GRC. Applicants will apply directly to managers as detailed in the advert. GRC do not select or match candidates for these roles. If you have already identified a suitable candidate for this role, who has the essential skills (e.g. requirement for research, specialist secretarial skills etc), you may offer the role to them without advertising the role with the Careers and Employability Service. title: Visiting Staff (from another institution) and the prevention of illegal working
headings: Human Resources Honorary and Visiting Appointments Procedure Introduction Definition and scope Visiting Academic, Professional or Professor Honorary Professor Appointment Process Review, Termination and Extension of Appointments Explore
body: An honorary or visiting appointment is a voluntary arrangement which allows esteemed individuals from other institutions and businesses to cooperate with academic and/or professional services colleagues at the University. It is awarded as a mark of individual standing and in expectation of a contribution that may benefit the University. It also allows them access to certain University facilities to further collaborative research to the mutual benefit of both themselves and the University. Members of UEB and Directors/Heads of Service have a responsibility for proactively seeking useful honorary and visiting relationships. N.B. There is a separate process for conferring the title of Emeritus Professor. This document defines the criteria and procedure to be followed in offering visiting or honorary appointments. The University Research and Innovation Committee (URIC) or Executive Deans or Directors/Heads (as appropriate) have responsibility for making appointments in line with this procedure and for notifying the People Operations team A letter of appointment for the Honorary/Visiting Professor or Visiting Academic/Professional must be issued by the Vice Chancellor’s Office, Dean’s or Directors /Heads office (as appropriate) Subsequently, the appropriate terms and conditions for the appointment must be issued by the People Operations team for agreement with the appointee All honorary/visiting appointments must be recorded on the University’s Human Resources management information system Appointments may be terminated at the discretion of the Vice Chancellor/Provost The duties of the individuals must be clearly defined and must involve collaboration (which can be virtual) and/or visiting the University of Portsmouth to undertake teaching and/or Research/enterprise. Honorary and visiting roles have different purposes and expectations, as outlined below: To allow someone to work temporarily with us but not paid directly by us, usually to collaborate on research, give lectures, or participate in departmental/school life. They may work with another HEI, in another sector or not currently be employed elsewhere. It will be for a time limited period (e.g. 3 months to 3 years). During that period active engagement in research, innovation or teaching will be guided by clear expectations of the role during that period. These roles are likely to be associated with international exchanges, formal agreements between organisations or as part of education or research grants. A professor from KCL spends 12 months at the University of Portsmouth to complete work as specified in a joint grant award using equipment at Portsmouth. A member of the Red Cross joins the University of Portsmouth for 3 months to co-develop a new education programme that will be delivered in partnership with the University of Portsmouth. It should be noted that where a person is paid directly by us, they will be employed, not contracted as a visiting member of staff. Honorary Professors are conferred as an honour to someone with a substantial connection with the University and the candidate will be a leading international authority in their field (whether academic or from another sector). They may work with another HEI, in another sector or not currently be employed elsewhere. These will be awarded for three years with review and potential to extend. A senior engineer from a company with international expertise in an area of joint interest has delivered lectures for us over many years and has expressed the motivation to collaborate with our staff to explore mutually beneficial bids over the next few years. A Professor from another HEI, renowned in Psychology, would like to deliver some lectures for us voluntarily over the coming years. An honorary and visiting position may be awarded, but if the individual will be working in the UK with us, rather than remotely, it will only be validated once the individual has provided (and had verified) their original Right to Work documents. This is especially important when appointing individuals from overseas who may need a visa to be able to undertake the honorary or visiting role. Further details can be found here. Where it has been agreed that travel expenses will be paid, then travel expenses associated with visits would normally be payable by the host department/school in the University, and this should be specified in the letter of terms and conditions. Appointees to Honorary and Visiting positions will be expected to subscribe to the University’s values and principles . Their endeavours should bring credit to the University . Any Honorary or Visiting appointment is made entirely at the discretion of the University. The criteria, application and approval process for all Honorary and Visiting appointments can be viewed here The Executive Dean or Director/Head of Professional Service must request the appointee completes the personal details form and ensure it is sent to the People Hub via Hornbill together with a copy of the letter confirming the offer of appointment and the relevant RTW document (if required). Heads of School/Service are required to undertake an annual review of all honorary and visiting appointees. Where the Head of School/Service is satisfied that the individual has made a sufficient contribution to the University over the year the appointment will continue. Where an honorary or visiting appointee has not made any or insufficient contribution to the University , or there is no evidence of their contribution, the Head of School/Service should write to the Provost to recommend that the role is terminated. If Provost accepts the recommendation the Executive Dean/Director/Head or Professional Service will notify the individual in writing of the decision to rescind the honorary/visiting title giving 1 months’ notice of the termination of their appointment. The individual may relinquish their honorary/visiting title by writing to their proposer and giving 1 months’ notice. Appointments may be terminated with or without notice by the Provost where: The individual is shown to have engaged in conduct or activities which are contrary to the University’s values The individual is shown to have breached the University’s regulations, policies or procedures The individual is shown to have brought the University into disrepute The individual is charged with, or convicted of, a criminal offence or subject to a civil judgement. A Head of School/Service may also request an extension to the period of appointment, extension can be for a maximum of 3 years in any single request. To request an extension, the following process should be followed: Honorary/Visiting Professor Head of School/Service should provide an updated CV to the Provost together with confirmation of their contribution during their appointment period Visiting Academic/Professional An updated CV should be provided to Dean/Director/Head of Service together with confirmation of their contribution during their visiting period If the Provost or Dean/Director/Head of Service approves the extension, a request should be submitted to the People Hub via Hornbill who will write to the individual to confirm the revised dates for the appointment and to update the terms and conditions of the appointment as appropriate. The University Research and Innovation Committee (URIC) will receive an annual report on honorary and visiting appointees, identifying new and ongoing appointments. title: Skills Workshop
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety First Aider Skills Workshops Why In-House? Is this instead of the Re-Certification Course? Explore
body: A refresher workshop is available in-house to which all First Aiders are strongly recommended to attend. The aims of the workshops are to provide an opportunity for the University's First Aiders to refresh their training, practice unused skills and discuss complex cases. There is a requirement to pre-book the workshop to ensure that there is enough resource available. The advantages of in-house refresher training are: Training would be on site and therefore candidates would be easily contactable by their departments if they were needed urgently. The training would be bespoke to the University situation and could include real scenarios as case studies. The first aiders would have the opportunity to develop networks and initiate future interactions between themselves. No - The skills workshops are an addition to the current First Aider Training Programme. To book onto the next available workshop please email: hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Emergency First Aid at Work (1 day)
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Emergency First Aid (1 day) Course leader Target audience Aims of the workshop Learning outcomes Other staff development opportunities relevant to this topic Explore
body: Academic and support staff who are or will be leading Field Work in the UK or overseas. For candidates to gain knowledge and skills in various subjects relating to First Aid. Understand the importance of basic hygiene in first aid procedures Assess the situation in order to act safely and effectively in an emergency Administer first aid safely to a casualty who is unconscious and/or in seizure Administer cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) promptly and effectively Administer first aid safely, promptly and effectively to a casualty who is wounded or bleeding and or / in shock Administer first aid safely and effectively to a casualty who is choking or suffering from Anaphylaxis Provide first aid for minor injuries For further information about Health and Safety training, please see here:https://staff.port.ac.uk/departments/services/corporategovernance/healthandsafety/training/ Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Emergency First Aid at Work (1 day) First Aid at Work (3 day) First Aid at Work Re-certification (2 Day) FAQs Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness First Aid Emergency First Aid at Work (1 day) First Aid at Work (3 day) First Aid at Work Re-certification (2 Day) FAQs Emergency First Aid at Work (1 day) First Aid at Work (3 day) First Aid at Work Re-certification (2 Day) Genetic Modification Safety Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Safe Use of Compressed Gas Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: First Aid at Work (3 day)
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety First Aid (3 day) Course leader Target audience Aims of the workshop Learning outcomes Other staff development opportunities relevant to this topic Explore
body: Members of staff who have been identified to become a First Aider at Work. To gain the required qualification to be a fully qualified First Aider at work as set by the Health and Safety Executive Acting safely, promptly and effectively in an emergency Treating an unconscious casualty (including seizure) Heart attack, choking and stroke Wounds, bleeding and shock CPR ( cardio-pulmonary resuscitation ) Fractures, sprains, strains spinal injuries and chest injuries Delegates will also gain the ability to maintain simple factual records. They will also gain knowledge on the importance of: Personal hygiene in first aid procedures The legal framework of first aid provision at work The use of first aid equipment provided in the workplace For further information about Health and Safety courses, please see here:https://staff.port.ac.uk/departments/services/corporategovernance/healthandsafety/training/ Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Emergency First Aid at Work (1 day) First Aid at Work (3 day) First Aid at Work Re-certification (2 Day) FAQs Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness First Aid Emergency First Aid at Work (1 day) First Aid at Work (3 day) First Aid at Work Re-certification (2 Day) FAQs Emergency First Aid at Work (1 day) First Aid at Work (3 day) First Aid at Work Re-certification (2 Day) Genetic Modification Safety Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Safe Use of Compressed Gas Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: First Aid at Work Re-certification (2 Day)
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety First Aid (2 day) Course leader Target audience Aims of the workshop Learning outcomes Other staff development opportunities relevant to this topic Explore
body: Members of staff who have the First Aid at Work certificate and need to recertify. To re-gain the required qualification to continue as a fully qualified First Aider at work as set by the Health and Safety Executive How to act in the event of an emergency CPR ( cardio-pulmonary resuscitation ) Wounds, bleeding and shock Injuries to bones, muscles and joints Eye injuries, poisoning Dealing with a casualty overcome by gas and fumes Dealing with major and minor illnesses Students will also gain the ability to maintain simple factual records and provide written information to a doctor or hospital if required and they will also recognise the importance of: Personal hygiene in first aid procedures The legal framework of first aid provision at work The use of first aid equipment provided in the workplace The role of first aiders in emergency procedures Further information about all Health and Safety courses can be found here: https://staff.port.ac.uk/departments/services/corporategovernance/healthandsafety/training/ Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Emergency First Aid at Work (1 day) First Aid at Work (3 day) First Aid at Work Re-certification (2 Day) FAQs Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness First Aid Emergency First Aid at Work (1 day) First Aid at Work (3 day) First Aid at Work Re-certification (2 Day) FAQs Emergency First Aid at Work (1 day) First Aid at Work (3 day) First Aid at Work Re-certification (2 Day) Genetic Modification Safety Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Safe Use of Compressed Gas Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: FAQs
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Who is responsible for restocking our First Aid Box? How many First Aider's should there be in my department? What do I do if I require a paramedic/Ambulance? Our first aid boxes are blue not green. Is this ok? Do halls of residences need to provide first aid supplies for the students as well as staff? How often should the contents of first-aid boxes be replaced? Do the provided Resuscitation Face Shields contain Latex? What should I do if someone suffers an eye injury? What should I do if someone suffers a injury involving chemicals? As a First Aider, am I allowed to administer an Epi-pen to a casualty? I have been asked by the University to be a first aider.  Am I insured? With the new waste disposal system in the University, how do I correctly dispose of Clinical Waste? What first aid supplies will I be provided with? Where do I get replacements for my Hygiene pack? Explore
body: If you have a first aid related question you would like answered please email: firstaid@port.ac.uk. Please put 'First aid FAQ' in the subject line. Who is responsible for restocking our First Aid Box? It is the department's responsiblity to restock the First Aid box. For suggested contents of a First Aid Box please refer to the HSE. Alternatively, first aid kits that are in line with BS 8599-1 can now be purchased as well. This standard sets out the minimum level that workplace first aid kits should conform to. Please ensure all First aid supplies purchased are latex free especially plasters. How many First Aider's should there be in my department? Guidance on numbers of First Aider's can be found on the HSE document First Aid at work. The Health & Safety office maintain a First Aid Needs Assessment document - please contact the hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk for further information. What do I do if I require a paramedic/Ambulance? If you require a paramedic/Ambulance, please call 3333. See click here for more information Our first aid boxes are blue not green. Is this ok? Depending on where you have bought your first aid boxes from will affect what colour they are. The colour does not matter, what is important is that the boxes are easily identifiable as a first aid box and that the contents meet requirements of the HSE. Do halls of residences need to provide first aid supplies for the students as well as staff? As this is a First Aid at Work policy the boxes only need to be stocked sufficiently for staff. This is different from the academic buildings as this is also the students place of work. How often should the contents of first-aid boxes be replaced? Although there is no specified review timetable, many items, particularly sterile ones, are marked with expiry dates. They should be replaced by the dates given and expired items disposed of safely. In cases where sterile items have no dates, it would be advisable to check with the manufacturers to find out how long they can be kept. For non-sterile items without dates, it is a matter of judgement, based on whether they are fit for purpose. Do the provided Resuscitation Face Shields contain Latex? The St Johns Ambulance Resuscitation Face Shields provided at the First Aid Refresher Sessions do not contain latex What should I do if someone suffers an eye injury? If someone suffers an injury to their eye, the first aider should advise them to seek medical attention as soon as possible What should I do if someone suffers a injury involving chemicals? If someone suffers an injury involving chemicals and needs to be transferred to hospital. Safety Data Sheets (SDS's) for that chemical should be taken with them to aid the medical team. This needs to be considered as part of the COSHH assessment before the work begins. As a First Aider, am I allowed to administer an Epi-pen to a casualty? The use of an Epi-pen to treat anaphylactic shock is prescribed to be self administered. The first aider's role in this situation is limited to helping them do so and contacting the emergency services as appropriate, unless specifically trained to administer it on their behalf. If you would like training in this area please contact hstraining@port.ac.uk I have been asked by the University to be a first aider. Am I insured? Please refer to the University's Liability Insurance webpages With the new waste disposal system in the University, how do I correctly dispose of Clinical Waste? The waste should be put into the yellow clinical waste bags which are kept in the First Aid boxes. Please see TGN MS05: Hygiene Guidance for First Aiders for disposal methods. What first aid supplies will I be provided with? When you complete your first aid at work course, the Health & Safety office will issue you with a Hygiene pack to help with your first aiders duties. The contents of this pack are as followed: HSE Booklet: Basic advice on first aid at work, a pair of Nitrile Gloves, a bio-hazard waste bag, St Johns Face shield (for rescuitation), two anti-bacterial and anti viral wipes to be used in accordance with TGN MS05: Hygiene Guidance for First Aiders and an apron. Extra first aid equipment including the contents of your first aid box is the departments responsiblity to keep fully stocked. Where do I get replacements for my Hygiene pack? For replacements for your Hygiene pack please contact the Health & Safety Office on firstaid@port.ac.uk. Will my first aider role be affected by pregnancy? This is entirely an individual decision. As long as you are happy to remain being a first aider, no further action is necessary. However, if you feel your pregnancy is affecting your ability to be a first aider you can talk to your manager about a referral to Occupational Health or alternatively, you can temporarily step down so you would not need to attend first aid incidents. What should I do if there is a first aid incident during a fire alarm evacuation and the injured casualty cannot be moved? First aiders who feel confident to do so can decide on what action to take in this scenario. However, if you are unsure we can provide the following guidelines: An injury in which the casualty cannot move but could be moved in an emergency i.e. inconsciousness, epilepsy etc. When the fire alarm activates, the casualty should be moved by the first aider into the stairwell (fire protected area). This action may have to be achieved by unconvenitonal methods, such as dragging the casualty. Once the stairwell (fire porotected area), first aid treatment can be continued. If the fire is confirmed, Security will begin evacuation procedures. Depending on the situation, an evacuation chair could be utilised to bring the casualty down to the ground floor An injury in which the casualty cannot move and shouldn't be moved i.e. spinal and neck injuries In line with first aid procedures, the emergency services should be contacted via the Security Lodge on ext. 3333 as the person's injuries require professional assistance. If the fire alarm activates, the first aider can stay with the casualty and continue care. They need to relay a message to the Fire Liaison Officer and/or Security (via a fire marshal, another person or phoning the Security Lodge) that they are currently giving first aid treatment and cannot move the person. In the event of visible fire and smoke in the area or a confirmed fire in the building, the casualty should be moved by the first aider into the stairwell (fire protected area). This action may have to be achieved by unconventional methods, such as dragging the casualty away from the risk area regardless of the casualty's injuries. Security will begin evacuation procedures. Depending on the situation, an evacuation chair could be utilised to bring the casualty down to the ground floor. Please contact the Health and Safety Office on ext. 3075 or via hsservicedesk@port.ac.uk if you would like any further information. title: Get your prospectus
headings: Discover our courses Get your copy of our prospectus Order a digital undergraduate prospectus Order your digital postgraduate degree guide We use cookies
body: View our digital undergraduate or postgraduate prospectus Our horizons are broad. Our instinct is to explore. Our promise is to inspire. View our undergraduate or postgraduate prospectus and step into our world. Whatever stage you're at in choosing a degree and university, you'll find everything you need to know about studying in Portsmouth in our prospectus. Inside our undergraduate prospectus you'll find many reasons to choose Portsmouth: from our Gold-rated teaching, to our student-friendly city, 4 miles of beaches and great nightlife. Explore our Master's courses and research degrees in our postgrad course guides. We may use cookies to analyse visitor data, improve our website, show personalised content and give you a great website experience. For more information about cookies on our site and to tailor the cookies we collect, select ‘Manage settings’.

title: Register
headings: Music Register for musical activities Explore
body: If you are interested in musical activities, please email music@port.ac.uk with the following information: Your email address (required) University of Portsmouth Graduate If you sing or play any instruments, please give details together and an approximate indication of your standard and whether you have your instrument with you in Portsmouth. A member of the Music team will be in contact shortly with further details. Contact and departments

title: What we offer
headings: Music What we offer Choir Orchestra Concert Band Big Band Private Tuition Practice Rooms Music Society Explore
body: Here at the University of Portsmouth, music plays an increasingly important part in our learning environment. Although it is a part of a few degree programmes, music is mainly recreational. That means that every student is given the same opportunity to join one of our ensembles, attend performances and perform in concerts. We aim to provide a positive musical experience to all who are interested - students, staff or members of the community, regardless of ability and experience. It's a wonderful way to meet people, make friends and engage in a collective and collaborative activity over and above studies. Listed below are the ensembles that we offer and a brief description of what they entail. If you would like to join one of these groups after reading this section, please go to our register here page. Full details of rehearsal times and venues are available on our news and events page. We offer two choirs: our Student Chorus, which performs lighter repertoire, and the main University of Portsmouth Choir, which tackles major choral works in addition to lighter repertoire. We encourage students to be in both and we run rehearsals back-to-back to facilitate that engagement. In recent years, our concerts have included songs from the shows and charts plus works such as Haydn’s Nelson Mass, Faure's Requiem and Handel’s Messiah. We have also collaborated with other choirs, including the Portsmouth Festival Choir and commissioned new works from internationally renowned composers. The choirs perform at least three times a year in a variety of local venues and it takes part in Campus Christmas, festive singing carols around campus at Christmas time. The student choir rehearses from 4.30pm with the rest of the choir joining them at 5.30pm until 7.30pm. Rehearsals take place on Wednesday evenings in Eldon Building, Room EW1.11. Our Orchestra performs a wide variety of repertoire with three programmes each year, usually performed in a shared concert with the Concert Band and/or Choirs. The orchestra plays music ranging from well-known movie soundtracks to Dvorak's New World Symphony to dance music. Players need to be a minimum of grade 5 standard, and wind players will usually be asked to audition in a friendly environment at the beginning of the year due to a high demand for places. Rehearsals take place on Thursday evenings from 6.00pm-8.00pm in Studios 4 and 5 of the White Swan Building. Our Concert Band performs a light repertoire of music from the stage and screen, pop songs from various decades and well-known classical pieces and folksongs. All woodwind and brass instruments are welcome, as well as string bass, drums and percussion. The recommended standard for members is Grade 3 minimum, although beginners are welcome to enhance their learning in a friendly, relaxed environment. The Concert Band rehearses every Tuesday evening from 6.30pm-8.30pm in Studios 4 and 5 of the White Swan Building. This is a full big band with saxophone, trumpet, trombone and rhythm sections plus singers. As well as jazz standards, the band also plays funk, soul and blues numbers. It plays in a wide variety of settings including local pubs, at concerts and balls. Auditions are held for singers and in sections that are over-subscribed. Rehearsals take place on Monday evenings from 6.00-8.00pm in Studio 5 of the White Swan Building. The Music Department is able to private tuition in voice, piano, violin, clarinet, saxophone and music theory. We may be able to find a teacher for most other instruments on request. For more information, contact the Music Department on music@port.ac.uk. The Music Department has some individual practice rooms in the White Swan Building, which is at the heart of the Guildhall campus. These rooms are not suitable for amplified instruments, due to poor sound proofing and are offered on a 'first come, first served' basis on payment of a small fee. For information on access, please contact the Music Department on music@port.ac.uk. Students are encouraged to join the Music Society which is run by students to promote concerts and organise social events. You do not have to be a member of an ensemble to join this but all ensemble members should consider membership. Contact and departments

title: FAQs
headings: Music FAQs How can I find out more about music activities at the University? Where are the rehearsal and practice rooms? Are instrument or voice lessons available? How can I start a group or find other musicians with similar interests? Explore
body: The best way is to email music@port.ac.uk. All the main music facilities are located in the White Swan Building, located on White Swan Road, just behind the New Theatre Royal. Our choirs rehearse in the first-floor lecture theatre (EW1.11) of Eldon Building, which is accessed from Eldon Street. The University offers lessons in voice, piano, music theory and some other instruments, at a cost of about £22 per half hour (£44 per hour). For more information, visit our What We Offer page or else contact music@port.ac.uk. If you are interested in musical activities, please register online. For other enquiries please email music@port.ac.uk. Contact and departments

title: Contact us
headings: Music Contact us Who are we? Cerys Coppins George Burrows Ken Wharton-Emms Further information Explore
body: Email: music@port.ac.ukPhone: 023 9284 3023Address: White Swan Building, White Swan Road, Portsmouth PO1 2GA, United Kingdom Cerys Coppins looks after the day-to-day running of our musical activity at the University of Portsmouth. When she is not administering musical activities, Cerys is a PhD student and a graduate of Portsmouth's BA Musical Theatre degree programme. George Burrows is Professor of Music and Theatre at the University of Portsmouth and interim Director of Music for the University. He has conducted the University's choirs for 20 years and advises Cerys on the running and development of musical activities at the University. Ken Wharton-Emms is director of the Big Band and Concert Band. He is a professional saxophonist and clarinetist, who trained with the Royal Marines and holds a degree in Music and Sound Technology from the University of Portsmouth. If you are interested in musical activities, please register online. If you have any comments about these pages or wish to provide feedback about any aspect relating to the musical life of the University, please email music@port.ac.uk. Contact and departments

title: Information for staff
headings: Freedom of Information Act 2000 Guidance for staff on dealing with individual requests under the Freedom of Information Act 2000 1. Background 2. How is a request defined? 4. Handling the receipt of a request 5. Dealing with a request Explore
body: 1.1 As from the 1st January 2005, the Freedom of Information Act 2000 (FOIA) has given any person the right of access to all information held by Public Authorities. A public authority is defined as a body that receives public funding to carry out some or all of its work. All education establishments (except for nursery schools) are deemed to be public authorities. 1.2 People have the right to request any information from the University. The request must be in writing and can be addressed to any member of University staff but it does not have to state that it is being made under the FOIA. The University has 20 working days from the day after the request has been received (not opened) in which to respond to the request. 2.1 The way the legislation has been written, that is, that a person is not required to state that their request is made under the FOIA, means that any request for information should be treated as a request made under the FOIA. Whilst in practical terms many of the requests the University receives will be the same as they were before the FOIA came into force, all requests must now be answered within 20 working days. 2.2 For a request to be considered under the FOIA it should:- be made in writing – emails, text messages and social media are all acceptable methods of making a request; state the name of the requester and an address for correspondence (which can be an email or other online account), and; describe the information requested. 3.0 Determining whether a request is simple or complex. 3.1 Many requests are for basic information and these are termed “simple” requests. Any request that can be answered easily by, for example, sending a copy of the course prospectus, or with little effort on behalf of the department, can be dealt with in the appropriate way without referral to Corporate Governance. 3.2 There will be, however, requests that are more complex in nature. For example, it may be necessary to find out information that the University holds but which it may not make freely available, or it may also include decisions on whether information can be released. These requests will need to be referred to the Information Disclosure Team, and are termed as "complex" requests. The Information Disclosure Team will alert the Vice-Chancellor and the Director of Corporate Governance, as appropriate. 4.1 All correspondence received in departments must be date-stamped. Where an email or other digital request is received, the date is automatically recorded. 4.2 All staff should have a system where both their letters and their emails can be monitored if a person is absent from the University for more than 2 working days. This is because the 20 working days in which a request must be dealt with begins with the day after the request is received in the University, not the day it is opened or read. Therefore, post or emails cannot go unread whilst someone is on holiday as there is a good risk that the timescale for replying could have expired by the time the request is read. 4.3 Out of Office Replies. It is not sufficient, in the case of an email, just to state in an automatic reply that the email will be dealt with on your return from leave as, by that time, the request will have been received; the 20 working days clock will have started, and some days will have been wasted. Therefore, any automatic reply should not only state that a person is unavailable, but should also state to whom the request should be passed. Including this information in an auto-response means that the email has not been “received” until the requestor sends a second email to the person named in the automatic reply, meaning that none of the 20 working days will be lost due to staff absence. 5.1 Simple requests can be dealt with in departments and so should be treated as any other piece of correspondence. The University has a Publication Scheme that contains a lot of general information about the University. If the information requested is on the Publication Scheme, enquirers can be directed to the Scheme for the information they require (www.port.ac.uk/foi). 5.2 If a request is made over the telephone, and it appears to be a simple request, then the information should be provided anyway. Although the legislation states that a request should be in writing, the spirit of the legislation is to allow access to information, which we might prevent if we insist that the request is made in writing. However, if it appears from the call that the request is a complex one, then the caller should be asked to make their request in writing to ensure we have all the relevant details to help in answering the request. 5.3All complex requests should be sent to the Information Disclosure Team using the email account freedom-of-information@port.ac.uk. 5.4 Complex requests will then be dealt with by the Information Disclosure Team with information from the appropriate departments. Copies of the reply sent will be passed to all appropriate departments for information. title: IG Resources All Staff
headings: Corporate Governance IG Resources for All Staff The Basics Who can I go to for more help? Explore
body: IG Core Training - This eLearning course is available on Docebo (Core Training Learning Plan) for all staff. It covers the basics of Data Protection, Records Management, Freedom of Information and Information Security. You should initially take and pass the course as part of your staff induction. You should retake the course to refresh and update your knowledge every 2 years. Policies - You should familiarise yourself with all IG policies. These are available on our website: Information Governance Policy Data Protection Policy Data Breach Notification Policy Email Policies - Staff and Students FoI Fee Charging Policy Records Management Policy Retention Policy Additional policies relevant to IG: ICT Acceptable Use Policy Information Security Policy Staff Access to University Facilities and Leavers Procedures Information Governance Policy Data Protection Policy Data Breach Notification Policy Email Policies - Staff and Students FoI Fee Charging Policy Records Management Policy Additional policies relevant to IG: ICT Acceptable Use Policy Information Security Policy Staff Access to University Facilities and Leavers Procedures ICT Acceptable Use Policy Information Security Policy Staff Access to University Facilities and Leavers Procedures The University Retention Schedule - This is the document which dictates how long we retain our records. Your area may have a local retention schedule, but it must be drawn from the University Retention Schedule. If you have a need to deviate from the University Retention Schedule, this must be formally raised to the University Records Manager (email recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk). Data Privacy Statements- These set out how the University will manage personal data. We have a number of over-arching statements to cover core University processing, but if your team is collecting data outside of core purposes (e.g. an event or research etc), your area must have its own Privacy Notice(s). Statement for Students Statement for Staff Statement for Applicants & Enquiries Statement for Students Statement for Applicants & Enquiries Staff Pages- Each IG team provides further information and guidance for staff on specific topics: Data Protection Records Management Freedom of Information Information Security Freedom of Information IG Champions- These are volunteers with an interest in IG. They can assist with basic IG queries. Find your local IG Champion What does an IG Champion do? Become an IG Champion - email recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk Find your local IG Champion What does an IG Champion do? Become an IG Champion - email recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk The Information Governance Team: Who are we? Contact us FileStore Key Contacts- FileStore is the term we use for our off site storage service. If your area uses FileStore, you will have a Key Contact, who is responsible for your local account. If you need access to FileStore, it is this person who will need to authorise it by emailing recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk Find your local FileStore Key Contact Find your local FileStore Key Contact Freedom of Information Information Governance Information Matters! title: IG Resources Managers
headings: Corporate Governance IG Resources for Line Managers & Supervisors When a new member of the team starts When a member of the team leaves IG Resources for Heads and Senior Management Information Governance Analytics Explore
body: As a member of staff, you should also refer to the resources for All Staff. HR Staff Induction Checklist - Containing details of all the IG policies a new starter should read can be found linked from the HR Staff Induction page. IG Core Training - Make sure your new starter takes the IG core training session. If they have transferred from another department, you should make sure they have taken the course within the last two years, otherwise they should take it again. Staff Induction Conference - Make sure your member of staff knows about the staff induction conference. Sam and Sarah have a stall in the Market Place... come and see us for information (and sweeties!). We also have a set of virtual resources. System Access- Make sure your member of staff has the right access to the right University systems. This may include: Access to group mailboxes Access to shared drives (K:drive, Google) Access to corporate systems If your member of staff transferred from another department, make sure that any access they had in their previous role that is no longer requried is removed. Access to group mailboxes Access to shared drives (K:drive, Google) Access to corporate systems If your member of staff transferred from another department, make sure that any access they had in their previous role that is no longer requried is removed. Local Rules - Make sure your member of staff is aware of any local protocols for managing information. Staff Leavers Policy- Make sure you follow the staff leavers policy. For IG pay particular attention to: Business Continuity - Make sure that any business critical files/emails are moved to shared storage, so that colleagues can continue working after the person leaves. If the staff member had particular IG responsibilities within the team, make sure that these are transferred to an appropriate colleague. Record Declaration - Make sure that anything which constitutes a University record has been declared into the correct corporate system for long term storage. System Access - Make sure their access to your teams group mailboxes, shared drives (K:drive, Google) and corporate systems is revoked. If they have access to your area's FileStore account, this needs to be removed (ask your FileStore Key Contact to email recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk). If they are transferring to another team, you may need to liaise with their new line manager to ensure the right access is kept/revoked. Return of UoP Property - If the staff member is leaving the University's employ, make sure they return their campus card and any UoP IT equipment for security purposes. Out of Office Message - Make sure they have an out of office message letting people know they have left the team/UoP and who to contact instead for both business matters and FoI requests. Sort Physical Files - If the staff member has a large amount of physical storage, make sure they clear it out before they leave. Anything that constitutes a record should be put into the departmental filing system. Anything no longer required should be appropriately disposed. Business Continuity - Make sure that any business critical files/emails are moved to shared storage, so that colleagues can continue working after the person leaves. If the staff member had particular IG responsibilities within the team, make sure that these are transferred to an appropriate colleague. Record Declaration - Make sure that anything which constitutes a University record has been declared into the correct corporate system for long term storage. System Access - Make sure their access to your teams group mailboxes, shared drives (K:drive, Google) and corporate systems is revoked. If they have access to your area's FileStore account, this needs to be removed (ask your FileStore Key Contact to email recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk). If they are transferring to another team, you may need to liaise with their new line manager to ensure the right access is kept/revoked. Return of UoP Property - If the staff member is leaving the University's employ, make sure they return their campus card and any UoP IT equipment for security purposes. Out of Office Message - Make sure they have an out of office message letting people know they have left the team/UoP and who to contact instead for both business matters and FoI requests. Sort Physical Files - If the staff member has a large amount of physical storage, make sure they clear it out before they leave. Anything that constitutes a record should be put into the departmental filing system. Anything no longer required should be appropriately disposed. Local Staff Files - Weed out anything you no longer need to retain from any local file(s) you may have on your member of staff. As a member of staff, you should also refer to the resources for All Staff. As a line manager, you should also refer to the resources for Line Managers & Supervisors. The Information Governance team are working on providing useful analytics. At present we can offer the following: Core Training Analytics - Details of your area's completion of IG core training is now available via Docebo. FileStore Analytics - If your area uses off site storage (FileStore), Sarah and Stacey can provide analytics on your storage holdings, including where your area may be spending money unnecessarily. Contact recordsmanagement@port.ac.uk Freedom of Information Information Governance Information Matters! title: Email FAQs
headings: Corporate Governance Frequently Asked Questions... Email Policy FAQs Q: Can I use UOP email for personal use? Q: Can I keep my email account open after I leave UoP? Q: I don't want my UoP email address displayed on the website, what can I do? Management of Email FAQs Q: Why do I need to manage my emails? Q: Why can't UoP set a blanket retention period for all emails? Q: Who is responsible for managing emails in group mailboxes? Q: When should emails be declared as records? Q: What is Conversation View and should I use it? Guidance and Advice FAQs Q: How can I make the most of Google's functionality to help me manage my emails? Q: What UoP Guidance is available on managing emails? Explore
body: ‌‌Frequently Asked Questions about the use of Google Email. Email PolicyManagement of EmailGuidance & Advice A: No. See Staff Email Policy A: See Staff Access to University Facilities and Leavers Procedure section 4.3. Prior to your departure, you should discuss with your line manager the need to transfer any correspondence and/or files held in your Google account (including email), which may be required by you colleagues. You are expected to set up Out Of Office messages to state that you have left UoP and provide appropriate forwarding addresses. If a valid business reason exists, accounts can be kept open after you leave, but for no more than 90 days from your leaving date. A “Staff Account Extension Request” can be made via the IS Service Desk and must be authorised by the Executive Director of Corporate Governance. A: Although the General Data Protection Regulation makes email addresses personal data, there is an implicit expectation upon staff that a work email address will used for work purposes. This includes making your email address available to colleagues, students and third parties who may need to contact you for work purposes, including where appropriate the general public. However, if you do not wish your UoP email address to be displayed on the website, you could use a generic address for contacting your business function. This could be a UoP group/shared mailbox, or a simple alias which then redirects straight to your UoP account. A: Managing your email correctly is an important part of modern working life and failure to do so can have significant consequences… just ask Hillary Clinton! For those of us with aspirations set a little lower than leader of the free world, the main impetus to manage our emails correctly comes from legislation, such as the Freedom of Information Act and the General Data Protection Regulation. Not only do these acts impose fines and other penalties for failing to manage information (including email) in accordance with the law; but the requirement to disclose information under such legislation also means that anyone not managing their emails correctly will find themselves quickly regretting that decision when a disclosure request comes in. A: Email is not a record type, it is a format. There is no one retention period that would be appropriate for all email. Whatever time period were to be set, we would end up keeping some emails far too long and others not long enough. If you wish to set a short blanket retention policy on your own email inbox, you can. However, this must be done manually using a search for all emails over your retention period. In addition, if you choose this method of managing your emails, you must have robust processes in place to ensure that any email constituting a record is declared (see below) to the appropriate file (be that physical file or electronic system) for longer storage, as required. A: Everyone with delegated access to the group mailbox has a responsibility to manage the emails within the mailbox. It is advisable to agree some rules and standards that everyone will apply. However, ultimate responsibility for the content of the group mailbox will rest with the designated owner of the mailbox. If you are not sure who the owner is, please contact IS Service Desk, who should be able to assist. A: Emails (or attachments to emails) may constitute a University record if they document the actions or decisions of the University. Attachments which constitute records should be downloaded and stored in an appropriate corporate system, along with the covering email if it adds relevant context. If the body of an email constitutes a record, it should be retained secure, shared location so that those who might need to access and refer to it in the future can do so. RM Factsheet 4 - Email and Modern Media goes into more detail on this subject, although the choice of secure, shared location will ultimately depend upon the type of record and your business area's processes for storing that record type. A: Conversation view is a feature of Google which groups together emails which have the same subject, so that they can be viewed and managed as a single entity or “thread”. This can make managing your emails easier, but it can also make it easier to mistakenly share information (for example: if you accidentally forward on the whole conversation instead of just the latest email). Whether you should use it or not is really a matter of personal preference. A: Google’s in built functionality allows you to sort, filter and search your emails in numerous ways, to assist in managing your email content. For suggestions on using Labels, Stars and Filters to manage your email – see the Email Hints & Tips page Build your own complex searches to find emails for deletion – see Google advanced search operators page For training on the use of Google products for education – IT Training link A: Managing your email in Google isn’t just about which buttons to press… it’s also about what rules to apply in what circumstances. title: Publication schemes
headings: Freedom of Information Act 2000 Introduction What is a publication scheme? What is in the publication scheme? How to access information in the publication scheme Charging Complaints and appeals Explore
body: The Freedom of Information Act 2000 ("the Act") was implemented to improve access to information held by public authorities in order to increase the transparency of how and why public authorities act and make decisions as they do. The University is a public authority under the Act. One of the requirements of the Act is to publish and maintain a publication scheme, which makes available a large amount of information to the public. The Publication List will give you access to documents which are available either through this website or in hard copy. The University also publishes information for the University of Portsmouth Enterprise Limited (UPEL) and University of Portsmouth Investments Limited (UPIL). These can be accessed from the menu on the left of the screen. Please click here for details of who to contact to obtain documents only available on paper or in other formats. A publication scheme is a document setting out groups of information that must be made accessible by the public authority to improve access to the information. This document is then expanded - in a publications list - to identify the actual documents that hold the required information.The first publication schemes for Higher and Further Education were validated by the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) until 31 December 2008. In renewing the schemes, the ICO has amended the format and content of the model scheme prepared for Higher Education institutions and therefore the following publication scheme (live from January 2009) is based on the new requirements. The new publication scheme contains seven classes of information designed to group together the policies and procedures of the University together into sets so that information can be found quickly.The classes of information are: Who we are and what we do What we spend and how we spend it What our priorities are and how we are doing How we make decisions Our policies and procedures The services we offer The Publication Scheme will help you to identify in which class the information you require can be found. You can then look this class up in the Publication List where you will find any relevant policies, procedures or other forms of information. Where possible you can view or download the information straight from the publication scheme. If the document is either not available electronically or you cannot download it, hard copies can be requested from the relevant contacts. There will normally be no charge for providing single items of information in the publication scheme. However, the University reserves the right to make a charge for multiple items, in order to cover administrative costs. Alternative formats may be arranged by special request, but there may also be a charge to cover production costs.Accessing information not covered by the publication scheme It is also possible to request information held by the University that is not available through this publication scheme. Requests should be made in writing, preferably to the Information Disclosure Team. The University has 20 working days in which to respond but will do so sooner, if possible. It is possible that a fee may be charged for the requested information and any information covered by an exemption under the Act may not be released. If you are not satisfied with the way that we have handled a request for information or believe that we have not included information in the publication list that you believe should appear, you have the right to complain / appeal. Questions, comments or complaints relating to the publication scheme should be sent to the Information Disclosure Manager at the address above. The ICO is responsible for enforcing the operation of publication schemes under the Act and, if you are unhappy with the outcome of your complaint to the University, you may appeal directly to the ICO (www.ico.org.uk) University of Portsmouth publication scheme University of Portsmouth Enterprise Limited publication scheme University of Portsmouth Investments Limited publication scheme Requesting information from the University Information for staff Contact and departments

body: This Freedom of Information (FOI) publication scheme highlights all information that is publicly available about the University. The information is arranged under 7 headings to help you find what you want quickly: Who we are and what we do What we spend and how we spend it What our priorities are and how we're doing How we make decisions Our policies and procedures The services we offer We have separate publication schemes for our subsidiary companies – University of Portsmouth Enterprise Limited (UPEL), University of Portsmouth Investments Limited (UPIL), University of Portsmouth Services Limited (UPSL), and ASTA Technology Limited (ASTA). If the information you want isn't available in our publication schemes, you can make a Freedom of Information request. This class has information on how the University is set up, including its legal status, location and contact details. It also includes information about organisations we work with. This includes information about the legal and corporate status of the University. The University of Portsmouth Higher Education Corporation is the legal name for the University, but we're more widely known as the University of Portsmouth. The Education Reform Act 1988 and the Further and Higher Education Act 1992 set out how universities must be constituted. The Instruments of Government (PDF) sets out the constitution for the Board of Governors, its officers and their roles The Articles of Government (PDF) sets out the rules for the conduct of the University, the procedures for the Board of Governors and Academic Council, and the appointment of senior officers of the University How we spend our money University archive (historical records covering the University and its preceding institutions) This section includes further information about the management and structure of the University, including descriptions of the boards and committees we're legally required to have. There's also information on the terms of reference and membership of these boards and committees. Board of Governors: Statement of Primary Responsibilities (PDF) – this highlights the responsibilities of the Board of Governors Standing Orders of the Board of Governors and its Committees (PDF) – this supplements the information in the Instruments of Government and Articles of Government Further information about the Board of Governors Academic Council – includes responsibilities of the Academic Council University Executive Board – members and contacts Central service departments list The University runs a number of key, strategic committees, in the first instance, information on these bodies can be obtained from Paul Mould, the Executive Director of Corporate Governance. University of Portsmouth Enterprise Limited (UPEL) University of Portsmouth Investments Limited (UPIL) University of Portsmouth Services Limited (UPSL) ASTA Technology Limited (ASTA) UOP Academic Services Limited (UOPAS) UOPM Sdn Bhd (UOPM – registered in Malaysia) List of approved collaborative and articulation partners The majority of student clubs, associations and non-academic organisations are run by the University of Portsmouth's Students' Union. Competitive sports teams are run by the University's Department of Sport and Recreation. This section includes information on where the University spends or plans to spend money, sources of funding and income: annual published financial reviews (includes funding information) undergraduate tuition fee information teaching training tuition fee information postgraduate taught tuition fee information postgraduate research tuition fee information (select a programme on this page to see tuition fee information) international tuition fees information The University's financial audit records are included in the annual Published Financial Reviews. Information about major plans for capital expenditure: Fees appeals procedures – see section 13 of the University Tuition Fee Policy (PDF) You can request a copy of the following policies from freedom-of-information@port.ac.uk: Financial regulations University Credit Control Policy Inventory Management Policy Information on the Higher Education Role Analysis (HERA) scheme: Salary Scales for academic and HE support staff The ‘pay multiple’ (the ratio between the highest paid salary and the median average salary of the whole of the HEI’s workforce) – see note 7 in the Annual Financial Accounts Contact the Procurement division of the Finance department by email on procurement@port.ac.uk to get details of our suppliers. Purchasing and tender information for the purchase of goods and services The Director of Corporate Governance has responsibility for ensuring the University keeps records of contracts it bids for. You can request copies of these records from the Executive Director of Corporate Governance by emailing corporate-governance@port.ac.uk. The Purchasing division of the Finance department has responsibility for contracts under tender. Further information is available from the procurement division of the finance department (procurement@port.ac.uk). HE Innovation Fund (HEIF) You can get further information on research funding amounts in the annual published financial accounts. This class outlines the University's strategies, plans and performance indicators and the results of audits, inspections and reviews of the University's priorities. The Annual Financial Reviews now include a detailed Operating and Financial Review (OFR) to complement the financial statements, which cover the University's priorities and measures against those statements. Our Mission and Vision The Department of Student and Academic Administration is responsible for maintaining academic quality and standards. This section includes information about the annual monitoring and review processes, and a statement of roles, responsibilities and authority of the different bodies within the institution involved in programme approval and review. QAA reports on University of Portsmouth Information on the University's links with employers and the development of learning programmes: career service – jobs and work experience pages Accreditation and monitoring reports by professional, statutory or regulatory bodies and information that an institution is legally obliged to make available to its funding and monitoring bodies. Accreditation information available on individual course pages. The University publishes equality information and gender pay gap reports annually. Reports are available on the Equality and Diversity web pages. This section includes documents relating to University Committee structures and minutes of boards and committees Board of Governors meetings minutes Academic Council – copies available on request University Learning and Teaching Committee minutes – copies available on request Policy on Promotion and Appointment to Reader and Professor (PDF) Student Voice Policy - Valuing Students' Views and Opinions Details of many appropriate procedures are available in class 3, What our priorities are and how we are doing. You can also view a list of all policies and view the procedure for requesting information from the University. This section includes details of relevant procedures (for example, external review and academic quality and standards documents) are included in class 2, What we spend and how we spend it. student accommodation information Policy for Recognition of Prior Learning (PDF) Student complaints procedure (PDF) Regulations – see section 9 of the Code of Student Behaviour (PDF) Examination and Assessment Regulations (PDF) Examination and Assessment Regulations for Credit Bearing Short Courses (PDF) Examination and Assessment Regulations for Learning at Work (PDF) Guide to the Appeals Procedure Code of student behaviour People Strategy (PDF) Grievance Procedure (PDF) Disciplinary Procedure (PDF) Dignity and Respect Policy (PDF) – incorporating information on sexual and racial harassment Management of Under-Performance Procedure (PDF) Whistleblowing Policy (PDF) Equality and Diversity Policy Statement (PDF) Declaration of Interests Policy (PDF) Recruitment and Selection Policy (PDF) Standing Orders of the Board of Governors and its Committees (PDF) The University includes equality statements in all of its policies rather than have separate equality policies. body: This Freedom of Information (FOI) publication scheme highlights all information that is publicly available about the University. The information is arranged under 7 headings to help you find what you want quickly: Who we are and what we do What we spend and how we spend it What our priorities are and how we're doing How we make decisions Our policies and procedures The services we offer We have separate publication schemes for our subsidiary companies – University of Portsmouth Enterprise Limited (UPEL), University of Portsmouth Investments Limited (UPIL), University of Portsmouth Services Limited (UPSL), and ASTA Technology Limited (ASTA). If the information you want isn't available in our publication schemes, you can make a Freedom of Information request. This class has information on how the University is set up, including its legal status, location and contact details. It also includes information about organisations we work with. This includes information about the legal and corporate status of the University. The University of Portsmouth Higher Education Corporation is the legal name for the University, but we're more widely known as the University of Portsmouth. The Education Reform Act 1988 and the Further and Higher Education Act 1992 set out how universities must be constituted. The Instruments of Government (PDF) sets out the constitution for the Board of Governors, its officers and their roles The Articles of Government (PDF) sets out the rules for the conduct of the University, the procedures for the Board of Governors and Academic Council, and the appointment of senior officers of the University How we spend our money University archive (historical records covering the University and its preceding institutions) This section includes further information about the management and structure of the University, including descriptions of the boards and committees we're legally required to have. There's also information on the terms of reference and membership of these boards and committees. Board of Governors: Statement of Primary Responsibilities (PDF) – this highlights the responsibilities of the Board of Governors Standing Orders of the Board of Governors and its Committees (PDF) – this supplements the information in the Instruments of Government and Articles of Government Further information about the Board of Governors Academic Council – includes responsibilities of the Academic Council University Executive Board – members and contacts Central service departments list The University runs a number of key, strategic committees, in the first instance, information on these bodies can be obtained from Paul Mould, the Executive Director of Corporate Governance. Competitive sports teams are run by the University's Department of Sport and Recreation. title: DSEAR
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations 2002 (DSEAR) Explore
body: The Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations 2002 (DSEAR) requires the University to make a suitable and sufficient assessment of the risks to his employees, where a dangerous substance is or is liable to be present in the workplace. Any requirement for a DSEAR assessment is identified through the fire risk assessment process and a specific assessment is completed by the Health & Safety Office. Additional information on DSEAR can be found on the HSE webpages Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Radiation Protection Unit
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Radiation Protection Unit Personnel Explore
body: The Radiation Protection Unit is responsible for ensuring that University staff, students, members of the public and the environment are protected from the low level radiation produced in certain research Equipment and experimental studies. For guidance and information in relation to ionising radiation, please speak to any of the following individuals: Dr Graham Malyon: University Radiation Protection Officer, IMS, 07756284614 Dr Darren Gowers: Deputy Radiation Protection Officer and Radiation Protection Supervisor, School of Biological Sciences, St Michael's Building, Ext. 2057 Mr Tom Campbell-Adams: Radiation Protection Supervisor for the Centre of Radiography Education, St Michael's Building, Ext. 4518 Mr Andrew Slack: Radiation Protection Supervisor for the Dental Academy, William Beatty Building, Ext. 5288 Dr Heather Massey: Radiation Protection Supervisor for the DEXA, Sports Science Centre, Extreme Environments Laboratory, Ext. 3545 Please be aware that no new work with ionising radiation may be performed without prior authorisation from the local Radiation Protection Supervisor and the University Radiation Protection Officer. Please see the 'Best Available Techniques' document below. This explains the management structure of radiation protection in the University and describes how we are working to minimise the environmental impact of our activities: Best Available Techniques This information can also be found via the following link: Radiation Protection Unit Please contact the Health and Safety Office if you require further information. Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety COSHH - Safe Use of Chemicals Explore
body: Pre-requisite for Technicians who use chemicals in their work and aspirational for Technical Staff who wish to widen their knowledge as part of development. This training will give participants an understanding of: - the legislative requirements for the use of chemicals and substances hazardous to health in the workplace. As a result of attending, participants will: - have awareness of the legislation that applies to the use of chemicals and substances hazardous to health - be able to outline the classifications of hazardous substances and associated ill-health risks - know what to include and where to find the relevant information for your COSHH risk assessments - be able to identify suitable and sufficient control measures and how to maintain them - understand when exposure monitoring and health surveillance is required - understand the requirements for emergency planning Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Genetic Modification Safety Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Safe Use of Compressed Gas Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Training Explore
body: Pre-requisite for Technicians who work with chemicals. This training will give participants an understanding of: - Impact of chemical spillages on the University - Overview of the relevant legislation - Risk assessment which includes identifying chemical hazards, routes of entry and people at risk - Procedure for dealing with chemical spillages - Control measures for chemical spillages As a result of attending, participants will be able to: - Explain why dealing with spillages correctly is important and the consequences of not doing so - Identify the legislation relevant to spillages of hazardous materials. - Outline the hazards associated with chemicals that are used on campus. - Outline the procedures for dealing with spillages. title: Fire Safety in Laboratories
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Fire Safety in Laboratories Explore
body: Technical, Academic and Research staff who work in laboratories. To give members of staff a greater understanding of fire hazards in laboratories; how to control them and how to comply with the Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations 2002 (DSEAR). To provide candidates with an understanding in the following subject areas: - The principles of fire and explosions - The properties of solids, liquids and gases - The mechanisms of fire and explosions (and provide an explanation of the fire triangle and fire quadrahedron) - Fire and explosion prevention - Requirements of DSEAR - Able to identify potential fuel and ignition sources. - Have an understanding of exothermic reactions, water reactive and pyrophoric substances - Have an understanding of control and mitigation measures - Have an understanding of the requirements of DSEAR Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Genetic Modification Safety Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Safe Use of Compressed Gas Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Pressure Systems Safety
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Pressure Systems Safety Explore
