title: Safe Use of Compressed Gas
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Safe Use of Compressed Gas – Theory and Practical Explore
body: Technical, Academic and Research staff who use Compressed Gases. To give members of staff a greater understanding of the hazards associated with compressed gases and how to comply with the Dangerous Substances and Explosive Atmospheres Regulations (DSEAR) 2002 and the Control of Substances Hazardous to Health 2002 (COSHH). The practical session will give candidates the opportunity to install gas handling equipment, such as 2 stage pressure reduction heads and flame arresters. This training will give participants an understanding of: - A legislative overview - The properties of gases - Types of cylinders and how to identify them - Hazards associated with compressed gases - Principles of combustion, including oxygen enrichment - Requirements of relevant legislation The practical session will include manual handling of gas cylinders, connection and testing of gas handling equipment, maintenance and use of personal protective equipment. As a result of attending, participants will be able to: - Know the properties and classification of compressed gas - Identify the different types of compressed gas and cylinders - Identify the health hazards associated with compressed gases - Identify potential fuel and ignition sources - Select suitable control measures - Outline the requirements of legislation, including DSEAR and COSHH in relation to compressed gases - Safely install and test gas handling equipment Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Genetic Modification Safety Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Safe Use of Compressed Gas Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Biological Safety
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Explore
body: Biological Safety Training Christina Scott, Biological Safety Officer Pre-requisite for anyone with responsibilities in biological laboratories; particularly those working in CL2 laboratories. Aspirational for staff and students who wish to widen their knowledge as part of development. This training will give participants an understanding of: - Why biological safety matters - How to contain biological agents - Risk assessment process relating to biological hazards As a result of attending, participants will be able to: - identify the legislation applicable to the safe use of biological agents - outline the requirements of containment level laboratories - outline the hazards associated with the biological agents they handle - outline the control measures that may be used. title: Gloves/ Hand Protection
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Chemical Protection DIN EN 374 – Protection from Chemical Risks Marking of Type A Gloves Marking of Type B Gloves Marking of Type C Gloves Mechanical Resistance to Abrasion Blade Cut Resistance Tear Resistance Puncture Resistance Thermal Protection from Ignition Protection from Contact Heat Protection from Convective Heat Protection from Thermal Radiation Protection from Droplets of Molten Metal Protection from Molten Metal Cold Convective Cold Contact Cold Water Permeability Electrostatic Important! What should the user look out for? What can gloves tested in accordance with DIN EN 16350:2014 be used for? Manual Handling Explore
body: The European standard DIN EN 374 defines the performance requirements for gloves, which protect the wearer from chemicals and micro-organisms. Most chemical resistant gloves will be tested to EN 374 - 1. EN 374 - 1 will test your gloves to a total of 18 different chemicals before awarding them with a certification of either A, B or C depending on how resistant the glove is to chemicals. Finally, this standard also includes a logo that quickly informs the user of which chemicals the glove can be used with. The letters correlate to a chemical that can be identified in the table below the logo. Finally, the type corresponds to the information below the chemical table. Protective gloves that comply with a permeation performance of at least level 2 (Table 1) against a minimum of six test chemicals listed in Table 2, the following pictograms shall be used with reference to this part of EN 374. The six tested chemicals shall be identified by their code letter which shall be marked under the pictogram as shown below. If other chemicals not present in the list have been tested, information about the performance levels shall be provided in the user instructions Protective gloves that comply with a permeation performance of at least level 2 (Table 1) against a minimum of three test chemicals listed in Table 2, the following pictograms shall be used with reference to this part of EN 374. The three tested chemicals shall be identified by their code letter which shall be marked under the pictogram as shown below. Protective gloves that comply with a permeation performance of at least level 1 against a minimum of one test chemical listed in Table 2, the following pictograms shall be used with reference to this part of EN 374. The tested chemical shall be identified by its code letter which shall be marked under the pictogram as shown below. Type C gloves offer lower levels of protection in terms of breakthrough time as well as for only one test chemical, type C gloves are designed to be for low chemical risks. Measured Breakthrough Time (min) Measured Breakthrough Time (min) Permeation Performance Level Permeation Performance Level Table 2: Permeation Resistance Ammonium Hydroxide 25% Ammonium Hydroxide 25% Hydrogen Peroxide 30% Hydrogen Peroxide 30% Hydroflouric Acid 69% Hydroflouric Acid 69% Table 3: Chemicals and their Letter Key Safety gloves are classified with a performance level in relation to each of the individual mechanical hazards. The relevant values (number from 0 to 5, with 4/5 being the best) can be seen next to the pictogram of the glove. The mechanical risks and stresses and/or their test methods are defined in DIN EN 388 as follows. To test the safety glove's resistance to abrasion, the material is treated with sandpaper under pressure. The number of cycles needed to abrade a hole in the material serves as a benchmark. (Highest performance level 4 = 8,000 cycles) Abrasion Resistance (number of rubs) Abrasion Resistance (number of rubs) To check the blade cut resistance of a safety glove, a rotating circular blade is used, which cuts through the glove at a constant speed. Comparison with a reference material serves as a benchmark and a resulting index. (Highest performance level 5 = index 20) Blade Cut Resistance (index) Blade Cut Resistance (index) To check tear resistance, the material of the safety glove is firstly slit. The force needed to tear the material serves as a benchmark. (Highest performance level 4 = 75 Newtons) Tear Resistance (Newtons) Tear Resistance (Newtons) To test the puncture resistance, the glove is punctured with a nail (established dimension). The force used serves as a benchmark. (Highest performance level 4 = 150 Newtons) Puncture Resistance (Newtons) Puncture Resistance (Newtons) DIN EN 407 regulates the minimum requirements and specific test methods for safety gloves in relation to thermal risks. Safety gloves certified in accordance with this standard protect the wearer from, for example, contact heat, radiant heat and small splashes from molten metal. However, it does not concern the specific application of heat-resistant gloves, such as firefighting or welding. Heat-resistant gloves are intended to fulfil the following properties in accordance with DIN EN 407: low flammability or flame spread low heat transmission (protective effect from radiant, convective and contact heat) high temperature resistance Safety gloves are classified and tested in DIN EN 407 in accordance with the following criteria. Safety glove is exposed to a gas flame for 15 seconds. Then measurement of the time until the glove material ceases to burn/glow. (Highest performance level 4 = afterflame time 2 seconds, afterglow time 5 seconds) Temperature measurement (100 °C to 500 °C) against which the glove provides protection for 15 seconds without the inside of the glove heating up by over 10 °C. (Highest performance level 4 = + 500 °C) Gradually penetrating heat: time measurement, the length of time the glove can delay a temperature increase of the inside of the glove – by heat penetration from an open fire – of over 24 °C. (Highest performance level 4) Safety glove is exposed to thermal radiation. Time measurement until a certain heat has penetrated the inside of the glove. (Highest performance level 4 = at least 150 seconds) Measurement of how many droplets of molten metal are needed to increase the temperature between the glove material and skin by 40 °C. (Highest performance level 4 = over 35 droplets) Measurement of how many grams of molten iron are needed to damage synthetic PVC skin (attached to the inside of the glove). (Highest performance level 4 = 200 grams) In accordance with the test in DIN EN 407, the safety gloves are classified with a performance level in relation to each of the individual thermal hazards (numbers from 1 to 4, with 4 being the best). What is important is that the glove must not come into contact with open fire if it does not fulfil performance level 3 in the fire resistance test. Minimum requirements for safety gloves in relation to protection from cold are regulated in standard DIN EN 511. The gloves certified under this standard are intended to protect the wearer from convective cold (penetrating cold) and contact cold (direct contact). As with protection from thermal and mechanical risks, the safety glove is classified into different performance levels for individual aspects. The performance levels are indicated with a number from 1 to 4 next to the pictogram, where 4 is the highest level. Determination of performance levels for safety gloves against cold is as follows. Measuring the quantity of energy needed to maintain the temperature of a heated hand model (30 to 35 °C) with a safety glove in relation to a constant room temperature. (Calculation of the thermal insulation properties based on the temperature of the hand model, room temperature and energy required to maintain the temperature.) Test in accordance with ISO 5085. Determination of thermal resistance of the safety gloves with a cold and a hot plate. The glove material is placed as an insulator between both plates and the change in the temperature gradient acts as a measure and comparison with normative reference. The glove can also be tested for water permeability in accordance with EN ISO 15383. If no water permeates into the glove over a 30-minute period, this test is deemed as passed (no specific performance levels). For workplaces with a risk of fire and explosion, there is now a European standard, DIN EN 16350:2014, which defines the test conditions and minimum requirements for the electrostatic properties of safety gloves: The contact resistance must be less than 1.0 × 108 ohms (Rv< 1.0 × 108 Ω). Test atmosphere: air temperature of 23 ± 1 °C, relative humidity 25 ± 5 %. Safety gloves with antistatic characteristics are only effective if the resistance between the wearer and the earth is less than 108 ohms. Our products are tested in accordance with DIN EN 16350:2014 and are suitable for both product and work protection. The old classification in accordance with DIN EN 1149-1:2006 is no longer permitted. The tested surface resistance only gives the charge transfer to the surface of the material and is not sufficient to guarantee effective protection. Safety gloves that have been successfully tested in accordance with DIN EN 16350:2014 can be used in workplaces with a risk of fire and explosion (e.g. refineries) and form an essential part in the earthing chain (glove – protective clothing – shoes – ground). In connection with electrostatic properties, the electrostatic discharge ("electrostatic discharge", or "ESD") is frequently considered in the area of product protection. Safety gloves tested in accordance with DIN EN 16350:2014 can be used for all applications of ESD product protection. There is no standard for manual handling gloves. Things to consider are dexterity, grips and water resistance depending on the task and the environment. Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Eye Protection
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Introduction Grade S Grade F Comparison Relevant Standards Explore
body: Each type of eye protection is specifically designed to protect the eyes from various hazards. Some equipment such as safety masks can also be classed RPE (Respiratory Protective Equipment) due to its additional protective qualities. The selection of face/ eye protection will depend on the task. It should be noted that standard spectacles are not adequate PPE, and should be worn with safety specs on top to offer protection from splashes. Alternatively prescription safety glasses are available. Can be used in hazardous areas where there is risk of small flying particles from other workers or processes in the vicinity but where the wearer is not close to the source of the hazard. Is suited for tasks like drilling, sawing, grinding, hammering or risk from any process which could emit particles whose energy equates to the test specifications above. Safety Glasses (Spectacles) Safety Glasses (Spectacles) Safety glasses are a stylish choice of eye protection, although they may not offer the highest level of protection. Safety glasses, also known as safety spectacles, are sold in various PPE stores. Safety glasses are classed as PPE and therefore will need to be provided by the employer at no charge to the employee. Smoke safety spectacles are popular due to some of them having protection against UV. Safety glasses are a stylish choice of eye protection, although they may not offer the highest level of protection. Safety glasses, also known as safety spectacles, are sold in various PPE stores. Safety glasses are classed as PPE and therefore will need to be provided by the employer at no charge to the employee. Smoke safety spectacles are popular due to some of them having protection against UV. Safety goggles are common in various environments such as laboratories and workshops. Safety goggles tend to be made up of an acrylic lens, rubber frame and elastic strap. These can be purchased for low prices and are readily available. Protective goggles are used to protect against particles, shrapnel and in some circumstances vapours and gasses. Safety goggles, also known as eye goggles, may not be rated for the level of protection required. This is why it is always best to check with a supplier to ensure you purchase the correct level of protection. Safety goggles are common in various environments such as laboratories and workshops. Safety goggles tend to be made up of an acrylic lens, rubber frame and elastic strap. These can be purchased for low prices and are readily available. Protective goggles are used to protect against particles, shrapnel and in some circumstances vapours and gasses. Safety goggles, also known as eye goggles, may not be rated for the level of protection required. This is why it is always best to check with a supplier to ensure you purchase the correct level of protection. Face shields offer full face protection against flying debris amongst other hazards. Face shields, also known as safety masks, can also offer protection against gases, particles and vapours. Visors share many similarities to face shields, however they do not offer full-face protection. Face shields offer full face protection against flying debris amongst other hazards. Face shields, also known as safety masks, can also offer protection against gases, particles and vapours. Visors share many similarities to face shields, however they do not offer full-face protection. Low resistance. No seal- does not offer ‘wrap around’ protection - risk from sides, below and above. Suitable for low risk activities Low resistance. No seal- does not offer ‘wrap around’ protection - risk from sides, below and above. Suitable for low risk activities Housing has higher impact resistance with greater protection from hazardous liquids, dust and gases. Gasket on the rear forms a tight seal with the wearers skin Housing has higher impact resistance with greater protection from hazardous liquids, dust and gases. Gasket on the rear forms a tight seal with the wearers skin Housing has even higher impact resistance but offering head protection and prevents damage to soft tissues of the face. Often safety spectacles are worn beneath in addition. Housing has even higher impact resistance but offering head protection and prevents damage to soft tissues of the face. Often safety spectacles are worn beneath in addition. Impact resistance for mechanical strength F is tested using a ballistic test - firing a 6mm steel ball weighing 0.86g travelling at maximum 45 m/s at the time of impact. Impact resistance for mechanical strength F is tested using a ballistic test - firing a 6mm steel ball weighing 0.86g travelling at maximum 45 m/s at the time of impact. Goggles provide the minimum protection with mechanical strength B (6mm 0.86g steel ball at max 120m/s on impact) Goggles provide the minimum protection with mechanical strength B (6mm 0.86g steel ball at max 120m/s on impact) Face shields provide protection with mechanical strength A (6mm 0.86g steel ball at max 190m/s on impact) Face shields provide protection with mechanical strength A (6mm 0.86g steel ball at max 190m/s on impact) Table 4: Comparison between Glasses/Goggles and Face Shield Other relevant EN standards are: EN 169, Personal eye-protection - Filters for welding and related techniques - Transmittance requirements and recommended use EN 170, Personal eye-protection - Ultraviolet filters - Transmittance requirements and recommended use EN 171, Personal eye-protection - Infrared filters - Transmittance requirements and recommended use EN 172, Personal eye protection - Sun glare filters for industrial use EN 175, Personal protection - Equipment for eye and face protection during welding and allied processes EN 207, Personal eye-protection - Filters and eye-protectors against laser radiation (laser eye-protectors) EN 208, Personal eye-protection - Eye-protectors for adjustment work on lasers and laser systems (laser adjustment eye-protectors) EN 379, Personal eye-protection - Automatic welding filters EN 1731, Personal eye protection - Mesh eye and face protector BS 8497-1:2008 – Eyewear for protection against intense light sources used on humans and animals for cosmetic and medical applications EN 14458 for Faceshields and visors for firefighters, ambulance and emergency services EN 174 for Ski goggles for downhill skiing BS 7930-1 for Eye protectors for racket sports - Squash. Less used regulations include: EN 165 for Personal eye protection – vocabulary. EN 167 for Personal eye protection - optical test methods EN 168 for Personal eye protection - non-optical test methods EN 1836 for Sunglasses and sunglare filters for general use. EN 1938 for Goggles for motorcycle and moped riders BS 4110 for Specification for visors for vehicle users BS 7028 for Eye protection for industrial and other uses. Guidance on selection, use and maintenance BS 8497-2 for Eyewear for protection against intense light sources used on humans and animals for cosmetic and medical applications EN 12254 for Screens for laser working places - Safety requirements and testing National foreword on correct selection procedure. title: Protective Footwear
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Summary of Basic Ratings Toe Protection Antistatic Protection (A) Midsole Penetration Protection (P) – SB-P, S1-P, S3, S5 Energy Absorption (E) Water Resistant Upper (WRU) Heat Resistant Insulation against Cold (CI) Insulation against Heat (HI) Non Metallic Footwear Explore
body: Protective footwear should be tried on if possible before purchase as ill fitting footwear can cause other health issues. You should also consider other comfort factors. Breathable safety boots prevent feet from becoming hot, sweaty and uncomfortable — they're an investment your workers will thank you for. There are also ways to increase comfort without changing the style of boot, including wearing insole inserts and good-quality socks. When your workers are comfortable, there'll be better equipped to do their job to their best ability, so investing in the right, comfortable footwear can really make a difference to your business's work output. Advise workers against the idea of "breaking in" new footwear because, contrary to popular belief, if the shoe fits well, it should feel comfortable straight away. If discomfort occurs early on and insoles and socks aren't making a difference, advise employees to try a different shoe, rather than grinning and bearing it. Uncomfortable safety footwear is not something that should be put up with and it's important to show workers that their comfort isn't a secondary consideration. The Health and Safety Executive (HSE) advises that PPE must be properly looked after and stored when not in use. Before wearing, safety footwear should always be checked for faults, damage, wear and tear, and dirt. Footwear should also be cleaned regularly. If boots are smooth leather, they should be polished and treated; if you provide suede footwear, it must be brushed and treated with a water-resistant spray. Soles should be brushed and washed to remove dirt and contaminants, and the shoes should be stored in a cool, dry place away from direct sunlight. For more detailed cleaning instructions, you should refer to the maintenance guidelines provided by the manufacturer of your chosen safety footwear. The most common type of safety footwear, incorporating protective toe-caps with many other safety features including slip-resistant soles, penetration-resistant mid-soles and insulation against extremes of heat and cold The most common type of safety footwear, incorporating protective toe-caps with many other safety features including slip-resistant soles, penetration-resistant mid-soles and insulation against extremes of heat and cold These usually have protective steel toe-caps although, as with safety boots, are available ‘metal free’ – so called composite footwear – which are lighter. These usually have protective steel toe-caps although, as with safety boots, are available ‘metal free’ – so called composite footwear – which are lighter. Perhaps considered more aesthetically appealing by wearers, these look more casual. Some have steel toe caps while others are plastic – referred to as composite toe caps (as above). Perhaps considered more aesthetically appealing by wearers, these look more casual. Some have steel toe caps while others are plastic – referred to as composite toe caps (as above). These have been described as ‘a real stalwart of industrial footwear’4. A rigger boot is a particular type of pull-on safety boot; the name “rigger” comes from the fact that they were standard issue for workers on the offshore oil rigs in the North Sea, but are nowadays worn by most types of manual worker as a general purpose work boot. Concerns with this type of safety footwear have been raised, including a lack of ankle support. Banned from some sites due to support concerns These have been described as ‘a real stalwart of industrial footwear’4. A rigger boot is a particular type of pull-on safety boot; the name “rigger” comes from the fact that they were standard issue for workers on the offshore oil rigs in the North Sea, but are nowadays worn by most types of manual worker as a general purpose work boot. Concerns with this type of safety footwear have been raised, including a lack of ankle support. Banned from some sites due to support concerns Usually made of rubber and used for working in wet conditions, these are also useful in jobs where the footwear needs to be washed and disinfected for hygiene reasons, eg in the food and the chemical industries. Usually made of rubber and used for working in wet conditions, these are also useful in jobs where the footwear needs to be washed and disinfected for hygiene reasons, eg in the food and the chemical industries. Table 5: Types of Footwear Available Safety basic, 200 joules Toe Protection, Oil Resistant outer sole. (Minimum Requirement) Safety basic, 200 joules Toe Protection, Oil Resistant outer sole. (Minimum Requirement) As SB plus Mid-Sole for penetration resistance. As SB plus Mid-Sole for penetration resistance. As SB plus Anti-Static properties and fully enclosed Energy Absorbing heel area. As SB plus Anti-Static properties and fully enclosed Energy Absorbing heel area. As S1 plus Mid-Sole for penetration resistance. As S1 plus Mid-Sole for penetration resistance. As S1 plus resistance to Water Penetration and absorption. As S1 plus resistance to Water Penetration and absorption. As S2 plus Mid-Sole for penetration resistance and cleated outsole As S2 plus Mid-Sole for penetration resistance and cleated outsole 200 joules Toe Protection. All rubber or polymer construction (waterproof). Anti-Static properties, Energy Absorbing heel area. 200 joules Toe Protection. All rubber or polymer construction (waterproof). Anti-Static properties, Energy Absorbing heel area. As S4 plus Mid-Sole for penetration resistance and cleated outsole. As S4 plus Mid-Sole for penetration resistance and cleated outsole. Source: Ultimate Industrial Limited Options for specific protection are categorised as: A – Antistatic: Comes as standard with level S1, S2 & S3 E – Energy absorbing heel: Comes as standard with level S1, S2 & S3 P – Puncture Resistant: Comes as standard with level SB-P, S1-P & S3 HRO – Heat Resistant Outsole: Comes as standard with level SB, S1, S2 & S3 The minimum requirements to meet each of the safety ratings and the most common are as follows. Your toes are a very vulnerable part of your body, especially in a workplace. Toe protection must withstand a 200 joule impact. Joule is the unit of energy and this standard is purposefully specific. Something heavy falling from a low height could have a lot less energy than something lighter from a much higher height. As well as impacts, the toe area must withstand a resting mass of well over 1000kg.Most people have heard of steel toe cap boots but the protection doesn’t have to be steel. In fact there are advantages to alternatives. Non-metallic protection can be equally as strong but lighter. Clothing, seating materials, and climate factors can cause a build up a static charge of electricity in the body. Some materials in footwear can over insulate the body causing the charge to be held. When you then touch something the charge can rush from your body quickly causing a spark and a small uncomfortable shock. Antistatic footwear will significantly reduce this effect but does not offer full protection for exposure to electronics and explosives work. You will need Electro-Static Protection (ESD) for this. Sharp objects where we walk and stand are a significant risk not only in the workplace but also outdoors and at home. Midsole protection will guard against nails and other objects. To meet this standard the footwear must be able to resist a penetration force of 1100N. Midsole protection is provided in one of these methods: a stainless steel insert in the sole, aluminium insert in the sole, or by Kevlar insole. The Aluminium and Kevlar solutions are the most flexible and lightest and cover the greatest area of the foot. Kevlar insoles also offer much higher thermal insulation. Energy Absorption in the heel region. Water resistant upper; not used on all rubber or polymeric footwear. Heat resistant outsole: to resist 300°C for 60 seconds. Insulation against cold: tested for 30 minutes at 1 -20°C. Insulation against heat: tested for 30 minutes at 150°C. Electrostatic Discharge (ESD) ESD footwear is designed for the electronics and explosives industry and has to meet ESD CEI EN 61340-5-1 standard. Safety footwear containing no metal parts is ideal for workplaces with metal detectors like airports, eliminating the hassle of removing them Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Laboratory Coats
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Selection of Lab coats: Lab Coat Use – DO’s and DON’Ts Emergency spill or splash: Lab Coat Selection Chart Explore
body: This document provides guidance for selection, use and care of lab coats. Failure/refusal to wear required PPE by employees is a basis for disciplinary action. A student not wearing required PPE in a laboratory/technical area may not participate in lab activities until such PPE is worn. Lab coats are an important part of personal protective equipment that serve to: Provide protection of skin and personal clothing from incidental contact Prevent the spread of contamination outside the lab (provided they are not worn outside the lab) Provide a removable barrier in the event of an incident involving a spill or splash of hazardous substances Individual departments, schools or faculties must determine what type of lab coat is necessary. Under no circumstances should the employee work without the necessary lab coat. Students in teaching lab settings are required to provide a lab coat at their own cost. Lab coats are required in all undergraduate labs where chemical, biological, or radiological materials are used. All laboratory workers are responsible for following the requirements for wearing and properly maintaining lab coats as outlined in this policy guideline and in laboratory-specific safety training. Lab coats are available in a variety of materials and provide varying degrees of protection. Examples include: splash resistant coats, static free coats, chemical resistant coats and flame resistant coats. When selecting a lab coat you must ensure that the coat you are selecting provides correct the type of protection that is appropriate for your needs as highlighted by a COSHH or risk assessment. After conducting the COSHH assessment you should select the appropriate lab coat. One coat may not provide protection against all the hazards present in the laboratory. A chart at the end of this document provides information about lab coats, materials, and protection offered. Addditional features which should be included in the laboratory coate are: Length - At least knee length or longer is recommended for most effective coverage. Wristband - It is recommended that a lab coat with a fitted wristband/cuff be used to reduce the potential for splashes up the arm and fire hazards. Top button - It is best to use a lab coat that provides for a high top button at the neck to provide most effective protection. Fire resistant - Use only those constructed of a flame resistant material. Lab coats made of polyester-cotton blends (no less than 35% cotton) are acceptable in labs where no open flames are present. Lab coats must be made of 100% cotton or flame resistant material in labs where open flames are used (such as alcohol burners). Labs that store large quantities (>10gallons) of flammable liquids outside a flammable storage cabinet must use lab coats made of 100% Cotton treated with flame retardant material. Lab coats of flame resistant (FR) material are required in labs where pyrophoric materials are handled. Persons working with pyrophoric liquids are also required to wear 100% cotton clothing underneath the FR lab coat on days that they handle these materials in the lab. DO wear a lab coat when a COSHH or risk assessment of the laboratory determines hazards to the body are present or likely to be present. A good rule of thumb is to wear a lab coat at all times when working in a lab. DO wear a lab coat of appropriate size. DO chose a lab coat based on the types of work hazards. Lab coats can be disposable or reusable. Consider a disposable lab coat when using high hazard materials. Lab coats made of cotton fabric are recommended for general lab use. Flame resistant fabric (e.g., Nomex) or flame resistant cotton fabrics are a must if there is a significant fire potential. DO wear lab coats that cover the knees and have full-length sleeves, preferably tight sleeves than the loose one. DO keep lab coats completely “buttoned” up. Snap closures are preferred over buttons or zippers to keep the body covered and allow quick removal in an emergency. DO immediately remove a lab coat if on fire or there is obvious hazardous contamination. DO consider the addition of a rubber apron when there is a significant chance of exposure to corrosive materials. DON’T wear disposable lab coats more than eight hours or if it has become contaminated. DON’T wear lab coats made of synthetic fabrics if there is a potential for fire. Synthetic fabrics burn, melt, shrink and stick to the skin. DON’T wear lab coats unbuttoned. DON’T roll up the sleeves on lab coats for comfort or ventilation. DON’T wear lab coats outside the lab. Lab coats and other protective wear used in a lab should be kept in the work area to minimize the possibility of spreading chemicals to public places including eating or office areas. Do not use lab coats outside the room even when transporting chemicals, radioactive materials or biological hazards. The recommended method of transporting hazardous material within lab buildings is to utilize secondary containment. Secondary containment must be plastic, securely sealed, Tupperware-type containers. This method allows your hands to be free from exposure to any hazardous material, thus eliminating your need to wear gloves or lab coat. In the event of a significant spill of a hazardous material on the lab coat, it should be immediately removed. If skin or personal clothing is impacted, it will be necessary to proceed to an emergency shower. Any contaminated clothing should also be removed. Guidance should be sought from OEHS about whether the coat and clothing should be cleaned or discarded as hazardous waste. There is little or no information provided by manufacturers or distributors about the capability of a lab coat for a combination of hazards. A coat that is “flame resistant”, such as treated cotton, may not be chemical resistant or acid resistant. The term “flame resistant” refers to the characteristic of a fabric that causes it not to burn in air. There are limited criteria for testing lab coat materials with respect to typical lab use scenarios, and some of the information is anecdotal. Polyester/Cotton Blend 80% Polyester / 20% Cotton, 65/35, and 40/60 are common blends. Polyester/Cotton Blend Liquid Resistance Splash resistant. No specific chemical resistance. Anecdotal evidence suggests polyester blends provide better protection against corrosive material than cotton. Flame Resistance No. Polyester blends burn more readily than 100% cotton or flame-resistant materials. Comfort Lightweight and breathable. More cotton in the blend results in better breathability. Splash resistant. No specific chemical resistance. Anecdotal evidence suggests polyester blends provide better protection against corrosive material No. Polyester blends burn more readily than 100% cotton or flame-resistant materials. title: LGPS Timeline: Adding to your Pension - APCs
headings: Human Resources LGPS: Additional Pension Contributions (APCs) Explore
body: Extra contributions to your LGPS pension as regular or one-off payments; arrange via payroll or by direct payments to the scheme Read through the guidance on the Hants LGPS website. You may not do this while in the 50-50 scheme but can easily switch back. You can make regular monthly deductions (from 1 year to as many years as you wish up until your normal retirement age), or by a one-off payment. Contribute via monthly payroll, or direct to LGPS. You’ll get tax relief as normal through payroll, or contact HMRC for refund if paying directly. The purchase cost will depend on personal circumstances (age, when you start paying etc.) and the number of months you wish to pay the contributions over. The online calculator will work out the cost to you. When using the calculator, you will be asked for the ‘Percentage your employer has agreed to pay (%)’ – put ‘0%’ for this as while we pay a large contribution towards your normal pension we can't support voluntary additional payments. Be aware that payments can be increased by LGPS each year if they are revalued. There’s a maximum increase to your annual pension value you can have each year without paying an additional tax charge, but this is unlikely to affect most members who are not very high earners or those who already have very valuable pension(s), or those paying very high extra pension amounts over the year (typically £1,000 per month or more). You can find out more on our Tax & Pensions pages and if you think this may be of relevance to you we recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice. Once you complete the calculator, click ‘Apply for extra pension’ and fill in your details to produce an application form to email direct to LGPS. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: LGPS: Additional Voluntary Contributions
headings: Human Resources LGPS: Additional Voluntary Contributions (AVCs) Explore
body: Invest in a separate Prudential pension associated with LGPS An AVC gives you a separate pension with increased flexibilities. It works differently to LGPS – you can contribute as much as you want and choose (either through payroll or how it is invested, and its value is dependent on how well those investments perform in the market. Be aware that because of this, its value could potentially go down as well as up. LGPS uses Prudential to manage these AVCs. You can agree a monthly amount, or select a percentage to contribute, and you may vary what you pay to suit your circumstances. You can access this pension when you retire, and can use the funds built up to do any combination of: topping up your LGPS, taking a lump sum (tax free to HMRC limits – normally 25% of total pensions value), and buying an annuity (regular pension payments). There’s a maximum amount you can save in your pensions each year without paying an additional tax charge, but this is unlikely to affect most members who are not very high earners or those who already have very valuable pension(s), or those paying very high extra pension amounts over the year (typically £1,000 per month or more). You can find out more on our Tax & Pensions pages and if you think this may be of relevance to you we recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice. First read through the AVC Guide, LGPS webpage and Prudential site for LGPS to ensure it’s the right choice for your circumstances. Make sure you have read and chosen an ‘investment fund’ at this point as you’ll be asked to select one during your application. At the bottom of the Prudential page you can click ‘Apply for an AVC plan’. For ‘Employment Details’ select ‘Hampshire Pension Fund’ then type ‘University of Portsmouth’ and complete the rest of your details. At ‘Investment Options’, choose the fund(s) you wish to invest in. Once you submit, if you’re making payroll contributions, Prudential will send over the details to us and you’ll start seeing deductions in your pay in the next available pay period. To stop or change the value of your AVCs contributions, contact Prudential directly at avc.admin@prudential.co.uk and they will instruct our payroll to make the change. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth is not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: LGPS: Salary Sacrifice AVCs
headings: Human Resources Explore
body: LGPS: Salary Sacrifice Shared Cost Additional Voluntary Contributions (AVCs) Invest in a separate Prudential pension associated with LGPS and benefit from both tax and National Insurance savings through 'My Money Matters' (previously known as 'AVC Wise'). Our ‘Salary Sacrifice’ AVC scheme works to give you the same Prudential pension as ‘normal’ AVCs, but with the extra benefit of saving the cost of National Insurance as well as tax on all contributions made. To do this, we have partnered with 'My Money Matters' who administer these contributions for you as a ‘salary sacrifice’ arrangement. Unlike most other salary sacrifice arrangements, our LGPS Salary Sacrifice AVC does this without reducing the pension you and we pay on your normal (unreduced) salary so you'll still get the full benefits of your full pensionable pay. AVCs give you a separate pension with increased flexibilities. They work differently to LGPS – you can contribute as much as you want and choose how it is invested, and its value is dependent on how well those investments perform in the market. Be aware that because of this, its value could potentially go down as well as up. LGPS uses Prudential to manage these AVCs. You can access this pension when you retire, and can use the funds built up to do any combination of: topping up your LGPS, taking a lump sum (tax free to HMRC limits – normally 25% of total pensions value), and buying an annuity (regular pension payments). If you already have a standard AVC you may wish to consider switching to Salary Sacrifice to benefit from extra NI savings. There’s a maximum amount you can save in your pensions each year without paying an additional tax charge, but this is unlikely to affect most members who are not very high earners or those who already have very valuable pension(s), or those paying very high extra pension amounts over the year (typically £1,000 per month or more). You can find out more on our Tax & Pensions pages and if you think this may be of relevance to you we recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice. Go to https://my-money-matters.co.uk/ and ‘sign in’. If you haven’t already registered, do so by selecting ‘University of Portsmouth’ as the organisation, use your UoP email address, and fill in the rest of your personal details. The My Money Matters site offers many ways to learn more about AVCs and what may work for you. There are explanatory videos and webinars you can sign up for on their knowledge hub (covering AVCs and general information about LGPS), and calculators to predict investments and savings. When applying, you will be guided through any limits, costs, and estimated values. When you are at the ‘Investment Choices’ step, you will be provided with the Prudential Fund Investment guide – this contains some technical information and can appear quite complicated but you should read through to get the best understanding of how AVCs work. This is also the point many people give up on applying for what could be a really beneficial arrangement! The most important thing to do here is to pick which fund(s) you wish to invest in according to your situation, beliefs and appetite for risk, but there is also a simpler ‘default’ option designed to suit on average the majority of members at all stages of membership by selecting the ‘Lifestyle Option’. It is possible to switch choices later if you change your mind and if you pick this one you should definitely plan to, so you can check if there's something even better tailored to your own circumstances. You will receive a confirmation email from AVC Wise first, and this will be followed within a few weeks with an email from Prudential. The Pru email will come from avc.admin@prudential.co.uk and will confirm your scheme details and invite you to register. Once registered, your Pru account is how you’ll keep your personal details up to date, see your pension grow over time and change any investment options. However, if you wish to increase or decrease your monthly payments, then you must do this through your My Money Matters account instead as they administer the Salary Sacrifice deductions. Your contributions will commence in the next available pay period and you will be able to keep track of all contributions on your payslip. Bear in mind this whole process can take over a month, so you can't guarantee payments will start in the month after applying. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: MyPensionPlus Auto Enrolment
headings: Human Resources MyPensionPlus Auto Enrolment Explore
body: You have been enrolled in the MyPensionPlus (MPP) scheme (using Aviva Workplace Pension), as you have been assessed as meeting the government’s criteria for ‘automatic enrolment’. Aviva will send you a welcome letter and then a new joiner 'welcome pack' by post to your registered address, normally within a few days after you are paid following your enrolment. This will invite you to register online and also provide details on how to opt out, if you choose to (see below). Aviva Workplace Pension is a ‘Defined Contribution’ or ‘Money Purchase’ employer pension. The contributions you and the University make are paid to a personal ‘pension pot’, and are invested according to your wishes. Members of MyPensionPlus are also provided ‘Death in Service’ Life Insurance and a £400 per year (pro-rated for part time employment) additional employer contribution which may be swapped with a range of other free Benefit Options each year. You may find out more information about what options you have and the benefits of being in this scheme on our intranet pages, and in the Aviva Workplace Pension Scheme Guide. The default rate of contributions for members is 6%, with 12% paid by the University. Depending on how much you earn, you may be enrolled to pay contributions by either Salary Sacrifice or Basic Deductions. You can confirm which method you were eligible for at enrolment by looking at your payslip. If you are eligible for Salary Sacrifice but would prefer to pay by Basic Deductions for any reason, you may elect to switch contribution methods. Further information on rates, options to pay higher or lower contributions and how you benefit from tax relief under Salary Sacrifice or Basic Deductions may be found on our intranet pages. We would encourage you to think carefully before deciding to opt out of a pension and ensure that it is the right decision for you. By opting out, you will also lose access to your Death in Service and Flexible Benefit Option. If you do still wish to opt-out there is more information found here. Opting out within 30 days of being registered (contributions are refunded) Your Welcome Pack from Aviva will contain instructions on how to opt out. It will be posted to you within a few days of being registered so you will need to decide quickly if you wish to receive a refund. Opting out after 30 days of being registered (contributions are stopped) You can request to stop further payments from as soon as the start of following month by submitting a People Hub pay enquiry request. Your pension will remain invested, or you may be able to transfer your pension to an alternative scheme. You can request re-enrolment by submitting a People Hub pay enquiry request, and you’ll be re-enrolled from the start of the following month. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth and its subsidiaries are not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth and its subsidiaries are not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: LGPS Auto Enrolment
headings: Human Resources LGPS Auto Enrolment Explore
body: You have been enrolled in the Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS), administered by Hampshire Pension Services, as you have been assessed as meeting the government’s criteria for ‘automatic enrolment’. LGPS is a ‘Defined Benefit’ employer pension scheme and you may find out more information about what this means and the many benefits of being in this scheme on our scheme pages and the Hampshire Pension Services LGPS website. Details on contribution rates (how much you pay) and tax relief may be found here. As a new joiner, you will be sent a welcome pack by email (normally within a month or so of paying your first contributions) to invite you to register for the LGPS Member Portal where you can view and manage your pension online. Opting Out or Reducing your Contributions We would encourage you to think carefully before deciding to opt out of a pension and ensure that it is the right decision for you. You may alternatively wish to consider the 50/50 scheme, where you pay only 50% of contributions in return for accruing 50% of the benefits. If you do still wish to opt-out there is more information found here, where you can follow instructions to complete the Hampshire – LGPS opt out form available from the Hampshire Pension Services website then email this to pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk. Opting out within 3 months of joining the scheme You will be treated as never having been a member. University of Portsmouth payroll will refund any contributions that you have paid, less normal tax and National Insurance deductions. Opting out after 3 months of joining the scheme Your membership will end from the 1st day of the month after you submit the form. After this date, if you have opted out with more than 3 months and less than 2 years’ membership, you may seek a refund or transfer (in most circumstances) by contacting Hampshire Pension Services at pensions@hants.gov.uk or 01962 845588. You may need your National Insurance number to help identify yourself. What if I opt out but want to opt back in later? You can request re-enrolment by emailing pensionenquiries@port.ac.uk and you’ll be re-enrolled from the start of the next month. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth and its subsidiaries are not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: TPS Auto Enrolment
headings: Human Resources Explore
body: Teachers' Pension Auto Enrolment You have been enrolled in the Teachers’ Pensions (TPS), as you have been assessed as meeting the government’s criteria for ‘automatic enrolment’. TPS is a ‘Defined Benefit’ employer pension scheme and you may find out more information about what this means and the many benefits of being in this scheme on the Teachers' Pension website. Details on contribution rates (how much you pay) and tax relief may be found here. As a new joiner, you should sign up for the ‘My Pension Online’ member portal, where you can update personal details, nominate beneficiaries and view and manage your pension online. We would encourage you to think carefully before deciding to opt out of a pension and ensure that it is the right decision for you. If you do still wish to opt out there is more information found here. Opting out within 3 months of joining the scheme University of Portsmouth payroll will refund any contributions that you have paid, less normal tax and National Insurance deductions. Opting out after 3 months of joining the scheme Your membership will end from the 1st day of the month after you submit the request. After this date, if you have opted out with more than 3 months and less than 2 years’ membership, you may seek a refund or transfer (in most circumstances) by contacting Teachers’ Pension directly using the ‘My Pension Online’ portal or on 0345 6066166. You may need your Teachers’ Pension reference and National Insurance number to help identify yourself. What if I opt out but want to opt back in later? You can request re-enrolment at any time via ‘My Pension Online’, and you may re-join the scheme from the start of the following month. title: NEST Auto Enrolment
headings: Human Resources Nest (except UASL Nest) Auto Enrolment Explore
body: You have been enrolled in the Nest Pension Scheme, as you have been assessed as meeting the government’s criteria for ‘automatic enrolment’. Nest is a ‘Defined Contribution’ or ‘Money Purchase’ workplace pension. The contributions you and the University make are paid to your personal ‘pension pot’, and are invested according to your wishes. You may find out more information about what options you have and the benefits of being in this scheme on the scheme website. Nest will send you a new joiner welcome pack by post, normally within a few days after you first get paid. This will invite you to register online and also provide details on how to opt out, if you choose to (see below for more information on opting out). The default rate of contributions for members is 4%, with a 3.2% deduction from your monthly basic salary and an extra 0.8% added to your pension pot as tax relief. If you wish, you may increase the amount you pay. The employer contribution is 6%. To find out more about contributions and tax relief, please refer to the scheme guide. We would encourage you to think carefully before deciding to opt out of a pension and ensure that it is the right decision for you. There is an initial Opt-Out period, which lasts one month starting three working days after you are enrolment, during which time you may elect to opt-out. You must apply to NEST directly. Your Welcome Pack from Nest will confirm the window you have to opt-out of the scheme and provide instructions on how. Nest will then close your account and instruct us to refund any contributions you paid in the next available payroll. Stopping Contributions / Leaving the Scheme If you are outside of the initial Opting Out period, you may still request to stop contributing to your pension at any time. Nest refers to this as a ‘break’ or ‘pause,’ but you’ll stop all contributions unless you join again later. You won’t receive a refund but all contributions will stay invested according to your wishes until you retire. You can request re-enrolment at any time by submitting a People Hub pay enquiry request, and you’ll be re-enrolled from the start of the following month. DISCLAIMERThe University of Portsmouth and its subsidiaries are not your pension provider and many queries you have may be best directed to the schemes themselves, where they have dedicated member helplines to assist you. These pages are for general informational purposes only and nothing contained within may be seen as financial advice. Where you have a decision to make regarding your pension, we would always recommend you pursue Independent Financial Advice, with a qualified advisor who is familiar with pensions and your particular scheme. title: Employee Assistance Programme (EAP)
headings: Human Resources Employee Assistance Programme (EAP) Accessing the Helpline Is it confidential? Explore
body: Supporting the mental health and wellbeing of all our colleagues is incredibly important to us, and our new Employee Assistance Programme will help us do just that. Whether you’re facing problems at work or at home, you can access impartial, independent and entirely confidential advice from qualified counsellors for any issues you may be facing, such as Anxiety; Bereavement; Stress; Depression; Workplace Issues; Trauma; Relationships; Finances and debt; and Family Difficulties. Confidential 24/7 Helpline: The telephone helpline is available 24/7, 365 days a year and is completely confidential and to anyone needing mental health support. To access the helpline, simply call 0800 023 9324 from any standard UK landline or mobile phone Digital Resources and Self-Help:A wide offering of resources is also available and includes downloadable self-help workbooks, debt advice and more. Visit the EAP Platform to find out more: www.port.ac.uk/myeap The University does not know who uses the service unless the individual personally chooses to tell someone about their contact with Vivup. Vivup do provide statistics to the University to show how many employees use the service and the broad types of issues raised, for example; ‘relationship breakdown at home’ or ‘bullying and harassment in the workplace. No information is ever passed on which could potentially identify an individual. title: Consultancy
headings: Department for Curriculum and Quality Enhancement Academic Consultancy Explore DCQE Sections
body: To support you in enhancing your practice and the student learning experience, we offer a free learning and teaching consultancy service for all staff in the University. We work with individuals, small and large groups or whole departments. We work with you as 'critical friends' providing in-depth development that meets both your teaching needs and the learning needs of your students. We work with you on your agenda, at a time, place, and in a manner that suits you. Consultancy can take many forms, from individual advice to larger 'events', all tailored to your requirements. Our consultancy service can help you in a number of ways, including: Making student feedback more effective Enhancing assessment practices Course design, curriculum planning, unit design / redesign Evaluation of courses / units / student focus groups Responding to NSS data and forward planning Effective learning and teaching using technology Working with external partners Working with international students Peer development and review Making student feedback more effective Enhancing assessment practices Course design, curriculum planning, unit design / redesign Evaluation of courses / units / student focus groups Responding to NSS data and forward planning Effective learning and teaching using technology Working with external partners Working with international students Peer development and review We can help with many other topics: in short, if you have a learning and teaching issue, we can help. To discuss your requirements, please contact acdev@port.ac.uk. Contact and departments

title: Erasmus
headings: Human Resources Erasmus+ Staff Opportunities What is Erasmus+ staff mobility? Eligibility & Criteria Availability of staff training events Recognition of staff mobility Funding How to apply Contact Testimonials Explore
body: Academic and Professional Services staff can apply for funding to undertake overseas activities within EU/EEA and Candidate countries (Turkey and FYROM) via the Erasmus+ programme. The programme is centrally administered by the Exchanges and Study Abroad (ESA) team in UoP Global. The University of Portsmouth is an active participant in the programme and offers the chance to: Provide a personal and professional development opportunity, especially for colleagues new to the HE sector Develop your career and skills in your field Experience different learning practices and teaching methods Take part in an exchange of expertise and experience Develop your social and intercultural experience Help to reinforce relationships between institutions Improve your foreign language skills Participating in such activites may bring new opportunities for collaboration in teaching and research. Any knowledge gained by such activities can also be shared with colleagues and can assist towards encouraging students to undertake an overseas opportunity. Erasmus+ is a European Commission funded programme that supports staff to undertake teaching and training activities. Academic (Teaching/Research staff) - opportunity offers Teaching mobility: Must be an academic (teaching) member of staff Must have an Erasmus+ agreement in place Participate in teaching activities at a partner institution in the same subject area Teach at any academic level for a minimum of 8 hours for a working week Minimum duration of two days Should assist in the modernisation and international agenda All staff - opportunity offers Training mobility: Both academic and professional services staff can participate Undertake a programme that involves job shadowing, seminar, workshops or similar It should be ideally related to your area of work, unless otherwise specified by manager Minimum duration of two days Colleagues can either arrange independent training directly with a host HEI or can search for a relevant week through the IMOTION platform. Erasmus+ staff mobility compliments the University of Portsmouth Global Engagement strategy and the People Strategy. UoP staff can reflect this in their yearly Professional Development Review. Staff mobility will also be noted on their development record on Employee Self Service Records. Funding is available via the EC funding Erasmus+ programme. Grant rates are determined nationally by the UK National Agency for Erasmus+, the British Council and Ecorys. Grant rates vary by country and duration, and provides a grant for both travel and subsistence. Grant rates change per year. But indicatively, subsistence average grant rates from 140 euros to 180 euros per day and an average travel grant is approximately 275 euros. Declare your interest by 21 January 2019 to check availability for funding for the Erasmus+ year 2018/19. Places are available on a first-come, first-served basis. If funds are limited, priority will be give to: Staff who have not undertaken an Erasmus+ staff mobility before Staff undertaking activities where UoP students are studying Staff from Academic Schools where Erasmus+ funding has not been provided before Please seek written confirmation from the ESA Team for the availability of funds prior to applying. If no written confirmation has been provided the mobility funding coverage is at the individual's own risk. Chrissy Hadjipanteli, Exchanges and Study Abroad Manager, for further information and guidance. Erasmus.staff@port.ac.uk James Brand, Support Staff, Graduate School: "The Erasmus+ staff training week I attended was a fantastic opportunity to visit a European University which specialises in distance education. I visited the Fernuniversitat in Hagen, Germany, where I attended workshops in distance learning and received a tour of the Universities resources. I was able to network with colleagues from the institution and also with attendees from across Europe. The training week provided an opportunity to gain knowledge of HEI practices from other colleagues, discuss and share ideas with fellow participants and also take part in the cultural exchange of visiting another country. I would highly recommend the Erasmus+ staff training weeks to staff. In terms of personal and career development it has been one of the most useful and rewarding activities I have participated in, I have made new networks and now have an understanding of HEI in a global market." Adam Francombe, Support Staff, Information Services: "I am very thankful to have had the opportunity to attend an Erasmus+ staff training week with the help and support of the Exchanges and Study Abroad team in UoP Global. The group of attendees were a mixture of IT and International Office staff from many different European countries and it was a pleasure to meet with them all. I was enlightened with a cultural tour of Brno, Czech Republic, along with a week-long IT Training programme on behalf of Masaryk University. I found the experience of identifying the IT challenges we shared in common to be very helpful and a collaborative effort was made by all to help each other on their particular topics. It was also great to explore a new city with some brilliant people, I couldn't recommend it enough!" title: Leader and Manager as Coach
headings: Human Resources Leader and Manager as Coach The Benefits to Coaching Explore
body: The Leader and Manager as Coach programme provides leaders and managers with additional skills to conduct PDRs in an effective and engaging way, work towards a coaching style of management and be empowered to lead staff to achieve institutional goals and support staff in dealing with change. The programme consists of three half-day workshops over a period of 3 months, including pre, post and interim exercises between sessions to embed the learning and provide an opportunity to use the tools learnt and put the skills into practice. A variety of further support and resources will be available including online resources and support from colleagues on the programme using an action learning style format. Cohorts will be no more than 16 delegates per programme to encourage interaction, sharing and a valuable learning experience. In order to be eligible for the Leader & Manager as Coach programme, you must currently lead and/or manage people, conduct PDRs and have the full support of your Line Manager. There are multiple benefits to cultivating the appropriate coaching skills and leadership / management styles, including: building on PDRs and ensuring effective reviews which are fully aligned with departmental plans and lead staff to achieve institutional goals improving performance and commitment improving job satisfaction and morale at all levels creating a shared language of leadership encouraging idea generation working towards a coaching style of management better communications across departments To ensure accessibility, the programme will run concurrently throughout the year, taking into consideration peak periods for departments. For a full list of programme dates and to book a place, please visit the programme of events. For further details on the aims and objectives for the programme and who should attend, please visit your questions answered or to self nominate for the programme, please contact learninganddevelopment@port.ac.uk confirming your eligibility for the programme. The programme is supported by a reading list where you can find associated books and online resources within the field of coaching. title: Module 5: Recruitment and Selection - Interview Practice
headings: Human Resources Recruitment and Selection Training Module 5: Recruitment and Selection: Interview Practice Target Audience Outline The aims of the workshops are to: Learning Outcomes: Format Presenter(s) Further Resources Explore
body: The Recruitment and Selection training will now be delivered in a modular style, allowing staff involved in the interview process to access the required modules in a timely manner. 'Trained Interviewer’ status is achieved once the pre programme elearning packages and modules 1, 3, 5 and 6 have been completed. The full programme and module outlines, including the target audience and aims of the module can be found here. Trained Interviewers and all staff who are new to the interview process and will be on a panel This workshop will focus on the preparation and conducting of interviews. - Give staff the opportunity to prepare for interviews - Practice interviewing skills in a safe environment - Explore listening and questioning skills and question types - Ensure the interviewing process is fair and effective - Provide guidance on giving feedback to successful and unsuccessful candidates By the end of this module participants will be confident in conducting fair and effective interviews and giving feedback in line with the University’s Recruitment and Selection process. title: Module 6: Recruitment and Selection:Online Recruitment
headings: Human Resources Recruitment and Selection Training Module 6: Recruitment and Selection: On-line Recruitment Presenter(s) Explore
body: The Recruitment and Selection training will now be delivered in a modular style, allowing staff involved in the interview process to access the required modules in a timely manner. 'Trained Interviewer’ status is achieved once the pre programme elearning packages and modules 1, 3, 5 and 6 have been completed. The full programme and module outlines, including the target audience and aims of the module can be found here. Any staff involved in the recruitment process, either as a Trained Interviewer, a Panel member or administrative support. The aim of the module is to guide users through the on-line recruitment system, explaining how it works and their responsibilities. The aims of the module are: To show delegates how to log in to the recruitment system To explain the end-to-end recruitment process (advert to offer) To confirm responsibilities and access for each role (Trained Interviewer, panel member, Administrative support, Candidate, HR) To highlight any changes in process. Confidence to log in and use the system Understanding of how the on-line recruitment system works. Pre-event Preparation In preparation for the attending the training you may wish to download the user guides from the HR website and bring along to the session to make notes against. 2 hour module where candidates will be provided with an overview of the system and be able to ask questions about how it works and processes they will need to use. title: Module 3: Recruitment and Selection: Shortlisting and Selection Process
headings: Human Resources Recruitment and Selection Training Module 3: Recruitment and Selection: Shortlisting and Selection Process Target Audience Outline The aims of the workshops are to: Learning Outcomes Format Presenter(s) Explore
body: The Recruitment and Selection training will now be delivered in a modular style, allowing staff involved in the interview process to access the required modules in a timely manner. 'Trained Interviewer’ status is achieved once the pre programme elearning packages and modules 1, 3, 5 and 6 have been completed. The full programme and module outlines, including the target audience and aims of the module can be found here. All staff involved in the shortlisting process (Trained Interviewers, Convenors and Panel Members) The shortlisting and selection module allows attendees to review a set of applications, work as a group to identify who they would/would not shortlist, understand how to update the online recruitment system and produce shortlisting feedback. They will also review information to be provided to the successful/unsuccessful candidates and the offer of employment stage. Provide staff with an understanding of the shortlisting process including updates to the online recruitment system Give staff the opportunity to undertake a shortlisting exercise involving discussion with colleagues and making valid shortlisting decisions. Allow attendees the opportunity to consider and produce a set of shortlisting decisions for each of the candidates. By the end of this module participants will: Understand the criteria for shortlisting Be comfortable undertaking shortlisting Be aware of the information that needs to be recorded in the online recruitment system. title: Module 1: Recruitment and Selection: United Kingdom Visas and Immigration
headings: Human Resources Recruitment and Selection Training Module 1: Recruitment and Selection: United Kingdom Visas and Immigration Target Audience Outline The aims of the module are to: Learning Outcomes Format Presenter Pre-event Preparation Explore
body: The Recruitment and Selection training will now be delivered in a modular style, allowing staff involved in the interview process to access the required modules in a timely manner. 'Trained Interviewer’ status is achieved once the pre programme elearning packages and modules 1, 3, 5 and 6 have been completed. The full programme and module outlines, including the target audience and aims of the module can be found here. Trained Interviewers, Chairs and Convenors This module is aimed mainly at Faculties that recruit applicants from non-UK/EEA countries and who require a visa to live and work in the UK. It includes the legislative and compliance requirements under the current Immigration Act (consolidated in 2015) enforced by UKVI. To clarify compliance when recruiting non-UK/EEA nationals Identify the responsibilities of those involved in the recruitment process Identify and clarify the records to be kept to ensure compliance By the end of this module participants will: Have a clear understanding of their responsibilities when recruiting non-UK/EEA nationals Have an up to date knowledge of UKVI compliance during recruitment Will understand what records need to be kept and for how long Online training module - approx 40 minutes to complete including a quick quiz. Please read the pre-event reading before undertaking the online training. 1.5 hour workshop including a quick quiz and hints and tips on best practice. HR Compliance Adviser, HR Service Centre Click to download a copy of Applying for a Visa and bring along to the session. Undertaking the online training is the quickest and most convenient way to complete this module. To access the online training please click here. However, if you would prefer to attend a face to face training session please see the Learning and Development Events calendar for a full list of scheduled dates and email learninganddevelopment@port.ac.uk to reserve a place.
