title: Leadership Foundation: Aurora Programme
headings: Human Resources Advance HE: Aurora Programme Leadership Development opportunity for women (and those who identify as women) Self-directed learning (online) Next steps Explore
body: The University has committed to the Advance HE Aurora Leadership programme: 'Since its launch in 2013 more than 8,300 women from nearly 200 different institutions across the UK and Ireland have participated in Aurora. Reflecting the challenges, we all currently face, Aurora has been re-designed as a fully virtual experience. Therefore, all Aurora activity will now be delivered online. Led by a team of four leadership experts, participants will explore four key areas associated with leadership success: Identity, Impact and Voice; Power and Politics; Core Leadership Skills; Adaptive Leadership Skills. Each of the four delivery days are held online, with delegates usually being drawn from a wide range of institutions in the same region. These days combine prominent keynote speakers, open space technologies, outstanding networking opportunities, energy and dynamism with the development workshops which include interactivity, small group work, individual reflection, sharing of experiences and learning from role models. The development days are intended to be introductory events which will provide inspirational stories, guidance for growth of skills and knowledge, opportunities to share solutions to common problems and signposting for further learning' (Advance HE 2021). Aurora is a development initiative for those who identify as women who are at senior lecturer level and equivalent in Professional Services and below, ambitious for a career in the sector and interested in exploring leadership and management as one option for progression. Aurora consists of six interlinked days – four development days and two action-learning sets. Throughout Aurora, four streams of self-directed learning that echo the subjects of the development days will be available online for participants to use and follow throughout the programme. The self-directed learning will include reading, reflection, activities and challenges and be provided via online links to specially commissioned materials, internet articles, websites and via an Aurora community mail list. Further information on the programme is available from the Advance HE webpages. Introductory Webinar including Guest Speaker Identity, Impact and Voice: By investigating the nature of leadership and what it means to them, participants will learn about the importance of visibility and ‘voice’, & how this can positively impact their working lives. Issues around values, authenticity & style will be addressed. Participants will begin to practice leadership and how they can exert leadership, true to their own style & identity. Core Leadership Skills: Introduces the key skills needed by all leaders and managers and considers whether there are any ‘gendered’ ways of leading. Participants’ underlying values and principles will be discussed, along with powerful communication styles, influencing, and the necessity of having a clear vision and appropriate strategy. Action Learning Set 1: The middle and final days of the programme are set aside for full-day action learning sets. Participants of each set will utilise the sessions to discuss their personal work-related challenges and seek support through the action learning process, to identify solutions and next steps. Politics and Influence: The cultures and politics of organisations will be investigated along with individual responses to them and ways of working within them. Participants will be encouraged to think about building coalitions, developing networks and make lasting mutually supportive connections. Adaptive Leadership Skills: Leadership is really tested when the environment is challenging or when there are no easy solutions. A leader’s attitude to risk and challenge, and their ability to take the long-term view, are essential in this context. This day will look at how the process of adaptive leadership could enhance your performance and success Action Learning Set 2 Recent Aurora attendees from the UoP had this to say: "Aurora has made me think a lot more about my career direction, I now think more strategically and am not afraid to have a go or put myself forward for things” “I have now become more self-aware and confident of my leadership style and identity.” “Senior leadership seemed completely out of reach before this programme but I've gained a lot of insight into the kind of attributes and skills that will make me more effective at work and thus more confident to pursue promotion”. ”Aurora is a great opportunity to reflect on your career and what you want from it. It provides you with a framework to develop yourself and network with other women from a variety of backgrounds and job roles. It is an excellent programme that has really helped me. If you are thinking of about applying and would like to explore if this is the right programme for you then email aurora@port.ac.uk and the Aurora Admin Champion will answer any questions you may have. There is a competitive application process due to the limited central fund for Aurora, which covers the cost of the programme, this year’s programme is being delivered virtually so there will be no travel costs. If you are grade 5-8 and are interested in being part of this programme, please speak to your line manager at the earliest opportunity to confirm their support. They will be required to write a brief supporting statement. Line Manager Statement: "Please explain how the applicant’s personal and professional development will be enhanced by taking part in this programme and why this is a good time for them to be considering the leadership side of their role." Once you have your line manager’s supporting statement, you will also need your Dean or Head of Professional Services to confirm whether they support/do not support the application. If a nomination has been submitted to Aurora@port.ac.uk from your Dean or Head of Service prior to your application, this is not required again in the application statement of support. When you have received your supporting statement/s please send your application, following these specific guidelines, along with your line manager’s supporting statement and confirmation from Dean/Head of Service (if applicable) to Aurora@port.ac.uk by Friday 20 August 21. Helen Keetch (Aurora Admin Champion) HR Business Partnering Team Learning and Development My Rewards and Benefits Occupational Health Service Equality and Diversity Contact and departments

title: Springboard
headings: Human Resources What is the Springboard Programme? Who can participate in Springboard? Why is Springboard for women only? What is involved? Where is the programme run? What is the evidence that it works? Testimonials Testimonials from UoP delegates Apply How do I apply? 2021-2022 programmes will be held on the following dates: Explore
body: The Springboard Programme is a three-month personal and work development programme for women (and those that identify as women) run across 35 countries, designed to help you recognise your own potential and reach your goals. Springboard – Springboard Consultancy Individuals speak of a new sense of purpose, an open approach to change and a more positive attitude. Employers report being delighted with the changes to their staff, who show a more focused and positive outlook, offering solutions rather than problems as well as increased motivation. Springboard helps organisations tackle the gender pay gap and work on the key issues of diversity and inclusion. Programmes 15 onwards will be facilitated by Chris Cross, one of Springboards Senior Consultants. Chris Cross has been a professional trainer, coach and facilitator for over 20 years. With extensive training and coaching experiences in universities across the UK. Working with teams, groups and individuals helping them make the most of their skills, their strengths and experiences. As one of Springboards senior trainers Chris has seen more than 2,000 women complete this award-winning programme. “I love creating a supportive and encouraging atmosphere. Springboard is designed to be interactive and I will help you feel comfortable and inspired to make the most of this opportunity. Whether you are ready for big changes, or time and space to reflect, I will be encouraging you and guiding you through the programme.” It is open to Academic, Research and Professional Services staff at any stage in their lives or careers, and from any background. Whether your focus is on developing your career, improving your life skills or setting practical, yet stretching goals, this programme will provide the tools to inspire and empower you to change your life. Some women focus on results in their personal life, such as sorting out difficult relationships, improving health, dealing with stress and gaining a work/life balance (Springboard 2020). The only requirement is an interest in developing yourself and attending all sessions. Extensive research in a variety of organisations shows that: women and men approach personal development issues in different ways women and men benefit from discussing some issues in a single gender group Each participant has their own reasons for attending the programme and these vary enormously. Some of these maybe: increase in confidence improved levels of assertiveness, better work and personal relationships a sense of direction – what next taking greater control of their lives (It can, where appropriate, help women prepare for opportunities, including the Aurora programme.) There are four one-day workshops and a workbook spread over 3 months. The workshops are designed to be supportive and encouraging. The workbooks are filled with ideas, exercises and examples to help you through the programme. The course is down-to-earth, practical and full of forward thinking information and includes: Workshop 1 - Be the authentic you Workshop 2 - Taking control Workshop 3 - Assertiveness Workshop 4 - The future You will need to spend some personal time working through the workbook in between workshops, in order to get the most out of the course. There is no set amount of time for this but allow between 2 and 4 hours between workshops to complete each section. Feedback has shown that those who have completed the independent work tend to get the most out of the course. For many the content has been a very valuable part of the experience. Springboard is run internally at UoP – however due to Covid 19 some programmes may be delivered virtually. We will confirm the format of delivery closer to the start date for each Programme. For more information please contact learninganddevelopment@port.ac.uk. "I really enjoyed the Springboard sessions. It created a safe, encouraging space to reflect on my own behaviour and consider my goals. The workbook meant that you had to sit down and really consider yourself and how you work and answer some tricky questions about yourself you normally try to avoid. My biggest take away is that you cannot wait to be confident or brave, you have to earn it by doing things that scare you." “I have learned so much - lots of information and lots of useful strategies, most importantly, I got to know more about myself, learnt to accept myself, and started making plans just for myself. Would recommend it to everyone!” Application process for Springboard Programmes for 2021/2022 If you are interested in joining please complete the following Springboard Programme Application for 2021-2022 confirming: What you wish to get from attending the programme That you are committed and able to attend all workshops Evidence of your Line Manager’s support Include all programmes you are able to attend. Places will be allocated to the programme on a first come, first served basis. Where there are more expressions of interest than places, a waiting list will be created and individuals will be contacted once future programmes have been scheduled. COVID-19 Note: Please note due to COVID-19 the format of delivery for these programmes cannot be confirmed at this stage. Therefore if face-to-face training is not appropriate these workshops will run virtually. If sessions are able to take place in face-to-face format they will run as 4 x full day sessions (09:30 - 16:30). If they are required to be delivered virtually they will be split into 8 x half day sessions (09:30 - 13:00). Therefore at this stage you will need to commit to both sets of dates (virtual and face-to-face) as currently we cannot confirm the format in which the workshops will be delivered. This will be confirmed shortly before the scheduled dates. Please see below scheduled dates for Springboard Programmes 15-17. title: Advanced Information Governance
headings: Human Resources Learning and Development Advanced Information Governance Target audience Prerequisite training Pre-event preparation Session outline The aims of the session are: Learning outcomes Format Presenters Delegate feedback Explore
body: Staff with responsibility for managing records (paper or electronic) and/or processing data on behalf of their department Staff with responsibility for supporting and encouraging colleagues in the management of records and/or processing data Staff contemplating any form of digitsation (scanning) project in the near future It would be helpfrul if attendees have successfully completed the Information Governance eLearning package to have some background knowledge of the subjects under discussion. Please click to access the Advanced Information Governance Training Handouts via EDM (Electronic Document Management) within Objective Navigator. Please print copies to bring along to the session if you would like to take notes against them. We advise that you do this the day before the event so that you are printing off the most current version. If you currently do not have access to Objective Navigator , click for details of how to add it to your PC/laptop. This session builds upon the foundation of the Information Governance eLearning package. The session will provide further detail on records management issues and details of the General Data Protection Regulations 2018. General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) – to explain the differences between the GDPR 2018 and the Data Protection Act 1998, and what we should be doing in order to be compliant with the 2018 legislation Records management in the 21st Century - what are the current challenges facing staff managing records, and how can you help your colleagues/department overcome these obstacles Information Governance Clinic - using attendee questions provided prior to the session, the aim is to both provide advice and stimulate debate around Data Protection, Records Management and Information Security. Understand the requirements of the new GDPR legislation Advise colleagues on what they need to do now to be compliant with the GDPR Identify where changes to current procedures / policies / documents may need to be made Understand current records management challenges that the University is facing Know where to find and how to interpret and apply the University Retention Schedules Be more confident in the weeding and management of locally held staff and student files Have a model for prioritising work on old departmental records Understand the minimum considerations for a successful digitisation project 2 x presentations and a workshop debate Sarah Arnold, University Records Manager Samantha Hill, Information Disclosure and Complaints Manager Attendees to provide questions, in advance, for the Information Governance Clinic section. Questions should be emailed to information-matters@port.ac.uk with the subject 'Advanced MIB Question' Click here for the Learning and Development calendar with all available dates. title: Autism Awareness
headings: Human Resources Learning and Development Title Target Audience Outline The aims of the workshop are to: Learning Outcomes Format: Presenter(s) Delegate Feedback Explore
body: All staff with an interest in increasing their awareness of autism. Presented by Autism Hampshire, this workshop aims to raise general autism awareness and how to support those with autism. Raise awareness of autism. By the end of this workshop participants will: Define autism in the context of the neurodiversity model Identify 4 broad areas in which people on the Autism Spectrum may have different perceptions Suggest examples of 'reasonable adjustments' that could accommodate such differences. title: Disability Awareness Training
headings: Human Resources Learning and Development Title Target Audience Outline The aims of the workshop are to: Pre-event Preparation Format Presenter(s) Explore
body: Disability Awareness Training All staff with an interest in increasing their awareness of disability and improving inclusivity in the workplace for staff with disabilities. Presented by Toucan Diversity Training, a user led, award winning social enterprise based in Portsmouth. The training is designed and delivered by a group of accredited trainers who all have a lived experience of a disability. The workshop aims to raise general disability awareness and offer guidance, advice and support on how to improve inclusivity in the workplace for staff with a disability, as well as provide a unique opportunity to ask questions from someone with a lived experience of disability, in a safe learning environment. Understand where attitudes come from and how they have changed over time Highlight the importance and value of the 'social model' of disability Gain a brief overview of the Equality Act, in relation to disability, and learn what is expected of service providers and employers in the UK Be aware of respectful language and etiquette Explore and understand the barriers people with disabilities face everyday Identify how to create a more inclusive environment. Please read through the following before attending the session: Disability Related Absence Guidance and Disability Guidance A 3.5 our workshop, including powerpoint presentation, group activities, quizzes, video work and discussion. Toucan Diversity Training Click for the Learning and Development calendar with all available dates. title: Fire Marshal Training
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Fire Marshal Training Leader Details Target audience Aims of the workshop Learning outcomes Other staff development opportunities relevant to this topic Explore
body: Laura Sayers - Health & Safety Advisor (Fire Safety) This training is aimed at members of staff who have volunteered to be a building Fire Marshal. As a result of attending participants will be competent to carry out the University's emergency evacuation procedure. Understand the principles of Fire Understand the legal requirements in relation to Fire Safety issues Understand human behaviour in a Fire situation Demonstrate an understanding of the roles and responsibilities of a Fire Marshal Manual Handling – particularly useful for staff in Caretaking and Library Services roles but may be relevant to any member of staff. Working at Height – for staff routinely required to work off ladders etc. First Aid at Work – initial and refresher training for those taking on the role of Workplace First Aider. Further information about all of the above staff development opportunities can be obtained from the Health and Safety Office, St Andrew's Court – see: www.port.ac.uk/departments/services/humanresources/healthandsafety/training/ Training Biological Safety Biosafety Awareness COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Drone Training DSE Online training Fire Awareness First Aid Genetic Modification Safety Manual Handling Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Risk Assessment Safe Use of Compressed Gas Working at Height COSHH - Chemical Safety and COSHH Dealing with Chemical Spills and Emergency Preparedness Genetic Modification Safety Mental Health First Aid Pressure Systems Safety Safe Use of Compressed Gas Health and Safety Team Contact and departments

title: Manual Handling
headings: Health, Safety and Compliance Health and Safety Explore

title: Mental DeClutter
headings: Human Resources Learning and Development Title Target Audience Outline The aims of the workshop are to: Learning Outcomes Format Presenter Explore
body: This 2 hour workshop will provide a reflective space for delegates to identify what is causing them 'mental clutter' and then provide them with strategies to manage mental clutter and how to 'de-clutter'. The facilitator will draw on neuroscience, metaphor and mindfulness in delivering this workshop - which will be practical and experiential in its delivery style. Delegates will be signposted to further reading and you-tube clips to support this short workshop. Provide delegates with approaches and strategies to 'de-clutter' their minds and thereby enhance focus Give an understanding of how very quickly and simply we can create 'mental clutter' from our throught processes alone Allow an opportunity to understand some of the more scientific aspects of what is going on for them during times of stress, overload and limited performance. By the end of this workshop participants will: Have learnt a variety of techniques/approaches to help de-clutter their minds and provide head-space to think and focus clearly. This is a practical workshop where delegates will be given the opportunity to 'try out' tools and approaches, it will be supported with facilitated discussion and a workbook. Julia Carden, Carden Consulting Click here for the Learning and Development calendar with all available dates. title: Exploring PDR Conversations
headings: Human Resources Learning and Development Title Target Audience The aims of the workshop are to: Learning Outcomes Format Pre-event Preparation Presenter Further Resources Explore
body: Exploring PDR Conversations This is core training for staff who are either: new to conducting Performance Development Review's (PDR's) new to the University and will be conducting PDR's This workshop is also open to all staff who are responsible for conducting PDR's who would like to further develop their skills. This workshop incorporates how to gain maximum value from the PDR process. Build skill and confidence in having quality conversations about performance and development Understand the barriers to having an effective PDR and explore how to create an environment for productive conversations Develop confidence in conducting effective PDR’s at the University. By the end of this workshop participants will have: Understood and built confidence in how to have meaningful conversations with their staff, about their performance and development Gain confidence in how to engage and motivate staff in completing an effective PDR. This is a 3 hour highly practical workshop where group work and facilitated discussion will be used. Delegates should familiarise themselves with the content of the PDR webpages and the People and Performance handout before attending the session. Julia Carden, Carden Consulting. Feedback Essentials How to Conduct a Performance Review MeetingHow to handle difficult conversations The Learning and Development Events Calendar lists all dates available. title: Training for PGR supervisors
headings: Training sessions run by the Doctoral College The Doctoral College offers comprehensive support and guidance for Postgraduate Research Degree (PGR) Supervisors through Introductory and Best Practice workshops, Development workshops, topics of advanced or specialist interest, and useful resources. Explore our supervision events Research supervision events programme Book your place Online Resources Useful resources for supervisors and staff External information and resources Research degree guidance The Doctoral College Enable University alerts Participate in User Research
body: A tailored programme for all supervisors In the workshops, we provide an overview of the processes involved in managing a research degree student at Portsmouth, as well as providing opportunities for you to pick up many hints and tips and to discuss the 'art' of supervision with experienced supervisors. As a research degree supervisor you can take advantage of high quality online training resources provided by the Doctoral College. If you can't come to a scheduled workshop, you can instead complete some of the mandatory training online at a more convenient time. We offer online training for: Update for Experienced Supervisors (coming soon!) Guidance for Internal/External Examiners and the University of Portsmouth Process Best Practice in the Selection and Admission of PGR students Compilation Style PhD Thesis The following resources will help you to support your research students: Research Degrees Operational Handbook Code of Practice for Research Students‌ Writing, Communication and Language Support for Research Students University forms for PGR procedures and processes can be found via the Department of Academic Services Research Degrees MyPort page. The following information and resources are by external bodies which may help to enhance your research supervisor experience: The Good Supervisory Practice Framework (UKCGE) A Guide to Online Supervision (UKCGE) Research Supervision Recognition programme (UKCGE) Postgraduate Research Supervisors Network (UKCGE) Supervision and key relationships (Vitae) UK Quality Code, Advice and Guidance: Research Degrees If you have any enquiries with regards to our supervisor workshops, please contact supervisorevents@port.ac.uk. Everything you need to know in relation to your time as a postgraduate research student. Get support to develop the research, professional and transferable skills you need to succeed. Turn on notifications for critical updates like closures, safety alerts, and urgent service disruptions. We'd love to hear your thoughts about the MyPort website.

title: Student Complaints - How to deal with them.
headings: Human Resources Learning and Development Title Target Audience Outline The aims of the workshop are to: Learning Outcomes Pre-event Preparation Format Presenter(s) Explore
body: Student Complaints - How to deal with them All staff - but of particular interest to academic staff with teaching/supervision roles and all administrative staff with a student facing role. The purpose of this workshop is to provide information on the University's Student Complaint Procedure and guidance for staff on dealing with issues at an informal level with the aim of resolving a complaint before it escalates. Further understanding of how the Student Complaint Procedure works Understand what this means for staff Learn how to handle complaints at an informal level. By the end of the workshop particpants will: Understand how the Student Complaint Procedure works Have an understanding of how to resolve a complaint informally. Participants are asked to read the Student Complaint Procedure. This 2.5 hour workshop will involve the completion of some case studies. Nicola Young, Complaints and Information Disclosure Officer Nathalie Derrick, Assistant Complaints and Information Disclosure Officer Click here for the Management and Staff Development calendar with all available dates. title: The Work Shadowing Toolkit
headings: Human Resources Work Shadowing Why do work shadowing? How does it work? What should I think about before taking part in work shadowing? Apply for the scheme Leaving feedback Senior staff case study- Bob Nichol and Graham Galbraith Explore
body: Work Shadowing provides an opportunity for staff to network and Shadow other areas of the university within similar or different roles. It offers a ‘fact finding’ opportunity and provides both personal and professional development. Find out more about the benefits The most beneficial Shadow can be gained from a well planned visit with clear objectives and goals. All parties including line managers should ideally be fully involved and engaged. Find out more about the process There are some things you should consider before the work shadow, whether you are the shadow or the host, such as your aims and objectives during the programme. Think about how to make the most out of your work shadowing experience. Find out more about things to consider You can apply to take part in the work shadowing scheme by completing this Google form. To ensure the successful running of the work shadowing programme, we need your feedback so we know where we can improve and to make sure staff are getting the most out of their experience. Fill out and email the below form to HR-Workshadowing@port.ac.uk and speak to your line manager about your learnings during the programme. Complete the evaluation form Senior staff case study- Bob Nichol and Graham Galbraith Bob Nichol, Pro Vice-Chancellor (Research and Innovation) took part in work shadowing around two years ago. After 13 years working at the University, he realised he’d like to find out more about more central University roles. Therefore, he decided to jump straight in at the deep end and ask to shadow Vice-Chancellor Graham Galbraith. Find out more about Bob's work shadowing experience. title: Coaching to Achieve our Goals
headings: Human Resources Learning and Development Title Explore
body: Creating a Coaching Culture - Coaching to Achieve our Goals “Coaching is unlocking a person’s potential to maximise their performance, helping them to learn rather than teaching them.” This is a two part, 3.5 hour interactive and participative workshop for all staff. It is an introduction to coaching, what coaching is, where it can be valuable and how you may already be coaching in everyday life without knowing it. At the end of this workshop participants will: Understand what coaching is and how it supports the University Mission, Vision and Values Recognise the benefits and value of coaching Understand the skills required to be an effective coach Be able to structure coaching conversations using the GROW model Have had an opportunity to practice coaching in a safe environment Have the foundations of a coaching toolkit and know where to find additional support and resources. A two part 3.5 hour interactive and participative workshop. Pre-Event Preparation Please come prepared with a coaching scenario that you are happy to discuss within the workshop. This could be work or home related e.g. you’ve reached a sticking point in a project, you’re exploring your career options or would benefit from coaching around a difficult situation that needs resolving. Kathryn Hawkley, Learning and Development Adviser The GROW Model and Template Coaching V Mentoring Infographic UoP Staff Mentoring Scheme This workshop supports the People Strategy and the Portsmouth Hallmark. title: Leading Change
headings: Human Resources Learning and Development Title Target Audience Outline The aims of the session are to: Learning Outcomes Format Presenter(s): Additional resources: Explore
body: The workshop is open to all leaders and managers. It is strongly recommend that delegates have a change project in mind or already involved in one that can be used to apply course concepts during the workshop. This one-day workshop takes the approach that in order to develop commitment to change, we must move beyond seeing change as only a rational process to be managed and see it as an emotional process that requires leadership too. We will use the simple 8-step model created by John Kotter as a framework of principles that can guide leadership focus in times of disruptive change. This framework begins by looking at the need to address the complacency that exists in many teams and how to overcome this with a directed sense of urgency. We will also look at the importance of effective communication and a compelling vision and give delegates the opportunity to develop their own messages and communications related to their own change. Linked to this will be the development of a business case that engages both rationally and emotionally with employees’ needs. Recognise that the leadership and management of change are different processes and that leadership is often overlooked in favour of management tasks Develop an understanding of what change leadership involves and identify how we can be better change leaders. By the end of this workshop participants will: Understand the challenges of changing cultures and behaviours at the University Introduce an 8 step model for leading change Identify a range of leadership tools, techniques and principles to implement change more effectively Develop a business case for your change that addresses the “hearts and minds” of your teams. The workshop will be a combination of videos, case study work, presentations, discussion and hands-on exercises. This workshop supports the development of the following Leadership Attributes: Pre-event Preparation Our Iceberg is Melting by John Kotter and Holger Rathgeber. Harvard Business Review by John Kotter, it is not essential that you read this but you may find it a useful additional resource. Craig Smith, Lead Consultant, Flint Spark Consulting John Kotter on Transformational Change Leading Change: John Kotter's Eight Step Model Kotter's Eight Step Change Management Model (Infographic) An Introduction to Change Managing the Change Process Acheiving Culture Change in Your Organisation Appreciative Inquiry: A Positive Approach to Change Change Impact Analysis Damian Hughes on Leading Change Click here for the Learning and Development calendar with all available dates. title: Preparing to Present
headings: Human Resources Learning and Development Title Target Audience Outline The aim of the workshops is: Learning Outcomes Pre-event Preparation Format Presenter(s) Explore
body: All staff where there is an expectation that they will deliver presentations. A full day of practical activity and feedback, working with some key concepts on a presentaton that you own. To help people understand and improve their presentation creation and delivery skills. By the end of this workshop participants will have: Understood some key principles of successful presenting Considered different structures and methods for developing the content of a presentation Considered the use of aids such as projections and props Understood and practiced simple approaches to the management of adrenaline, apprehension and techniques for the projection of confidence Understood their strengths and development areas Created or modified, and delivered an aspect of a presentation Afterwards, a video of their final presentation will be distributed confidentially to each participant. Please bring along ideas for a presentation that you wish to work on live. This means bringing any key content information, but ideally not in a finished form. The best way of thinking about this is to answer the following: What do I need to tell a group of people, and why do they need to know? If you seek to work with presentation software, eg. Prezi/Powerpoint, please bring along a device, eg. laptop/tablet, that you can create and or/edit them. Some theoretical ideas, mostly practical and interactive, working on your unique presence and material you bring to work on. Anthony Richards is director of Dramatic Improvement, a company specialising in effective communication skills. Having trained in England and the US as a Theatre Director, he spent 20 years directing and acting with his own company. Since 2001 he has been working with businesses, charities and higher educational establishments in Europe and the US, helping people open up their emotional intelligence and find the positive energy to connect with others. title: SWIMM Programme
headings: Human Resources What is the SWIMM Programme? (South West Institutions’ Middle Managers Programme) Who can participate in SWIMM? What is involved? What are the benefits? Where is SWIMM held and how much does it cost? 2020 programme information (no details as yet for 2021) Explore
body: A regional management programme for middle managers working in HE SWIMM is a unique and innovative regional programme designed to allow middle managers in professional and support services (our grades 6, 7 and 8) working in South West universities, to network with peers in similar roles across the HE sector. It gives delegates the chance to reflect on their role and to further develop the key management skills and techniques needed today. The universities involved recognise that there is huge benefit in working regionally with exposure to a wider range of practices, and developing managers as a future resource for the region. Participants benefit greatly (see feedback below) from this opportunity to network with peers in similar roles across the sector, within a supportive atmosphere, and away from the normal working environment. SWIMM is designed for middle managers (our grades 6,7 and 8 ) who are new to their role or already in post who would like to further develop their management skills. (Please see flyer for more information). The content may also be relevant for project managers. The programme was originally devised for managers of professional staff but over the years there have been a handful of academic staff with people managing responsibilities who have also found it relevant. If you are in an academic role and interested in finding out more about the programme, please contact learninganddevelopment@port.ac.uk initially – we can forward your query onto SWIMM. If you are involved in the following areas of management activity, the programme may be of interest to you: Appointment, development and motivation of team members Managing performance, sickness and absence of staff Providing leadership and direction for one or more teams Taking and implementing decisions within the team/unit/Dept/School/University Collaboration with peers and senior staff within and outside the University Managing projects within the department or across the University Continuous improvement – seeking ways to meet customer’s needs, improve systems, processes Contribution to the strategic planning and development of the Dept/University The Programme gives managers the chance to reflect on their role and to further develop some key management skills and techniques, needed in today’s universities. Participants benefit greatly (see below) from the opportunity to liaise and network with peers in similar roles from across the sector, in a supportive atmosphere, away from the normal working environment. A significant part of the programme involves accessing and sharing the current knowledge and experience of being a Manager, so it will not be appropriate for anyone other than managers already in post. As a result of attending this programme, you will Further your understanding of the context in which HEI's operate by networking with managers undertaking similar roles across the sector and other institutions Look at the way in which you see yourself as a manager, your management style, and the ways in which people work together in teams and are motivated to perform. Develop management tools and techniques which can be applied back in your own place of work. Consider a range of management skills affecting your work and created strategies for your own development. Consider your personal development needs, and have completed a learning diary. SWIMM will be held in the luxurious, historic setting of Bailbrook Hotel and Conference Centre in Bath Please find attached flyer with more information about the programme along with the nomination form. The cost of the programme for 2020 is £795, which represents excellent value for money, with similar residential programmes costing approx £1200. The price includes single room accommodation, all meals, 2 full days conference facilities, course fees and a 24 page individualised profile. Please note that the full costs (including travel costs) will be payable from your local budget and a 50% deposit will be payable when we confirm places. Please ensure that the relevant budget code is included in the application document and indicate that you are happy to accept the costs and any cancellation charges in your local budget. The 2020 programme will now run for 2 days from Thursday 19 March to Friday 20 March 2020. Places for 2020 are currently full. If you are interested in future programmes or would like more information on the programme please email learninganddevelopment@port.ac.uk Feedback from previous delegates It was great to network with people from other Universities, find out how they deal with certain problems. It covered good basis of people management, performance management, delegating and influencing upwards, change management, working in a team with different personalities and many more. The programme covered a wide range of issues, from managing poor performance to personal career development HR Business Partnering Team Learning and Development My Rewards and Benefits Occupational Health Service Equality and Diversity Contact and departments

title: Promoting Equality, Dignity and Respect in the Workplace
headings: Human Resources Learning & Development Title Target Audience Outline The aims & objectives of the workshop are to: Pre-event Preparation Format: Presenter(s) Explore
body: Promoting Equality, Dignity and Respect in the Workplace All those who manage or supervise staff This workshop is provided in addition to the following core eLearning packages, which are a pre-requisite of attending this session: Equality and Diversity in the workplace Anti Bullying and Harassment To request access to this training please email james.ross@port.ac.uk The workshops aims to further explore these areas, supporting managers in delivering an inclusive workplace for their staff where all are treated with dignity and respect. Explore further aspects of Equality and Diversity Explore further aspects of Dignity and Respect Understand the responsibilities of everyone with both the Dignity and Respect policy and the Equality and Diversity Policy Statement Identify how to create an inclusive working environment Explore Bias and Micro Behaviours Taking a lead in changing workplace culture Resolving issues at a local level What to do when things go wrong and where to find support and guidance. Apart from completion of the core eLearning packages (links provided above) which are a pre-requisite of attending this session, you are also asked to read the Dignity and Respect Policy A 3.5 hour workshop which includes powerpoint presentation, group activities and Q&A. title: Introduction to Islam and the Muslim Culture Course
headings: Human Resources Learning and Development Target Audience Outline The aims of the workshop are to: Learning Outcomes Pre-event Reading Format: Presenter Explore
body: Introduction to Islam and the Muslim Culture Course Any University Staff member or Associates Affiliated to the University With the increasing number of terrorist attacks associated with Islam and Muslims across the globe, and Europe in particular, the constant negative portrayal of Islam in the Media, and public sector organisations including universities required to report radicalization under the “Prevent” programme, isn’t it time to learn more about Islam and Muslims and from authentic sources? Provide a better understanding of the basic beliefs and practices Provide information regarding the background to Muslims in Britain Give confidence in understanding what is Islam and what is not Question the actions of ISIS, Boko Haram, and the Paris Attack, Islamic? Provide confidence to know what “extremism” is and what is not Ensure cultural competency in carrying out your work with the Muslim Community. By the end of this workshop participants will understand: What are the push and pull factors of radicalization Who is Prophet Muhammad Why there is segregation of the sexes in Muslim communities Why some Muslim women wear a headscarf or the veil What actually happens inside a Mosque What is the status of women in Islam Why Muslims pray five times a day What is the meaning of Jihad What Islam says about Education? Endeavour to answer any questions you have about Islam and Muslims Itinerary and Etiquette for visiting a Mosque Click here for the Learning and Development calendar with all available dates. title: Introduction to Trans Awareness
headings: Human Resources Learning and Development Title Target Audience Outline Explore
body: Introduction to Trans Awareness Gendered Intelligence is a not-for-profit Community Interest Company, established in 2008. It works predominantly with the trans community and those who impact on trans lives; it particularly specialises in supporting young trans people aged 8-25. They deliver trans youth programmes, support for parents and carers, professional development and trans awareness training for all sectors and educational workshops for schools, colleges, Universities and other educational settings. The organisations mission is to increase understandings of gender diversity. “Our vision is of a world where people are no longer constrained by narrow perceptions and expectations of gender, and where diverse gender expressions are visible and valued” Everyone can be more intelligent about gender: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=upAX-glEJBM The aims of the workshops are to: Set the wider context for trans identities – how sex, gender and sexual orientation interact Explore key terms and usage of language Offer basic grounding in the legislation around the rights and responsibilities around trans identities; Equality Act 2010, Gender Recognition Act 2004 Begin to explore how an organisation can ensure it is being trans-inclusive Provide links to a wide range of resources. Have wider understanding of trans identities, key terms and usage of language Have understanding of the legal framework around trans equality and the responsibilities that places on the University and us as employees Have understanding of how we can support and deliver a trans-inclusive environment for staff and students Increase your confidence in work with trans colleagues and students. Prior to coming to the workshop please can you familiarise yourself with the Gender Reassignment and Trans Equality Policy. It would also be helpful if you could think about a situation in which you would like to communicate more confidently. We will explore these in greater detail during the session using some of the learnt techniques. Workshop and discussion Jason Barker (Gendered Intelligence) Click here for the Learning and Development calendar with all available dates. title: Meeting Support and Minute Writing
headings: Human Resources Learning and Development Title Target Audience Outline The aims of the workshop are to: Explore
body: Meeting Support and Minute Writing Staff who take notes at various types of meetings who would like to learn more about this activity within the University. Types of meetings can include: Committees, Departmental, Team, Focus Groups, Operational or Working Groups. This workshop is a practical session all about identifying and practicing the skills of supporting a committee or group and taking notes effectively during meetings. It provides the opportunity for discussion with colleagues, sharing good working practices such as; an organised approach, preparing for the meeting, summarising when writing up, using the past tense, looking at the format and templates to make your notes easy to read. The workshop will help delegates to produce a concise and consistent look for documents that can be circulated with confidence. Promote collaborative working between Chairs and Minute Takers Look at setting an agenda Provoke thought and reflection on current practice to identify possible improvements Share working practices for supporting a meeting and minute writing Improve the experience of taking minutes at meetings Practice minute writing skills such as listening, organisation of minutes, templates and format for writing up and summarising skills Inspire confidence when supporting a meeting. Please note that this session does not cover Unit Assessment Boards (UAB) and Boards of Examiners (BoE) specifically, although the skills discussed and practiced can be applied to these meetings. Sessions dedicated to UAB and BoE training take place in June and can/or be accessed via these links: Introduction to Unit Assessment Boards and Introduction to Boards of Examiners. The workshop will be delivered in one session and will include: Preparation and planning, including agenda preparation, briefing the Chair, using templates and checklists. The opportunity to practice minute taking and summarising skills and discuss this activity with colleagues. Developing a deeper understanding of skills needed for minute taking and writing them up. By the end of this workshop participants will: Have a shared understanding of the purpose of supporting a meeting and minute writing Understand the benefit of a pre-meeting discussion with whoever is leading/Chairing the meetings Have looked at the meeting cycle and how to plan a timetable to manage activities Have identified and practiced the core skills for a note-taker Have explored techniques for minute writing Have looked at different layouts for presenting meeting notes Be able to develop a clear, concise and effective layout Have considered what to include/exclude when writing up minutes Have been encouraged to use plain English, develop a style for writing up and polish their grammar. A 2.5 hour workshop which includes presentation with group discussion and practical activities. Lisa Scott, Manager, Office of the Academic Registrar & Director Victoria Giles, Senior Administrator (Committee Secretariat), Department of Student & Academic Administration (DSAA) Click here for the Learning and Development calendar with all available dates. title: Staff Essentials - Support Staff Conference 2017
headings: Staff Essentials Support/Professional Services Staff Conference 2017 Working Collaboratively to Achieve our Goals
body: Welcome to Staff Essentials, providing news, events and useful information for University of Portsmouth staff. The University’s Support/Professional Services Staff Conference took take place on Thursday 5 January 2017 in Richmond Building. The conference aims were to: Celebrate how staff work together across the institution to achieve our strategic goals. Inspire and encourage delegates to continue to make the University a great place to work, both for themselves and others. Share information and resources that can help improve and promote collaborative working. Provide an opportunity for staff to meet with other colleagues they may not normally come into contact with The conference was opened by Graham Galbraith and included two rounds of parallel sessions each on a different subject. As well as parallel sessions we ran a 'speed networking' event and the event closed with a student and staff question panel. We are pleased to share the presentations (internal only) from the day (including the parrallel sessions). The feedback received has been extremely positive, some of the comments can be found below: "The Nifty Networking was presented really well, and met my expectations for obtaining tools to deal with networking. This was then followed by the Speed Networking event which again was really useful, especially having completed the Nifty Networking prior to this event. I found one person I met with that I wouldn't necessarily come in contact with, and although she is new, I think I will be contacting her in the future for possible shadowing" "I learned a bit more about relevant topics such as cash handling, student finance, and the break out session on coaching gave me some new ideas. The speedy networking was fun too!" "The mentoring and social media sessions were both really informative. It is always interesting to hear from UEB about their direction/focus for the upcoming year" "I particularly enjoyed the Social Media parallel session because the information will enhance what we do in our department" "The mental health session was really helpful. I am actually going to follow this up with the Chaplaincy and Student Wellbeing services in order to provide better support and guidance to my students" "The whole event makes me feel valued as an employee and I do always learn something new and meet colleagues that I wouldn't ordinarily have contact with" "Talks from the technical support staff were very interesting and good to know what colleagues are doing. The talk on My Port was very helpful as was the speed presentations at the start of the conference" "I particularly enjoyed the speed networking - although I thought I would not. The slides and presentations at the beginning of the conference were also excellent as we were provided with snap/brief bite size pieces of information" Members of the 2017 Support Staff Conference Working Group: Gareth Morgan, Faculty Manager (Chair) Helen Keetch, Learning and Development Adviser, HR (Conference Organiser) Claire Unwin Reed, Business Architect Peter Coxhead – Technical Manager - School of Biological Sciences Lisa White - Staff Development Adviser - University Library Lee Mingay – Student Recruitment Officer Louisa Caine – Faculty Registrar – Humanities Lisa Egan – Press Officer Louise Bragg – Team Leader International Support Kimberley Spencer, HR Administrative Assistant (Information Fair)

title: Emily Parry
headings: Human Resources Emily Parry - Employability and Enterprise Manager Explore
body: Student Placement and Employability Centre (SPEC) - Technology Learning Centre I have been at the university for 13 years in a range of admin roles, having previously worked in a call centre and in purchasing for a local sewage treatment company. I joined the uni in 2002, starting as a Course Administrator in the Undergraduate Centre in PBS based at the old Milton Campus. I then moved sideways to the same role within the Post Graduate Centre of PBS, looking after the admin for the AMBA accredited MBA programmes, eventually progressing to Senior Course Administrator with some supervisory responsibility. With my experience of the student records system, I moved to Academic Registry as a Student Records System Adviser where I provided front line support to end users and training on the ‘Discoverer’ reporting system. I then realised I missed working with students, and, given my experience with senior management level staff when working on the MBA programmes, I was able to demonstrate the competencies to secure my current role of Employability and Enterprise Manager. Over that time, I have developed and honed my customer service, relationship management and information advice and guidance skills, working with a range of different students and external companies. Strengths – I am a people person (ok, I like a chat), I like processes and can be very organised (when needs be!). I also like spotting opportunities for closer working relationships between the University and our placement employers. Weaknesses – numbers are not a strong point, I have terrible handwriting that even I have trouble understanding and I have always had a very poor short term memory. I have always been one for taking up opportunities that will add something to me as a person, as well as the department I am working in at that point – it is something that not all organisations are able or willing to offer their staff. I was in the first cohort for the FdA Business and Management back in 2004 and followed this up with an MA Business Management, which I studied part time via the Learning at Work programme and graduated from in 2010. I loved that I was able to tailor my studies to my roles at that point. I have complemented this with other CPD activity, taking advantage of the excellent in house training that the University provides (my recent favourite is the Mental Declutter) and, in my current role, attending external conferences too. Currently I am the Employability and Enterprise Manager for the Faculty of Technology – I work with students during their second year to help them see the potential benefits of a placement and also help them to build the skills to secure a suitable role. I am passionate about the benefits of undertaking course relevant work experience and am proud of the service that the faculty, and university as a whole, is able to provide. I love seeing the result of a student’s hard work – how they have changed both physically and mentally when they come back from their time in the work place or simply the little boost in confidence when they have finally got a CV good enough to submit to a company. In this role, my strength is definitely that I like talking to people – whoever they are. I love hearing about what students want to do or what they do in their spare time and how to show that it definitely is relevant to an employer, as well as talking to companies about how our students could be of real benefit to them. My previous roles within the uni have given me the confidence and ability to talk to people at all levels of an organisation and build effective relationships. My time as a Course Administrator has given me excellent organisational skills and an in-depth understanding of how the student life cycle works from the perspective of different areas of the Uni– and now, how placements (and employability in general) fit in to that journey. I have also built strong relationships with people across the Uni over the past 13 years – if I don’t know the answer to a question or problem, I probably know someone who can help! Student Employability & Enterprise Officer but from 2013 has been Placement & Employability Manager. title: Helen Keetch
headings: Human Resources Helen Keetch – Registry Support Systems Analyst Explore
body: Faculty of Science - Course Administrator I started at the University twelve years ago as a temp. My first role was as a Course Administrator for the School of Earth and Environmental Sciences for half the week, the other half was spent in the School of Pharmacy and Biomedical Sciences. A full time role came up in Pharmacy where I stayed for about a year, then a full time role came up in the School of Earth and Environmental Sciences, which although a smaller school was somewhere I felt was a more comfortable fit at that time. Using the Student Records System Minutes and administration of Boards – Unit Assessment Boards and Board of Examiners, Board of Studies Annotating exam papers Registration of students Pharmacy only- Admin for Admissions interviews - Admin of PhD and Research students Faculty of Technology and Central Project Team Secondment - Jupiter support team Having become well versed in the Student Records system and the role of a C.A, a secondment opportunity came up for a project role to assist with the implementation of a new Student Records system. This would mean liaising between the central project team and one of the Faculties, helping guide the users through the new processes. At the same time an option of applying for a School Manager role in Science came up. I had to think very hard about which path I wanted to take, as both interviews would be within weeks of each other. For me I realised that at that point I was not ready for a role that I felt was an end result from a career perspective. So I interviewed for the secondment post and was successful. Networking and collaborating with other professional services staff in Registry, Information Services and the wider project team. Soft skills development Negotiating and creating trust with the new Faculty Working in a small internal project team and pitching for particular roles with tight time constraints Working with IT training who were rolling out corporate training sessions for staff Working autonomously more often and being staff’s point of contact, whilst dealing with a project roll out which often means processes and guidance changing on a daily basis at times. Faculty of Technology - Deputy Programme Area Manager Nearing the end of my secondment the Technology Faculty asked me to help with an internal course project. Once that was completed an opportunity arose to apply for a new role. Three departments were being combined to make a Programme Area. I was successful in gaining a role as the Deputy Programme Area Manager, giving me supervisory responsibility but staying on the same pay grade as my secondment role. Supervision for staff around systems and departmental processes Management of Programme Area Exam timetable and Induction timetable Manage all external and internal invigilation for exams Manage Subject and Award externals, including booking their accommodation and ensuring all coursework and paperwork was available to them on arrival Stepped up to Programme Area Manager role when line manager was on leave Faculty of Technology - Secondment – Programme Area Manager I really enjoyed my role as DPAM and after some time another secondment role came up. This role was as a Programme Area manager within the same Faculty. Again I was lucky enough to be successful. I really enjoyed the people management side of the role; however there were other elements of the role that did not suit what I wanted from my career at the time. It was a very difficult decision but ultimately I decided to leave the secondment early and return to by substantive DPAM role. This decision was not taken lightly but was completely the right decision for me at that time. Staff management role of a large team Oversee all programme area processes Management of Unit and Exam Boards Meetings with Heads and Course leaders regularly to ensure admin and academic teams working cohesively Overseeing Programme area Moodle site Back to Substantive role of Deputy Programme Area Manager Academic Registry - Academic Management Information Division - Registry Support Systems Analyst A role came up in Academic Registry with staff I had worked closely with on my first secondment. The grade of the role was the same as my DPAM role but it would mean working more specifically with student records and being a point of support and guidance for multiple departments. The role also offered an opportunity for me to train staff, which is something I was very keen to be involved with. I applied and was successful and have been in this role for just over 4 years now. Expert in student records system and Unit Database Trainer on Board and Assessment processes and the Unit Database Write all training manuals Book rooms and manage staff attendance Log all training on ITrent Assist with preparation and organisation of project roll out of new screens Human Resources - Secondment – Management and Staff Development Adviser Last year I had the opportunity to write and deliver two training sessions on a Delivering Excellence programme for one of the Management and Staff Development Advisers in HR. This came about after attending the Train the Trainer course on the open programme. The MSDA I had worked with was going on maternity leave and I was lucky to be shortlisted to interview for the part time maternity cover. I was successful and this has meant working in the mornings in HR and the afternoons in my substantive role in Registry for the past year. Coordinated two Delivering Excellence programmes, and created and delivered two separate sessions over the two programmes. Booking and organising and evaluating a keynote speaker event. Seeking new staff and facilitators to help and deliver on these and future programmes and writing advertisements for the communicator. Delivery of two parallel sessions at the Support Staff Conference on the Delivering Excellence programme Delivery of two interventions, several times on the open programme, Working with Assertiveness Working with our external consultants and observing and feeding back on their delivery. Learning and development provision for dedicated client areas around the University Attendance at two full day conferences on Customer service excellence Since being in the University it has been made very clear for me that: Hard work and dedication to the task at hand is well recognised and rewarded. Opportunities do not just land in your lap, you need to look for them and have the courage to put yourself out there and possibly face failure. Any failure should be embraced and used as a tool to develop and improve. Take all training opportunities available to you; just attending is not enough you have to fully engage! Ask what else you can get involved in within your role, seek out more responsibility Don’t be blinkered about the options that may be available to you. Most of your skills may be transferable. Finally be brave you don’t know if you don’t try. Experiences / Learning points Every experience I have had in the different roles I have undertaken have had an impact on me in some way, as have the staff I have worked for and alongside. My advice would always be if you have outgrown your role move on. If you are interested in a role then take some time to find out more about it from someone, this could be shadowing or just having a meeting and asking. Promotion or new roles are not an entitlement, you have to work hard, do your research and make sure you have the necessarily skills to put yourself in the best position. If you don’t get it don’t give up ! Other roles are out there that may be better for you. Networking is key to progression, if people know about you they may think of you for future roles. Starting at the bottom and working your way up, worked really well for me and gives you a great overview of the organisation and how it works. You will not progress if you don’t show your passion and drive for what you do. Has had a promotion to L&D Adviser from March 2016 and signed up to the CMDA. title: Jackie Seymour
headings: Human Resources Jackie Seymour – Undergraduate Centre Manager Explore
body: Portsmouth Business School I joined Portsmouth Polytechnic back in 1989 as a course secretary on the Milton site - the main requirement on the person specification was for RSA typewriting grade 3 which I had achieved by attending adult education. I was from Priory School and back then no one from my peer group went on to college, let alone University. My mum said to me when I was about 15 - learn to type and you'll always have a job - and she was right. I was the first Business School employee to have a desktop computer, can you imagine producing transcripts in carbon copy on a typewriter! I spent my early years at the University engaged in course administration, working part time for quite a while whilst raising a family. The excellent family friendly policies of the University enabled me to change my working hours to suit this family life. It was after about 15 years that things started to change for me, mainly because my children were growing up and I returned to work full time. My attitude to work changed and I wanted something more than 'just working'. The University created Senior Course Administrators and I was appointed to be one having worked unofficially in this capacity for quite some time. At the same time Portsmouth Business School developed the Foundation degree in Business and Management and I was lucky to be encouraged and funded to be on the first cohort. Then began five years of very hard work, part time study and working full time, achieving firstly the FDA, and then a BA (Hons) Business and Management, achieving a 2:1. During this time I applied for and was appointed to a secondment role, as a Jupiter Implementation Specialist on the project team for the roll out of the current student records system. I did this for a year and then my current role was advertised, Undergraduate Centre Manager for Portsmouth Business School. I would not have stood a chance of getting this role if I had not taken the secondment opportunity, always taken any training opportunities as they were offered, and embarked on the FDA. Every day is a school day as I have learnt to lead and manage staff. I have a commitment to continuous development for both myself and my staff and would like to think we are one of the most highly trained units in the University. I would urge staff to complete all of the training offered by MSD, and also to consider external suppliers. The Association of Business Schools development programme for senior managers exposed me to national issues affecting all universities, I was weak on budgets and the NOV8 Budgets for non-financial managers was extremely useful, and ACAS training on extreme persistent workplace behaviours enlightening. I have found that whilst the University has a whole raft of policies and procedures that I must comply to and that help me do my job - it is only by attending training that brings them to life, and the opportunity to discuss real life scenarios is essential. I have a keen interest in behaviour in the workplace, and have lately been attending CIPD events learning about neuroscience. I am also a member of the Association of University Administrators and attend their conferences when I can. Portsmouth Business School and the University have been absolutely great in enabling me as a late starter to develop myself as an individual and a manager. My advice to others - if a window of opportunity opens, don't be scared - jump through it, you have no idea where you might land Has signed up to the MBA Strategic Leadership DA. I am also a certified Business Analyst holding the BCS International Diploma in Business Analysis. As a Course Administrator I completed the following training sessions: Effective report writing Minutes' secretary Training on using Corporate systems – HEMIS During my Jupiter Implementation work I attended the following training: Developing your supervisory skills, course Effective recruitment and selection process, course Making effective presentations, course Training on using Corporate systems – Jupiter/Student Records/Reporting tools As a Course Administrator I completed the following training sessions: Effective report writing Training on using Corporate systems – HEMIS During my Jupiter Implementation work I attended the following training: Developing your supervisory skills, course Effective recruitment and selection process, course Making effective presentations, course Training on using Corporate systems – Jupiter/Student Records/Reporting tools As a Business Analyst I successfully completed the following training: Introductory certificate in Project management, course ITIL course - The Information Technology Infrastructure Library for managing IT as a service Programme of learning modules leading towards the Business Analyst certification - Organisation context, Requirements Engineering, Business Analysis Essentials, Modelling Business Processes. The conclusion to this programme is an Oral Exam at the headquarters of BCS (The Chartered Institute for IT) in London Influencing and Communicating with Customers, course Leader and Manager as Coach, course Safeguarding: Governor's responsibility, course As a Business Analyst I successfully completed the following training: Introductory certificate in Project management, course ITIL course - The Information Technology Infrastructure Library for managing IT as a service Programme of learning modules leading towards the Business Analyst certification - Organisation context, Requirements Engineering, Business Analysis Essentials, Modelling Business Processes. The conclusion to this programme is an Oral Exam at the headquarters of BCS (The Chartered Institute for IT) in London Influencing and Communicating with Customers, course Leader and Manager as Coach, course Safeguarding: Governor's responsibility, course HE experience - Key skill areas I love my current job: it has “novelty factor” and every day brings something new. I embrace challenges and genuinely enjoy being at the heart of change initiatives – be it a new computer system we implement or simply changing how we do things as an organisation. I always look for opportunities to develop and learn, and my work allows me (with support of my line manager) to explore different analysis techniques, meet new people and learn more about the University as a business. Analytical and critical thinking I feel confident making presentations and consider myself an engaging speaker I enjoy sharing my knowledge empowering other people I am open-minded and creative, always trying to think outside the box I have always been a very organised person, and through my work experience I now have mastered the organisational and prioritisation skills. These are the transferrable skills that any job would require. Also critical thinking, flexible approach and ability to adapt are very useful no matter what you do. Starting as a Course Administrator has helped me to understand the main purpose of the University business: enabling young people to have the best start in life, setting aspirations and opening career opportunities for them. No matter what work I do I always have this core business perspective in mind. Thinking of my future progression I am currently exploring options and work experience opportunities that might bring me to the Project Management career path. Experiences / Learning points Throughout my studies I have learnt a few important lessons: Education is only an enabler of further development – one can always learn through self-study and experience Not be afraid to make mistakes – failure is a learning experience too! Promoted from Business Analyst to Project Manager from January 2017 and attended Aurora programme in 2016/17.
