title: Things to Consider
headings: Human Resources UNIVERSITY WIDE WORK SHADOWING OPPORTUNITIES Considerations for Work Shadowing For the Shadow: For the Host: Explore
body: What are the key areas you would like to learn more about from the host department? Do you have any specific questions? Do you have key objectives you would like to achieve? Do you have a draft outline of what the shadow could include? Where will the shadow help you in your role? What do you hope to gain from the experience? What do you hope to do differently as a result of the shadow? How do you think will this change your behaviour/practice? How will you capture your learning? How will you share your experience/findings with your own department and host department? When is the best time for a shadow to get the good overview of the role? What does the shadow hope to get from the process? What would you need them to know more about? Are there any confidential issues? Are there any health and safety issues? Are there any additional support requirements that you need to be aware of ? Do you need to clarify the aims and objectives of the shadow? Do you need to agree the structure of the shadow, an outline of what is included and inform staff involved? Could this be a development opportunity for a member of staff to be a host? title: Top Tips
headings: Human Resources University-Wide Work Shadowing Opportunities Top Tips for Making the Most of the Work Shadowing Experience Explore
body: Read the information sheets & be realistic about what can be achieved in the time available. A work shadow or placement will be useful if you have thought about what you want to achieve. A genuine interest in the area is vital as it can be a time consuming process for the host Take notes – you may come away with a very useful list of contacts, names, phone numbers, email addresses etc , including facts thoughts and observations. Spend some time sorting through these notes afterwards to make the most of them. Talk to a variety of people at different levels. This will give you a better insight into the organisation and how the department works. Volunteer to do things (if appropriate) – people like someone who is helpful – if you do basic things well you are more likely to be offered work with more responsibility. Watch and observe – you can learn a lot by watching how others work. Ask questions – it is easy for others to assume you know how things work when often you don’t. Make sure you achieve what you wanted to from the experience – if it falls short let someone know how to address it. Consider what could be gained if the Work Shadow was extended. title: Case Studies
headings: Human Resources Work Shadowing case studies Senior case study: Bob Nichol and Graham Galbraith Explore
body: Bob Nichol, Pro Vice-Chancellor (Research and Innovation) took part in work shadowing two years ago. After 13 years working at the University, he realised he’d like to find out more about more central University roles. Therefore, he decided to jump straight in at the deep end and ask to shadow the Vice-Chancellor Graham Galbraith. From his position at the time, as Director of the Institute of Cosmology and Gravitation, Bob saw the shadowing scheme as a great opportunity to understand how his work interacted with the wider University and how he may get involved. Bob met with Graham to discuss what he wanted to achieve from this opportunity, which allowed them to develop a mutual understanding. Bob really feels this is key to the shadowing scheme to ensure it is personal and to establish if you will be able to build a good rapport. The programme was flexible to suit both sides. Bob was invited to attend three events, including a University Executive Board (UEB) meeting, where he took pride in seeing how passionate the University’s leaders were in supporting and promoting the success and wellbeing of the University. He also attended a Governors’ meeting, where he was impressed at how complex problems were dealt with. Lastly, Bob was invited to an away day with Universities UK, when 19 Vice-Chancellors of universities around the UK got together. He was impressed at the complexities and diversity of problems discussed, and often there were no easy answers. Bob summarised by saying: “I had no expectations going into the programme and just knew that I wanted to know more about the central services provided. Graham was very supportive and we worked together very well. I definitely had a much better appreciation of the difficulty of his role and it was the first of many of a wider programme of development.” Graham said: “It was a great experience to work with Bob and show him some of my duties as Vice-Chancellor of the University. It’s good to see that this didn’t put him off now that he is the Pro Vice-Chancellor (Research and Innovation)! I felt we both learned a lot from the whole process and would encourage more staff to take up the opportunity to shadow colleagues across the University.” HR Business Partnering Team Learning and Development My Rewards and Benefits Occupational Health Service Equality and Diversity Contact and departments

title: Staff Essentials - 2017 Presentations
headings: Staff Essentials University Roundup Parallel Session Summaries Nifty Networking! - How to Enhance Your Ability to Network* Athena SWAN Supporting Support Staff* Understanding and Working Through Change Working Collaboratively To Achieve Our Own Wellbeing -A Focus on Mental Health* Getting to Know our Technical Staff -1/Getting to Know our Technical Staff- 2* Working Collaboratively and Sharing Best Practice-1 /Sharing Best Practice -2* Coaching To Achieve Our Goals* MyPort – Changing Service Delivery Across The University* Working With Social Media to Achieve Our Goals* * Internal only
body: Welcome to Staff Essentials, providing news, events and useful information for University of Portsmouth staff. Finance presentation* Academic Registry presentation* Library presentation* Student Union Presentation* This is a practical and interactive session that will give you the tools to improve and enhance your networking skills. The session will encourage you to…… Reflect on what you hope to achieve from networking Introduce some ideas for conversation openers Consider various opportunities to network and your current network Consider the value and benefit of wider University skills and knowledge Athena Swan is a robust and challenging framework which aims to tackle gender inequality in Higher Education. This interactive session aims not just to inform staff about Athena SWAN but to learn from their experiences as support staff. What are the key issues facing staff at the University and how can we address them? What actions can we take that will help us move forward? Learning from this session will inform the drafting of the University's submission for Athena SWAN Bronze status under the extended principles in November 2017. Introduction to Prosci’s ADKAR® Model: Individuals make changes successfully when they have the necessary Awareness, Desire Knowledge, Ability and Reinforcement to do so. Users of Prosci’s ADKAR Model have the outcome-orientation to change that enables successful personal transitions and achievement of organisational results. This short introduction to focuses on the five key building blocks that form the basis of the Prosci® ADKAR® Model. This session will fully inform you about the resources and services on offer at the University of Portsmouth to support your own Mental Health Wellbeing and that of your colleagues and students. In this collaborative and interactive approach you’ll hear from experts in Occupational Health, Student Wellbeing and the Chaplaincy. Do you want to know more about the role of a technician? Discover the diverse range of services covered by the university’s technical team by looking at a snapshot of internal and external projects driven by University of Portsmouth Technician’s. The ever changing needs of the university's customers mean we need to continuously improve the support that we provide. There is lots of great work going on around the university to improve the customer experience, but often we don't get a chance to hear about what our colleagues in other departments do. The Delivering Excellence programme is an opportunity for staff to share the excellent service they provide and focus on developing a service initiative in the form of a project. This parallel session focuses on 5 of those presentations with emphasis on work that was produced via collaboration with other university colleagues, or can be shared as an example of service that could be applied in other departments. An interactive and thought provoking session, providing an opportunity to explore: The astonishing results and positive outcomes of Coaching. The MyPort session will aim to give an update on the background of, and reasons for the programme, as well as show how the new digital service is being used. We will also take a look ahead at what changes are being planned. We live in a world of digital by default, something that can be capitalised upon by companies, institutions and individuals alike. This session will look at the future of the University on social media, what we as staff can do to support this as well as using social media to build your own profile and progress your career. Whether you want to make your LinkedIn profile sparkle, engage with more people on Twitter, or simply want to understand what the University is doing so you can use best practice to support this, this session can offer insight into the world of social media.

title: Delivering Successful Change- Additional Resources:Analyse
headings: Human Resources Delivering Successful Change Additional Resources ‌ Explore

title: Analyse- extended
headings: Human Resources Delivering Successful Change Extended Guidance 1. Why is the change important? 2. What are you changing to? 3. What resources will be needed? 4. What might go wrong? 5. How might others be affected or affect the success of the change? 6. When will your planned change happen? Explore
body: Every change situation is unique and, although previous experience allows you to identify what has worked and not worked in the past, you’ll want to make sure that the approach you use is the best for your particular circumstances. You can ensure this by considering a series of questions and using your answers to design your approach. The reasons behind your planned change will affect the path you follow and style you use (for example, if in response to a crisis then more likely to require a directive, ‘big bang’ approach, whereas a longer term change in direction is likely to be more participative and evolutionary). The reasons for change that you identify are going to be key to your communications, and will help individuals to buy in to the change. What do I need to do? This will depend on the size and scope of your change, but you should be able to: Identify the reasons for change, including why it has to happen now. For example there may be changes to the funding environment or the University’s strategic priorities, or technology may be reaching obsolescence. Identify the benefits of the change, which may include efficiency savings, improvements to the services you provide, or compliance with standards or regulations. Identify the likely impact if the change doesn’t happen. What should this look like? This may be no more than a short text, summarising the reasons, benefits and likely ‘do-nothing’ outcomes. For major change programmes, this is likely to be a more detailed analysis. Your vision of the future needs to be sufficiently compelling for staff to feel that the effort to change will be worth it. It also represents a valuable reference to guide the behaviours that staff will be expected to adopt to reinforce the changes required. What do I need to do? Think about the result you’re trying achieve, in terms of the services you offer (or plan to offer), new or changed practices and procedures, behaviours you will expect, any symbols that are representative of the new state, new workforce structures, or new monitoring and control systems. If the change you are planning might have an contractual impact on staff, you need to identify any formal change processes or procedures that will be necessary. For example, if your change may result in redundancies, redeployments, regradings or relocation, or require formal staff consultation you must seek advice from your HR Business Partner team. What should this look like? This could be no more than a short text, describing your future vision. For major change programmes, or those involving formal change processes, this will need to be a detailed definition of the future state. Identifying the resources you’ll need to introduce your change serves several purposes: it will help to decide if the change is feasible and justifiable; it allows you to make the necessary preparations to ensure resources are available when you need them; and it helps you to identify key stakeholders who control resources you will require. If your planned change requires substantial resources, you will be expected to submit a business case to justify the allocation of these resources. What do I need to do? Consider the resources you will need, including: finance, time, people, equipment and technology. You may need to talk to relevant stakeholders to understand what your dependencies on specific resources might be. You also need to think about alternative resources if those you require aren’t available within the budget and/or timescale available to you. What should this look like? For a simple change, you should be able to produce a simple grid linking the quantity of each resource type required to stages in your planned change: as you refine the timings of your planned change this builds into a resource profile for your change. For a major change programme, you will need to prepare a detailed resource profile and associated costings. Change generally involves doing things you haven’t done before, or that are likely to disrupt day-to-day operations. By considering what might go wrong, you can put plans in place so that you know what course of action to take should these situations occur. What do I need to do? Consider any unexpected events (“risks”) which, if they occurred, could derail the introduction of your planned change. The key is to focus on those most likely to happen, or with the greatest potential impact, rather than to identify every possible eventuality. It can be useful to use a framework to help identify potential events (e.g. PESTLE). What should this look like? You should be able to produce a simple grid listing the identified events which could occur, and for each event: the consequence of the event, resulting impact on the change, the likelihood of the event occurring, the scale of the impact, and the appropriate response should the event occur. For major change programmes, a detailed risk plan may be necessary. There will be individuals or groups who have the power to influence the success of your planned change. By getting onside those who could hold back the change, and gaining the active support of those who could facilitate it, you create a powerful force for change. By identifying these individuals and groups (known as “stakeholders”), and their attitudes and influence, you form the basis for planning your communications. Your stakeholders may include: senior managers, members of your team, recipients of the services you provide, other colleagues who deal with the same people, or those who provide services to you. If you are able to address any issues or concerns they may have and to get their support for your planned change, you greatly increase your chances of success. What do I need to do? It’s usually best to take a systematic approach to assessing stakeholders, by: Identifying your key stakeholders; Deciding the degree of influence each stakeholder has on your change; Deciding the level of interest each stakeholder has in your change; Using this information to identify your key stakeholders and their potential impact on the change; Considering any actions that will be necessary to manage these stakeholders. What should this look like? You should be able to list your key stakeholders, and possibly plot them on a grid showing current levels of interest and influence, and the levels you would wish them to be at. For each stakeholder, you should be able to list the actions required to move them to the desired levels, together with when this needs to happen. There are usually multiple change activities happening at any one time, so knowing when you expect to introduce your change helps colleagues to plan accordingly, and flag up if and when difficulties are likely to occur. The expected duration of your change may affect the path and style (for example, a rapid change may require a directive, ‘big bang’ approach, whereas a long term change may be more participative and evolutionary). What do I need to do? It’s usually best to take a systematic approach to working out your timescales, by: Identifying the key stages and/or activities in your planned change. Using any significant decision points as key milestones. Deciding when the implementation of your planned change will start. Plotting the key stages and decision points on a simple timeline. What should this look like? You should be able to produce a timeline for the change, including: The key stages or activities in your planned change, with planned start and end dates. The key decision points, with expected decision dates. title: Prepare-extended
headings: Human Resources Delivering Successful Change Extended Guidance 1. Understanding the context of your planned change 2. Supporting personal transitions 3. Identifying potential areas of resistance 4. Designing appropriate interventions 5. Linking communications to your planned change Pulling it all together Explore
body: Once you understand the change you want to put in place, you can put the necessary preparations in place, including support for individuals to deal with change, identifying and sequencing change activities, developing your stakeholder management and communications plans, and revising relevant policies and processes ready for your change. You can prepare for your change by considering the series of activities below. Successful change is heavily dependent on the environment in which it occurs. By understanding the specific features of your planned change you can make appropriate choices about the design of your planned change which will maximise your chances of success. In examining the context of your planned change, areas you will want to consider include: The time available to achieve the change. The scope of the change. The extent existing practices or assets must be preserved. The diversity of the staff group involved. The capability of the organisation in managing change successfully. The capacity for change, in terms of the resource available. The degree of readiness for change within the staff group. Where power is vested in the organisation. This information can be used to make appropriate decisions, including: The path the change will follow The starting point for the change The management style which will be applied to the initiative. The target of the change initiative. The roles which will be leading and implementing the changes. What do I need to do? The degree of analysis you undertake will depend on the size and scope of your change, but you may wish to use this context assessment template to understand the nature of your planned change, and this options assessment to examine your implementation options. What should this look like? You can record your findings using the two assessment templates (context assessment, options assessment) Individuals will deal with your planned change in different ways, and adapt at different speeds. If you can understand how individuals are likely to respond to and deal with your planned changes you can provide the support they need to successfully adapt to your change. Individual transitions can be likened to the bereavement and mourning process, with individuals experiencing an initial sense of loss, and working through a series of feelings before coming to terms with the change. This can be modelled using the transition curve. It is also important to understand that for individuals to be prepared to change they need to have accepted that: Problems with the current situation mean it cannot continue. The planned changes are a viable way to resolve the acknowledged problems. The effort to change is outweighed by the need to change. What do I need to do? Consider those individuals and groups you need to engage with your planned change and consider what interventions you will need to put in place to change skills, values, attitudes and behaviours. What should this look like? It may be appropriate to capture the actions identified in a Personal Change Plan. The transition curve mentioned above shows that change can be an emotional and difficult process for many individuals. Initial resistance may be a natural response to change, which will need to be worked through before individuals can start to accept, and adapt to, change. Individual personal change plans will highlight individuals' barriers to change. You can also carry out a force field assessment to identify the drivers for change, along with potential barriers to change. There are many potential causes of resistance, including: Self-interest and politics Issues associated with personal loss and potential loss of status, separation from colleagues, or personal inconvenience. Psychological reasons Issues such as fear of the unknown, or concern about ability to develop new skills. May include lack of motivation or demoralisation, a denial of the need to change, or concerns about job security and impact on income levels. Lack of participation, involvement and communication. Individual perceptions To do with lack of understanding about why change is needed and its implications, different assessments of what is needed and the likely outcomes of proposed changes. There may be a lack of trust resulting from experiences of previous change initiatives. Entrenched ways of thinking, or conflict between proposed changes and existing values and beliefs. Traditional relationships between managers and unions or the workforce, rivalries or poor relationships between functions or departments. What do I need to do? Carrying out a force field assessment for your planned change will help you to identify the key barriers and drivers for change. In addition to potential areas of resistance this will help you to identify areas of positive influence which could be used to guide the change. Potential areas of resistance may also have been identified in any personal change plans produced earlier, or in the stakeholder assessment carried out in the Analyse stage. By identifying potential areas of resistance, and referring to the table below, you can identify the most useful types of intervention to overcome this resistance. Education and communication Sharing information such as reasons for change, scope of change, benefits and timetables. Helps overcome negative perceptions and psychological fears of change. Participation and involvement Staff can be included in working groups, or through consultation. If staff feel that they are involved and have contributed to the change they are less likely to resist it. Can help reduce concerns about the impact of change and the ability to cope. Facilitation and support Including the creation of peer support networks, providing access to counselling or to support and advice networks, or training in new technical or business skills. May be critical for some staff who are fearful of the impact of change. Negotiation and agreement Common approach in a unionised organisation, involving discussions with staff and their representatives to resolve areas of dispute. Can help overcome resistance due to self-interest. Manipulation and co-option Attempting to influence others in a more covert manner, by managing how information is presented, or inviting influential and obstructive stakeholders to join working groups. Can help to gain support of influential individuals but will react negatively if perceive they are being manipulated. Explicit or implicit coercion Individuals are forced to accept change, through implicit or explicit threats. Only likely to be successful if rapid change is essential. Identifying and sequencing appropriate interventions allows you to facilitate the process of individual transitions. Your interventions should all lead to the desired changes in values and behaviours. Your choice of interventions will be influenced by the context of your change and the implementation choices, for example at least some interventions may be determined by any formal or statutory procedures. These interventions can be targeted at three levels: Changing skills, values, attitudes and behaviours. Structures and Systems Changing formal and informal organisational structures, systems, processes and procedures. Organisational Environment Changing the management style and the way people relate to each other. and will be different for each stage in your change. You can also model these with the personal transition curve, to help you design appropriate interventions at each stage of your change. You can also use the drivers for change you identified in the force field assessment to design positive interventions to help drive your change. Although not an exhaustive list, your interventions are likely to include: Changes to supporting structures and systems. Visible early wins to demonstrate success. Changes to day-to-day activities to align to new practices. The removal of barriers to change. Symbolic changes to reinforce your change. Communications, training and personal development. What do I need to do? Consider the ideas you identified above, and information from the Analyse stage, and use the transition curve model to help identify the interventions you will need. What should this look like? You could record your planned interventions using this template. In a change situation, recipients of communications messages will act upon their interpretation of a message, which may not be the same as was intended. This interpretation may be affected by previous ways of doing things, or by personal circumstances and experiences. Communications during change must take this into account. A key technique for overcoming misunderstanding is to use message repetition: where the same message is repeated in different ways, or conveyed using different means of communication. The purpose of communications will change during each stage of your planned change and it is important to communicate relevant information at the time it is needed, in a way that meets the needs of the audience. As a general rule, for more complex change, richer forms of communication (for example group meetings and workshops) are more effective. They allow for the expression of concerns, answers to questions, the sharing of interpretations and experiences and the sharing of problems and solutions. Less rich forms (such as newsletters, manuals or guidance) can be used to provide updates on progress, confirm the outcomes of face-to-face meetings, or as reference material. Overly rich communication for a routine change can cause confusion, whereas overuse of less rich communication for complex change can result in too little information being shared, leading to mistrust and lack of commitment. It is important to actively seek, and act upon, feedback to ensure your communications are having the desired impact. Communication purpose Creating readiness Prepare staff Challenge the status quo Spread understanding of need for change Requires rich communication (e.g. face-to-face) Challenge the status quo Spread understanding of need for change Requires rich communication (e.g. face-to-face) Providing explanation Reduce uncertainty/ambiguity. Provide staff with information to fulfil their role during change. Enable staff to undertake needed change. Predominantly requires rich communication Reduce uncertainty/ambiguity. Provide staff with information to fulfil their role during change. Enable staff to undertake needed change. Predominantly requires rich communication Providing updates Keep staff informed of progress. Prevent uncertainty/anxiety. More likely to be routine communication (e.g. written) Keep staff informed of progress. Prevent uncertainty/anxiety. What do I need to do? The type and volume of communication required will obviously depend on the individual change situation, although for any change it is important to identify the messages you need to communicate during each stage of your planned change. For each message, you should be able to identify: the change stage, the headline message, the intended audience, the message content and the communications medium. The interventions you identified earlier will have a bearing on the communications you will need, as may any actions you identified during your previous stakeholder analysis. What should this look like? You should be able to record your key messages in a communications plan, for example using this template. During the Analyse and Prepare stages you will have identified a range of actions which need to take place at specific times during your planned change. Co-ordinating these effectively is an important factor in the success of your change, and by collating these actions and interventions into a single schedule you can: Ensure all activities are included and scheduled to happen when they need to. Prepare decision-making bodies for the decisions they need to take. Plan the resources you need to carry out the identified actions. Identify dependencies between actions. Highlight areas of concern, such as overlaps with key University events. Once you have your schedule in place, you can use this as the basis for future planning: as actions are completed, timings change, or new actions identified you can update your schedule and see the impact on the rest of your planned change. What do I need to do? For each stage in your planned change you should be able to identify the actions which need to take place. These will include actions identified: During the Analyse stage: - Actions to deliver the planned change - Resource planning actions - Risk management actions - Stakeholder management actions - Stage and decision points timeline During the Prepare stage: - Personal change plans - Communications plan Within each stage, the actions should be arranged in the order they need to happen, and any dependencies between them noted. Once the actions have been ordered, you can arrange the resources you’ll need. What should this look like? For each stage you should be able to identify the required activities, and for each activity, the owner, forecast approximate start and end dates, the resources required, and any dependencies on other activities. These actions can be presented in a delivery schedule. title: Deliver-extended
headings: Human Resources Delivering Successful Change Extended Guidance 1. Measuring change outcomes 2. Managing change roles 3. Managing change activities 4. Monitoring progress 6. Managing communications and feedback Explore
body: Although a fundamental factor in the success of any transition is the quality of thought and planning that has gone into it, the implementation is unlikely to progress in a convenient linear fashion in accordance with carefully laid plans. The implementation is more likely to be an evolving process, with uncertain and unpredictable outcomes. The nature of the implementation usually means that active management is needed to resolve problems as they arise and to keep the change process on track. To spell out how behaviours and outputs need to change, you need to establish some measures of success for the change process, based on the outcomes you wish to achieve. Identifying measurable aspects of the change means you can assess whether the desired behaviours and outputs are happening as expected. Outcomes are typically assessed through a mix of qualitative measures (such as levels of commitment, customer satisfaction or absenteeism), or quantitative measures (such as costs, quality measures, or cycle times). What do I need to do? If you consider the outcomes you are trying to achieve, you should be able to identify measures you could use to judge how successful your change is. It is important to take care that your measures aren’t also affected by unexpected outcomes (such as absenteeism reducing because staff are scared for their jobs rather than being more motivated) and so may need a mix of measures to give you a true picture. Bear in mind that it is preferable for the measures you use to be simple to measure and not require additional resource to obtain them. Once you have identified your measures you need to judge how you expect them to change during the implementation of the change. What should this look like? For each measure you should be able to record the measure title, a brief explanation of what outcomes the measure is assessing, and a profile of the expected measure values throughout the change implementation. The measures can be recorded using this template. There are often three key change roles participating in the implementation of the change: The facilitators of the change (change agents) Need to understand the business, buy-in to the change vision, and understand the techniques of change management. Need to have change process skills, including: communication, consultation, team building and motivation skills. Need to have change process skills, including: communication, consultation, team building and motivation skills. Those in a management role Often have to fulfil a complex role, including: undertaking personal change themselves, implementing the change in their business areas, leading and supporting their staff through change, and maintain operational performance. Need to have: Verbal communication skills to help sell the change to their staff. Team building skills to form new teams to work together in the new environment. Coaching and counselling skills to help team members deal with change. Negotiating skills to help resolve problems encountered with new practices. Verbal communication skills to help sell the change to their staff. Team building skills to form new teams to work together in the new environment. Coaching and counselling skills to help team members deal with change. Negotiating skills to help resolve problems encountered with new practices. The recipients of the change Those on the receiving end of change must adapt to new practices, and the provision of help and support should be designed to ease their journey along the transition curve. Reducing ambiguity and uncertainty during the implementation can contribute greatly to individual transitions. Reducing ambiguity and uncertainty during the implementation can contribute greatly to individual transitions. It is important that you ensure the individuals participating in each of these roles have the necessary skills, or know where they can go to get help to develop them. They also need a clear understanding of their role in the implementation of the change and what is expected of them at each stage of the implementation. What do I need to do? The first step is to identify the key individuals in each of these roles: they may form part of a team specifically to implement the change, they may be separate individuals, or a combination of the two. The stakeholder analysis from the Prepare stage may help here, along with any change plans identified for specific individuals in the Analyse stage. Once you have identified the individuals, you should make sure they have the skills they will need, or are able to get help to develop them. You should also make sure they understand their role and what is expected of them. What should this look like? For each role, record the: Named individuals who have that role - What the role is responsible for providing - What the role can expect to receive (such as any support available) These can be recorded on this template. This is the point where your careful planning pays dividends: the delivery schedule you produced in the Prepare stage forms the basis of the activity you need to manage. By following the schedule you can make sure activities are taking place as and when they should, and also that resources are in place in readiness for future activities. As with most change initiatives, unexpected issues, lessons learned and external factors are likely to influence your plans and mean that you will want to revisit areas covered in previous stages. You will be able to use your initial schedule as the basis on which to judge the impact of any proposed alterations. What do I need to do? Your role as change manager is to ensure the change is delivered to the required standard according to the anticipated schedule. As issues arise, you will need to revisit your plans and amend them accordingly. Any changes to your plans to deliver the change may result in changes to other aspects of your change. Depending on the scale of the change, these may include some or all of: your resource plan, risk management actions, stakeholder management actions, key decision points, personal change plans, interventions plans or to your communications plan. An important role you have is to make sure you carry out the activities when they are required, or to delegate activities appropriately to give people time to complete then to your desired timescales. If your are delegating activities, it is important that you make sure that what is required, when it is required, the standard expected, the resources available, and how issues and progress are to be communicated are clearly understood. When delegating work, you may wish to adopt the following tactics to improve your chances of success: Rather than give people completion dates to work to, ensure they focus on completing the activity, to the required standard, as quickly as possible. Limit the amount of multitasking you expect people to take on. When checking on progress, ask people to forecast when they expect to complete their activity, rather than asking for a percentage complete. If you are both the person responsible for managing the change and for carrying out some or all of the change activities, you will need to take care to balance the two roles: it is easy to focus on the change activities to the detriment of the overall management of the change initiative. Similarly, if there is significant change to your plans, you can spend so much time replanning that little change progress is made. What should this look like? If you make alterations to your plans you can simply create a new version of that plan, often using one of the templates described in earlier stages. To report on the progress of the change, it is useful to use a highlight report. These are usually produced at regular intervals throughout the change initiative and provide a useful structure to ensure you include all relevant detail. Highlight reports can be used to update relevant management teams or boards, and to communicate progress to stakeholders. Implementation management is necessary to ensure things happen as they were planned. Standard project management techniques can be used to monitor implementation progress against plans, expenditure against budgets, exposure to risks, and so on. You also need to monitor the impact on the change outcomes. Throughout the implementation, you need to compare the actual profiles of the measures you identified for the outcomes with their forecast profiles. This will give you an indication of where the change is progressing as expected, progressing better than expected (which may provide opportunities for faster or more innovative change), or not progressing as well as expected (which might mean that additional interventions are required). Regular monitoring of both the change implementation and the change outcomes mean that any corrective action that is required can be identified and put in place before any lasting impact is experienced. It also helps to identify positive news stories which can be used to promote the change. What do I need to do? You should be able to regularly report on implementation progress against the plan, spend against any implementation budget, the management of identified risks and any issues that arise which need to be escalated. You should also be able to regularly report on the impact of implementation progress on the anticipated change outcomes, any corrective action that is required, and the forecast impact of that corrective action. Implementation plans and schedules must be updated with any planned corrective actions. What should this look like? Implementation progress is generally reported through a highlight report. A more detailed report template is also available from Information Services. The communications plan developed in the Prepare stage will provide the main input to the nature and timing of communications during the implementation of the change. Your plan should include activities to understand the impact of the implementation on the change recipients. Various techniques can be used to gain feedback on the impact of the implementation, including: Focus groups and workshops Groups from different faculties, departments, and services, and from different levels, can be drawn together to review the change process, and potentially offer solutions to issues raised Increasing senior managers' visibility by department or office visits, or explicitly inviting contact by those experiencing the change implementation Regular briefings to keep team members aware of progress and to gain relevant feedback, with an emphasis on issues that affect the staff present. Question and answer sessions Informal Q&A sessions can be used to provide information relating to issues of concern, and to identify what is important to staff Staff feedback schemes To acquire feedback and suggestions for improvements. Surveys and questionnaires To get an indication of attitudes across a wide range of staff. Feedback received may indicate a need to amend your communications approach, which should be reflected in an updated communications plan. What do I need to do? This is a case of carrying out the communications activities identified in your communications plan, learning from feedback received to modify the change implementation, the forecast outcomes, or the communications activities in light of this feedback. What should this look like? This will depend on the context of the change, and the contents of your communications plan. title: Embed-extended
headings: Human Resources Delivering Successful Change Extended Guidance 1. Sharing and promoting new operating policies and practices 2. Supporting staff through continued clarification and skills updates 3. Capturing and fixing failures and deviations in the new state 4. Realising the benefits of the change 5. Capturing lessons learned Explore
body: Once the change implementation is complete, it is vital to ensure new practices are integrated into day-to-day working. Without appropriate support and integration mechanisms in place, there is a danger that people slip back into old ways of working. To ensure new practices are integrated successfully, there need to be: Mechanisms in place to make new operating practices clear and visible Mechanisms in place to ensure that those working in the new way are supported through continued clarification and upgrades of skills and knowledge to carry out the new practices Mechanisms in place to capture and fix failures in new policies, practices or processes A way for people to deal with situations which require a departure from standard practice For people to develop new habits the transition from their old ways of working needs to be as easy as possible. Throughout the change process you will have developed an understanding of individual and team needs, and so will have a clear view of the risk areas which are more vulnerable to slipping into old ways, and of the forces for change which can be used to further promote the new ways of working. Some simple practices you can consider, include: Making new policies and practices accessible and easy to use. Making new policies and practices accessible and easy to use. Removing all references to old policies and practices. Removing all references to old policies and practices. Providing mechanisms for staff to check their work meets the expectations of the new working environment, such as checklists or sample scenarios. Providing mechanisms for staff to check their work meets the expectations of the new working environment, such as checklists or sample scenarios. Linking reward mechanisms to change outcomes, perhaps to reinforce and support demonstrated behavioural change, or to reinforce quality and performance standards. Linking reward mechanisms to change outcomes, perhaps to reinforce and support demonstrated behavioural change, or to reinforce quality and performance standards. Introducing symbolic interventions to reinforce change, such as changes to management behaviours, to team rituals (such as meetings or events), or to job titles or the terminology used within teams Introducing symbolic interventions to reinforce change, such as changes to management behaviours, to team rituals (such as meetings or events), or to job titles or the terminology used within teams What do I need to do? If you consider the new practices from your team’s perspective, you should be able to work out how to make it as easy as possible for them to adopt the new ways of working and gain confidence in working with the new practices. You can also consider introducing symbolic changes to reinforce change, and finding ways of rewarding adoption of the new ways of working. What should this look like? This will depend on the mechanisms you identify, but your expectations in terms of the adoption of new practices, and the reasons for the actions you take, should be clearly communicated. Despite extensive training and communications, it is unlikely that every scenario and eventuality was considered during the planning and implementation of the change, and sometimes those working in the new way are uncertain about what to do differently, find that the skills and knowledge that were good enough for the old way of working no longer suit the new ways, or simply feel that it was easier to work in the old way. By monitoring performance and standards, you can identify when, and how, to intervene to provide appropriate support to remedy deviations from the new way of working. Some approaches to consider include: Reducing ambiguity and uncertainty through additional guidance, case studies or scenario analysis. Reducing ambiguity and uncertainty through additional guidance, case studies or scenario analysis. Enforcing new practices and standards through coaching or additional training. Enforcing new practices and standards through coaching or additional training. Sharing good practice and lessons learned through peer reviews, workshops, or knowledge sharing exercises. Sharing good practice and lessons learned through peer reviews, workshops, or knowledge sharing exercises. What do I need to do? You need to continually evaluate performance, both at an individual and a team level, and actively seek feedback. Where performance standards do not meet expectations you need to identify the most appropriate actions to take. Staff participation in development activities can help with engagement, consolidation of their learning, and provides opportunities for peer support. What should this look like? This will depend on the interventions you decide are appropriate, but it is important to make sure you actively seek feedback on whatever you put in place so that you can learn what works, and what doesn’t, so you can modify them accordingly. When staff encounter problems with the new working practices, there can be a tendency for them to find workarounds for specific problems in order to maintain service levels. These workarounds are often shared informally with the result that new practices start to be applied inconsistently and fail to provide the anticipated benefits of the change. To avoid this happening there must be a mechanism in place for staff to flag up problems and for staff to be empowered to innovate and find appropriate solutions for these problems, possibly through appropriate reward schemes. It is important that solutions are assessed across the team to avoid unforeseen consequences and maintain consistency. Any agreed changes to policies or practices must be shared and promoted in the same way that the original practices were, and all references to policies and practices updated. What do I need to do? By providing appropriate guidance and support, and monitoring performance standards, you should get early warnings of issues with the new working practices. You should encourage staff to identify issues and to propose appropriate solutions. Your reward mechanisms should be aligned to encourage this. Where you make changes to working practices, it is vital that you share and promote these appropriately. What should this look like? You need to provide your team with the means to raise issues and propose potential solutions. You also need to provide a forum where the team can consider these and agree appropriate solutions. Any decisions must be documented and shared appropriately. To share and promote any changes put in place you may adopt similar approaches to those you used to share and promote the original changes. In the Analyse stage, the planned change was justified on the basis of the forecast benefits. These benefits should each have baseline measures of their levels prior to the change, and a forecast profile to indicate how the levels are expected to change as a result of the change. The benefits measures should be regularly monitored and compared to the forecast profile to assess the performance of the benefit realisation. Where benefits fall below the expected values appropriate interventions will need to be taken to bring the realisation back on track. Should benefit realisation proceed better than expected, or if additional opportunities for benefits are identified, then action should be taken to make best use of these. Throughout the benefits realisation, it is important to celebrate success: people bought into the change because they thought the effort was justified by the expected result, so by demonstrating the effectiveness of the change it’ll be easier to get their support next time. What do I need to do? If a benefits realisation plan was agreed in the Analyse you should be able to capture relevant measures to compare to the forecast profiles in the plan. Where there are deviations from the forecast, you should identify the actions to be taken to recover under-performance, or to take advantage of over-performance. Progress should be communicated with relevant stakeholders, to recognise success and to acknowledge where recovery actions are required. What should this look like? You can use a Benefits monitoring log to capture and monitor the realisation of the benefits. Each change implementation will be unique and so there will always be the potential for learning throughout the change. Some lessons learned will be applicable only to the specific change instance, but other lessons will be relevant across the team, the department or across the wider organisation. Lessons could be learned in three key areas: the change approach selected will always be a judgement call, so experience may tell you particular approaches are more, or less, successful in particular circumstances. Policies and practices as you start to work with the new policies and work practices, you may discover omissions, better ways to do things, or assumptions which proved incorrect. Opportunities for change as you gain experience with the new working practices, you may come across new opportunities to make further improvements. For each lesson, you need to answer these questions: What is the area of interest? What is the area of interest? How might this be applied in future? How might this be applied in future? What actions will take place as a result? What actions will take place as a result? What do I need to do? As soon as you become aware of learning which will be of use in the future, you should record it so it doesn’t get forgotten. By formally reviewing the lessons learned you can decide how best to share them and apply to future activities. What should this look like? You can use this template to record your lessons learned. A desktop widget is a small program such as a clock or a calendar that runs on the Desktop. A mobile widget offers the same functionality on a smartphone. WIFI – A local area network that uses high frequency radio signals to transmit and receive data over distances of a few hundred feet; uses ethernet protocol. XML – A versatile type of document that can store various types of information. (Short for Extensible Markup Language.) Back to top XML – A versatile type of document that can store various types of information. (Short for Extensible Markup Language.) ZIP file – A file that can contain a number of compressed documents or files. Back to top ZIP file – A file that can contain a number of compressed documents or files. Contact and departments

title: TEL Training calendar
headings: Department for Curriculum and Quality Enhancement Developing digital competencies For enquiries regarding booking, please contact elearn@port.ac.uk. Digital Learning: Basic Building Blocks Digital Learning: Next Steps Digital Learning: Exploring Other Technologies Explore DCQE Sections
body: The Technology Enhanced Learning (TEL) team, in collaboration with the Academic Development section of DCQE, have developed a new familiarisation programme to help colleagues find out more about using digital technologies to complement and enhance their teaching and interactions with students. The programme covers a range of different areas, from Moodle basic building blocks to utilising social media, copyright material and the virtual classroom. The Developing Digital Competencies programme is designed for a wide range of colleagues – those who are new to digital learning technologies to those who have a wide knowledge (but can always learn more!). The programme comprises a series of sessions grouped under three headings: Basic Building Blocks; Next Steps; and Exploring other technologies. The programme not only considers the technical aspects but also the underpinning pedagogical rationale needed to answer such questions as: “Why should I use a forum (group activity, wiki or other functionality) in my teaching?” “How will this tool improve my teaching materials?” Basic Building Block sessions: ‘First steps in Moodle’ considers the basics of creating a course in Moodle, for example: adding a file or a folder to a unit; adding images and videos; adding a page (single page, similar to a webpage) and a book (a collection of pages grouped together – very useful for a handbook or similar resource). ‘First steps in Moodle’ considers the basics of creating a course in Moodle, for example: adding a file or a folder to a unit; adding images and videos; adding a page (single page, similar to a webpage) and a book (a collection of pages grouped together – very useful for a handbook or similar resource). ‘Finding out about the Forum and Quiz tools’ demonstrates how to setup and use the forum tool to promote discussion and, in addition, how it can be used as for assessment. The session will also show how the to set up a quiz, use a number of question types and consider some case studies of how colleagues have used the quiz tool to support learning. ‘Finding out about the Forum and Quiz tools’ demonstrates how to setup and use the forum tool to promote discussion and, in addition, how it can be used as for assessment. The session will also show how the to set up a quiz, use a number of question types and consider some case studies of how colleagues have used the quiz tool to support learning. ‘Online assessment’ explores how colleagues can use Moodle and Turnitin for assignment handling. We will consider the entire workflow from setting up a submission, marking on and offline, and finally creating online feedback. In the session we will also look at the advantages (and disadvantages) of using desktops, laptops, iPads, Nexus tablets and other equipment for the electronic management of assessment. ‘Online assessment’ explores how colleagues can use Moodle and Turnitin for assignment handling. We will consider the entire workflow from setting up a submission, marking on and offline, and finally creating online feedback. In the session we will also look at the advantages (and disadvantages) of using desktops, laptops, iPads, Nexus tablets and other equipment for the electronic management of assessment. ‘Rubrics: Moodle and Turnitin’ explores how colleagues can use Moodle and Turnitin rubrics to standardise their assignment marking. We will consider the entire workflow from setting up a rubric and then attaching it to the assignment, marking and creating online feedback with the rubric as a backbone to the process. ‘Rubrics: Moodle and Turnitin’ explores how colleagues can use Moodle and Turnitin rubrics to standardise their assignment marking. We will consider the entire workflow from setting up a rubric and then attaching it to the assignment, marking and creating online feedback with the rubric as a backbone to the process. ‘Moodle Baseline’ is a combination of a Module template and a set of best practice that content creators can follow. In this way the University can provide a consistent and comprehensive online learning experience for all of our students at the University of Portsmouth. This session will demonstrate each tab within the baseline and how you can easily incorporate it within your unit. For more information on Moodle Baseline click here. ‘Moodle Baseline’ is a combination of a Module template and a set of best practice that content creators can follow. In this way the University can provide a consistent and comprehensive online learning experience for all of our students at the University of Portsmouth. This session will demonstrate each tab within the baseline and how you can easily incorporate it within your unit. For more information on Moodle Baseline click here. ‘Groups and Groupings’ covers how to create groups and groupings. We will also discuss how groups can be used to allocate marking groups in large courses and how the new functionality linking Moodle to Google groups can be used to email student groups and share documents. ‘Groups and Groupings’ covers how to create groups and groupings. We will also discuss how groups can be used to allocate marking groups in large courses and how the new functionality linking Moodle to Google groups can be used to email student groups and share documents. ‘Creative use of Moodle’ explores the virtual classroom and web conferencing software that is supported by the University; we will discuss how the software can be used inside and outside the classroom, particularly to support distance learners. ‘Creative use of Moodle’ explores the virtual classroom and web conferencing software that is supported by the University; we will discuss how the software can be used inside and outside the classroom, particularly to support distance learners. ‘More about the Moodle Quiz’ expands on the Theme 1 session and looks at all the question types. We will consider how the Moodle quiz can be used as a revision tool, as an examination tool and how to use Moodle in an exam environment. We will look at how to create a quiz workflow to help structure assessments and, finally, we will discuss how to create and use a question bank. ‘More about the Moodle Quiz’ expands on the Theme 1 session and looks at all the question types. We will consider how the Moodle quiz can be used as a revision tool, as an examination tool and how to use Moodle in an exam environment. We will look at how to create a quiz workflow to help structure assessments and, finally, we will discuss how to create and use a question bank. ‘Building in interactivity’ highlights the useful Moodle tools that encourage student interaction and engagement. It will demonstrate how colleagues can use the Workshop tool for peer learning, the database tool for collaboration and the Questionnaire to gain an insight into the students’ perspective. We will also briefly look at the Lesson tool, possibly one of the most powerful tools in Moodle. ‘Building in interactivity’ highlights the useful Moodle tools that encourage student interaction and engagement. It will demonstrate how colleagues can use the Workshop tool for peer learning, the database tool for collaboration and the Questionnaire to gain an insight into the students’ perspective. We will also briefly look at the Lesson tool, possibly one of the most powerful tools in Moodle. ‘The power of the Lesson’ explores the Lesson tool and how it can be used to create workbooks or guide students through the decision making process. We will consider positive and negative aspects of using the tool and how it can be used in a teaching scenario. ‘The power of the Lesson’ explores the Lesson tool and how it can be used to create workbooks or guide students through the decision making process. We will consider positive and negative aspects of using the tool and how it can be used in a teaching scenario. Exploring Additional Technologies sessions: ‘Content Capture and Reusable Learning Materials’ considers various other technologies that can be used for teaching and learning. For example, Relay, Fuse and Studio for lecture capture to deliver to students who are not campus based. These technologies allow students to revisit material for reflection or revision. ‘Enhancing lectures’ looks at technologies that can be used to complement and enhance traditional lectures, for example creating dynamic presentations using Prezi and setting up interactive sessions with Nearpod. We will discuss how these options, and others, can be utilised to accommodate colleagues’ preferred teaching styles and enhance students’ learning. ‘Social networks for teaching and learning’ investigates the potential of using social networking and media to engage students. The session will investigate how lecturers can use blogs, Twitter, Google+ and Facebook to create a more varied and vibrant learning environment. ‘Accessing and using valid, legitimate online resources’ discusses how to access online educational resources from a range of sources in order to provide a varied learning experience. The session will also consider copyright and how to avoid contravening copyright laws. Colleagues attending this session are encouraged to share their data sources and experience of online resources with others in the group. ‘Digital tools for distance learning’ looks at the creation of digital materials and is focused predominantly on distance learning courses - although the materials discussed can easily be transferred to a campus-based course. We examine the equipment you might need to get started, and how you can rework existing content into a digital framework. Colleagues are encouraged to share their experience of developing online resources with others in the group. ‘Facilitating Scenario Based Learning’ with Degree Apprenticeships a new challenge and Distance Learning a growing part of the university global strategy, what can we all do to create interesting resources for students? Scenario based learning is one way to get the students applying their real world experience and knowledge into a learning context. It provides them with a framework to develop their knowledge and look at examples that other students can offer. This session will demonstrate a course workflow that combines the workshop, database, forum and lesson tools to provide an interactive and collaborative learning experience for students. It will look at how each tool is an individual part of a whole process that can help provide a cohesive learning framework. ‘H5P - The Moodle Multi-tool’ highlights the useful H5P suite of tools that is available through Moodle. H5P is a tool that allows the building of interactive and engaging resources. The session will demonstrate how colleagues can use H5P in a variety of ways, briefly looking at all the resources and activities available as part of H5P. Once a brief overview of the tools has been done we will investigate a few specific tools in accordance with the needs of the session attendees. Assessing how best each tool can fit a pedagogic purpose will be a key part of helping decide how to use H5P within a unit site. “Where it is best to use these activities?” Is a question to consider during the selection process so that developing a student's understanding of the course materials is always the driving factor behind each tool's use. title: Building Resilience
headings: Human Resources Learning and Development Title Target Audience Outline The aims of the workshop are to: Learning Outcomes Format Presenters Further Resources Delegate Feedback Explore
body: This workshop is available to all staff. Resilience can be described as 'The human capacity to face, overcome and ultimately be strengthened by adversities and challenges.' Masten A and Yates T M (2004) This half-day workshop aims to increase participants' understanding of personal resilience and ways of developing helpful cognitive, behavioural and emotional responses to situations where pressure has exceeded an ability to cope. Understand what 'resilience' really means Explore factors that impact on resilience and why Identify personal stress triggers Develop practical approaches to build personal resilience. By the end of this workshop participants will: Understand the various factors that impact on resilience Be confidently able to employ strategies to build their own personal resilience. Activites will include fun workshop activities, along with an opportunity for individual reflection, discussion and structured input. Michelle Spirit, FCIPS, Stress Management Consultant Lynsey Sharp: Personal Resilience The Road to Resilience Top Tips for Developing Personal Resilience Staying the Course Personal Resilience 'Really enjoyed this course, one of the best I have attended. I have learnt so many useful tips and information.' 'Many different new ideas and tools to use in different situations.' 'Fabulous course, full of practical solutions.' Click here for the Learning and Development calendar with all available dates. To book a place on this event, please e-mail learninganddevelopment@port.ac.uk HR Business Partnering Team Learning and Development My Rewards and Benefits Occupational Health Service Equality and Diversity Contact and departments

title: Moodle
headings: Department for Curriculum and Quality Enhancement Moodle What is Moodle? Who is it for? Why should I be using it? How do I use Moodle? Where can I access Moodle? Explore DCQE Sections
body: Moodle is a browser-based learning environment that hosts a range of activities and resources for students and staff. Moodle is for students and staff. Students benefit from the wide range of resources available to support their studies. Staff can take advantage of Moodle as a flexible teaching tool. Moodle is the central hub for learning materials and activities for students. Moodle is available 24-hours a day, 7-days a week. If you can access the internet you can access your online unit materials from Moodle via smartphone, laptop or desktop computer. To use Moodle simply visit https://moodle.port.ac.uk and log in with your normal network login. You will see a list of the course and unit sites you are enrolled on. Simply select the link to the unit you want to use and you're ready to get started. If you are a student you will find a range of unit-related activities and resources to engage with. You may also find the student 'Help Pages' useful. Lecturers can create resources and activities by pressing the 'Turn editing on' button in the top-right of unit pages. If you are new to Moodle or would like help creating Moodle activities you can contact the team of Online Course Developers (OCDs) in your Faculty. The central eLearn help desk will also be happy to point in the direction of your local OCD. eLearn are available on 02392 843 355 or elearn@port.ac.uk Help guides can be found on the Staff Help sites page. Why not come along to one of our training sessions to find out more about Moodle, find out what sessions are coming up on our training calendar. Students can access Moodle by clicking the Moodle button in MyPort (myport.ac.uk). Moodle is also available to students via: http://moodle.port.ac.uk Staff can access Moodle via a link in the top-right of the Staff Essentials page (www.port.ac.uk/staff) or directly via: https://moodle.port.ac.uk More technology we advise on Contact and departments

title: ePortfolio
headings: Department for Curriculum and Quality Enhancement ePortfolio What is ePortfolio? Who is it for? Why should I be using it? How do I use ePortfolio? Explore DCQE Sections
body: An electronic portfolio or ePortfolio is a collection of resources and evidence managed by a user online. Such resources and evidence could include PDFs/Word documents, images, multimedia, blog entries, RSS feeds and hyperlinks. ePortfolios are a great tool for showcasing a user’s achievements, skills and self reflection, particularly throughout their journey through higher education. What is an ePortfolio video Mahara is the University’s ePortfolio system. Mahara is an open source stand-alone system that can be integrated into a wider virtual learning framework. The developers behind Mahara have also been heavily involved in the Moodle community, which is why Mahara is often referred to as the ‘sister’ application to Moodle. Once networked together, users can access Mahara via a single sign on to Moodle. Click here for more information on the features of Mahara. ePortfolio is for staff and students who are looking for a platform to showcase their achievements, skills and for self-reflection. ePortfolio is managed by the Department of Employability (DoE) as a tool for student’s Personal Development Planning (PDP). Students can create CVs and ePortfolio pages to showcase their achievements and skills for their tutors and potential employers. ePortfolio is also a great tool for reflection. Academics are currently using the blog tool for students to use as self reflection and the group tool for collaborative work. For help in getting started in ePortfolio please contact elearn@port.ac.uk More technology we advise on Contact and departments

title: WebEx
headings: Department for Curriculum and Quality Enhancement WebEx What is WebEx? Who is it for? Why should I be using it? Explore DCQE Sections
body: WebEx is a proprietary webconferencing system from Cisco, one of the world’s largest networking suppliers (and the suppliers of the University’s telephone system). WebEx is widely used in business. WebEx is for staff who would like to communicate or interact with students in real time, through some combination of voice, video, desktop sharing and text chat. A typical use for this apps is in distance education, but there is other potential uses: one could imagine operating small-group tutorials or virtual “drop-in” centres, for example. Students can create Webex meetings (via http://uop.webex.com) in order to conduct online meetings, presentations or even job interviews. Web conferencing can add great value to a distance learning course. Webex is now integrated with Moodle which allows staff to easily create timed links to Webex classrooms. How do I use/access WebEx? The University has purchased a site license for Webex allow staff and students to create Webex meetings directly from http://uop.webex.com with their normal login credentials. Staff can also create classrooms via Moodle which students can join. More technology we advise on Contact and departments

title: TechSmith – Relay and Fuse
headings: Department for Curriculum and Quality Enhancement TechSmith Relay and TechSmith Fuse What is TechSmith Relay, and TechSmith Fuse, and what is the difference? Who is it for? Why should I be using TechSmith Relay? When might I want my students to use TechSmith Fuse? Where do I find TechSmith Relay? How do I use TechSmith Relay? I have a question... Case Studies: Explore DCQE Sections
body: A simple to use service providing a means to create instructional video by capturing the actions visible upon a computer screen or through the camera of a mobile device using the TechSmith Fuse app. The video can then be shared easily by embedding within Moodle. TechSmith Relay is for University staff to use primarily for content creation, although it may be possible for your students to create content themselves – if you require this option please contact elearn@port.ac.uk with details. Video is a powerful alternative to text-based materials as it can be more engaging and memorable as well as faster and easier for students to consume. It also allows you to 'flip the classroom' (http://www.techsmith.com/education-flipped-classroom.html) allowing you to deliver content in the student's own time and at their own pace, freeing up contact time for interactive discussion. The Fuse app is really straightforward and easy to use. Lecturers around the University have successfully utilised it to record students giving presentations or participating in role-playing scenarios. Students are then able to learn from watching themselves rather than just being told what they did. Ask elearn@port.ac.uk for a demonstration. The TechSmith Relay desktop recorder is available via the Application Jukebox Player on University networked computers. To install on your own device please visit http://relay.port.ac.uk and sign in with your University of Portsmouth account details before selecting 'Download Recorders'. The TechSmith Fuse App for recording from your mobile device is available on Android, iOS and Windows compatible phones and tablets from the appropriate App Store: Android: https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.techsmith.apps.fuse&hl=en iOS: https://itunes.apple.com/gb/app/techsmith-fuse/id725534683 Windows: http://apps.microsoft.com/windows/en-gb/app/techsmith-fuse/286c1f60-ec8d-4159-a6e7-cb6acae5e58b The interface is easy to use, with a big red button to click or press to start your recording, and a stop button to finish and the ability to pause your recording part way through. 'Set up and use' guides will appear here shortly. For more information or further assistance please contact elearn@port.ac.uk. Using TechSmith Relay to record lectures for distance learning students – Michelle Juchau, Lecturer, Learning at Work. Using TechSmith Fuse to record student presentations and mock court room interviews – Charles Barker, Principal Lecturer, School of Law. Using TechSmith Fuse to record students in clinical role-playing scenarios – Mike Leech, Senior Lecturer, School of Pharmacy & Biomedical Sciences. More technology we advise on Contact and departments

title: Turnitin
headings: Department for Curriculum and Quality Enhancement Turnitin What is Turnitin? Who is it for? Why should I be using it? How do I use Turnitin? How can I access Turnitin? Explore DCQE Sections
body: Turnitin is an internet-based plagiarism prevention service that also provides the ability to mark assignments online and provide feedback to students. Turnitin is the worldwide standard in online plagiarism prevention. The software helps staff and students take full advantage of the internet's educational potential. Current policy on the use of Turnitin is available in Academic Regulations. Staff and students can use Turnitin, though staff may choose which functionality they allow students to use: the originality check, allowing submissions of work, or both. Students - to check your work is correctly referenced and to submit assignments electronically if specified. Staff - to check whether your students have referenced correctly and to mark assignments online. Students - student help guides include text and video guides demonstrating how to create originality reports and submit your work. Staff – staff help guides cover: setting up Turnitin assignments on Moodle, working with student submissions both online and offline, and giving other staff access to your Turnitin assignments (for second marking or similar situations). If you would like to attend a workshop or one-to-one session on how to use Turnitin then see our training calendar or contact Tom Langston (tom.langston@port.ac.uk) If you are experiencing technical issues whilst using Turnitin, please contact elearn@port.ac.uk in the first instance. You can access Turnitin within any Moodle site. More technology we advise on Contact and departments
